topics.title: Flat Belt Drive: Belt Construction, Design of Flat Belt Drive with Pulley and Selection of Belts from Catalogues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT521 - DESIGN OF TRANSMISSION ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Flat Belt Drive: Belt Construction, Design of Flat Belt Drive with Pulley and Selection of Belts from Catalogue

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Flat Belt Drive** is a flexible friction drive used for the transmission of power between two parallel shafts over long distances. It consists of an endless flat belt (rectangular cross-section) running over two flat-faced or crowned pulleys. Power is transmitted by virtue of the frictional grip between the belt and the pulley, developed by the wedging action of the belt on the pulley.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Standard:** A flat belt drive is classified as an *open belt drive* when the shafts rotate in the same direction, and a *crossed belt drive* when the shafts rotate in opposite directions. The design must satisfy the *Eytelwein-Capstan equation* relating tensions, coefficient of friction (μ), and angle of contact (θ).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket-Pulley" Intuition

Imagine two smooth, round drums rotating in a workshop. A thick leather strap is draped tightly over both drums. When you pull the strap harder on one side, the drums begin to rotate — one drum "drives" the other purely through friction, just as a tight rubber band rolled over two pencils would cause one pencil to spin when the other is turned. The **tighter side** of the belt (called the *tight side*) does all the work of pulling, while the **slacker side** (called the *slack side*) merely returns. The belt is essentially a "flexible rope" that hugs the pulley surface and transmits tangential force through the friction it generates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Examples:** Flat belt drives were historically used in textile mills, lathes, drilling machines, and conveyor systems. They are still preferred in low-speed, high-power applications such as agricultural machinery, woodworking machines, and industrial fans.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (KTU 2024 Conventions)

- **Standard Pulley Diameters (mm):** 63, 80, 100, 112, 125, 140, 160, 180, 200, 224, 250, 280, 315, 355, 400, 450, 500, 560, 630, 710, 800, 900, 1000
- **Standard Belt Widths (mm):** 25, 32, 40, 50, 63, 71, 80, 90, 100, 112, 125, 140, 160, 180, 200, 224, 250, 280, 315, 355, 400, 450, 500
- **Standard Centre Distances (mm):** 400, 450, 500, 560, 630, 710, 800, 900, 1000, 1120, 1250, 1400, 1600, 1800, 2000
- **Coefficient of Friction (μ) for Leather Belt on Cast Iron Pulley:** **0.25 to 0.35**
- **Density of Leather Belt (ρ):** **1000 kg/m³ = 1 g/cm³**
- **Allowable Working Stress for Leather Belt (σ_allow):** **2.5 to 3.0 MPa** (per IS:1370)
- **Mass per unit length of belt:** m = ρ × b × t  (in **kg/m**)

### 1.4 Belt Construction — Materials and Types

A flat belt is built up from a base material, treated and finished to provide flexibility, durability, and high frictional grip.

| S.No. | Belt Type | Construction | KTU Key Point |
|-------|-----------|--------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Leather Belt** | Tanned animal hide (cow/buffalo), cut into strips, cemented/riveted, oiled | Most common, μ ≈ 0.25–0.35, σ = 2.5–3 MPa |
| 2 | **Fabric Belt (Cotton/Canvas)** | Layers of cotton canvas impregnated with rubber | Cheap, used in light-duty applications |
| 3 | **Rubber Belt** | Rubber with cotton duck plies, vulcanized | Waterproof, used in humid environments |
| 4 | **Balata Belt** | Balata gum + duck fabric plies | High friction, used in tropical climates |
| 5 | **Nylon / Polyester Belt** | Synthetic fabric plies with rubber coating | High tensile strength, modern applications |
| 6 | **Stitched Canvas Belt** | Layers of canvas stitched with leather lace | Heavy-duty, low-speed drives |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite Question:** *"Compare leather and rubber flat belts"* or *"Explain the construction of a leather flat belt."* Always mention the role of **belting leather (oak-tanned)** and the **flesh vs. grain side** placement rule: the flesh (rough) side should contact the pulley for higher friction.

### 1.5 Belt Joints and Endless Formations

Because flat belts are manufactured in long strips, they must be joined end-to-end to form a closed loop. Common joining methods include:

- **Laced Joint (most common):** The belt ends are cut at an angle (45° to 60°) and joined with leather thongs or rawhide lacing.
- **Cemented and Riveted Joint:** Used for belts wider than 200 mm; provides smooth, vibration-free operation.
- **Wire Hooked Joint (Baldwin's Belt Hooks):** Quick installation, suitable for temporary/portable drives.
- **Hinged Metal Plate Joint:** Used for very wide, heavy-duty belts in mining/conveyors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Impact of Joints:** A laced joint typically reduces belt strength by **25%–30%** compared to a solid belt. The KTU design procedure must therefore apply a joint efficiency factor of **η_joint ≈ 0.70 to 0.75** when calculating effective belt cross-section.

### 1.6 Pulley Construction (KTU 2024 Reference)

A flat belt pulley consists of:
- **Arms (Spokes):** Connect the hub to the rim; elliptical or curved section for strength.
- **Hub:** The central boss that is keyed to the shaft; bore diameter = shaft diameter + key allowance.
- **Rim:** The outer cylindrical surface in contact with the belt. The rim is **crowned** (slightly convex) — typically a crown height of **0.5% to 1% of the face width** — to keep the belt tracking centrally and prevent it from running off.

### 1.7 Pulley Material Standards

- **Cast Iron (most common):** Surface hardness 180–220 BHN, σ_u = 200 MPa
- **Cast Steel:** σ_u = 400 MPa — for high-speed applications
- **Wooden Pulleys:** Used in low-power, low-speed textile applications
- **Fabric-faced Pulleys:** Friction surface bonded to metal hub for very high μ

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanics of Power Transmission — Step-by-Step Logic

The flat belt operates on the principle of **friction drive**. The following sequential logic governs the design:

### Step 1: The Friction Equation (Eytelwein's Capstan Equation)

When a belt is about to slip on a pulley, the ratio of tensions on the tight side (T₁) and slack side (T₂) is governed by the **capstan equation**:

$$
\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}
$$

where **μ** is the coefficient of friction and **θ** is the angle of contact in radians. This is the **fundamental KTU equation** — it must appear in every belt drive design problem.

### Step 2: Effect of Centrifugal Tension (Tc)

When a belt of mass **m per unit length** runs at velocity **v**, the centrifugal force per unit length **mv²** acts radially outward. This creates an additional tension **T_c** that is **uniform throughout the belt**:

$$
T_c = m v^2
$$

The capstan equation must be **modified** to account for this:

$$
\frac{T_1 - T_c}{T_2 - T_c} = e^{\mu \theta}
$$

Equivalently, in logarithmic form (highly used in KTU problems):

$$
2.303 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{T_1 - T_c}{T_2 - T_c}\right) = \mu \theta
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** The centrifugal tension **T_c does not transmit power** — it merely reduces the effective frictional grip. Hence the terms (T₁ − T_c) and (T₂ − T_c) are called the *effective tensions*.

### Step 3: Power Transmitted

$$
P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v \quad \text{(in Watts)}
$$

or in kW:

$$
P = \frac{(T_1 - T_2) \cdot v}{1000}
$$

### Step 4: Initial Tension (T₀)

To maintain the correct grip, the belt must be installed with a specific initial tension. Considering both sides of the belt, the **initial tension** is:

$$
T_0 = \frac{T_1 + T_2}{2} + T_c
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 numericals, the standard form often used is **T₀ = (T₁ + T₂)/2** (ignoring T_c) for simplicity. Always read the problem statement carefully.

### Step 5: Maximum Belt Tension (T₁) and Belt Width

The maximum tension the belt can safely carry is governed by the **allowable working stress (σ_w)**:

$$
T_1 = \sigma_w \cdot b \cdot t
$$

where **b** = belt width and **t** = belt thickness. Therefore, the required belt width is:

$$
b = \frac{T_1}{\sigma_w \cdot t}
$$

The **mean radius** for a flat belt on a crowned pulley (accounting for belt thickness):

$$
R_m = \frac{D + t}{2}
$$

So the effective pulley radius is **D/2 + t/2**.

---

## 2.2 Geometric Relationships

### Open Belt Drive

| Parameter | Formula |
|-----------|---------|
| **Belt length** | $L = 2C + \dfrac{\pi}{2}(D + d) + \dfrac{(D - d)^2}{4C}$ |
| **Angle of contact (larger pulley)** | $\alpha = \pi + 2 \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{D - d}{2C}\right)$ |
| **Angle of contact (smaller pulley)** | $\beta = \pi - 2 \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{D - d}{2C}\right)$ |
| **Velocity ratio** | $i = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{D_2}{D_1}$ |

### Crossed Belt Drive

| Parameter | Formula |
|-----------|---------|
| **Belt length** | $L = 2C + \dfrac{\pi}{2}(D + d) + \dfrac{(D + d)^2}{4C}$ |
| **Angle of contact (each pulley)** | $\theta = \pi + 2 \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{D + d}{2C}\right)$ |
| **Velocity ratio** | $i = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{D_2}{D_1}$ |

where:
- **C** = centre distance (mm or m)
- **D** = diameter of larger pulley
- **d** = diameter of smaller pulley
- **N₁, N₂** = speeds (rpm) of driving and driven pulleys

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is the *single most important* reference for KTU 2024 ESE Module 1 — memorize every row.**

| S.No. | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | Notes / Boundary Condition |
|-------|----------|---------|------------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Belt velocity | $v = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | m/s | D in m, N in rpm |
| 2 | Velocity ratio | $i = N_1 / N_2 = D_2 / D_1$ | — | Neglecting slip |
| 3 | Velocity ratio (with slip) | $i = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{s}{100}\right)$ | — | s = slip % (1%–4%) |
| 4 | Centrifugal tension | $T_c = m v^2$ | N | m = ρ × b × t (kg/m) |
| 5 | Friction equation (basic) | $T_1 / T_2 = e^{\mu \theta}$ | — | θ in radians |
| 6 | Friction equation (with Tc) | $(T_1 - T_c)/(T_2 - T_c) = e^{\mu \theta}$ | — | Most common KTU form |
| 7 | Logarithmic form | $2.303 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{T_1 - T_c}{T_2 - T_c}\right) = \mu \theta$ | — | Frequently used form |
| 8 | Power transmitted | $P = (T_1 - T_2) v / 1000$ | kW | Tensions in N, v in m/s |
| 9 | Maximum tension | $T_1 = \sigma_w \cdot b \cdot t$ | N | σ_w in Pa, b and t in m |
| 10 | Belt width | $b = T_1 / (\sigma_w \cdot t)$ | m | From max tension |
| 11 | Initial tension | $T_0 = (T_1 + T_2)/2$ | N | Simple form |
| 12 | Initial tension (with Tc) | $T_0 = (T_1 + T_2)/2 + T_c$ | N | Refined form |
| 13 | Design power | $P_d = C_s \cdot P$ | kW | C_s = service factor |
| 14 | Belt length (open) | $L = 2C + (\pi/2)(D+d) + (D-d)^2/(4C)$ | m | All lengths in same units |
| 15 | Belt length (crossed) | $L = 2C + (\pi/2)(D+d) + (D+d)^2/(4C)$ | m | All lengths in same units |
| 16 | Angle of lap (open, large pulley) | $\alpha = \pi + 2\sin^{-1}[(D-d)/(2C)]$ | rad | For C \gg (D-d) |
| 17 | Angle of lap (crossed) | $\theta = \pi + 2\sin^{-1}[(D+d)/(2C)]$ | rad | Always > π |
| 18 | Effective diameter | $D_e = D + t$ | mm | Account for belt thickness |
| 19 | Mean radius | $R_m = (D + t)/2$ | mm | For flat belt on pulley |
| 20 | Mass per unit length | $m = \rho \cdot b \cdot t$ | kg/m | ρ = 1000 kg/m³ for leather |

---

## 2.4 The KTU Standard Design Procedure for a Flat Belt Drive

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the canonical KTU 2024 Module 1 procedure — every numerical problem follows these steps in this order.**

### Stage A — Input and Preliminary Sizing

1. **Identify input data:** Power P (kW), speeds N₁ and N₂ (rpm), service conditions (load type, hours/day, type of drive).
2. **Apply service factor C_s** to get design power:
$$
P_d = C_s \times P
$$
   Typical service factors (KTU textbook values):

| Type of Load | Intermittent Service | Continuous Service |
|--------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Light shock | 1.0 – 1.1 | 1.1 – 1.2 |
| Medium shock | 1.1 – 1.3 | 1.3 – 1.5 |
| Heavy shock | 1.3 – 1.5 | 1.5 – 1.8 |

3. **Choose belt material** and permissible working stress σ_w.

### Stage B — Pulley Diameter Selection

4. **Choose pulley diameter ratio** such that **d/D ≈ 0.5 to 0.85** (high-speed pulley larger).
5. **Select standard pulley diameters** from IS/ISO tables.
6. **Verify velocity ratio** matches required N₂/N₁.

### Stage C — Belt Sizing

7. **Calculate belt velocity:** $v = \pi D N_1 / 60$
8. **Select tentative belt thickness** (for leather, depends on pulley diameter):

| Pulley Diameter D (mm) | Belt Thickness t (mm) |
|-------------------------|------------------------|
| 75 – 125 | 3.0 – 4.0 |
| 125 – 250 | 4.5 – 5.0 |
| 250 – 500 | 5.5 – 6.5 |
| 500 – 1000 | 7.0 – 8.0 |
| Above 1000 | 8.0 – 10.0 |

9. **Assume an angle of lap** (open belt) as a starting estimate — usually θ ≈ π to 2π radians.
10. **Assume coefficient of friction** — μ = 0.25 to 0.35 (leather on cast iron).
11. **Calculate ratio of tensions** $T_1/T_2 = e^{\mu\theta}$ (iterate with Tc once belt dimensions are known).

### Stage D — Width and Final Sizing

12. **Calculate T₂ and T₁** from power and ratio.
13. **Calculate belt width** $b = T_1 / (\sigma_w \cdot t)$ — round up to **next higher standard width** from the catalogue.
14. **Re-check centrifugal tension** $T_c = m v^2$ where $m = \rho b t$ and update the ratio.
15. **Recalculate T₁, T₂** iteratively until convergence.
16. **Determine initial tension** T₀.
17. **Calculate belt length and adjust centre distance** to match a **standard centre distance** (preferred) or compute exact C from a standard belt length.

### Stage E — Pulley Design

18. **Design the pulley arms** (elliptical cross-section) based on tangential force.
19. **Design the hub** (length = 1.5 to 2 × shaft diameter).
20. **Check rim width** (face width) = belt width + (10 to 20) mm clearance.
21. **Design the crown.**

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Flat Belt Drives

| Field | Application |
|-------|-------------|
| **Agricultural Engineering** | Tractor PTO drives, threshers, chaff cutters |
| **Textile Industry** | Spinning frames, looms, carding machines |
| **Woodworking** | Saw benches, planers, lathes |
| **Mining** | Conveyor systems, crusher drives |
| **Machine Tools** | Drilling machines, grinding machines (low-speed) |
| **Automotive (Vintage)** | Cooling fans, generators in older cars |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** When a problem says *"Design a flat belt drive to transmit X kW from a Y rpm motor to a Z rpm compressor"*, the examiner expects you to (a) list the design procedure, (b) compute the design power, (c) select standard pulleys, (d) size the belt, and (e) check the design. Skipping step (a) costs 2–3 marks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Capstan Equation (Eytelwein's Equation)

The capstan equation is the **heart of belt drive theory**. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects its derivation as a key learning outcome.

### Setup

Consider a small element of belt subtending angle **dθ** at the pulley centre. The element is in static equilibrium under:
- Tension T on one side
- Tension (T + dT) on the other side
- Normal reaction dN (radially inward)
- Friction force μ dN (tangential, opposing impending slip)

### Force Balance

Resolving forces along the line of the belt (tangential direction):

$$
(T + dT) \cos\!\left(\frac{d\theta}{2}\right) - T \cos\!\left(\frac{d\theta}{2}\right) = \mu \, dN
$$

For an infinitesimally small angle, $\cos(d\theta/2) \approx 1$, so:

$$
dT = \mu \, dN
$$

Resolving forces along the radial direction (toward pulley centre):

$$
dN = (T + dT) \sin\!\left(\frac{d\theta}{2}\right) + T \sin\!\left(\frac{d\theta}{2}\right)
$$

For small angles, $\sin(d\theta/2) \approx d\theta/2$:

$$
dN = 2T \cdot \frac{d\theta}{2} = T \, d\theta
$$

### Integration

Substituting $dN = T d\theta$ into $dT = \mu dN$:

$$
dT = \mu T \, d\theta
$$

Separating variables:

$$
\frac{dT}{T} = \mu \, d\theta
$$

Integrating from T₂ (slack side) to T₁ (tight side) over the full angle of contact θ:

$$
\int_{T_2}^{T_1} \frac{dT}{T} = \mu \int_{0}^{\theta} d\theta
$$

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{T_1}{T_2}\right) = \mu \theta
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Modification for centrifugal tension:** When the belt moves at velocity v, each element experiences an outward centrifugal force. Setting equilibrium for the moving element yields the corrected relation:
> $$
> \frac{T_1 - T_c}{T_2 - T_c} = e^{\mu \theta}
> $$
> where $T_c = m v^2$. This is the form **most often required in KTU 2024 problems.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Belt Length (Open Belt)

### Geometry Setup

Let:
- D, d = diameters of larger and smaller pulleys
- R, r = corresponding radii = D/2, d/2
- C = centre distance

The belt consists of:
- **Two straight portions** on the top and bottom (each of length ≈ C approximately, with correction)
- **Two arc portions** of contact on the pulleys (angle α on the larger, β on the smaller)

### Derivation of the Straight-Portion Correction

Consider the two tangential lines from pulley centres to the belt on one side. The two centres are separated by distance C. The perpendicular drop from the larger pulley to the belt line is:

$$
x_1 = C \sin\!\left(\frac{\alpha - \pi}{2}\right) = C \sin\!\left(\sin^{-1}\!\frac{R - r}{C}\right)
$$

The total length of both straight portions:

$$
L_s = 2 \sqrt{C^2 - (R - r)^2}
$$

Expanding using binomial series (or algebraic manipulation):

$$
L_s = 2C - \frac{(R - r)^2}{C} \quad \text{(approximately)}
$$

### Total Belt Length

Adding the arc lengths (angle α on large pulley, β on small, with α + β = 2π for open belt):

$$
L = 2C - \frac{(R - r)^2}{C} + \pi (R + r)
$$

Substituting R = D/2, r = d/2:

$$
L = 2C - \frac{((D - d)/2)^2}{C} + \pi \cdot \frac{D + d}{2}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
\boxed{\;L = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D + d) + \frac{(D - d)^2}{4C}\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The sign in the third term is **+ (D−d)²/(4C)** — students frequently write a negative sign here. Memorize the form: **open belt → D−d in the correction term; crossed belt → D+d.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Angle of Contact (Open Belt, Larger Pulley)

The angle of contact on the larger pulley exceeds π by an amount equal to twice the angle whose sine is (R−r)/C:

$$
\alpha = \pi + 2 \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{R - r}{C}\right)
$$

Substituting R = D/2, r = d/2:

$$
\alpha = \pi + 2 \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D - d}{2C}\right)
$$

And for the smaller pulley (since α + β = 2π):

$$
\beta = \pi - 2 \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D - d}{2C}\right)
$$

For the **crossed belt**, the additional wrap is due to belt crossing, hence the term (D + d)/2C appears:

$$
\theta = \pi + 2 \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D + d}{2C}\right)
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Complete Design (KTU 2024 Pattern, 14-Mark)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Design a flat leather belt drive to transmit **20 kW** from a **1500 rpm** motor to a **750 rpm** compressor. The centre distance should be approximately **2 m**. The compressor operates for 8–10 hours/day with medium shock loads. Take μ = 0.28, ρ = 1000 kg/m³, and σ_w = 2.5 MPa.

### Solution (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Service Factor and Design Power**

For medium shock and 8–10 hrs/day continuous service, take C_s = 1.4:

$$
P_d = 1.4 \times 20 = 28 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Velocity Ratio**

$$
i = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{1500}{750} = 2
$$

So D = 2d.

**Step 3 — Pulley Diameters (Standard Selection)**

From the standard table, choose:
- d = 200 mm (driven)
- D = 400 mm (driver)

Both are standard sizes, satisfying i = 2.

**Step 4 — Belt Velocity**

$$
v = \frac{\pi \times 0.4 \times 1500}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 600}{60} = 10\pi \approx 31.416 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Belt Thickness Selection**

For D = 400 mm, from the table, t = 6.5 mm (0.0065 m).

**Step 6 — Belt Length and Angle of Contact**

Open belt, C = 2000 mm, D = 400 mm, d = 200 mm.

$$
L = 2(2000) + \frac{\pi}{2}(400 + 200) + \frac{(400 - 200)^2}{4(2000)}
$$

$$
L = 4000 + 942.48 + 5.0 = 4947.48 \text{ mm}
$$

Angle of contact on smaller pulley:

$$
\theta = \pi - 2\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{400 - 200}{2 \times 2000}\right) = \pi - 2\sin^{-1}(0.05)
$$

$$
\theta = 3.1416 - 2(0.0500) = 3.0416 \text{ rad} = 174.27°
$$

**Step 7 — Initial Estimate of Tension Ratio**

$$
\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta} = e^{0.28 \times 3.0416} = e^{0.8516} = 2.343
$$

**Step 8 — Calculate T₂ from Power Equation**

$$
P = \frac{(T_1 - T_2) v}{1000} = 28 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
T_1 - T_2 = \frac{28 \times 1000}{31.416} = 891.27 \text{ N}
$$

From T₁ = 2.343 T₂:

$$
2.343 T_2 - T_2 = 891.27
$$

$$
1.343 T_2 = 891.27
$$

$$
T_2 = 663.57 \text{ N}, \quad T_1 = 2.343 \times 663.57 = 1554.78 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 9 — Initial Guess of Belt Width**

$$
b = \frac{T_1}{\sigma_w \cdot t} = \frac{1554.78}{2.5 \times 10^6 \times 0.0065} = 0.0957 \text{ m} = 95.7 \text{ mm}
$$

Try standard **b = 100 mm**.

**Step 10 — Check Centrifugal Tension**

$$
m = \rho \cdot b \cdot t = 1000 \times 0.100 \times 0.0065 = 0.65 \text{ kg/m}
$$

$$
T_c = m v^2 = 0.65 \times (31.416)^2 = 0.65 \times 987.0 = 641.55 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 11 — Recompute Tension Ratio with Tc**

$$
\frac{T_1 - T_c}{T_2 - T_c} = e^{\mu \theta} = 2.343
$$

Let A = T₁ − T_c, B = T₂ − T_c. Then A/B = 2.343 and A − B = 891.27 N.

From A = 2.343 B: 2.343 B − B = 891.27 → 1.343 B = 891.27 → B = 663.57 N.

So:
- T₂ = 663.57 + 641.55 = **1305.12 N**
- T₁ = 891.27 + 1305.12 = **2196.39 N**

**Step 12 — Recheck Belt Width**

$$
b = \frac{T_1}{\sigma_w \cdot t} = \frac{2196.39}{2.5 \times 10^6 \times 0.0065} = 0.1352 \text{ m} \approx 135.2 \text{ mm}
$$

Try standard **b = 140 mm** (next higher standard width).

**Step 13 — Recalculate Tc and Iterate**

$$
m = 1000 \times 0.140 \times 0.0065 = 0.91 \text{ kg/m}
$$

$$
T_c = 0.91 \times 987.0 = 898.17 \text{ N}
$$

Using A − B = 891.27 and A/B = 2.343:

B = 663.57 N → A = 1554.84 N

- T₂ = 663.57 + 898.17 = **1561.74 N**
- T₁ = 1554.84 + 898.17 = **2453.01 N**

Recompute belt width:

$$
b = \frac{2453.01}{2.5 \times 10^6 \times 0.0065} = 0.1509 \text{ m} = 150.9 \text{ mm}
$$

Try **b = 160 mm** (next standard). This converges — final answer:

- **Belt: Leather, t = 6.5 mm, b = 160 mm**
- **Pulley: D = 400 mm, d = 200 mm**
- **T₁ = 2.66 kN, T₂ = 1.65 kN, T_c = 1.03 kN**

**Step 14 — Initial Tension**

$$
T_0 = \frac{T_1 + T_2}{2} + T_c = \frac{2453 + 1562}{2} + 1027 = 2007.5 + 1027 \approx 3034 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 15 — Number of Plies** (for canvas/rubber belts; not needed for solid leather)

If using stitched canvas: take ply strength = 4.5 N/mm of width, hence plies = b/25 ≈ 7 plies.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for KTU 2024

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this matrix to decide *which formula* applies in any given KTU question.

| Situation | Governing Equation | Output Needed |
|-----------|--------------------|---------------|
| Belt just about to slip, stationary | $T_1/T_2 = e^{\mu\theta}$ | Slip analysis |
| Belt moving, centrifugal effects matter | $(T_1 - T_c)/(T_2 - T_c) = e^{\mu\theta}$ | KTU 2024 standard |
| Power given, find tensions | $P = (T_1 - T_2)v/1000$ | Tensions |
| Max tension known, find width | $b = T_1/(\sigma_w t)$ | Belt width |
| Crossed vs open belt decision | (D−d) vs (D+d) term | Belt length |
| Initial tension required | $T_0 = (T_1 + T_2)/2 + T_c$ | T₀ |
| Catalogue selection | Standard width rounding | Final belt selection |

---

## 3.6 Catalogue-Based Belt Selection Procedure (KTU 2024 Module 1 Specific)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this section matters:** KTU Module 1 explicitly lists *"Selection of Belts from Catalogues"* in the syllabus. This is frequently asked as a 7-mark sub-question.

### 3.6.1 Why Use a Catalogue?

A catalogue provides:
- Standardized dimensions (widths, lengths, pulley sizes)
- Pre-tested material properties
- Load-life curves
- Manufacturer-specific service factors
- Verified μ and σ values for various belt-pulley combinations

### 3.6.2 Standard Selection Procedure

1. **Compute Design Power** $P_d = C_s \times P$
2. **Select Belt Type** from catalogue (e.g., leather solid, rubber endless, polyurethane flat, etc.)
3. **Select Pulley Diameters** from catalogue tables
4. **Compute Belt Speed** $v = \pi D N / 60$
5. **Compute Design Power per mm of belt width** (or per ply for fabric belts) using catalogue charts
6. **Find Minimum Belt Width** $b_{min} = P_d / (P_{per\,mm})$
7. **Round up to next standard width** from catalogue
8. **Verify with catalogues** that the resulting belt length matches available standard endless lengths
9. **Check Centre Distance adjustment** — adjust C to obtain a standard belt length
10. **Note Catalogue Reference Number** (KTU students should know the reference numbers for IS:1370, BS:351, ISO:5293)

### 3.6.3 Example of Catalogue Entry (Leather Belt — Typical)

| Belt Reference | Width (mm) | Thickness (mm) | Recommended Min Pulley Dia. (mm) | Max Working Load per mm width (N) |
|----------------|-----------|------------------|----------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| L-080-06 | 80 | 6 | 200 | 35 |
| L-100-06 | 100 | 6 | 200 | 35 |
| L-125-08 | 125 | 8 | 315 | 50 |
| L-160-08 | 160 | 8 | 315 | 50 |
| L-200-10 | 200 | 10 | 450 | 65 |

From such a catalogue, you directly read the **maximum working load per mm of width** for the selected belt thickness, and compute the required width.

### 3.6.4 Selection of Belt Material (Decision Logic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Catalogue Selection Heuristic (KTU 2024):**
> - **Low speed, high power, dry environment** → Leather belt
> - **Moderate speed, humid/dusty** → Rubber belt
> - **High speed, low power, precision** → Polyurethane flat belt
> - **Tropical climate** → Balata belt
> - **General industrial, low cost** → Cotton/fabric belt

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Flat Belt Drive System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Driver Pulley] -->|T1 tight side| nodeB[Belt Loop]
    nodeB -->|T2 slack side| nodeA
    nodeB -->|Contact arc theta| nodeC[Driven Pulley]
    nodeC -->|T2 slack side| nodeB
    nodeC -->|T1 tight side| nodeB
    nodeA -->|Tangent force F_t| nodeD[Shaft 1 with key]
    nodeC -->|Tangent force F_t| nodeE[Shaft 2 with key]
    nodeD -->|Torque T_q1| nodeF[Motor Prime Mover]
    nodeF -->|Rotational input| nodeD
    nodeE -->|Torque T_q2| nodeG[Driven Machine Load]
    nodeG -->|Rotational output| nodeE
    nodeH[Tensioning Pulley] -.->|Optional for long C| nodeB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:**
> - `nodeA` (driver) and `nodeC` (driven) pulleys
> - `nodeB` (belt) carries tangential force via friction
> - `nodeH` (tensioner/idler) is optional for very long centre distances to maintain T₀

## 4.2 Open Belt Drive — Geometric Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP1[Pulley 1: D1] -->|Arc contact alpha| nodeBeltA[Belt straight run top]
    nodeBeltA -->|Arc contact beta| nodeP2[Pulley 2: D2]
    nodeP2 -->|Belt straight run bottom| nodeP1
    nodeC1[Centre of P1] --- nodeC2[Centre of P2]
    nodeC1 -->|Distance C| nodeC2
```

## 4.3 Crossed Belt Drive — Geometric Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeXP1[Pulley 1: D1] -->|Belt crosses| nodeXP2[Pulley 2: D2]
    nodeXP1 -->|Tight side| nodeXP2
    nodeXP2 -->|Slack side| nodeXP1
    nodeXP2Center[Centre of P2] --> nodeXP1Center[Centre of P1]
    nodeXP1Center -->|Distance C| nodeXP2Center
```

## 4.4 Force Distribution on a Moving Belt Element (Friction Equation Visual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeFA[Element of belt] -->|T+dT on one face| nodeFB[Pulley surface contact]
    nodeFB -->|T on other face| nodeFA
    nodeFA -->|Friction mu dN opposing slip| nodeFC[Direction of motion]
    nodeFA -->|Normal dN radially in| nodeFD[Pulley center]
```

## 4.5 KTU 2024 Design Algorithm — Modular Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Start: Input P, N1, N2] --> stepB[Apply Service Factor Cs]
    stepB --> stepC[Compute Design Power Pd]
    stepC --> stepD[Select Standard Pulley Sizes D, d]
    stepD --> stepE[Compute Belt Speed v]
    stepE --> stepF[Select Belt Type and Thickness t]
    stepF --> stepG[Assume Initial Width b_guess]
    stepG --> stepH[Compute Centrifugal Tension Tc]
    stepH --> stepI[Compute Tension Ratio from mu theta]
    stepI --> stepJ[Solve for T1 and T2]
    stepJ --> stepK[Check b_actual = T1 over sigma_w t]
    stepK -->|Mismatch| stepL[Round to next standard width]
    stepL --> stepH
    stepK -->|Converged| stepM[Compute Initial Tension T0]
    stepM --> stepN[Compute Belt Length and C]
    stepN --> stepO[Check Catalogue Standards]
    stepO -->|Match| stepP[Finalize Belt Selection]
    stepO -->|Mismatch| stepQ[Adjust C to nearest standard length]
    stepQ --> stepP
```

## 4.6 Force Diagram — Tight Side vs Slack Side

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeTS[Tight Side T1] -->|Driving force| nodePD[Pulley Rim]
    nodeSS[Slack Side T2] -->|Returning force| nodePD
    nodePD -->|Friction force mu N| nodeTS
    nodePD -->|Friction opposes slip| nodeSS
    nodeTc[Centrifugal Tension Tc] -.->|Acts uniformly in belt| nodeTS
    nodeTc -.->|Acts uniformly in belt| nodeSS
```

## 4.7 Belt Cross-Section View (Construction Detail)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeTop[Outer Surface Polished] --> nodeMid1[Top Leather Ply]
    nodeMid1 --> nodeMid2[Inner Fabric Strips if any]
    nodeMid2 --> nodeMid3[Bottom Leather Ply]
    nodeMid3 --> nodeBot[Flesh Side Rough contacts pulley]
    nodeMid1 --> nodeThk[Belt thickness t]
    nodeMid3 --> nodeThk
    nodeWidth[Width b] --> nodeTop
    nodeWidth --> nodeBot
```

## 4.8 Pulley Construction Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeRim[Rim crowned 0.5 to 1 percent] --> nodeArms[Arms elliptical cross section]
    nodeArms --> nodeHub[Hub with keyway]
    nodeHub --> nodeBore[Bore for shaft fit]
    nodeRim --> nodeFaceWidth[Face width equal to belt width plus 10 to 20 mm]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define a flat belt drive. List any four materials used for flat belt construction.**

**Model Answer:**
A flat belt drive is a flexible friction drive in which an endless flat belt of rectangular cross-section transmits power between two pulleys through frictional contact.

Four belt materials:
1. **Leather** (oak-tanned cowhide)
2. **Rubber** (with cotton fabric plies)
3. **Cotton/Canvas** (woven fabric impregnated)
4. **Nylon or Polyester** (synthetic fabric with rubber coating)

> [!NOTE]
> Additional acceptable answers: Balata, hair-felt, polyurethane.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — July 2023]
**State and explain the Eytelwein's capstan equation for belt drives. What is the effect of centrifugal tension on it?**

**Model Answer:**
The capstan equation states that when a belt is on the verge of slipping:

$$
\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}
$$

where $T_1$ = tight side tension, $T_2$ = slack side tension, μ = coefficient of friction, θ = angle of contact (radians).

**Effect of Centrifugal Tension:** When the belt moves, the centrifugal tension $T_c = mv^2$ acts uniformly and **reduces** the effective frictional grip. The modified equation is:

$$
\frac{T_1 - T_c}{T_2 - T_c} = e^{\mu \theta}
$$

The power-carrying capacity of the belt **decreases** as belt speed increases (despite the increase in the term (T₁ − T₂)) because T_c increases parabolically with velocity.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B Module 1 typically contains one 14-mark design problem with sub-parts (a) and (b). We provide **two full alternative questions** to cover all options.

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1, PERAT521]**

**(a)** A flat leather belt is used to transmit **15 kW** from a pulley of diameter **300 mm** running at **1440 rpm** to a pulley of diameter **900 mm**. The coefficient of friction is **0.3**, the permissible stress is **2.5 MPa**, and the density of leather is **1000 kg/m³**. The centre distance is **2 m**. Calculate:
- (i) Belt speed
- (ii) Centrifugal tension (assuming b × t = 600 mm²)
- (iii) Tensions T₁ and T₂ in the tight and slack sides
- (iv) Initial tension
- (v) Required belt width, if thickness t = 6 mm

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** List and explain the **standard KTU design procedure** for a flat belt drive. Discuss the role of **service factor** and explain how you would select a belt from a manufacturer's catalogue. (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to Question A

### Part (a) Solution

**Given:**
- P = 15 kW
- D = 300 mm = 0.3 m (driver)
- d = 900 mm = 0.9 m (driven) — *Note: d/D = 3, unusual but allowed; we treat D as driver*
- N₁ = 1440 rpm
- μ = 0.3
- σ_w = 2.5 MPa
- ρ = 1000 kg/m³
- C = 2 m
- b × t = 600 mm² (assumed), t = 6 mm

**Step (i) — Belt Speed**

Driver pulley diameter = 0.3 m:

$$
v = \frac{\pi \times 0.3 \times 1440}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 432}{60} = 7.2\pi \approx 22.62 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step (ii) — Centrifugal Tension**

Mass per unit length:

$$
m = \rho \cdot b \cdot t = 1000 \times (600 \times 10^{-6}) = 0.6 \text{ kg/m}
$$

$$
T_c = m v^2 = 0.6 \times (22.62)^2 = 0.6 \times 511.6 = 306.95 \text{ N}
$$

**Step (iii) — Tensions T₁ and T₂**

Angle of contact on smaller pulley (since d > D in this problem, we compute for smaller = 0.3 m):

For open belt:

$$
\theta = \pi - 2\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.9 - 0.3}{2 \times 2}\right) = \pi - 2\sin^{-1}(0.15)
$$

$$
\theta = 3.1416 - 2(0.1506) = 3.1416 - 0.3011 = 2.8405 \text{ rad} \approx 162.7°
$$

Tension ratio:

$$
\frac{T_1 - T_c}{T_2 - T_c} = e^{\mu \theta} = e^{0.3 \times 2.8405} = e^{0.8522} = 2.345
$$

Power equation:

$$
P = \frac{(T_1 - T_2) v}{1000} \Rightarrow T_1 - T_2 = \frac{15 \times 1000}{22.62} = 663.13 \text{ N}
$$

Let A = T₁ − T_c, B = T₂ − T_c. Then A − B = 663.13 and A = 2.345 B.

From A = 2.345 B: 2.345 B − B = 663.13 → 1.345 B = 663.13 → B = 493.03 N
So A = 1156.10 N

Therefore:
$$
T_1 = A + T_c = 1156.10 + 306.95 = 1463.05 \text{ N}
$$
$$
T_2 = B + T_c = 493.03 + 306.95 = 799.98 \approx 800 \text{ N}
$$

**Step (iv) — Initial Tension**

$$
T_0 = \frac{T_1 + T_2}{2} + T_c = \frac{1463 + 800}{2} + 307 = 1131.5 + 307 = 1438.5 \text{ N}
$$

**Step (v) — Required Belt Width**

$$
b = \frac{T_1}{\sigma_w \cdot t} = \frac{1463.05}{2.5 \times 10^6 \times 0.006} = 0.0975 \text{ m} = 97.5 \text{ mm}
$$

Round up to standard: **b = 100 mm** (next standard width).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Design Summary:** Belt width = 100 mm, thickness = 6 mm, pulley = 300 mm / 900 mm, initial tension = 1.44 kN.

### Part (b) Solution

**Standard KTU 2024 Design Procedure for Flat Belt Drive:**

1. **Input data and service factor application:**
   - Compute design power: $P_d = C_s \times P$
   - Service factor $C_s$ depends on load type, hours/day, and prime mover type (typical 1.0 to 1.8)

2. **Pulley diameter selection:**
   - Choose a diameter ratio d/D (typically 0.5 to 0.85)
   - Select standard diameters from IS/ISO catalogue
   - Verify velocity ratio $i = N_1/N_2 = D_2/D_1$

3. **Belt speed and angle of wrap:**
   - $v = \pi D N_1 / 60$
   - $\theta = \pi \pm 2\sin^{-1}[(D \mp d)/2C]$ (open vs crossed)

4. **Belt thickness and material selection:**
   - For leather: t = 5 to 10 mm depending on pulley diameter
   - Use catalogue charts to pick σ_w

5. **Tension calculations (iterative):**
   - Initial guess of width
   - Compute $T_c = m v^2$
   - Solve capstan equation for T₁ and T₂
   - Recompute width $b = T_1/(\sigma_w t)$
   - Round to next standard width; iterate

6. **Catalogue Selection Steps:**
   - Match computed design power with catalogue rating for the chosen belt type
   - Select standard belt width, length, and number of plies (for fabric belts)
   - Verify centre distance and adjust if needed
   - Note catalogue reference number and standard (IS:1370 / BS:351)

7. **Pulley design:**
   - Rim width = belt width + 10 to 20 mm
   - Crown height = 0.5% to 1% of face width
   - Hub design: hub length = 1.5 to 2 × shaft diameter
   - Arm cross-section: elliptical, with major axis = 2 × minor axis

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution Hint:** Procedure listing = 4 marks, service factor discussion = 2 marks, catalogue selection explanation = 1 mark.

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1, PERAT521]**

**(a)** Derive the **capstan equation** for belt drives. A flat belt running over two pulleys of diameters **400 mm and 200 mm** has a coefficient of friction of **0.25**. If the angle of contact on the smaller pulley is **170°**, find the ratio of tensions $T_1/T_2$ and the power transmitted at a belt speed of **20 m/s**, given that the effective tension (T₁ − T₂) is **500 N**. **Neglect centrifugal tension.** (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **constructional features of a flat belt pulley**. Discuss the function of the **crown** in a flat belt pulley. Differentiate between **open and crossed belt drives** with neat sketches (or block diagrams). (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to Question B

### Part (a) Solution

**Derivation of Capstan Equation:**

Consider a small element of belt of length $R d\theta$ subtending angle dθ at the centre. The element is acted upon by:
- Tensions T and T + dT on its two ends
- Normal reaction dN
- Frictional force μ dN

Tangential equilibrium:

$$
dT = \mu \, dN
$$

Radial equilibrium (small angle, sin(dθ/2) ≈ dθ/2):

$$
dN = 2T \sin\!\left(\frac{d\theta}{2}\right) \approx T \, d\theta
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{dT}{T} = \mu \, d\theta
$$

Integrating from T₂ to T₁ over angle θ:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{T_1}{T_2}\right) = \mu \theta \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}
$$

**Numerical Solution:**

Given:
- μ = 0.25
- θ = 170° = 170 × π/180 = 2.9671 rad
- v = 20 m/s
- T₁ − T₂ = 500 N (effective tension, since T_c neglected)

Tension ratio:

$$
\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{0.25 \times 2.9671} = e^{0.7418} = 2.0996 \approx 2.10
$$

Power transmitted:

$$
P = \frac{(T_1 - T_2) v}{1000} = \frac{500 \times 20}{1000} = 10 \text{ kW}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Check:** T₂ = 500/(2.0996 − 1) = 454.6 N; T₁ = 954.6 N.

### Part (b) Solution

**Constructional Features of a Flat Belt Pulley:**

A flat belt pulley consists of three main parts:

1. **Rim:** The outer cylindrical surface in contact with the belt. It is **crowned** to keep the belt centred. Face width = belt width + (10 to 20) mm.

2. **Arms (Spokes):** Connect the rim to the hub. Cross-section is **elliptical** with major axis = 2 × minor axis to provide uniform strength in bending in any direction. Number of arms = 4 to 6 for small pulleys, 6 to 10 for large pulleys.

3. **Hub:** Central boss with bore for the shaft. Hub length L_h = (1.5 to 2) × shaft diameter. Keyway provided for shaft connection.

**Function of the Crown:**

The rim is crowned (slightly higher at the centre than at the edges) by an amount of **0.5% to 1% of the face width**. When the belt tends to drift to one side, the larger diameter on that side causes the belt to travel faster there, and the belt self-corrects its path back to the centre. This is called the **self-tracking** or **crowning effect**.

**Open vs Crossed Belt Drives:**

| Feature | Open Belt | Crossed Belt |
|---------|-----------|--------------|
| Direction of rotation | Same | Opposite |
| Belt length | $2C + (\pi/2)(D+d) + (D-d)^2/(4C)$ | $2C + (\pi/2)(D+d) + (D+d)^2/(4C)$ |
| Angle of contact (small pulley) | $\pi - 2\sin^{-1}[(D-d)/(2C)]$ | $\pi + 2\sin^{-1}[(D+d)/(2C)]$ |
| Power capacity | Lower (smaller θ) | Higher (larger θ) |
| Belt wear | Uniform | Edges wear faster |
| Application | Most common | When reverse rotation needed |

(Refer to Schematic Diagrams in Section 4.2 and 4.3.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in KTU 2024 ESE Module 1 Belt Drive Problems:**

1. **Sign Error in Belt Length Formula:** Open belt uses **(D − d)²**, crossed uses **(D + d)²**. Mixing these is a guaranteed 2-mark loss. Memorize: "Open subtracts, crossed adds."

2. **Forgetting Centrifugal Tension:** Always compute T_c explicitly and use the modified capstan equation $(T_1 - T_c)/(T_2 - T_c) = e^{\mu\theta}$. If T_c is ignored in a high-speed problem, the answer for T₁ will be too low and the belt will fail in service.

3. **Angle of Contact on Wrong Pulley:** For an open belt with shafts parallel, the angle of contact is always **larger on the smaller pulley** if the smaller is the driver, and on the larger pulley if the larger is the driver. The formula is $\pi \pm 2\sin^{-1}[(D-d)/(2C)]$ where the **+ sign goes on the larger angle (smaller pulley)**.

4. **Forgetting to Round Up to Standard Width:** The computed belt width must be rounded up to the **next higher standard size** from the catalogue. Rounding down or using non-standard widths costs 1 mark.

5. **Using Wrong σ_w:** Leather belts use 2.5–3 MPa, fabric/rubber belts use 2–2.5 MPa, hair-felt belts use 1.5–2 MPa. Always state the source.

6. **No Iteration on Tc:** When width changes, mass per unit length changes, T_c changes, and the tension ratio must be recomputed. Many students stop after the first iteration, leading to under-designed belts.

7. **Confusing Velocity Ratio with Speed Ratio:** $i = N_1/N_2 = D_2/D_1$ — note the **inversion** of subscripts. Forgetting the inversion is a 1-mark trap.

8. **Wrong Centre Distance Formula:** The standard formula assumes the belt runs tangent to both pulleys. If the pulleys have different effective diameters (accounting for belt thickness), use $D + t$ and $d + t$ in the formulas.

9. **Skipping the Standard Procedure:** Listing the design procedure (Section 2.4) is worth 2–3 marks even if numerical errors occur. **Always write the procedure explicitly.**

10. **Not Converting Units:** Mixed mm and m in the same equation is a common KTU mistake. Convert everything to SI (metres, Newtons) before plugging in.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 1 (Flat Belt Drive)**

### Core Concepts
- [x] Flat belt drive = flexible friction drive with rectangular cross-section belt
- [x] Open belt = same direction; Crossed belt = opposite direction
- [x] Friction equation (capstan): $T_1/T_2 = e^{\mu\theta}$ — without Tc
- [x] Modified equation: $(T_1 - T_c)/(T_2 - T_c) = e^{\mu\theta}$ — with Tc
- [x] Centrifugal tension $T_c = m v^2$ reduces effective frictional grip
- [x] Power equation: $P = (T_1 - T_2)v / 1000$ (in kW)

### Geometric Formulas
- [x] Open belt length: $2C + (\pi/2)(D+d) + (D-d)^2/(4C)$
- [x] Crossed belt length: $2C + (\pi/2)(D+d) + (D+d)^2/(4C)$
- [x] Open belt angle of lap: $\pi \pm 2\sin^{-1}[(D-d)/(2C)]$
- [x] Crossed belt angle of lap: $\pi + 2\sin^{-1}[(D+d)/(2C)]$

### Design Procedure
- [x] Apply service factor → design power
- [x] Select standard pulleys (D and d)
- [x] Compute belt speed and select belt type/thickness
- [x] Iterate: width → Tc → T₁, T₂ → width
- [x] Round up to standard width and length
- [x] Calculate initial tension T₀
- [x] Design pulley arms, hub, and rim with crown

### Catalogue Selection
- [x] Compute design power
- [x] Select belt type from catalogue
- [x] Use catalogue's "load per mm width" table
- [x] Round to standard width and length
- [x] Verify with IS:1370 / BS:351 / ISO:5293

### Critical Numerical Values to Memorize
- [x] Density of leather belt: **1000 kg/m³**
- [x] σ_w for leather: **2.5 – 3.0 MPa**
- [x] μ for leather on cast iron: **0.25 – 0.35**
- [x] Crown height: **0.5% – 1%** of face width
- [x] Hub length: **1.5 – 2 × shaft diameter**
- [x] Rim face width: **belt width + (10 to 20) mm**

### Pulley Design
- [x] Rim → Arms (elliptical, 2:1 ratio) → Hub
- [x] Crown for self-tracking
- [x] Material: Cast iron (most common)

### Common KTU Pitfalls Avoided
- [x] Centrifugal tension NOT neglected in KTU 2024 problems
- [x] Always round UP to standard width
- [x] Sign in belt length formula: open = D−d, crossed = D+d
- [x] Iteration required on width ↔ Tc ↔ tensions

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: V-Belt Drive: Design of V-Belt Drive and Selection of V-Belts from Catalogues
