> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT521 - DESIGN OF TRANSMISSION ELEMENTS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** V-Belt Drive: Design of V-Belt Drive and Selection of V-Belts from Catalogues # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **V-Belt Drive** is a friction-based power transmission system in which a flexible, endless belt with a **trapezoidal (V-shaped) cross-section** runs in matched sheaves (pulleys) having corresponding V-grooved flanges. The wedging action of the belt in the groove generates a normal reaction force that is significantly greater than the applied tangential (driving) force, thereby multiplying the effective frictional grip and transmitted torque. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** > *"Design of Flat Belt Drives, V-Belt Drives (including selection from manufacturer's catalogues), and Rope Drives — Determination of belt length, angle of contact, velocity ratio, slip, and power rating."* ### Standard V-Belt Section Designations (IS 2494 / ISO 4184) | Standard Section | Top Width $b$ (mm) | Depth $h$ (mm) | Angle $\alpha$ | Mass per metre (kg/m) | Typical Applications | |------------------|---------------------|------------------|------------------|------------------------|----------------------| | **A** | 13 | 8 | 38° | 0.12 | Light-duty fractional kW | | **B** | 17 | 11 | 38° | 0.20 | General-purpose machine tools | | **C** | 22 | 14 | 38° | 0.33 | Industrial machinery | | **D** | 32 | 19 | 38° | 0.60 | Heavy industry, crushers | | **E** | 38 | 25 | 38° | 0.90 | Mining, large compressors | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Metric:** A V-belt operates on the principle of **wedge friction**. Because the groove angle (≈ 34°–38°) is smaller than 90°, the normal force $N$ between belt and pulley becomes: > $$N = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{2 \sin(\alpha/2)}$$ > This generates **2 to 3 times** more friction force than an equivalent flat belt, allowing compact drive packages. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Mountain Climber's Wedge" Imagine trying to pull a heavy crate across a rough floor using a flat rope. The rope keeps slipping because the friction is limited by the **weight** pressing the rope down (i.e., normal force = rope tension only). Now imagine the same crate, but with the rope wedged into a **V-shaped trench** carved into the crate's bottom. As you pull, the sides of the trench push *inward* on the rope — these **side reactions** add to the normal force. The rope can never "rise out" of the trench because wedging locks it deeper as tension rises. **The V-belt works identically:** - Flat belt ⇒ normal force = belt tension alone (limited friction). - V-belt ⇒ side walls of groove push on belt flanks ⇒ normal force = tension / (2 sin(α/2)). - Since α/2 ≈ 17°, sin(17°) ≈ 0.29, so the multiplier ≈ **1.72× per side, totaling ≈ 3.45×**. This is why a V-belt of the same width as a flat belt can transmit **~3 times more power** at the same tension. > [!TIP] > **Intuition Check:** If you ever change only the **groove angle** of the pulleys (e.g., from 38° to 34°), the wedge becomes "tighter" and grip increases — but belt life decreases due to higher bending stress at the root. --- ## 1.3 Geometric Anatomy of a V-Belt A standard V-belt cross-section consists of three functional regions: 1. **Tension Carcass (top region):** Embedded cords (cotton, polyester, aramid/Kevlar) carry the tensile load $T_1$. 2. **Compression Section (middle):** Vulcanized rubber/polyurethane that maintains shape and absorbs vibration. 3. **Neoprene Cover (bottom):** Oil/heat-resistant rubber sleeve that contacts the groove walls. The **neutral axis** lies roughly at the pitch line (≈ top of the tension cords), which is the line on which the belt length is measured for design purposes. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pitch Diameter (Standard Reference):** > For V-belt design, the effective diameter is the **pitch diameter** $D_p$ — not the outer or inner diameter of the pulley. All belt-length, speed, and slip calculations use $D_p$. --- # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Operating Principle — Wedge Action The **capstan equation** for a flat belt is: $$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$$ For a V-belt, the wedging action modifies this to the **Eytelwein–Corley equation**: $$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta \, / \, \sin(\alpha/2)}$$ where: | Symbol | Meaning | Unit | |--------|---------|------| | $T_1$ | Tension on tight side | N | | $T_2$ | Tension on slack side | N | | $\mu$ | Coefficient of friction (belt-pulley) | dimensionless | | $\theta$ | Angle of wrap / contact (radians) | rad | | $\alpha$ | Groove angle | degrees (°) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Effective Friction Coefficient:** > $$\mu_{eff} = \frac{\mu}{\sin(\alpha/2)}$$ > For α = 38° and μ = 0.30, $\mu_{eff}$ = 0.30 / 0.326 = **0.92** — equivalent to a flat belt with rubber-on-metal with μ ≈ 0.92 (which is unattainable in practice). --- ## 2.2 Geometric Relations for V-Belt Drives ### 2.2.1 Velocity Ratio $$i = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{D_p^{\,2}}{D_p^{\,1}} = \frac{\omega_1}{\omega_2}$$ ### 2.2.2 Belt Velocity $$v = \frac{\pi D_p^{\,1} N_1}{60} \quad \text{[m/s]}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Recommended Belt Velocity:** > $$v = 10 \text{ to } 20 \text{ m/s} \quad (\text{optimum} \approx 17 \text{ m/s})$$ > Below 10 m/s → poor utilization; above 25 m/s → excessive centrifugal tension. ### 2.2.3 Centrifugal Tension $$T_c = m \, v^2 \quad \text{[N]}$$ where $m$ = mass per unit length of the belt (kg/m). ### 2.2.4 Effective Tight-Side Tension $$T_1^{\,eff} = T_1 - T_c$$ ### 2.2.5 Maximum Transmissible Power (per belt) $$P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v = (T_1 - T_c)\left(1 - \frac{1}{e^{\mu\theta/\sin(\alpha/2)}}\right) \cdot v$$ --- ## 2.3 Angle of Contact (Wrap Angle) For an **open belt drive** with centre distance $C$ and pulley pitch diameters $D_p^{\,1}$, $D_p^{\,2}$: $$\theta_1 = \pi - 2 \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D_p^{\,2} - D_p^{\,1}}{2C}\right) \quad \text{[rad]} \quad \text{(small pulley)}$$ $$\theta_2 = \pi + 2 \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D_p^{\,2} - D_p^{\,1}}{2C}\right) \quad \text{[rad]} \quad \text{(large pulley)}$$ In degrees: $$\theta_1 = 180° - 2 \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D_p^{\,2} - D_p^{\,1}}{2C}\right)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Design Rule:** The angle of contact on the smaller pulley should **never fall below 120°** for V-belts. If $\theta_1 < 120°$, use an idler pulley or increase centre distance. --- ## 2.4 Standard Belt Length For an open V-belt drive, the pitch length $L_p$ is: $$L_p = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D_p^{\,1} + D_p^{\,2}) + \frac{(D_p^{\,2} - D_p^{\,1})^2}{4C}$$ In catalogues, $L_p$ is given as a **standard value**, and the required centre distance is back-calculated: $$C = \frac{L_p - \frac{\pi}{2}(D_p^{\,1}+D_p^{\,2})}{4} + \sqrt{\left[\frac{L_p - \frac{\pi}{2}(D_p^{\,1}+D_p^{\,2})}{4}\right]^2 - \frac{(D_p^{\,2}-D_p^{\,1})^2}{16}}$$ --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — MEMORIZE FOR KTU EXAMS** | # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Critical Note | |---|-----------|---------|------|---------------| | 1 | Belt velocity | $v = \pi D_p^{\,1} N_1 / 60$ | m/s | Use pitch diameter, not OD | | 2 | Velocity ratio | $i = D_2 / D_1$ | — | Neglects slip in preliminary design | | 3 | Centrifugal tension | $T_c = m v^2$ | N | $m$ from catalogue (kg/m) | | 4 | Tension ratio | $T_1/T_2 = e^{\mu\theta/\sin(\alpha/2)}$ | — | α = groove angle | | 5 | Effective tension | $T_{eff} = (T_1 - T_c) - (T_2 - T_c) = T_1 - T_2$ | N | $T_c$ cancels in difference | | 6 | Max power per belt | $P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v / 1000$ | kW | After correction factors | | 7 | Required no. of belts | $n = P_{design} / P_{rated}$ | — | Round **up** to next integer | | 8 | Wrap angle (small) | $\theta_1 = 180° - 2\sin^{-1}\!\big((D_2-D_1)/(2C)\big)$ | deg | Must be ≥ 120° | | 9 | Wrap angle (large) | $\theta_2 = 360° - \theta_1$ | deg | Always larger | | 10 | Belt length (open) | $L_p = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D_1+D_2) + \frac{(D_2-D_1)^2}{4C}$ | mm | Match to catalogue value | | 11 | Centre distance | Use quadratic formula (above) | mm | Solve for $C$ from given $L_p$ | | 12 | Initial tension | $T_0 \approx (T_1 + T_2)/2 + T_c$ | N | For installation/take-up | | 13 | Slip correction | $v_{actual} = v(1-s)$, $s \approx 1\%-2\%$ | — | For driven pulley speed | | 14 | Design power | $P_d = P_{trans} \times S_f$ | kW | $S_f$ = service factor | | 15 | Power capacity | $P_{rated} = P_{rated,0} \times K_\theta \times K_v \times K_L$ | kW | Correction factors from catalogue | ### Service Factors $S_f$ (Typical) | Type of Driven Machine | Light Duty (uniform load) | Medium Duty | Heavy Duty (shock loads) | |------------------------|----------------------------|--------------|----------------------------| | Agitators, fans, blowers | 1.0 | 1.1 | 1.3 | | Compressors, conveyors | 1.1 | 1.2 | 1.4 | | Crushers, mills, hoists | 1.2 | 1.4 | 1.6 | --- ## 2.6 Engineering Applications > [!TIP] > **Real-World Utility of V-Belt Drive Design:** > - **Automotive engines:** Camshaft and accessory drive (alternator, A/C compressor). > - **Industrial machinery:** Machine tools, lathes, milling machines. > - **Agricultural equipment:** Tractor PTOs, threshers, irrigation pumps. > - **HVAC systems:** Fan and blower drives for air-handling units. > - **Mining and construction:** Crusher and conveyor drives under shock loading. The catalogue-based selection method used in KTU problems is the **industry-standard procedure** followed by engineers globally using Gates, ContiTech, Fenner, and Dunlop catalogues. --- # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation: Tension Ratio in a V-Belt ### Step 1 — Free Body Diagram of a Belt Element Consider an infinitesimal element of V-belt subtending angle $d\theta$ on a pulley. Let $T$ and $T + dT$ be the tensions at the two ends. The belt flanks contact the groove walls with normal reaction $dN$ per side. > The element is in equilibrium under three forces: tension differential, friction forces, and normal reactions. ### Step 2 — Resolving Forces Resolving tangentially (along belt direction): $$ \begin{aligned} (T + dT) \cos(d\theta/2) - T \cos(d\theta/2) &= \mu \, dN_1 + \mu \, dN_2 \\ \Rightarrow dT &= 2 \mu \, dN \quad (\text{by symmetry, } dN_1 = dN_2 = dN) \end{aligned} $$ Resolving radially (toward centre): $$ \begin{aligned} (T + dT) \sin(d\theta/2) + T \sin(d\theta/2) &= dN_1 + dN_2 \\ \Rightarrow T \, d\theta &= 2 \, dN \\ \Rightarrow dN &= \frac{T \, d\theta}{2} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Combine and Integrate Substituting $dN$ into $dT$: $$ \begin{aligned} dT &= 2\mu \cdot \frac{T \, d\theta}{2} = \mu \, T \, d\theta \\ \frac{dT}{T} &= \mu \, d\theta \end{aligned} $$ Integrating from $T_2$ (slack side, $\theta = 0$) to $T_1$ (tight side, $\theta = \theta_w$): $$ \begin{aligned} \int_{T_2}^{T_1} \frac{dT}{T} &= \int_0^{\theta_w} \mu \, d\theta \\ \ln\!\left(\frac{T_1}{T_2}\right) &= \mu \, \theta_w \\ \Rightarrow \frac{T_1}{T_2} &= e^{\mu \theta_w} \end{aligned} $$ This recovers the **flat-belt capstan equation**. ### Step 4 — Wedge Effect Modification For a V-belt, the normal reaction $dN$ is not in the plane of the belt, but inclined at angle $\alpha/2$ from the centre-line. The radial equilibrium gives: $$ \begin{aligned} T \, d\theta &= 2 \, dN \sin(\alpha/2) \\ \Rightarrow dN &= \frac{T \, d\theta}{2 \sin(\alpha/2)} \end{aligned} $$ The tangential (frictional) force is still $\mu \, dN$, so: $$ \begin{aligned} dT &= 2\mu \, dN = \frac{\mu \, T \, d\theta}{\sin(\alpha/2)} \\ \int_{T_2}^{T_1} \frac{dT}{T} &= \frac{\mu}{\sin(\alpha/2)} \int_0^{\theta_w} d\theta \\ \boxed{\;\frac{T_1}{T_2} = \exp\!\left(\frac{\mu \, \theta_w}{\sin(\alpha/2)}\right)\;} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** The V-belt's tension ratio equals that of an equivalent flat belt with friction coefficient $\mu_{eff} = \mu/\sin(\alpha/2)$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Centrifugal Tension Consider a small belt element of mass $dm$ and radius of curvature $r$ (= $D_p/2$) moving at velocity $v$. The centripetal acceleration is $v^2/r$. The inertial force equals tension difference: $$ \begin{aligned} dT_c &= dm \cdot \frac{v^2}{r} \\ dm &= m \cdot r \, d\theta \\ \Rightarrow dT_c &= m \cdot v^2 \, d\theta \end{aligned} $$ Total centrifugal tension (constant along the belt since it depends only on speed and mass): $$ \begin{aligned} \oint dT_c &= m v^2 \oint d\theta \\ \Rightarrow T_c &= m v^2 \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > Centrifugal tension acts **radially outward** and reduces the effective normal pressure between belt and pulley, lowering the friction force. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Step-by-Step V-Belt Design Procedure > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner's Gold Standard — The 8-Step V-Belt Design Procedure** ### STEP 1: Identify Input Data - Power to be transmitted: $P$ (kW) - Speeds of driver and driven: $N_1$, $N_2$ (rpm) - Service conditions (duty cycle, type of driven machine) - Centre distance (if specified, else assumed). ### STEP 2: Compute Design Power $$ P_d = P \times S_f $$ where $S_f$ comes from Table 2.5 (selected above). ### STEP 3: Assume Belt Velocity & Choose Section For preliminary selection, assume $v \approx 10$–$20$ m/s. Calculate the pitch diameter of the smaller pulley using: $$ D_p^{\,1} = \frac{60 \, v}{\pi \, N_1} \times 1000 \quad [\text{mm}] $$ Round up to the **standard pitch diameter** for the chosen section from the catalogue. > [!TIP] > **Catalogue Reference (B-Section, typical values):** > Standard pitch diameters: 75, 90, 100, 112, 125, 140, 160, 180, 200, 224, 250, 280, 315, 355, 400 mm. ### STEP 4: Determine Velocity Ratio and Larger Pulley Diameter $$ i = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{D_p^{\,2}}{D_p^{\,1}} $$ $$ D_p^{\,2} = i \times D_p^{\,1} \quad \text{(round to nearest catalogue value)} $$ Recompute actual velocity ratio $i_{actual} = D_p^{\,2}/D_p^{\,1}$. ### STEP 5: Compute Belt Speed and Wrap Angles $$ v = \frac{\pi \, D_p^{\,1} \, N_1}{60 \, 1000} \quad [\text{m/s}] $$ $$ \theta_1 = 180° - 2\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D_p^{\,2}-D_p^{\,1}}{2C}\right) \quad [\text{deg}] $$ Check: $\theta_1 \geq 120°$. If not, increase $C$ or use an idler. ### STEP 6: Select Standard Belt Length & Compute Centre Distance Iterate using: $$ C = p + \sqrt{p^2 - q} $$ where $$ p = \frac{L_p - \frac{\pi}{2}(D_p^{\,1}+D_p^{\,2})}{4}, \quad q = \frac{(D_p^{\,2}-D_p^{\,1})^2}{16} $$ Choose $L_p$ from catalogue and back-calculate $C$. ### STEP 7: Determine Rated Power per Belt (Catalogue-Based) From the manufacturer's catalogue (e.g., Gates Super HC), read: - $P_0$ = basic power rating for chosen section, diameter, and speed. - $K_\theta$ = angle-of-contact correction factor (function of $\theta_1$). - $K_v$ = speed ratio correction factor. - $K_L$ = belt length correction factor. $$ P_{rated} = P_0 \times K_\theta \times K_v \times K_L $$ ### STEP 8: Compute Number of Belts $$ n = \frac{P_d}{P_{rated}} \quad \text{(round UP to next integer)} $$ Standard practice: $n \leq 6$ for standard V-belts (use multiple-groove sheave for higher counts). --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern) **Problem:** Design a V-belt drive to transmit 12 kW from an electric motor running at 1440 rpm to a centrifugal pump running at 480 rpm. Centre distance ≈ 600 mm, moderate duty, slip = 1.5%. **Step 1 — Design Power:** Service factor for centrifugal pump under moderate duty (from Table): $S_f = 1.2$. $$ P_d = 12 \times 1.2 = 14.4 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 2 — Belt Velocity and Section:** Assume $v = 17$ m/s (optimum). Pitch diameter of small pulley: $$ D_p^{\,1} = \frac{60 \times 17}{\pi \times 1440} \times 1000 = \frac{1020}{4523.89} \times 1000 \approx 225.5 \text{ mm} $$ Round up to standard catalogue value: **$D_p^{\,1} = 224$ mm (B-section)**. **Step 3 — Velocity Ratio and Large Pulley:** $$ i_{ideal} = \frac{1440}{480} = 3.0 $$ $$ D_p^{\,2} = 3.0 \times 224 = 672 \text{ mm} $$ Round to nearest catalogue value: **$D_p^{\,2} = 670$ mm (B-section)**. Actual velocity ratio: $i_{actual} = 670/224 = 2.991$. **Step 4 — Actual Belt Speed:** $$ v = \frac{\pi \times 224 \times 1440}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{1{,}013{,}760}{60{,}000} = 16.896 \text{ m/s} \approx 16.9 \text{ m/s} $$ This is within the recommended range. ✓ **Step 5 — Wrap Angle:** $$ \theta_1 = 180° - 2\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{670 - 224}{2 \times 600}\right) = 180° - 2\sin^{-1}(0.3717) $$ $$ \sin^{-1}(0.3717) \approx 21.81° $$ $$ \theta_1 = 180° - 43.62° = 136.38° \geq 120° \quad \checkmark $$ **Step 6 — Belt Length (Standard):** $$ L_p = 2(600) + \frac{\pi}{2}(224+670) + \frac{(670-224)^2}{4 \times 600} $$ $$ L_p = 1200 + 1403.71 + 121.34 = 2725.05 \text{ mm} $$ Nearest standard B-section pitch length (catalogue): **$L_p = 2800$ mm**. Back-calculate actual centre distance: $$ p = \frac{2800 - \frac{\pi}{2}(894)}{4} = \frac{2800 - 1403.71}{4} = 349.07 $$ $$ q = \frac{(446)^2}{16} = \frac{198{,}916}{16} = 12{,}432.25 $$ $$ C = 349.07 + \sqrt{349.07^2 - 12{,}432.25} = 349.07 + \sqrt{109{,}330.6} = 349.07 + 330.65 \approx 679.7 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 7 — Catalogue Power Rating (sample values from B-section):** Assume catalogue gives $P_0 = 6.5$ kW at $v = 16.9$ m/s and $D_p = 224$ mm. - $K_\theta$ (at 136°): from catalogue ≈ **0.94** - $K_v$ (ratio 2.991): ≈ **1.05** - $K_L$ (length 2800 mm): ≈ **1.04** $$ P_{rated} = 6.5 \times 0.94 \times 1.05 \times 1.04 = 6.68 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 8 — Number of Belts:** $$ n = \frac{14.4}{6.68} = 2.16 \Rightarrow n = 3 \text{ belts (round up)} $$ **Final Design:** - B-section V-belts, 3 nos. - Pitch diameters: $D_1 = 224$ mm, $D_2 = 670$ mm - Pitch length: $L_p = 2800$ mm - Centre distance: $C ≈ 680$ mm - Slip correction: $N_2 = N_1 \times D_1/D_2 \times (1 - s) = 1440 \times (224/670) \times 0.985 ≈ 474.5$ rpm --- # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 V-Belt Drive — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[MOTOR: Power Source
P = 12 kW, N1 = 1440 rpm] --> B[Belt Length and Diameter Calculator
L_p, D_p1, D_p2] B --> C[Design Power
P_d = P x S_f] C --> D{Belt Velocity
Check 10-25 m/s} D -->|In Range| E[Select Standard Section
A, B, C, D, E] D -->|Out of Range| F[Revise D_p1 or N1] E --> G[Velocity Ratio and D_p2
i = N1/N2] G --> H[Wrap Angle Calculation
theta_1, theta_2] H --> I{theta_1 >= 120 deg?} I -->|Yes| J[Standard Belt Length Lookup
from Catalogue] I -->|No| K[Increase Centre Distance
or Add Idler] K --> H J --> L[Centre Distance Back-Calc
Quadratic Formula] L --> M[Apply Correction Factors
K_theta, K_v, K_L] M --> N[Rated Power per Belt
P_rated = P_0 x K factors] N --> O{Number of Belts
n = P_d / P_rated} O --> P[Round Up to Integer
n = 3 typical] P --> Q[FINAL DESIGN OUTPUT
Section, n, D_p1, D_p2, L_p, C] stepA[Step 1: Input] --> stepB[Step 2: Design Power] stepB --> stepC[Step 3: Section] stepC --> stepD[Step 4: Velocity Ratio] stepD --> stepE[Step 5: Wrap Angle] stepE --> stepF[Step 6: Belt Length] stepF --> stepG[Step 7: Rated Power] stepG --> stepH[Step 8: Number of Belts] ``` ## 4.2 V-Belt Cross-Section and Wedge Geometry ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Pulley Groove
Angle alpha] --> W1[Wedge Action] W1 --> N[Normal Reaction N
acts on belt flanks] N --> F[Frictional Force
mu times N] F --> GRIP[Enhanced Grip
3 to 5 times flat belt] GRIP --> POWER[Higher Power
Transmission per cm width] P2[Belt Section A: 13 mm] --> SEL[Selection] P3[Belt Section B: 17 mm] --> SEL P4[Belt Section C: 22 mm] --> SEL P5[Belt Section D: 32 mm] --> SEL P6[Belt Section E: 38 mm] --> SEL SEL --> DECISION[Decide Section
Based on P_d] ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Flow — Power Transmission ```mermaid flowchart TD Input1[INPUT: P, N1, N2, C] --> Proc1[Process 1: Service Factor Multiplication] Proc1 --> Proc2[Process 2: Velocity and Diameter Determination] Proc2 --> Proc3[Process 3: Section and Length Selection] Proc3 --> Proc4[Process 4: Correction Factor Application] Proc4 --> Proc5[Process 5: Number of Belts Decision] Proc5 --> Output[OUTPUT: Section, n, D_p1, D_p2, L_p, C] Data1[Catalogue Data 1
Pitch Diameters] -.-> Proc2 Data2[Catalogue Data 2
Standard Lengths] -.-> Proc3 Data3[Catalogue Data 3
Power Ratings and K factors] -.-> Proc4 ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## PART A — 3-Mark Questions ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Explain the principle of V-belt drive. How does it differ from a flat belt drive in terms of power transmission?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A V-belt drive transmits power through **friction between the belt flanks and the V-grooved pulley sides**. The wedging action multiplies the normal reaction force by a factor of $1/\sin(\alpha/2)$, where $\alpha$ is the groove angle. **Differences from flat belt:** | Feature | Flat Belt | V-Belt | |---------|-----------|--------| | Contact area | Inner surface | Two inclined flanks | | Normal force | $N = T\sin\theta$ | $N = T / (2\sin(\alpha/2))$ | | Tension ratio | $T_1/T_2 = e^{\mu\theta}$ | $T_1/T_2 = e^{\mu\theta/\sin(\alpha/2)}$ | | Power per cm width | Low | **3–5× higher** | | Pulley shape | Flat or crowned | V-grooved | | Alignment sensitivity | Low | High | For a groove angle α = 38°, the effective friction multiplier is $1/\sin(19°) ≈ 3.07$. Hence for the same coefficient of friction, **a V-belt can transmit roughly 3 times the power** of an equivalent flat belt. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define pitch diameter of a V-belt pulley. Why is it used instead of the outer diameter in design calculations?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **pitch diameter** $D_p$ is the diameter of the circle on which the effective neutral axis of the belt lies, measured at the level of the **tension cords** (top of the belt cross-section). **Reasons for using pitch diameter:** 1. Belt length is measured along the pitch line, not the outer surface. 2. Belt speed is calculated using $D_p$ because the cord neutral axis moves at the design velocity. 3. Slip and creep effects are consistent with the pitch line. 4. Standard catalogue data (length, power rating, pitch diameters) are all referenced to $D_p$. If the outer diameter is used, the calculated belt length will be too long, and slip will be overestimated — leading to incorrect design. --- ## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]** **A V-belt drive is required to transmit 22 kW from a 1450 rpm induction motor to a compressor running at 720 rpm. The centre distance should be approximately 1500 mm and the drive must operate under moderate shock loading conditions. The following catalogue data is available:** | Section | Pitch diameter range (mm) | Power rating per belt (kW) at v = 18 m/s | |---------|----------------------------|--------------------------------------------| | B | 140 – 400 | 4.5 – 9.0 | | C | 200 – 560 | 7.5 – 18.0 | **Correction factors:** $K_\theta$ (at 150°): 0.97; $K_v$ (ratio 2.0): 1.04; $K_L$ (length 4000 mm, B-section): 1.07; (C-section): 1.06. **Design the V-belt drive completely, including selection of section, pulley diameters, standard belt length, centre distance, and number of belts.** --- #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Determine Pulley Sizes and Belt Length** **Step 1: Design Power** Service factor for compressor under moderate shock loading: $S_f = 1.4$ (from table). $$ P_d = 22 \times 1.4 = 30.8 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 2: Select Belt Section** For $P_d = 30.8$ kW, the C-section is suitable (B-section max ≈ 9 kW/belt, would require ≥ 4 belts — uneconomical). **Step 3: Pitch Diameter of Small Pulley** Assume $v = 18$ m/s. $$ D_p^{\,1} = \frac{60 \times 18}{\pi \times 1450} \times 1000 = \frac{1{,}080{,}000}{4555.3} = 237.1 \text{ mm} $$ Round up to standard C-section value: **$D_p^{\,1} = 250$ mm**. **Step 4: Velocity Ratio and Large Pulley** $$ i = \frac{1450}{720} = 2.014 $$ $$ D_p^{\,2} = 2.014 \times 250 = 503.5 \text{ mm} \rightarrow \text{round to } 500 \text{ mm} $$ Actual velocity ratio: $i_{actual} = 500/250 = 2.0$. **Step 5: Actual Belt Speed** $$ v = \frac{\pi \times 250 \times 1450}{60{,}000} = 18.98 \text{ m/s} \quad \checkmark $$ **Step 6: Belt Length (assumed $C = 1500$ mm)** $$ L_p = 2(1500) + \frac{\pi}{2}(250+500) + \frac{(500-250)^2}{4 \times 1500} $$ $$ L_p = 3000 + 1178.1 + 10.42 = 4188.5 \text{ mm} $$ Round to nearest standard C-section pitch length: **$L_p = 4250$ mm**. **Step 7: Centre Distance (back-calc):** $$ p = \frac{4250 - 1178.1}{4} = 767.97 $$ $$ q = \frac{(250)^2}{16} = 3906.25 $$ $$ C = 767.97 + \sqrt{767.97^2 - 3906.25} = 767.97 + \sqrt{589{,}747.7} = 767.97 + 767.95 = 1535.92 \text{ mm} $$ So **$C \approx 1536$ mm**, close to the 1500 mm requirement. ✓ --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Determine Number of Belts** **Step 8: Wrap Angle Check** $$ \theta_1 = 180° - 2\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{500-250}{2 \times 1536}\right) = 180° - 2\sin^{-1}(0.0814) $$ $$ \sin^{-1}(0.0814) = 4.67° $$ $$ \theta_1 = 180° - 9.34° = 170.66° \geq 120° \quad \checkmark $$ **Step 9: Catalogue Power Rating per Belt** From catalogue, for C-section at $D_p = 250$ mm, $v = 19$ m/s: $P_0 = 14.2$ kW (interpolated). Apply correction factors: - $K_\theta$ at 170.66° ≈ 0.99 - $K_v = 1.04$ (given) - $K_L = 1.06$ (C-section, given) $$ P_{rated} = 14.2 \times 0.99 \times 1.04 \times 1.06 = 15.51 \text{ kW/belt} $$ **Step 10: Number of Belts** $$ n = \frac{30.8}{15.51} = 1.99 \rightarrow n = 2 \text{ belts (round up)} $$ **Final Design Summary:** - C-section V-belts, **2 nos.** - $D_p^{\,1} = 250$ mm, $D_p^{\,2} = 500$ mm - Pitch length $L_p = 4250$ mm - Centre distance $C = 1536$ mm - Belt speed $v = 19$ m/s - Wrap angle on small pulley $= 170.66°$ - Driven speed (with 1.5% slip): $N_2 \approx 720 \times 0.985 = 709$ rpm --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** **A 4 kW, 1440 rpm electric motor is to drive a wood-working machine at 600 rpm. The drive is an open V-belt drive with a centre distance of approximately 500 mm. The motor pulley diameter is restricted to 100 mm. Take slip as 1.5%, groove angle as 38°, coefficient of friction μ = 0.25. Determine:** **(a)** The diameter of the driven pulley, the actual velocity ratio, and the belt length. **(b)** The tensions in the tight and slack sides of the belt, the initial tension required, and the factor of safety against slipping. --- #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Pulley and Belt Geometry** **Step 1: Theoretical Velocity Ratio** $$ i = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{1440}{600} = 2.4 $$ **Step 2: Driven Pulley Diameter (ignoring slip)** $$ D_p^{\,2} = i \times D_p^{\,1} = 2.4 \times 100 = 240 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 3: Actual Velocity Ratio (with slip)** Actual driven speed: $N_2^{\,actual} = N_1 \times D_1/D_2 \times (1 - s)$ For design, accept 240 mm (the slip affects only the running speed, not the design). **Step 4: Belt Length** For open belt drive with $C = 500$ mm, $D_1 = 100$ mm, $D_2 = 240$ mm: $$ L_p = 2(500) + \frac{\pi}{2}(100+240) + \frac{(240-100)^2}{4 \times 500} $$ $$ L_p = 1000 + 534.07 + 19.6 = 1553.67 \text{ mm} $$ Round to standard value: **$L_p \approx 1575$ mm** (nearest A-section catalogue value). **Step 5: Wrap Angles** $$ \theta_1 = 180° - 2\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{240-100}{2 \times 500}\right) = 180° - 2\sin^{-1}(0.14) $$ $$ \sin^{-1}(0.14) = 8.05° $$ $$ \theta_1 = 180° - 16.1° = 163.9° \quad \text{(in radians: } 2.861\text{)} $$ $$ \theta_2 = 360° - 163.9° = 196.1° $$ --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Belt Tensions and Factor of Safety** **Step 6: Belt Velocity** $$ v = \frac{\pi \times 100 \times 1440}{60{,}000} = 7.54 \text{ m/s} $$ **Step 7: Centrifugal Tension** For A-section belt, $m \approx 0.106$ kg/m: $$ T_c = 0.106 \times (7.54)^2 = 0.106 \times 56.85 = 6.03 \text{ N} $$ **Step 8: Power Equation** Required tangential force (effective): $$ (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v = P \times 1000 \Rightarrow T_1 - T_2 = \frac{4000}{7.54} = 530.5 \text{ N} $$ **Step 9: Tension Ratio** $$ \frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu\theta/\sin(\alpha/2)} = e^{0.25 \times 2.861 / \sin(19°)} = e^{0.715 / 0.326} = e^{2.194} = 8.97 $$ So $T_1 = 8.97 \, T_2$. **Step 10: Solve for Tensions** $$ 8.97 T_2 - T_2 = 530.5 \Rightarrow 7.97 T_2 = 530.5 \Rightarrow T_2 = 66.56 \text{ N} $$ $$ T_1 = 8.97 \times 66.56 = 597.04 \text{ N} $$ **Step 11: Initial Tension** $$ T_0 = \frac{T_1 + T_2}{2} + T_c = \frac{597.04 + 66.56}{2} + 6.03 = 337.83 \text{ N} $$ **Step 12: Factor of Safety** Maximum allowable tension (assumed breaking limit or maximum permissible): For A-section, allowable tension $T_{allow} \approx 800$ N (from catalogue). $$ \text{FoS} = \frac{T_{allow}}{T_1 + T_c} = \frac{800}{597.04 + 6.03} = \frac{800}{603.07} = 1.33 $$ If FoS < 2, redesign with a wider section (B-section). **Final Answer Summary:** - $D_2 = 240$ mm, $L_p = 1575$ mm - $T_1 = 597$ N, $T_2 = 66.6$ N, $T_0 = 338$ N - Factor of safety = 1.33 (marginal → consider B-section) --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Using outer diameter instead of pitch diameter** — KTU examiners explicitly mark 1 mark for pitch-diameter identification. > 2. **Forgetting to multiply by service factor** before finding number of belts (results in 40–60% under-design). > 3. **Failing to apply correction factors** $K_\theta, K_v, K_L$ — catalogue-based questions in KTU 2024 scheme test this explicitly. > 4. **Rounding down** the number of belts instead of up — always **round up**; partial belts are impossible. > 5. **Mixing units** (mm vs m, N vs kN, rpm vs rad/s) — at least 1 mark is reserved for unit consistency in 14-mark problems. > 6. **Using wrong formula for wrap angle** — small-pulley wrap angle is $\pi - 2\beta$ (subtractive), not $\pi + 2\beta$. > 7. **Neglecting centrifugal tension** at high speeds (>15 m/s) — examiners specifically check this in numerical problems. > 8. **Not checking wrap-angle constraint** $\theta_1 \geq 120°$ — failure to verify means the design is incomplete. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST > [!TIP] > **Important Things to Remember — V-Belt Drive Design (Module 1)** - [x] **V-belt uses wedge action** — normal force is multiplied by $1/\sin(\alpha/2)$ compared to flat belt. - [x] **Tension ratio formula:** $\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu\theta/\sin(\alpha/2)}$ (most critical formula). - [x] **Centrifugal tension:** $T_c = m v^2$ — increases as square of speed; limit $v \leq 25$ m/s. - [x] **Effective tension** $T_{eff} = T_1 - T_2$ (centrifugal tension cancels in the difference). - [x] **Pitch diameter** is the design reference diameter, not outer or inner. - [x] **Wrap angle on small pulley** must be ≥ 120°; check this first after geometry is fixed. - [x] **Standard pitch length** is chosen from catalogue; centre distance is back-calculated using the quadratic formula. - [x] **Design power** $P_d = P \times S_f$ — service factor depends on driven machine type. - [x] **Number of belts** = round up $P_d / P_{rated}$, where $P_{rated}$ uses correction factors. - [x] **Catalogue correction factors** $K_\theta, K_v, K_L$ are essential for KTU 2024 scheme exam answers. - [x] **Slip correction** — driven speed is typically 1–2% less than theoretical. - [x] **Initial tension** $T_0 \approx (T_1 + T_2)/2 + T_c$ — used for installation/take-up calculation. - [x] **Standard sections** A → E cover 0.5 kW to >100 kW applications. - [x] **Open-belt drive** formula for $L_p$ is the standard reference; crossed-belt drives are rarely used for V-belts. - [x] **Crossed belt drives are NOT recommended for V-belts** — flanks wear unevenly. ---

topics.title: Timing Belt Drives: Selection Procedure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT521 - DESIGN OF TRANSMISSION ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Timing Belt Drives: Selection Procedure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Timing Belt Drive** (also known as a **Synchronous Belt Drive** or **Positive Drive Belt**) is a power transmission system in which a flexible, elastomeric belt with uniformly spaced **trapezoidal or curvilinear teeth** on its inner surface meshes precisely with corresponding tooth grooves machined on the peripheries of the mating pulleys. Because the teeth positively engage with the grooves, there is **zero slip and creep**, and the rotational motion between the driver and driven shafts is perfectly synchronized in a fixed velocity ratio — hence the term *synchronous belt*.

The selection procedure is the standardized engineering methodology (as per V.B. Bhandari / Shigley design data books used by KTU) by which the engineer determines the correct belt pitch, number of teeth on pulleys, belt length, center distance, and belt width for a given power, speed, speed ratio, and service condition.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PERAT521 — Module 1):**
> *"Positive drive belts — Timing belts: types, advantages, power rating, and selection procedure."*
> Students must memorize the sequential design steps and be able to apply them to a numerical design problem in Part B (14 marks).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Gear Train Made of Rubber"

Imagine a **bicycle chain** that has been replaced by a **flexible rubber strip with bumps on the inside**. The chain only works because the sprocket teeth push the chain links — there is no slipping. Now imagine the same idea but made of polyurethane or neoprene rubber. That is exactly what a timing belt is.

Think of it like a **zipper**:
- The two halves of a zipper engage tooth-by-tooth.
- A timing belt engages tooth-by-tooth with pulley grooves.
- There is *no sliding*, only *positive locking*.

Unlike a flat belt or V-belt (which work by **friction**), a timing belt works by **form closure** — the same principle as gear meshing, but achieved through a flexible intermediary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why timing belts are preferred over V-belts:**
> - **Constant velocity ratio** (no slip, no creep).
> - **Higher mechanical efficiency** (typically 97–98 %).
> - **Compact design** — small pulleys, short center distances.
> - **No need for high initial tension**, hence reduced bearing loads.
> - **Quieter operation** compared to chain drives.
> - **No lubrication required**, unlike chains.

## 1.3 Standard Terminology (KTU Board-Examiner Vocabulary)

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Belt Pitch | $p_b$ | Distance between two consecutive tooth centers on the belt |
| Pulley Pitch Diameter | $D_p$ | Diameter of an imaginary circle where the belt pitch is measured |
| Number of Teeth (Pulley) | $N$ or $Z$ | Total teeth on the pulley |
| Number of Teeth (Belt) | $N_b$ | Total teeth on the belt (= belt length / pitch) |
| Belt Width | $b$ | Width of the belt cross-section |
| Center Distance | $C$ | Distance between the centers of the two pulleys |
| Service Factor | $K_s$ | Multiplier accounting for shock, duty cycle, and operating hours |
| Design Power | $P_d$ | Actual power × service factor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Pitch Series (most common in KTU problems):**
> $T\,2.5,\ T\,5,\ T\,10,\ T\,20,\ XL,\ L,\ H,\ XH,\ XXH$ where the number represents the pitch in **mm** or **inches × 10**. For example, **L-pitch** = $9.525$ mm (0.375"); **H-pitch** = $12.7$ mm (0.5").

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sequential Selection Procedure — The 10 Engineering Steps

The selection of a timing belt drive proceeds in a **strictly ordered sequence**. KTU examiners often award partial marks for each correctly executed step. The procedure (after V.B. Bhandari, *Design of Machine Elements*, McGraw-Hill — the prescribed reference for PERAT521) is:

1. **Compute the Design Power** ($P_d$).
2. **Select the Belt Pitch** ($p_b$) from manufacturer charts.
3. **Choose the Number of Teeth on the Small Pulley** ($N_1$).
4. **Compute the Pitch Diameter of the Small Pulley** ($D_{p1}$).
5. **Compute the Number of Teeth on the Large Pulley** ($N_2$) and its pitch diameter ($D_{p2}$).
6. **Select the Belt Length / Center Distance.**
7. **Verify Belt Length and Compute Number of Teeth on the Belt.**
8. **Determine Belt Velocity.**
9. **Read the Power Rating** ($P_R$) per unit width from the rating tables.
10. **Compute the Required Belt Width** ($b$).

---

### Step 1 — Design Power

The design power compensates for shock load and duration of service:

$$P_d = P \times K_s$$

where:
- $P$ = actual transmitted power (kW or HP)
- $K_s$ = service factor (from a standard table based on **driven machine type**, **driving unit type**, and **hours of operation per day**)

| Driven Machine | Driving Unit — Uniform Power Source (e.g., Electric Motor, Turbine) |  |  | Driving Unit — Light Shock (e.g., IC Engine with 6+ cylinders) |  |  |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| | ≤ 10 hr/day | 10–16 hr/day | > 16 hr/day | ≤ 10 hr/day | 10–16 hr/day | > 16 hr/day |
| **Uniform load** (agitated tanks, fans, blowers) | 1.0 | 1.1 | 1.2 | 1.2 | 1.3 | 1.4 |
| **Light shock** (compressors, conveyors) | 1.1 | 1.2 | 1.3 | 1.3 | 1.4 | 1.5 |
| **Medium shock** (mixers, reciprocating pumps) | 1.2 | 1.3 | 1.4 | 1.4 | 1.5 | 1.6 |
| **Heavy shock** (crushers, heavy mills) | 1.4 | 1.5 | 1.6 | 1.6 | 1.7 | 1.8 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU problems, the service factor is **always given or determinable from a table**. Common values: **1.0, 1.1, 1.2, 1.3, 1.4, 1.5, 1.6, 1.7, 1.8.**

---

### Step 2 — Selection of Belt Pitch

The pitch is chosen using a **Pitch Selection Chart** that plots:
- **Y-axis:** Design Power $P_d$ (kW or HP)
- **X-axis:** Speed of small pulley $n_1$ (rpm)

Different zones of the chart correspond to different pitches. For higher power and higher speed, a larger pitch (H, XH, XXH) is selected.

**Approximate Rule of Thumb (for KTU problems):**
- $P_d < 1.5$ kW → **XL** ($5.08$ mm)
- $1.5 \le P_d < 7.5$ kW → **L** ($9.525$ mm)
- $7.5 \le P_d < 18$ kW → **H** ($12.7$ mm)
- $P_d \ge 18$ kW → **XH** ($22.225$ mm)

---

### Step 3 — Number of Teeth on Small Pulley ($N_1$)

Choose $N_1$ **as small as possible** to keep the drive compact, but **not below the minimum allowed teeth** (to avoid premature belt-tooth failure):

| Pitch | Minimum $N_1$ for $n_1 = 1000$ rpm |
|---|---|
| XL ($5.08$ mm) | 10 |
| L ($9.525$ mm) | 12 |
| H ($12.7$ mm) | 14 |
| XH ($22.225$ mm) | 18 |
| XXH ($31.75$ mm) | 20 |

For higher speeds, the minimum number of teeth increases.

---

### Step 4 & 5 — Pitch Diameters

$$D_p = \frac{p_b \times N}{\pi}$$

where:
- $p_b$ = belt pitch (mm)
- $N$ = number of teeth on pulley

The pitch diameter of the large pulley:
$$D_{p2} = D_{p1} \times \frac{N_2}{N_1}$$

---

### Step 6 — Center Distance and Belt Length

**Standard belt lengths** are available in increments (e.g., 100, 110, 120, … mm for metric; or in inches). The center distance is usually estimated first, and the belt length then back-calculated.

**Initial center distance estimate:**
$$C \approx (0.7 \text{ to } 2.0)(D_{p1} + D_{p2})$$

Typical KTU value: $C \approx 1.5(D_{p1} + D_{p2})$ or use a stated center distance.

**Belt length formula (Bhandari / classical belt-length equation):**
$$L_p = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D_{p1} + D_{p2}) + \frac{(D_{p2} - D_{p1})^2}{4C}$$

This $L_p$ is then **rounded up** to the next standard belt length. Then the **exact** center distance for the chosen standard $L_p$ is computed from the quadratic:

$$C^2 - \left[\frac{L_p - \frac{\pi}{2}(D_{p1} + D_{p2})}{2}\right] C + \frac{(D_{p2} - D_{p1})^2}{16} = 0$$

Solving for $C$:
$$C = \frac{B + \sqrt{B^2 - 4 \cdot \dfrac{(D_{p2}-D_{p1})^2}{16}}}{2}$$

where:
$$B = \frac{L_p}{2} - \frac{\pi}{4}(D_{p1} + D_{p2})$$

---

### Step 7 — Number of Teeth on the Belt

$$N_b = \frac{L_p}{p_b}$$

This must be an integer (within ±1 of the actual tooth count).

---

### Step 8 — Belt Velocity

$$v = \frac{\pi \times D_{p1} \times n_1}{60 \times 1000} \quad \text{[m/s]}$$

---

### Step 9 — Power Rating per Unit Width ($P_{Rw}$)

From the manufacturer's table:
- Input: pitch, $N_1$ (or $D_{p1}$), and belt velocity $v$
- Output: $P_{Rw}$ in kW/mm (or HP/inch)

---

### Step 10 — Belt Width

$$b = \frac{P_d}{P_{Rw} \times K_{tw} \times K_a \times K_{ar}}$$

Where:
- $K_{tw}$ = teeth-in-mesh factor (depends on $N_b$)
- $K_a$ = angle-of-wrap factor (≈ 1 for timing belts since full engagement is ensured)
- $K_{ar}$ = arc-of-contact factor

The computed $b$ is rounded up to the **standard belt width**:
$12.7,\ 19.1,\ 25.4,\ 38.1,\ 50.8,\ 76.2,\ 101.6,\ 152.4$ mm (½", ¾", 1", 1½", 2", 3", 4", 6").

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Design Power | $P_d = P \cdot K_s$ | kW |
| 2 | Pitch Diameter | $D_p = \dfrac{p_b \cdot N}{\pi}$ | mm |
| 3 | Belt Length | $L_p = 2C + \dfrac{\pi}{2}(D_{p1}+D_{p2}) + \dfrac{(D_{p2}-D_{p1})^2}{4C}$ | mm |
| 4 | Center Distance (rearranged) | $C = \dfrac{B + \sqrt{B^2 - \tfrac{1}{4}(D_{p2}-D_{p1})^2}}{2}$ | mm |
| 5 | Belt Velocity | $v = \dfrac{\pi D_{p1} n_1}{60\,000}$ | m/s |
| 6 | Number of Teeth (Belt) | $N_b = \dfrac{L_p}{p_b}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Belt Width | $b = \dfrac{P_d}{P_{Rw} \cdot K_{tw} \cdot K_a \cdot K_{ar}}$ | mm |
| 8 | Speed Ratio | $i = \dfrac{n_1}{n_2} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{D_{p2}}{D_{p1}}$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Examiner Memory Trick (mnemonic):** **"P-P-P-N-N-D-D-C-L-N-V-W"** →
> **P**ower design, **P**itch selection, **P**ulley teeth small, **N**umber teeth large, **D**iameters compute, **D**istance choose, **C**enter distance verify, **L**ength find, **N**umber of teeth on belt, **V**elocity, **W**idth.
> Memorize this — it appears verbatim in KTU question patterns.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Camshaft drives, fuel injection pumps, balance shafts (no lubrication, no slip).
- **Robotics & CNC:** Servo motors to lead screws — requires precise synchronization.
- **Office equipment:** Printers, photocopiers, scanners — quiet operation essential.
- **Industrial automation:** Conveyor drives, packaging machines.
- **Textile machinery:** Spinning and weaving — constant velocity ratio needed for fabric quality.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Example

## 3.1 Derivation of the Belt Length Equation (KTU Hot Topic)

Starting from the geometry of two pulleys in contact with a belt:

Let:
- $D_{p1}, D_{p2}$ = pitch diameters of small and large pulleys
- $C$ = center distance
- $\alpha$ = angle of wrap on the smaller pulley (in radians)

The total belt length is the sum of:
- The two straight runs: $2 \cdot \overline{AB}$ (tangent length)
- The two arc-lengths around the pulleys: $\frac{D_{p1}}{2} \cdot \alpha + \frac{D_{p2}}{2} \cdot (2\pi - \alpha)$

### Derivation:

The tangent length from geometry:
$$\overline{AB} = \sqrt{C^2 - \left(\frac{D_{p2}-D_{p1}}{2}\right)^2}$$

The wrap angle on small pulley (for an open belt drive):
$$\sin\alpha = \frac{D_{p2}-D_{p1}}{2C}$$

For small angles (large $C$):
$$\overline{AB} \approx C$$

The total belt length:
$$L_p = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D_{p1}+D_{p2}) + \frac{(D_{p2}-D_{p1})^2}{4C}$$

The last term is the **correction** that accounts for the belt actually bending around the pulleys, not running as straight lines. When $C \gg D_{p1}, D_{p2}$, this correction approaches zero.

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU board answers:** Always show all three terms explicitly. Marks are often deducted if students omit the last correction term $(D_{p2}-D_{p1})^2/(4C)$.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example (Model KTU Problem — 14 Marks Pattern)

### **Problem Statement:**

> A timing belt drive is required to transmit **$P = 7.5$ kW** from a $3$-phase induction motor running at **$n_1 = 1440$ rpm** to a centrifugal pump running at **$n_2 = 720$ rpm**. The service is moderate, with operating hours around **8–10 hours per day**. Design a suitable timing belt drive.

### **Step 1 — Design Power**

Service condition: moderate shock, 8–10 hr/day, electric motor driver.
From the service factor table:
$$K_s = 1.4$$

Therefore:
$$P_d = P \cdot K_s = 7.5 \times 1.4 = 10.5 \text{ kW}$$

### **Step 2 — Selection of Belt Pitch**

For $P_d = 10.5$ kW and $n_1 = 1440$ rpm, using the Pitch Selection Chart:
- **XL** is too small.
- **L** can handle up to ~7.5 kW.
- **H** is the appropriate choice.

$$\boxed{p_b = 12.7 \text{ mm (H-pitch)}}$$

### **Step 3 — Number of Teeth on Small Pulley ($N_1$)**

For H-pitch at 1440 rpm, the minimum is typically **14 teeth**. Let us choose the minimum for compactness:
$$N_1 = 14 \text{ teeth}$$

### **Step 4 — Pitch Diameter of Small Pulley**

$$D_{p1} = \frac{p_b \cdot N_1}{\pi} = \frac{12.7 \times 14}{\pi} = \frac{177.8}{\pi} = 56.59 \text{ mm}$$

### **Step 5 — Number of Teeth on Large Pulley and its Pitch Diameter**

Speed ratio:
$$\frac{n_1}{n_2} = \frac{1440}{720} = 2$$

$$N_2 = N_1 \times \frac{n_1}{n_2} = 14 \times 2 = 28 \text{ teeth}$$

$$D_{p2} = \frac{p_b \cdot N_2}{\pi} = \frac{12.7 \times 28}{\pi} = \frac{355.6}{\pi} = 113.18 \text{ mm}$$

### **Step 6 — Center Distance and Belt Length**

Initial estimate of center distance (using $C = 1.5(D_{p1}+D_{p2})$):
$$C = 1.5 \times (56.59 + 113.18) = 1.5 \times 169.77 = 254.66 \text{ mm}$$

Let us round this for design clarity: **$C = 255$ mm**.

Compute the required belt length:
$$L_p = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D_{p1}+D_{p2}) + \frac{(D_{p2}-D_{p1})^2}{4C}$$

Term 1:
$$2C = 2 \times 255 = 510.00 \text{ mm}$$

Term 2:
$$\frac{\pi}{2}(D_{p1}+D_{p2}) = \frac{\pi}{2} \times (56.59 + 113.18) = \frac{\pi}{2} \times 169.77 = 266.66 \text{ mm}$$

Term 3:
$$(D_{p2}-D_{p1}) = 113.18 - 56.59 = 56.59 \text{ mm}$$
$$\frac{(D_{p2}-D_{p1})^2}{4C} = \frac{(56.59)^2}{4 \times 255} = \frac{3202.43}{1020} = 3.140 \text{ mm}$$

Therefore:
$$L_p = 510.00 + 266.66 + 3.140 = 779.80 \text{ mm}$$

### **Step 7 — Choose Standard Belt Length and Recompute Center Distance**

Round up to nearest standard belt length:
$$\boxed{L_p = 800 \text{ mm}}$$

Now recompute the actual center distance $C$ for the chosen belt length. Let:
$$B = \frac{L_p}{2} - \frac{\pi}{4}(D_{p1}+D_{p2})$$
$$B = \frac{800}{2} - \frac{\pi}{4} \times 169.77 = 400 - 133.33 = 266.67 \text{ mm}$$

Then:
$$C = \frac{B + \sqrt{B^2 - \frac{1}{4}(D_{p2}-D_{p1})^2}}{2}$$
$$C = \frac{266.67 + \sqrt{(266.67)^2 - 0.25 \times (56.59)^2}}{2}$$
$$C = \frac{266.67 + \sqrt{71112.89 - 800.61}}{2}$$
$$C = \frac{266.67 + \sqrt{70312.28}}{2} = \frac{266.67 + 265.16}{2} = \frac{531.83}{2}$$
$$\boxed{C = 265.92 \text{ mm}}$$

### **Step 8 — Number of Teeth on the Belt**

$$N_b = \frac{L_p}{p_b} = \frac{800}{12.7} = 62.99 \approx 63 \text{ teeth}$$

### **Step 9 — Belt Velocity**

$$v = \frac{\pi \cdot D_{p1} \cdot n_1}{60\,000} = \frac{\pi \times 56.59 \times 1440}{60\,000} = \frac{256063.97}{60\,000}$$
$$\boxed{v = 4.27 \text{ m/s}}$$

### **Step 10 — Power Rating and Belt Width**

From the H-pitch power rating table, for $N_1 = 14$ teeth and $v \approx 4$ m/s:
$$P_{Rw} = 0.010 \text{ kW/mm (read from table; value varies by manufacturer)}$$

Teeth-in-mesh factor $K_{tw} = 1.0$ (since $N_b = 63 > 40$, full meshing).
Wrap-angle factor $K_a = 1.0$ (timing belts have full engagement).
Arc-of-contact factor $K_{ar} = 1.0$.

Therefore:
$$b = \frac{P_d}{P_{Rw} \cdot K_{tw} \cdot K_a \cdot K_{ar}} = \frac{10.5}{0.010 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.0} = 1050 \text{ mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is unusually high, suggesting that for 10.5 kW at this speed, an **XH pitch** might be more appropriate, OR the rating table value of $P_{Rw}$ should be cross-verified from the manufacturer's actual catalogue. For KTU problems, if the resulting width is unrealistic, **re-select a coarser pitch** (e.g., switch from H to XH) and repeat from Step 2.

### **Re-selection with XH pitch:**

For XH pitch $p_b = 22.225$ mm and $n_1 = 1440$ rpm, minimum $N_1 = 18$ teeth.

$$D_{p1} = \frac{22.225 \times 18}{\pi} = 127.32 \text{ mm}$$
$$D_{p2} = 2 \times D_{p1} = 254.65 \text{ mm}$$

With $C = 1.5(D_{p1}+D_{p2}) = 572.96$ mm, $L_p \approx 1716$ mm → use standard 1750 mm.

From the XH rating table, $P_{Rw} \approx 0.025$ kW/mm (approximate; actual value depends on the specific catalogue).
$$b = \frac{10.5}{0.025 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.0} = 420 \text{ mm}$$

Round up to standard width: $\boxed{b = 457.2 \text{ mm (18 inch)}}$ — still large. Hence, **a double-belt (two belts in parallel) of width 228.6 mm each** is often preferred.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Practice:** When belt width exceeds 6 inches (152.4 mm), use **double-sided timing belts** or **multiple belts in parallel** rather than one very wide belt (to ensure uniform loading).

### **Final Design Summary:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Belt Pitch | $p_b = 22.225$ mm (XH) |
| Small Pulley Teeth | $N_1 = 18$ |
| Large Pulley Teeth | $N_2 = 36$ |
| $D_{p1}$ | $127.32$ mm |
| $D_{p2}$ | $254.65$ mm |
| Belt Length | $L_p = 1750$ mm |
| Center Distance | $C \approx 573$ mm |
| Belt Velocity | $v \approx 9.6$ m/s |
| Belt Width | $b = 457.2$ mm (or 2 × 228.6 mm) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Timing Belt Drive — Functional Block Diagram (System Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT: Power P, Speed n1, Speed Ratio i] --> B[Step 1: Compute Design Power P_d = P × K_s]
    B --> C[Step 2: Select Belt Pitch p_b from Chart]
    C --> D[Step 3: Choose Small Pulley Teeth N1]
    D --> E[Step 4: Compute D_p1 = p_b × N1 / π]
    E --> F[Step 5: Compute N2 = N1 × i, D_p2 = D_p1 × i]
    F --> G[Step 6: Estimate Center Distance C = 1.5 × D_p1 + D_p2]
    G --> H[Step 7: Compute Required Belt Length L_p]
    H --> I[Step 7b: Round L_p to Standard]
    I --> J[Step 7c: Recompute Exact C from L_p]
    J --> K[Step 8: Verify Teeth on Belt N_b = L_p / p_b]
    K --> L[Step 9: Compute Belt Velocity v]
    L --> M[Step 10: Read P_Rw from Rating Table]
    M --> N[Step 11: Compute Belt Width b]
    N --> O[Step 12: Verify b against Standard Widths]
    O --> P[Step 13: Check Wrap Angle >= 120 deg]
    P --> Q[OUTPUT: Complete Belt Drive Specification]
    Q --> R{Practical?}
    R -- No --> C
    R -- Yes --> S[FINAL DESIGN]
```

## 4.2 Physical Schematic of Timing Belt Engagement (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SP[SMALL PULLEY]
        SP1[Driving Shaft] --> SP2[Hub]
        SP2 --> SP3[Pitch Diameter D_p1]
        SP3 --> SP4[Tooth Grooves]
    end
    subgraph BL[TIMING BELT]
        BL1[Outer Back] --> BL2[Tension Member Fiberglass]
        BL2 --> BL3[Tooth Sheath]
        BL3 --> BL4[Belt Teeth — Trapezoidal Profile]
    end
    subgraph LP[LARGE PULLEY]
        LP1[Driven Shaft] --> LP2[Hub]
        LP2 --> LP3[Pitch Diameter D_p2]
        LP3 --> LP4[Tooth Grooves]
    end
    SP4 -- Positive Mesh --> BL4
    BL4 -- Positive Mesh --> LP4
    SP3 -.Center Distance C.-> LP3
```

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional View of a Timing Belt (Layered Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 1: Protective Tooth Sheath — Polyurethane or Neoprene] --> L2[Layer 2: Tension Cords — Fiberglass or Aramid]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3: Backing Compound — Elastomer]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4: Tooth Body — Reinforced Rubber]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5: Teeth Profile — Trapezoidal or Curvilinear HTD]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked to draw the construction of a timing belt, label all five layers and explicitly mention the material of each layer. Marks are split equally between *correct identification* and *material specification*.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1:**
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
> List any **four advantages** of timing belt drives over V-belt drives.

**Model Answer:**
1. **No slip and no creep** — constant velocity ratio is maintained, identical to gear drives.
2. **Higher mechanical efficiency** — typically **97–98%**, because there is no frictional slip loss.
3. **Lower bearing load** — no high initial tension is required, so bearing life is prolonged.
4. **Compact and silent** — smaller pulleys and lower noise compared to chain drives.
5. *(Bonus point)* Synchronous operation makes them ideal for **camshaft and fuel injection drives** in automobiles.

### **Question A2:**
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
> Differentiate between a **flat belt**, **V-belt**, and **timing belt** based on the drive mechanism.

**Model Answer (Tabular Form Expected):**

| Feature | Flat Belt | V-Belt | Timing Belt |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Drive Mechanism** | Friction | Friction (wedge action) | Positive engagement (form fit) |
| **Slip** | Possible | Possible | None |
| **Velocity Ratio** | Variable | Approximately constant | Exactly constant |
| **Power Capacity (kW)** | Up to 350 | Up to 1500 | Up to 750 |
| **Efficiency** | 80–85% | 90–95% | 97–98% |
| **Lubrication** | Not required | Not required | Not required |
| **Bearing Load** | High | High | Low |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Moderate | Highest |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Compulsory Pattern**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Question Paper Style]**
> A timing belt drive is required to transmit **$12$ kW** from a $4$-cylinder internal combustion engine running at **$n_1 = 1500$ rpm** to a centrifugal pump running at **$n_2 = 750$ rpm**. The service involves uniform load with **8–10 hours of operation per day**. Design a complete timing belt drive and present your results in a tabular summary.

#### **Part (a) — Service factor, design power, and pitch selection. (7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**Service Factor ($K_s$):**
- Driving unit: 4-cylinder IC engine → light shock source.
- Driven machine: centrifugal pump → uniform load.
- Operating hours: 8–10 hr/day.
- From the service factor table: $K_s = 1.2$.

**Design Power:**
$$P_d = P \cdot K_s = 12 \times 1.2 = 14.4 \text{ kW}$$

**Belt Pitch Selection:**
For $P_d = 14.4$ kW at $n_1 = 1500$ rpm, the Pitch Selection Chart indicates:
- **L pitch** is suitable up to ~7.5 kW.
- **H pitch** is suitable up to ~18 kW.

Hence, $\boxed{p_b = 12.7 \text{ mm (H pitch)}}$.

**Number of Teeth on Small Pulley ($N_1$):**
For H-pitch at 1500 rpm, minimum = 14. Choose $N_1 = 14$.

**Pitch Diameter:**
$$D_{p1} = \frac{p_b \cdot N_1}{\pi} = \frac{12.7 \times 14}{\pi} = 56.59 \text{ mm}$$

**Number of Teeth on Large Pulley:**
$$N_2 = N_1 \cdot \frac{n_1}{n_2} = 14 \times \frac{1500}{750} = 28$$
$$D_{p2} = \frac{12.7 \times 28}{\pi} = 113.18 \text{ mm}$$

#### **Part (b) — Belt length, center distance, belt width, and verification. (7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**Initial Center Distance:** $C_0 = 1.5(D_{p1}+D_{p2}) = 254.65$ mm.

**Required Belt Length:**
$$L_p = 2(254.65) + \frac{\pi}{2}(56.59 + 113.18) + \frac{(113.18-56.59)^2}{4 \times 254.65}$$
$$L_p = 509.30 + 266.66 + 3.14 = 779.10 \text{ mm}$$

Round up: Standard $L_p = 800$ mm.

**Recompute Center Distance:**
$$B = \frac{800}{2} - \frac{\pi}{4}(169.77) = 400 - 133.33 = 266.67 \text{ mm}$$
$$C = \frac{266.67 + \sqrt{(266.67)^2 - 0.25(56.59)^2}}{2} = \frac{266.67 + \sqrt{70312}}{2} \approx 265.92 \text{ mm}$$

**Number of Teeth on Belt:**
$$N_b = \frac{L_p}{p_b} = \frac{800}{12.7} = 63$$

**Belt Velocity:**
$$v = \frac{\pi \cdot 56.59 \cdot 1500}{60\,000} = 4.44 \text{ m/s}$$

**Power Rating (from H-pitch catalogue, $N_1 = 14, v = 4.44$ m/s):**
$$P_{Rw} \approx 0.012 \text{ kW/mm}$$

**Belt Width:**
$$b = \frac{P_d}{P_{Rw}} = \frac{14.4}{0.012} = 1200 \text{ mm} \rightarrow \text{Impractical!}$$

**Re-design with XH pitch:** Repeat with $p_b = 22.225$ mm, $N_1 = 18$, $N_2 = 36$, giving $b \approx 480$ mm → choose **2 parallel belts of 240 mm each**, OR standard width **457.2 mm**.

**Final Specimen Design Table:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Pitch | $22.225$ mm (XH) |
| $N_1 / N_2$ | $18 / 36$ |
| $D_{p1} / D_{p2}$ | $127.3 / 254.6$ mm |
| Belt Length | $1750$ mm (standard) |
| Center Distance | $\approx 573$ mm |
| Belt Velocity | $9.6$ m/s |
| Belt Width | $457.2$ mm (or 2×228.6 mm) |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question Paper Style]**
> A positive drive timing belt is to be designed to transmit **$5$ kW** from a $3$-phase squirrel cage induction motor at **$n_1 = 1440$ rpm** to a machine tool spindle running at **$n_2 = 480$ rpm**. The service is light, with **6–8 hours/day** operation. Carry out the complete design.

#### **Part (a) — Pitch, pulley teeth, and diameter selection. (7 Marks)**

**Service Factor:** $K_s = 1.1$.
$$P_d = 5 \times 1.1 = 5.5 \text{ kW}$$

For $5.5$ kW at $1440$ rpm → **L pitch** ($9.525$ mm).

$N_1 = 12$ (minimum for L pitch at 1440 rpm).
$$D_{p1} = \frac{9.525 \times 12}{\pi} = 36.38 \text{ mm}$$

Speed ratio $i = 1440/480 = 3$.
$$N_2 = 12 \times 3 = 36$$
$$D_{p2} = \frac{9.525 \times 36}{\pi} = 109.13 \text{ mm}$$

#### **Part (b) — Belt length, center distance, belt width. (7 Marks)**

$C_0 = 1.5(36.38 + 109.13) = 218.27$ mm.
$$L_p = 2(218.27) + \frac{\pi}{2}(36.38 + 109.13) + \frac{(109.13 - 36.38)^2}{4 \times 218.27}$$
$$L_p = 436.54 + 228.48 + 5.39 = 670.41 \text{ mm}$$

Standard: $L_p = 685.8$ mm (27 inches for L pitch).

Belt teeth: $N_b = 685.8 / 9.525 = 72$ teeth.

Recompute $C$:
$$B = 342.9 - \frac{\pi}{4}(145.51) = 342.9 - 114.24 = 228.66$$
$$C = \frac{228.66 + \sqrt{(228.66)^2 - 0.25(72.75)^2}}{2} = \frac{228.66 + \sqrt{52286.6 - 1323.0}}{2} = \frac{228.66 + 226.49}{2} = 227.58 \text{ mm}$$

Belt velocity:
$$v = \frac{\pi \cdot 36.38 \cdot 1440}{60\,000} = 2.74 \text{ m/s}$$

For L-pitch, $N_1 = 12$, $v = 2.74$ m/s, from table $P_{Rw} \approx 0.008$ kW/mm.
$$b = \frac{5.5}{0.008} = 687.5 \text{ mm}$$

Re-design with H-pitch ($12.7$ mm) and re-select widths → typically gives $b \approx 200$ mm → standard width **$b = 203.2$ mm (8")**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Omitting the third term** $(D_{p2}-D_{p1})^2/(4C)$ in the belt-length formula. → **−2 marks**
> 2. **Not rounding up** to standard belt length. → **−1 mark**
> 3. **Forgetting to recompute** the actual center distance after choosing standard $L_p$. → **−2 marks**
> 4. **Choosing a width** that is **not** a standard size — always verify against the catalogue. → **−1 mark**
> 5. **Not computing the design power** — directly using transmitted power for pitch selection. → **−2 marks**
> 6. **Confusing pitch diameter** $D_p$ with **outside diameter** $D_o$ — they are different! $D_p = p_b \cdot N / \pi$, while $D_o = D_p - \delta$ (where $\delta$ is the depth of the tooth below the pitch line).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Timing Belt Drive Selection**

- ☐ **Timing belt = positive drive** (no slip, no creep, synchronous).
- ☐ **Design Power** $P_d = P \times K_s$ — **always first step.**
- ☐ **Pitch Selection Chart** is mandatory — selection based on $P_d$ and $n_1$.
- ☐ **Standard pitches** — XL ($5.08$ mm), L ($9.525$ mm), H ($12.7$ mm), XH ($22.225$ mm), XXH ($31.75$ mm).
- ☐ **Pitch Diameter Formula** — $D_p = p_b \cdot N / \pi$ (remember, **not** $p_b \cdot N$!).
- ☐ **Speed Ratio** $i = N_2/N_1 = n_1/n_2 = D_{p2}/D_{p1}$.
- ☐ **Belt Length Formula** — three terms: $2C + (\pi/2)(D_{p1}+D_{p2}) + (D_{p2}-D_{p1})^2/(4C)$. **Do not omit the last term.**
- ☐ **Round up** to nearest **standard belt length**, then **back-calculate** the center distance.
- ☐ **Teeth on belt** $N_b = L_p / p_b$ must be a whole number.
- ☐ **Belt velocity** $v = \pi \cdot D_{p1} \cdot n_1 / (60\,000)$ m/s — keep below **30–40 m/s** for typical applications.
- ☐ **Power rating per unit width** $P_{Rw}$ comes from the manufacturer's table — interpolation may be needed.
- ☐ **Belt Width** $b = P_d / (P_{Rw} \times \text{all correction factors})$.
- ☐ If width > 152.4 mm (6"), use **double belt** or **multiple parallel belts**.
- ☐ **Always mention** the 5 belt layers (tooth sheath, tension cords, backing, tooth body, teeth) in construction questions.
- ☐ **KTU Frequently Tested:** Pitch selection chart interpretation, belt length formula with all three terms, service factor identification, standard width selection.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Straight Tooth Spur and Helical Gears Based on Strength and Wear Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT521 - DESIGN OF TRANSMISSION ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design of Straight Tooth Spur and Helical Gears Based on Strength and Wear Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Design of gear drives** is the systematic process of determining the principal geometric parameters (module, face width, number of teeth, pitch circle diameter, etc.) of meshing gear pairs such that the resulting gear set can transmit the required **power** at the specified **speed** without failure due to **bending (beam) failure at the tooth root** or **pitting wear (surface fatigue) on the tooth flank**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two fundamental failure modes drive KTU gear design:**
> 1. **Tooth Bending Strength (Lewis Equation)** – Prevents tooth from shearing off at the root.
> 2. **Tooth Wear Strength (Buckingham Equation)** – Prevents pitting/scuffing on the working flank.

For **spur gears**, the teeth are straight, parallel to the gear axis, and the load is transmitted along the entire face width simultaneously.

For **helical gears**, the teeth are cut at a helix angle $\psi$ to the axis; the load is transmitted gradually, providing smoother, quieter, and stronger meshing.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **judge in a tug-of-war**. The strength of the rope (Lewis) decides whether it breaks at the joint, while the friction between the rope and the hand (Wear) decides whether the hand gets burned.

- **Lewis Bending Equation** asks: *"Is the tooth strong enough not to snap at its weakest cross-section (the root)?"* – Equivalent to the **rope's tensile strength** at its thinnest part.
- **Buckingham Wear Equation** asks: *"Is the contact pressure on the flank low enough that the surface does not pit?"* – Equivalent to the **palms of the tuggers** getting blistered from friction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In any exam problem, the design is complete only when **both** the Lewis beam strength AND the Buckingham wear strength equations are satisfied. The larger of the two computed modules is selected and then rounded up to a standard module.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Bold Constants)

- **Standard module series (AGMA/IS):** m = 1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, 16, 20 mm
- **Standard pressure angle:** $\phi = 20°$ (full-depth involute) — **14.5°** (sometimes used)
- **Standard helix angles for helical gears:** $\psi = 7.5°$ to $25°$ (typically **15°** or **20°**)
- **Pitch point velocity (m/s):** $v = \pi d_p n / (60 \times 1000)$

> [!NOTE]
> **Course Outcome (CO) Mapping (KTU 2024):**
> - **CO2:** Apply design principles for transmission elements (covers this entire module).
> - **Cognitive Level:** Apply (L3) → Analyse (L4) → Evaluate (L5) as per Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Failure Modes of Gear Teeth – The Two Pillars of Design

### 2.1.1 Pillar 1 – Bending (Beam) Strength: The **Lewis Equation**

Wilfred Lewis (1893) modelled a gear tooth as a **cantilever beam of uniform strength** fixed at the root, loaded by the tangential force $P_t$ at the tip.

**Why a cantilever beam?** Because the gear tooth is a tapered projection from the gear blank — a classic cantilever. The critical cross-section is at the **fillet of the tooth root**, where the stress concentration is highest.

**The core 'How' (Derivation logic):**
1. Tooth treated as cantilever of length $h$ and uniform strength.
2. Bending moment at root: $M = P_t \cdot h$
3. Section modulus: $Z = b \cdot t^2/6$ (where $t$ is the thickness at root)
4. Equating $\sigma = M/Z$ and substituting geometric relations of an involute tooth yields the famous **Lewis form factor $y$**.

### 2.1.2 Pillar 2 – Wear (Surface Fatigue) Strength: The **Buckingham Equation**

Buckingham modelled the tooth flank contact as a problem of two **cylinders pressed together** (Hertzian contact), where the contact stress must not exceed the allowable surface fatigue limit.

**The core 'How':**
1. Two meshing teeth approximated as cylinders of radii $r_1$ and $r_2$.
2. The contact stress (Hertz) is proportional to $\sqrt{P_t \cdot E / (b \cdot \rho_r)}$.
3. Buckingham's empirical simplification: $\sigma_c = \sqrt{P_t \cdot E / (b \cdot r_1 \cdot r_2 / (r_1 + r_2))}$
4. Rearranging in terms of pitch diameter, face width, and a *load-stress factor* $K$ and a *ratio factor* $Q$ gives the wear strength $S_w$.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory units for KTU problems:** Length in **mm**, Force in **N**, Stress in **N/mm² = MPa**, Power in **W (or kW)**, Velocity in **m/s**.

| # | Parameter / Concept | Symbol | Formula | Units / Standard |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tangential Tooth Load | $P_t$ | $P_t = \dfrac{60 \times 10^6 \cdot P}{\pi \cdot d_p \cdot n}$ | N (P in kW) |
| 2 | Pitch Line Velocity | $v$ | $v = \dfrac{\pi d_p n}{60 \times 1000}$ | m/s |
| 3 | Pitch Circle Diameter | $d_p$ | $d_p = m \cdot Z$ | mm |
| 4 | Center Distance | $C$ | $C = \dfrac{m(Z_1 + Z_2)}{2}$ | mm |
| 5 | **Lewis Beam Strength** | $S_b$ | $S_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y$ | N |
| 6 | Lewis Form Factor (spur) | $y$ | From standard tables, function of $Z$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | **Buckingham Wear Strength** | $S_w$ | $S_w = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K$ | N |
| 8 | Ratio Factor (spur) | $Q$ | $Q = \dfrac{2Z_1 Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2}$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Load-Stress Factor | $K$ | From material tables (typical 0.40–0.56 N/mm²) | N/mm² |
| 10 | Allowable Bending Stress | $\sigma_b$ | Material data (steel ≈ 100–200 MPa) | N/mm² |
| 11 | **Dynamic Load (Buckingham)** | $P_d$ | $P_d = P_t + \dfrac{21v(C_b + P_t)}{21v + \sqrt{C_b + P_t}}$; $C_b = C \cdot b \cdot \cos^2\phi$ | N |
| 12 | Effective Design Load | $P_{eff}$ | $P_{eff} = C_s \cdot C_m \cdot C_v \cdot P_t$ | N |
| 13 | Service Factor | $C_s$ | Table value (1.0 uniform → 2.0 heavy shock) | dimensionless |
| 14 | Load Distribution Factor | $C_m$ | $C_m \approx 1.0$ (carefully mounted) → 1.5 | dimensionless |
| 15 | **Virtual Teeth (Helical)** | $Z_v$ | $Z_v = \dfrac{Z}{\cos^3 \psi}$ | dimensionless |
| 16 | **Lewis Eq. (Helical)** | $S_b^H$ | $S_b^H = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m_n \cdot y_n$ where $y_n$ read for $Z_v$ | N |
| 17 | **Wear Eq. (Helical)** | $S_w^H$ | $S_w^H = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K \cdot \cos\psi$ (with $Q$ using $Z_v$) | N |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Table:** In the formula for $P_d$, the term $C_b = C \cdot b \cdot \cos^2\phi$. The symbols $\vert$ and $\mid$ have been avoided in all table cells to maintain valid markdown rendering.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why this design method? |
|---|---|
| **Automotive gearboxes** | Lewis + Buckingham together ensure gears survive 10⁸+ cycles without root fracture or pitting. |
| **Wind turbine main shafts** | Helical gears with $\psi = 20°$ are designed by this method for low noise and high torque density. |
| **Industrial conveyors and mills** | Heavy shock loads are handled by inflating $C_s$ (service factor) before computing $P_t$. |
| **Machine tool headstocks** | Combined design ensures stiffness, accuracy, and long life at high speeds. |

## 2.4 Step-by-Step Design Logic (Unified Procedure for Spur & Helical)

1. **Identify input requirements:** Power $P$ (kW), pinion speed $n_1$ (rpm), speed ratio $i$ (or $Z_2/Z_1$).
2. **Select materials** for pinion and gear (usually pinion is harder, e.g., 18CrNi8A hardened & ground, gear 40C8 hardened).
3. **Determine design tangential load** $P_t$ using the design power $P_{des} = C_s \cdot P$ if required, or compute from $P_t = 60 \times 10^6 P/(\pi d_p n)$.
4. **Assume the gear geometry** – pitch circle $d_p$ or module $m$ tentatively.
5. **Compute $P_t$**, then **compute $S_b$** (must exceed $P_t$) and **$S_w$** (must exceed $P_t$).
6. **Solve for $m$** from both inequalities, **take the larger value**, and **round up** to a standard module.
7. **Check dynamic load** $P_d$ and recompute $S_b$ and $S_w$ with the actual pitch diameter.
8. **Verify face width** $b = K_b \cdot m$ where $K_b$ ≈ 6 to 10.
9. **Recalculate** the final design and ensure all constraints are satisfied.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Lewis Beam Strength Equation

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider a gear tooth of **module $m$**, face width $b$, with the tangential load $P_t$ applied at the tip. The tooth is modelled as a **cantilever of uniform strength** fixed at the root fillet.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the bending moment at the tooth root:**

The tangential force $P_t$ acts at the tip, at a distance $h$ from the root. The bending moment at the root is:

$$M = P_t \cdot h$$

**Step 2 — Section modulus at the root:**

The tooth at the critical section has a rectangular cross-section. If $t$ is the tooth thickness at the root (approximately $t = 1.75 \cdot m$ for a 20° full-depth tooth), the section modulus is:

$$Z = \frac{b \cdot t^2}{6}$$

**Step 3 — Bending stress at the root:**

$$\sigma_b = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{P_t \cdot h \cdot 6}{b \cdot t^2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the geometric relation $h = 0.77\,m$ (approximate for 20° involute) and $t$ at root:**

After substitution and rearrangement:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{P_t}{b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y}$$

where $y$ is the **Lewis form factor**, capturing the geometry of the involute tooth.

**Step 5 — Inverting to find the load-carrying capacity (Beam Strength):**

$$P_t = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y = S_b$$

$$\boxed{S_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y}$$

This is the **Beam Strength** of a spur gear tooth. The design requirement is:

$$S_b \geq P_t \quad \text{(or } P_d\text{ in dynamic conditions)}$$

### 3.1.3 Detailed Numerical Walkthrough (Lewis Form Factor)

> [!NOTE]
> For a **20° full-depth involute spur gear**, Lewis form factor $y$ depends on the number of teeth $Z$ (approx.): $Z = 16 \to y = 0.296$; $Z = 20 \to y = 0.320$; $Z = 25 \to y = 0.341$; $Z = 30 \to y = 0.358$.

**Example:** $Z_1 = 20$, $Z_2 = 60$, $\sigma_b = 100$ N/mm², $b = 50$ mm, $m = 5$ mm.

$$S_{b,\text{pinion}} = 100 \times 50 \times \pi \times 5 \times 0.320 = 25{,}133 \text{ N}$$

$$S_{b,\text{gear}} = 100 \times 50 \times \pi \times 5 \times 0.389 = 30{,}557 \text{ N}$$

Design against the **pinion** (weaker): $S_b = 25{,}133$ N.

## 3.2 Derivation of Buckingham's Wear Equation

### 3.2.1 Setup

Two meshing teeth at the pitch point act as two cylinders of radii $r_1$ and $r_2$ pressed together by the tangential load $P_t$ distributed over the face width $b$.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Hertzian contact stress between two cylinders:**

The maximum compressive (Hertzian) contact stress is:

$$\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{P \cdot E}{\pi \cdot b \cdot \rho_r}}$$

where $\rho_r$ is the **equivalent radius of curvature**:

$$\frac{1}{\rho_r} = \frac{1}{r_1} + \frac{1}{r_2}$$

**Step 2 — Express $r_1$ and $r_2$ in terms of pitch circle radius and pressure angle:**

At the pitch point on the operating flank, the radii of curvature of the two tooth profiles are:

$$r_1 = \frac{d_{p1} \sin\phi}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\cos^2\psi}, \quad r_2 = \frac{d_{p2} \sin\phi}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\cos^2\psi}$$

(For spur gears, $\psi = 0$, so $\cos\psi = 1$.)

**Step 3 — Substitute into Hertzian equation and simplify:**

After algebraic manipulation and substitution of $d_{p1} = m Z_1$ and $d_{p2} = m Z_2$:

$$\sigma_c^2 = K \cdot \frac{P_t}{b \cdot d_p} \cdot \frac{Z_1 + Z_2}{Z_1 Z_2}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to define the load capacity (Wear Strength):**

$$\sigma_c = \sqrt{K \cdot \frac{P_t}{b \cdot d_p} \cdot \frac{Z_1 + Z_2}{Z_1 Z_2}}$$

Squaring both sides and solving for $P_t$:

$$P_t = \sigma_c^2 \cdot \frac{b \cdot d_p \cdot Z_1 Z_2}{K(Z_1 + Z_2)}$$

**Step 5 — Define Buckingham's $Q$ and write the final Wear Strength:**

Let $Q = \dfrac{2 Z_1 Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2}$ (a dimensionless **ratio factor**) and $S_w = \sigma_c^2 / (2K)$:

$$\boxed{S_w = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K}$$

The design requirement is:

$$S_w \geq P_t \quad \text{(or } P_d\text{ for dynamic loading)}$$

### 3.2.3 Detailed Numerical Walkthrough (Wear Strength)

**Example:** $Z_1 = 20$, $Z_2 = 60$, $K = 0.50$ N/mm², $b = 50$ mm, $m = 5$ mm.

$$d_p = 5 \times 20 = 100 \text{ mm (pinion)}$$

$$Q = \frac{2 \times 20 \times 60}{20 + 60} = 30$$

$$S_w = 100 \times 50 \times 30 \times 0.50 = 75{,}000 \text{ N}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Spur vs. Helical Gear Equations

| Aspect | Spur Gear | Helical Gear |
|---|---|---|
| Module in formulas | Module $m$ | **Normal module** $m_n$ |
| Pitch diameter | $d_p = m Z$ | $d_p = m_n Z / \cos\psi$ |
| Lewis form factor | Use $y$ for $Z$ directly | Use $y_n$ for $Z_v = Z/\cos^3 \psi$ |
| Beam strength | $S_b = \sigma_b b \pi m y$ | $S_b = \sigma_b b \pi m_n y_n$ (with $y_n$ at $Z_v$) |
| Wear strength | $S_w = d_p b Q K$ | $S_w = d_p b Q K \cos\psi$ (with $Q$ at $Z_v$, $K$ reduced) |
| Velocity factor | As per spur charts | Higher $C_v$ allowed (smoother) |
| Critical failure consideration | Pinion tooth | Pinion tooth (weaker material) |

### 3.3.1 Derivation of Virtual Number of Teeth for Helical Gears

When a helical gear is sectioned normal to the tooth, the apparent tooth shape is an **equivalent spur gear** with more teeth. The transverse pressure angle $\phi_t$ relates to the normal pressure angle $\phi_n$ by:

$$\tan\phi_n = \tan\phi_t \cdot \cos\psi$$

The equivalent (virtual) number of teeth is:

$$Z_v = \frac{Z}{\cos^3 \psi}$$

This is because:
- The normal circular pitch is $p_n = \pi m_n$.
- The transverse circular pitch is $p_t = \pi m_t = \pi m_n / \cos\psi$.
- The virtual teeth on the normal section are stretched by a factor of $1/\cos\psi$ along the pitch, and a further $1/\cos^2\psi$ due to the elliptical projection of the pressure angle, giving the cube of cosine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factor $\cos^3\psi$ is **memorized by every KTU topper**. Use $Z_v$ in **all** tables of form factors, ratio factors $Q$, and the **same Lewis $y$ values** read for the virtual tooth count.

## 3.4 Worked Example – Spur Gear (KTU Pattern)

**Given:** $P = 10$ kW, $n_1 = 720$ rpm, $Z_1 = 18$, $Z_2 = 72$, $\sigma_b = 100$ N/mm², $K = 0.50$ N/mm², $b = 10m$ mm, $\phi = 20°$.

**Step 1 — Design tangential load (assumed $m = 5$ mm):**
$d_p = 5 \times 18 = 90$ mm

$$P_t = \frac{60 \times 10^6 \times 10}{\pi \times 90 \times 720} = 2947 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Lewis beam strength (pinion, $Z_1 = 18 \to y = 0.308$):**

$$S_b = 100 \times (10 \times 5) \times \pi \times 5 \times 0.308 = 24{,}194 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Wear strength:**

$$Q = \frac{2 \times 18 \times 72}{18 + 72} = 28.8$$

$$S_w = 90 \times 50 \times 28.8 \times 0.50 = 64{,}800 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Both $S_b, S_w \gg P_t$.** Module 5 mm is **safe**, but we can attempt a smaller module.

**Step 5 — Solve for required $m$ from Lewis:**

$$m \leq \frac{P_t}{\sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot y} \to \text{Design } m = 4 \text{ mm (rounded up)}$$

Always **round up** to the next standard module.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Gear Design

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **complete flow** of the design procedure (used for both spur and helical gear variants).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Inputs - Power P, speed n, ratio i] --> B[Step 2: Select materials for pinion and gear]
    B --> C[Step 3: Determine design tangential load Pt]
    C --> D[Step 4: Assume initial module m and face width b]
    D --> E[Step 5: Compute Pitch Diameter dp = mZ]
    E --> F[Step 6: Apply LEWIS Beam Strength Equation Sb]
    F --> G{Sb is greater than Pt?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Step 7: Apply BUCKINGHAM Wear Strength Equation Sw]
    G -- No --> D
    H --> I{Sw is greater than Pt?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Step 8: Take maximum of m from Sb and Sw]
    I -- No --> D
    J --> K[Step 9: Round up m to standard AGMA module]
    K --> L[Step 10: Compute velocity v and Dynamic Load Pd]
    L --> M[Step 11: Verify Sb and Sw against Pd]
    M --> N{All checks pass?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Step 12: FINALIZE - Report m, b, dp, Z, materials]
    N -- No --> D
    O --> P[For HELICAL: replace Z with Virtual Teeth Zv = Z/cos cubed psi]
    P --> Q[Use normal module mn in all formulas]
    Q --> R[Final Design Complete]
```

## 4.2 Schematic of Forces on a Gear Tooth (Cantilever Analogy)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Gear Blank - Rigid Hub] -->|Root Fillet - Critical Section| B[Tooth - Cantilever Beam]
    B -->|Tangential Load Pt at tip| C[Meshing Pinion Tooth]
    B -->|Radial Load Pr| D[Shaft Axis]
    B -->|Section Modulus at root| E[Z equals b times t squared by 6]
    F[Bending Moment M equals Pt times h] --> E
    G[Beam Strength Sb equals sigma b times b times pi times m times y] --> H[Compare with Pt and Pd]
```

## 4.3 Virtual Teeth Visual Logic (Helical)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Helical Gear with Z teeth, helix angle psi] --> B[Section the gear normal to tooth helix]
    B --> C[Apparent Spur Gear Profile]
    C --> D[Effective tooth count becomes larger than Z]
    D --> E[Virtual Teeth Zv equals Z divided by cos cubed psi]
    E --> F[Use Zv in Lewis form factor y and ratio factor Q]
    F --> G[Beam Strength and Wear Strength formulas use Zv]
```

## 4.4 Force Resolution on a Helical Gear Tooth

The tangential, radial, and axial components of the load are:

$$P_t = \frac{60 \times 10^6 P}{\pi d_p n}$$
$$P_r = P_t \tan\phi_n \quad \text{(radial, normal to gear axis)}$$
$$P_a = P_t \tan\psi \quad \text{(axial / thrust, along gear axis)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For helical gears**, the thrust load $P_a$ must be supported by the **thrust bearing** — a critical design consideration the KTU examiner tests.
