# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the two theories used in the design of plate clutches. Which one is preferred and why?**

**Model Answer:**

The two theories are:

**(i) Uniform Pressure Theory (UPT):** Assumes that the pressure intensity `p` acting on the friction surface is **uniform** over the entire contact area. This is true only for a *new, perfectly machined* clutch.

**(ii) Uniform Wear Theory (UWT):** Assumes that the **rate of wear** is uniform over the contact surface, leading to a pressure distribution where $p \cdot r = \text{constant}$, so pressure is **inversely proportional to radius** and maximum at the inner radius.

**Preferred Theory:** **Uniform Wear Theory (UWT)** is preferred for actual design because:
- It represents the **realistic long-term operating condition** after initial wear-in.
- UPT overestimates torque capacity at the inner radius, leading to **unsafe designs**.
- KTU and IS standards recommend UWT for final dimensioning.

---

### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**What is meant by the "number of friction surfaces" in a multi-plate clutch? How is it related to the number of discs?**

**Model Answer:**

A **friction surface** is each individual contact interface where frictional torque is transmitted between a driving and a driven member.

For a multi-plate clutch:
- Each driven disc provides **2 friction surfaces** (one on each face).
- Each pair of contact faces = 1 friction surface.
- If there are `m` driven discs (and `m + 1` driving discs interleaved), then:

$$\boxed{\,n = 2m = \text{Number of driven plates} \times 2\,}$$

Equivalently, if the total number of discs (driven + driving) is $N$, then the **number of friction surfaces** is $n = N + 1$ when there is a pressure plate on one side, or $n = N - 1$ in alternate arrangements.

**Example:** A 6-disc multi-plate clutch has $n = 2 \times 3 = 6$ friction surfaces (if 3 are driven).

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Part-B questions on this module carry **14 marks** and typically have **two sub-parts of 7 marks each** (one theory + one numerical, OR two numericals). Two full choices (Q-A or Q-B) are provided.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`

**A multi-plate wet clutch has 5 driving and 4 driven plates.** The clutch is required to transmit **60 kW at 1500 rpm**. The inner radius of the friction disc is **75 mm** and the outer radius is **150 mm**. The coefficient of friction is **0.12** (wet) and the permissible pressure is **0.6 MPa**.

**Determine the following using the Uniform Wear Theory:**
- **(a)** The axial thrust force `W` required for engagement. **(7 marks)**
- **(b)** The maximum pressure developed and the average pressure. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Torque to be transmitted:**
$$T = \dfrac{60 \cdot P}{2\pi N} = \dfrac{60 \cdot 60{,}000}{2\pi \cdot 1500} = 381.97 \text{ N·m} = 381{,}970 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Number of friction surfaces:**
- 4 driven plates → each provides 2 surfaces → $n = 8$

**Step 3 — Mean radius (UWT):**
$$C = R - R_i = 150 - 75 = 75 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — (a) Compute $P_{\max}$ first, then W:**

Using UWT formula:
$$P_{\max} = \dfrac{2T}{\pi \cdot \mu \cdot n \cdot (R^{2} - R_{i}^{2})}$$

$$P_{\max} = \dfrac{2 \cdot 381{,}970}{\pi \cdot 0.12 \cdot 8 \cdot (150^{2} - 75^{2})}$$

$$= \dfrac{763{,}940}{\pi \cdot 0.96 \cdot (22500 - 5625)}$$

$$= \dfrac{763{,}940}{\pi \cdot 0.96 \cdot 16875}$$

$$= \dfrac{763{,}940}{50{,}894.4} = 15.01 \text{ N/mm}^{2} = 15.0 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Check:** $P_{\max} = 15.0 \text{ MPa} \gg p_{allow} = 0.6 \text{ MPa}$.

> This means the **current design is unsafe** — the clutch cannot transmit 60 kW. We must **redesign** the geometry. To do so, we set $P_{\max} = 0.6 \text{ MPa}$ and find the new $R$ (taking $R_i = 0.5 R$):

$$0.6 = \dfrac{2 \cdot 381{,}970}{\pi \cdot 0.12 \cdot 8 \cdot (R^{2} - (0.5R)^{2})}$$

$$0.6 = \dfrac{763{,}940}{3.016 \cdot 0.75 \cdot R^{2}} = \dfrac{763{,}940}{2.262 \cdot R^{2}}$$

$$R^{2} = \dfrac{763{,}940}{0.6 \cdot 2.262} = 562{,}800 \Rightarrow R = 750 \text{ mm}$$

This is **impractically large** — confirming that with such a low $\mu = 0.12$ and small surfaces, the design is infeasible. The student should **state this conclusion explicitly** for full marks.

**Step 6 — (a) Axial force with the *original* geometry** (as set by the problem):

$$W = 2\pi \cdot P_{\max} \cdot R_i \cdot C = 2\pi \cdot 15.01 \cdot 75 \cdot 75$$

$$W = 530{,}390 \text{ N} = 530.4 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 7 — (b) Average pressure:**

In UWT, $p(r) = k/r$ where $k = P_{\max} \cdot R_i = 15.01 \cdot 75 = 1125.75$ N/mm.

$$p_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{R - R_i} \int_{R_i}^{R} \dfrac{k}{r} dr = \dfrac{k \ln(R/R_i)}{R - R_i}$$

$$= \dfrac{1125.75 \cdot \ln(150/75)}{75} = \dfrac{1125.75 \cdot 0.6931}{75} = 10.4 \text{ MPa}$$

**Alternatively (the standard KTU shortcut):** For UWT, $p_{avg} = P_{\max}/2 = 7.505$ MPa if $R = 2R_i$ (since $\ln 2 \approx 0.693 \approx $ constant). The Board accepts this approximation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question]`

**A single plate clutch (both sides effective) transmits 25 kW at 3000 rpm.** The coefficient of friction is **0.35** and the maximum allowable pressure is **1.0 MPa**. The ratio of inner to outer radius is **0.6**.

**Determine the following using the Uniform Pressure Theory:**
- **(a)** The outer and inner radii of the friction plate. **(7 marks)**
- **(b)** The axial thrust and check for heat generation rate. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Torque:**
$$T = \dfrac{60 \cdot 25{,}000}{2\pi \cdot 3000} = 79.58 \text{ N·m} = 79{,}577 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Friction surfaces:**
$$n = 2 \text{ (both sides effective)}$$

**Step 3 — (a) Apply UPT formula with $R_i = 0.6 R$:**

UPT torque equation: $T = \tfrac{1}{2} \mu W n (R + R_i)$, with $W = \pi p (R^2 - R_i^2)$.

Substituting:
$$T = \tfrac{1}{2} \mu n \cdot \pi p (R^2 - R_i^2)(R + R_i)$$

Using the identity $(R^2 - R_i^2)(R + R_i) = (R^2 - R_i^2)(R + R_i) = (R - R_i)(R + R_i)^2$:

$$T = \tfrac{1}{2} \mu n \cdot \pi p (R - R_i)(R + R_i)^{2}$$

**Substitute $R_i = 0.6 R$:**
$$R - R_i = 0.4 R, \quad R + R_i = 1.6 R$$

$$T = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 0.35 \cdot 2 \cdot \pi \cdot 1.0 \cdot (0.4R)(1.6R)^{2}$$

$$79{,}577 = 1.0996 \cdot (0.4R)(2.56 R^{2}) = 1.0996 \cdot 1.024 \cdot R^{3}$$

$$79{,}577 = 1.126 \cdot R^{3}$$

$$R^{3} = 70{,}690 \Rightarrow R = 41.3 \text{ mm}$$

**Hmm — this is too small.** Let me recheck using the **more direct KTU formula**:

$$P_{\max} = \dfrac{4T}{\mu \cdot n \cdot (R + R_i)^{2}}$$

$$\Rightarrow (R + R_i)^{2} = \dfrac{4T}{\mu \cdot n \cdot P_{\max}} = \dfrac{4 \cdot 79{,}577}{0.35 \cdot 2 \cdot 1.0}$$

$$= \dfrac{318{,}308}{0.7} = 454{,}726$$

$$R + R_i = 674.3 \text{ mm}$$

With $R_i = 0.6 R$: $1.6 R = 674.3$ → $R = 421.4 \text{ mm} \approx 421 \text{ mm}$

**Step 4 — Inner radius:**
$$R_i = 0.6 \cdot 421.4 = 252.8 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — (b) Axial thrust:**
$$W = \pi p (R^2 - R_i^2) = \pi \cdot 1.0 \cdot (421.4^{2} - 252.8^{2})$$

$$= \pi \cdot (177{,}578 - 63{,}908) = \pi \cdot 113{,}670$$

$$W = 357{,}062 \text{ N} = 357.1 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 — Heat generation rate (during slip):**
$$Q = T \cdot \omega = 79.58 \cdot \dfrac{2\pi \cdot 3000}{60} = 79.58 \cdot 314.16$$

$$Q = 25{,}000 \text{ W} = 25 \text{ kW}$$

This matches the input power, as expected (during slip, **all** input power is dissipated as heat).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (KTU 2024 Board):**
>
> 1. **Confusing `n` with number of discs:** Many students write `n = 4` for a "4-disc clutch" when they should write `n = 2 × 4 = 8` (friction surfaces). **−2 marks penalty.**
>
> 2. **Using UPT formula for the wrong theory:** When the problem says "uniform wear," students often plug in the UPT pressure equation. **−3 marks penalty.**
>
> 3. **Forgetting to square $(R + R_i)$ in the UPT $P_{\max}$ formula:** A very common algebraic slip. **−1 mark per occurrence.**
>
> 4. **Not specifying units:** $T$ must be in **N·mm** (not N·m) when radii are in mm. **−1 mark.**
>
> 5. **Forgetting the logarithmic term for $p_{avg}$ in UWT:** The exact integral gives $\ln(R/R_i)$, not $P_{\max}/2$. However, the Board accepts the approximation $p_{avg} \approx P_{\max}/2$ for $R = 2R_i$. State the assumption explicitly.
>
> 6. **Heat/Energy not asked but omitted:** Always include the energy equation in the conclusion — KTU examiners reward comprehensive solutions.
>
> 7. **Skipping the "feasibility check":** If the calculated $P_{\max}$ exceeds the allowable pressure, students must state that the **design is not feasible** and propose a redesign. This is a **7-mark trap** in Part-B questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Plate Clutches):**

- ☐ **Single Plate:** 1 friction surface; **Multi-Plate:** `n = 2m` for `m` driven discs.
- ☐ **Two theories:** **UPT** (uniform `p`, new clutch) vs **UWT** ($p \cdot r = $ const., real clutch).
- ☐ **UWT is preferred** for final design.
- ☐ **Mean radius UPT:** $C = (R + R_i)/2$ ; **Mean radius UWT:** $C = R - R_i$.
- ☐ **Master torque equation** (common to both): $T = \tfrac{1}{2} \mu W n (R + R_i)$.
- ☐ **UPT pressure:** $P_{\max} = \dfrac{4T}{\mu n (R + R_i)^{2}}$.
- ☐ **UWT pressure:** $P_{\max} = \dfrac{2T}{\pi \mu n (R^{2} - R_{i}^{2})}$.
- ☐ **UWT axial force:** $W = 2\pi P_{\max} R_i C$.
- ☐ **UPT axial force:** $W = \pi p (R^{2} - R_{i}^{2})$.
- ☐ **Energy absorbed during engagement:** $E = \tfrac{1}{2} I (\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2) = T \cdot \theta$.
- ☐ **Power dissipated:** $Q = T \cdot \omega$ (during slip).
- ☐ **Torque from power:** $T = \dfrac{60 \cdot P}{2\pi N}$ in N·m.
- ☐ **Typical μ:** 0.1–0.5; **Typical p:** 0.07–2.0 MPa (depending on lining).
- ☐ **Typical radius ratio:** $R_i / R = 0.5$ to $0.7$ (commonly 0.5 or 0.6 in KTU problems).
- ☐ **Heat dissipation surface area** must be designed to remove the engagement energy without exceeding lining temperature limits (typically < 200 °C for organic linings).
- ☐ **Multi-plate advantage:** Higher torque in a smaller axial envelope (compact design) — uses $n > 1$ to multiply torque capacity.

> **End of Module 3 — Design of Plate Clutches Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Axial Clutches, Cone Clutches, Internal Expanding Rim Clutches, Band and Block Brakes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT521 - DESIGN OF TRANSMISSION ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Axial Clutches, Cone Clutches, Internal Expanding Rim Clutches, Band and Block Brakes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Friction Clutches — Fundamental Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Friction Clutch (KTU Definition):** A friction clutch is a machine element used to connect a driving shaft to a driven shaft, permitting the transmission of power through **surface contact** and **frictional engagement**, while allowing smooth engagement/disengagement without shock.

**Classification relevant to Module 3:**

| Type | Engagement Action | KTU Module Topic |
|------|-------------------|------------------|
| Axial (Disc) Clutch | Friction faces pushed **axially** together | ✓ |
| Cone Clutch | Friction faces wedged along a **conical** surface | ✓ |
| Internal Expanding Rim Clutch | Rim expanded outward by **centrifugal/lever** action | ✓ |
| Band Brake | Flexible band wraps & squeezes a drum | ✓ |
| Block (Shoe) Brake | Rigid block pressed onto a drum | ✓ |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Daily-Life Friction Devices"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture:** Imagine a *bicycle brake* (block brake), the *front-plate holder* in a food mixer (cone clutch), and the *chapati-maker press* (axial plate clutch). All of them **transmit motion through friction**, but the geometry of the contact surface changes the math entirely.

- **Axial Clutch →** Like pressing two coins together face-to-face and twisting one. The normal force is purely axial.
- **Cone Clutch →** Like pressing a *cup* onto its matching *saucer-shaped base* — wedging action gives a mechanical advantage of **1/sin α**.
- **Internal Expanding Clutch →** Like a *centrifugal governor* — rotating weights are flung outward and engage an inner rim.
- **Band Brake →** Like tying a rope tightly around a pole and pulling — the *capstan effect* multiplies the friction force exponentially.
- **Block Brake →** Like a *wooden stick pressed against a spinning grinding wheel*.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Design Assumptions (must memorize):**
> - Coefficient of friction (μ): **0.1 to 0.5** (typical cast iron on steel μ ≈ 0.25–0.4)
> - **Uniform Pressure Theory** → assumes *new* clutch, uniform heat distribution
> - **Uniform Wear Theory** → assumes *worn/old* clutch, design basis (used in KTU problems)
> - Semi-cone angle α typically between **8° and 15°** (standard practice for cone clutches)
> - Maximum pressure allowed: **p_max = 0.5 to 1 MPa** for asbestos-based linings

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Two Fundamental Clutch Design Theories

> [!NOTE]
> **Theory 1 — Uniform Pressure (UPT):** Pressure *p* is constant over the entire contact surface.
>
> **Theory 2 — Uniform Wear (UWT):** The product *p × r = constant*, because wear rate ∝ (pressure × sliding velocity) ∝ p·r. This is the **preferred design assumption** for KTU problems.

---

## 2.2 AXIAL (DISC / PLATE) CLUTCH — Detailed Theory

### Geometric Setup

Consider an annular friction face with:
- Outer radius = **R**
- Inner radius = **r**
- Axial engaging force = **W**
- Coefficient of friction = **μ**

### Uniform Pressure Theory (UPT) — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1:** Total axial force equals pressure × contact area
$$W = p \cdot \pi \cdot (R^2 - r^2)$$

**Step 2:** Consider an elemental ring of radius *x* and thickness *dx*. Friction force on it:
$$dF_f = \mu \cdot p \cdot 2\pi x \, dx$$

**Step 3:** Torque contributed by elemental ring:
$$dT = dF_f \cdot x = \mu \cdot p \cdot 2\pi x^2 \, dx$$

**Step 4:** Integrating from *r* to *R*:
$$T_{UPT} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot \frac{R^3 - r^3}{R^2 - r^2}$$

### Uniform Wear Theory (UWT) — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1:** Wear law: $p \cdot x = C$ (constant)
$$\therefore p = \frac{C}{x}$$

**Step 2:** Axial force equilibrium:
$$W = \int_{r}^{R} p \cdot 2\pi x \, dx = 2\pi C(R - r)$$

**Step 3:** Torque integral:
$$T_{UWT} = \int_{r}^{R} \mu \cdot p \cdot 2\pi x^2 \, dx = \pi \mu C (R^2 - r^2)$$

**Step 4:** Substituting C = W / [2π(R−r)]:
$$T_{UWT} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot (R + r)$$

### Multi-Plate Clutch Extension

For *n* friction surfaces (= n+1 discs total − 1):
$$W = n \cdot \pi \cdot p \cdot (R^2 - r^2)$$
$$T_{multi} = \frac{n \cdot \mu \cdot W}{2}(R + r) \quad \text{(UWT)}$$

---

## 2.3 CONE CLUTCH — Detailed Theory

### Geometry

- Semi-cone angle = **α**
- Mean radius = **R_m = (R + r)/2**
- Width of cone contact = **b = (R − r)/sin α**

### Key Geometric Insight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Normal Force Decomposition:** When an axial force *W* is applied, only the *component W sin α* acts normal to the cone surface. Hence the effective normal force on the cone is **W sin α**, giving a **mechanical advantage of 1/sin α**.

### Uniform Pressure Theory — Cone

$$T_{cone,\,UPT} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{\mu W}{\sin \alpha} \cdot \frac{R^3 - r^3}{R^2 - r^2}$$

### Uniform Wear Theory — Cone

$$T_{cone,\,UWT} = \frac{\mu W}{\sin \alpha} \cdot \frac{R + r}{2}$$

### Axial Force vs Cone Angle Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> **Advantage of Cone Clutch:** For the *same torque and same R, r*, the axial force required is **W × sin α** — much smaller than a disc clutch. This is why cone clutches are used where space is limited (e.g., synchromesh gears, lathe spindles).

---

## 2.4 INTERNAL EXPANDING RIM CLUTCH — Detailed Theory

### Construction

- A **rim** lined with friction material is mounted on the driven shaft.
- **Two shoes** are pivoted inside the rim.
- When rotation begins, **centrifugal force** throws the shoes outward → engages the rim.
- Engagement force may be **purely centrifugal** (Type A) or **spring-assisted** (Type B).

### Centrifugal Force on a Shoe

For a shoe of mass *m* with centre of gravity at radius *R_c*:
$$F_c = m \omega^2 R_c$$

### Effective Normal Force

If spring force is *N_s* (acting inward):
$$N_{eff} = N_s - m\omega^2 R_c$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students **forget to subtract centrifugal force**. If shoes are centrifugal-only, then N_s = 0 and N_eff = mω²R_c (this *enables* engagement at high speed).

### Torque Equation

$$T = \mu \cdot N_{eff} \cdot R_m$$

where *R_m* is the mean friction radius.

---

## 2.5 BAND BRAKE — Detailed Theory

### Capstan Equation (Euler's Belt Friction)

For a band wrapped around a drum through angle θ (in radians):
$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$$

where:
- *T₁* = tension in tight side
- *T₂* = tension in slack side

### Braking Torque

$$T = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot R$$

### Differential Band Brake with Lever

For a lever of length *l* pivoted at one end, operating force *P* at other end, distance *a* from pivot to band tight side:
$$P \cdot l = T_1 \cdot a + T_2 \cdot b$$

where *b* is distance from pivot to slack side.

---

## 2.6 BLOCK (SHOE) BRAKE — Detailed Theory

### Short Shoe Brake (Pivoted)

Consider a block brake with:
- Block at radius *R* from pivot
- Block subtends angle 2θ at centre
- Tangential friction force = *μN*
- Radial normal force = *N*

### Moments about Pivot

$$P \cdot l = N \cdot x - \mu N \cdot y$$

where (*x*, *y*) are coordinates of block centre from pivot.

### Self-Energizing Condition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Self-Energizing:** If *μ y > x*, the friction force *helps* pressing the shoe, so a smaller *P* is needed.
> **Self-De-Energizing:** If *μ y < x*, friction *opposes* engagement — a larger *P* is needed.

---

## 2.7 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (MASTER CHEAT SHEET)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — it covers ~90% of KTU numerical questions on Module 3.**

| # | Element | Theory | Torque Equation | Axial / Normal Force | Notes |
|---|---------|--------|-----------------|----------------------|-------|
| 1 | Axial Clutch | UPT | $T = \frac{2}{3}\mu W \frac{R^3-r^3}{R^2-r^2}$ | $W = \pi p(R^2-r^2)$ | p constant |
| 2 | Axial Clutch | UWT | $T = \frac{1}{2}\mu W (R+r)$ | $W = 2\pi C(R-r)$ | pr = C |
| 3 | Multi-plate | UWT | $T = \frac{n\mu W}{2}(R+r)$ | $W = n\pi p(R^2-r^2)$ | n friction surfaces |
| 4 | Cone Clutch | UPT | $T = \frac{2}{3}\frac{\mu W}{\sin\alpha}\frac{R^3-r^3}{R^2-r^2}$ | Uses $W\sin\alpha$ as normal | α = semi-cone angle |
| 5 | Cone Clutch | UWT | $T = \frac{\mu W}{2\sin\alpha}(R+r)$ | Normal force = $W\sin\alpha$ | α = semi-cone angle |
| 6 | Expanding Rim | Centrifugal | $T = \mu (N_s - m\omega^2 R_c) R_m$ | $N_{eff}$ reduced by $F_c$ | $F_c = m\omega^2 R_c$ |
| 7 | Band Brake | Capstan | $T = (T_1 - T_2)R$ | $T_1 = T_2 e^{\mu\theta}$ | θ in **radians** |
| 8 | Block Brake | Pivoted | $T = \mu N R$ | $Pl = Nx \pm \mu N y$ | Sign = self-energizing |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Axial Clutches** → Automotive manual transmissions, machine tool spindles, heavy earthmoving equipment
- **Cone Clutches** → Synchromesh gearboxes, lathe headstocks, small engine drives (motorcycles, scooters)
- **Internal Expanding Clutches** → High-inertia drives (rolling mills, printing presses, ship propulsion)
- **Band Brakes** → Winches, elevators, conveyor belts, railway hand-brakes, hoisting machinery
- **Block Brakes** → Automotive disc/drum brakes, crane brakes, industrial machinery, trailer brakes

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — Axial Clutch Torque under Uniform Pressure

**Given:** Outer radius R, inner radius r, axial force W, friction coefficient μ, uniform pressure p.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Force Equilibrium (Axial direction):**

The axial force W is balanced by pressure acting over the annular area:
$$W = p \cdot A = p \cdot \pi (R^2 - r^2)$$

Therefore:
$$p = \frac{W}{\pi (R^2 - r^2)}$$

**Step 2 — Elemental Friction Force:**

Consider an elemental ring at radius x, width dx. Its area:
$$dA = 2\pi x \, dx$$

Friction force on this ring (since p is uniform):
$$dF_f = \mu \cdot p \cdot dA = \mu p \cdot 2\pi x \, dx$$

**Step 3 — Elemental Torque:**
$$dT = dF_f \cdot x = 2\pi \mu p \cdot x^2 \, dx$$

**Step 4 — Total Torque (integration):**
$$T = \int_{r}^{R} 2\pi \mu p \, x^2 \, dx$$

$$T = 2\pi \mu p \left[ \frac{x^3}{3} \right]_{r}^{R} = 2\pi \mu p \cdot \frac{R^3 - r^3}{3}$$

$$T = \frac{2\pi \mu p}{3} (R^3 - r^3)$$

**Step 5 — Substituting p from Step 1:**
$$T = \frac{2\pi \mu}{3} \cdot \frac{W}{\pi(R^2-r^2)} \cdot (R^3-r^3)$$

$$\boxed{T_{UPT} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot \frac{R^3 - r^3}{R^2 - r^2}}$$

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2 — Axial Clutch Torque under Uniform Wear

**Wear Law:** Rate of wear ∝ (pressure × sliding velocity). At radius x, sliding velocity ∝ x.
Therefore:
$$p \cdot x = C \quad \Rightarrow \quad p = \frac{C}{x}$$

**Step 1 — Axial Force Equilibrium:**
$$W = \int_{r}^{R} p \cdot 2\pi x \, dx = \int_{r}^{R} \frac{C}{x} \cdot 2\pi x \, dx = 2\pi C \int_{r}^{R} dx$$

$$W = 2\pi C (R - r) \quad \Rightarrow \quad C = \frac{W}{2\pi(R-r)}$$

**Step 2 — Elemental Friction Force:**
$$dF_f = \mu p \cdot 2\pi x \, dx = \mu \cdot \frac{C}{x} \cdot 2\pi x \, dx = 2\pi \mu C \, dx$$

**Step 3 — Elemental Torque:**
$$dT = dF_f \cdot x = 2\pi \mu C \cdot x \, dx$$

**Step 4 — Integration:**
$$T = \int_{r}^{R} 2\pi \mu C \cdot x \, dx = 2\pi \mu C \left[\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_{r}^{R}$$

$$T = \pi \mu C (R^2 - r^2)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute C:**
$$T = \pi \mu \cdot \frac{W}{2\pi(R-r)} \cdot (R^2 - r^2)$$

$$\boxed{T_{UWT} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot (R + r)}$$

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3 — Cone Clutch Torque (Uniform Wear)

**Geometric Setup:** A cone with semi-angle α, contacting from radius r to R.

**Step 1 — Elemental Ring on Cone Surface:**

The slant length of an elemental ring:
$$dl = \frac{dx}{\sin\alpha}$$

Its area on the cone (radius x, slant width):
$$dA = 2\pi x \cdot dl = \frac{2\pi x \, dx}{\sin\alpha}$$

**Step 2 — Axial Force Equilibrium:**

Uniform wear gives p·x = C. Normal force on elemental ring:
$$dN = p \cdot dA = \frac{C}{x} \cdot \frac{2\pi x \, dx}{\sin\alpha} = \frac{2\pi C \, dx}{\sin\alpha}$$

Total axial force:
$$W = \int_{r}^{R} dN \cdot \sin\alpha = \int_{r}^{R} 2\pi C \, dx = 2\pi C (R-r)$$

(Same as axial case: $C = \frac{W}{2\pi(R-r)}$)

**Step 3 — Elemental Friction Force:**

Friction force is tangential to cone surface, perpendicular to radius:
$$dF_f = \mu \cdot dN = \mu \cdot \frac{2\pi C \, dx}{\sin\alpha}$$

**Step 4 — Elemental Torque:**
$$dT = dF_f \cdot x = \frac{2\pi \mu C}{\sin\alpha} \cdot x \, dx$$

**Step 5 — Integration:**
$$T = \frac{2\pi \mu C}{\sin\alpha} \int_{r}^{R} x \, dx = \frac{2\pi \mu C}{\sin\alpha} \cdot \frac{R^2-r^2}{2}$$

$$T = \frac{\pi \mu C (R^2-r^2)}{\sin\alpha}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute C:**
$$T = \frac{\pi \mu}{\sin\alpha} \cdot \frac{W}{2\pi(R-r)} \cdot (R^2-r^2)$$

$$\boxed{T_{cone,\,UWT} = \frac{\mu W (R+r)}{2\sin\alpha}}$$

---

## 3.4 DERIVATION 4 — Band Brake (Capstan Equation)

**Setup:** A flexible band wraps around a drum through wrap angle θ. Tensions on tight side = T₁, slack side = T₂.

**Step 1 — Elemental Equilibrium:**

Consider an elemental arc of angle dθ at radius R. Forces:
- Normal force on element: $dN = p \cdot R \, d\theta \cdot b$ (where b = band width)
- Friction force on element: $dF_f = \mu \, dN$
- Tension difference: $dT$ across the element

**Step 2 — Equilibrium of Element:**

Tangential: $dT = \mu \, dN = \mu p R b \, d\theta$
Radial: $T \, d\theta = dN = p R b \, d\theta \Rightarrow T = p R b$

**Step 3 — Combining:**
$$\frac{dT}{T} = \mu \, d\theta$$

**Step 4 — Integration from T₂ to T₁ (angle 0 to θ):**
$$\int_{T_2}^{T_1} \frac{dT}{T} = \mu \int_{0}^{\theta} d\theta$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{T_1}{T_2}\right) = \mu\theta$$

$$\boxed{\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu\theta}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical:** θ must be in **radians**. If given in degrees, convert: θ_rad = θ_deg × π/180.

---

## 3.5 DERIVATION 5 — Differential Band Brake

**Setup:** Lever of length *l*, pivot at one end, operating force *P* at other end. Band tight-side *T₁* attached at distance *a* from pivot, slack-side *T₂* at distance *b*.

**Step 1 — Moment Equilibrium about Pivot:**

If both tensions tend to rotate lever in same direction:
$$P \cdot l = T_1 \cdot a + T_2 \cdot b$$

If *T₁* rotates lever clockwise and *T₂* rotates counter-clockwise (differential action):
$$P \cdot l = T_1 \cdot a - T_2 \cdot b$$

**Step 2 — Differential Effect:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Differential Band Brake Self-Locking:** When $T_1 \cdot a = T_2 \cdot b$ (in same direction), the brake becomes self-locking — no force P needed. From capstan, $T_1 = T_2 e^{\mu\theta}$, so:
> $$\frac{a}{b} \leq e^{\mu\theta}$$
> If $a/b > e^{\mu\theta}$, the brake cannot engage → this is the **self-locking condition**.

---

## 3.6 DERIVATION 6 — Internal Expanding Rim Clutch (Centrifugal Type)

**Setup:** Two shoes, each of mass *m*, CG at radius *R_c* from shaft axis. Spring force *N_s* pulls shoes inward. Drum radius *R*.

**Step 1 — Centrifugal Force on Each Shoe:**
$$F_c = m \omega^2 R_c$$

**Step 2 — Effective Outward Force (Engagement Force):**

If only centrifugal force acts (no spring):
$$N_{eff} = m \omega^2 R_c$$

If spring opposes engagement (pulls shoe inward):
$$N_{eff} = m \omega^2 R_c - N_s$$

If spring assists engagement (pushes shoe outward):
$$N_{eff} = m \omega^2 R_c + N_s$$

**Step 3 — Torque from Two Shoes:**
$$T = 2 \cdot \mu \cdot N_{eff} \cdot R_m$$

where $R_m = (R_o + R_i)/2$ is the mean friction radius.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trick Question:** At *low* speeds (small ω), centrifugal force < spring force, so N_eff may become negative — meaning the clutch **disengages**. This is why such clutches are used in high-speed applications only.

---

## 3.7 DERIVATION 7 — Pivoted Block Brake (Short Shoe)

**Setup:** A single rigid block pivoted at point P, contacting the drum at radius R, subtending angle 2θ at the centre. Block centre coordinates from pivot: (x, y).

**Step 1 — Forces on Block:**
- Normal force *N* directed from drum centre toward pivot
- Friction force *μN* tangential to drum (tangent direction)

**Step 2 — Take Moment about Pivot P:**
$$P \cdot l = N \cdot x - (\pm) \mu N \cdot y$$

The sign depends on direction of rotation:
- If friction tends to **press** the block: use + (self-energizing)
- If friction tends to **release** the block: use − (self-de-energizing)

**Step 3 — Torque:**
$$T = \mu N \cdot R$$

**Step 4 — Solving for N:**
$$N = \frac{P \cdot l}{x \mp \mu y}$$

(Plus sign in denominator → self-de-energizing → larger P needed)

---

## 3.8 ANALYTICAL COMPARISON MATRIX

| Design Theory | When to Use | Pressure Distribution | Heat Dissipation | KTU Preference |
|---------------|-------------|----------------------|------------------|----------------|
| Uniform Pressure | New clutch, short engagement | p = constant | Uniform | Theoretical only |
| Uniform Wear | Steady-state operation | p ∝ 1/r | Higher at inner radius | **PREFERRED for design** |
| Centrifugal (Rim) | High-speed only | Depends on ω | Inner zone hot | Edge cases |

---

## 3.9 WORKED EXAMPLE — Numerical Verification

**Problem:** A single-plate clutch has effective outer radius R = 150 mm, inner radius r = 100 mm. Axial force W = 5 kN, μ = 0.3. Find torque by both theories.

**Solution using UWT:**
$$T_{UWT} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot (R+r)$$
$$T_{UWT} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 0.3 \cdot 5000 \cdot (0.150 + 0.100)$$
$$T_{UWT} = 0.15 \cdot 5000 \cdot 0.250$$
$$\boxed{T_{UWT} = 187.5 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Solution using UPT:**
$$T_{UPT} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot \frac{R^3-r^3}{R^2-r^2}$$

Numerator: $R^3 - r^3 = (0.150)^3 - (0.100)^3 = 3.375 \times 10^{-3} - 1.0 \times 10^{-3} = 2.375 \times 10^{-3}$ m³

Denominator: $R^2 - r^2 = (0.150)^2 - (0.100)^2 = 0.0225 - 0.0100 = 0.0125$ m²

Ratio: $2.375 \times 10^{-3} / 0.0125 = 0.190$ m

$$T_{UPT} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot 0.3 \cdot 5000 \cdot 0.190 = 0.667 \cdot 0.3 \cdot 5000 \cdot 0.190$$

$$\boxed{T_{UPT} = 190.05 \text{ N·m}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** UPT gives slightly higher torque (≈1.4% more). For real clutches, UWT is the design basis because it accounts for wear patterns.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Clutch/Brake Classification & Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Friction Power Transmission Devices] --> B[CLUTCHES]
    A --> C[BRAKES]
    
    B --> D[Axial Plate Clutch]
    B --> E[Cone Clutch]
    B --> F[Internal Expanding Rim Clutch]
    
    D --> D1[Single Plate]
    D --> D2[Multi Plate]
    
    D1 --> D3[Uniform Pressure Theory]
    D1 --> D4[Uniform Wear Theory]
    
    E --> E1[Uniform Pressure]
    E --> E2[Uniform Wear]
    
    F --> F1[Centrifugal Type]
    F --> F2[Spring Assisted Type]
    
    C --> G[Band Brake]
    C --> H[Block Shoe Brake]
    
    G --> G1[Simple Band]
    G --> G2[Differential Band]
    
    H --> H1[Short Shoe]
    H --> H2[Pivoted Long Shoe]
    H --> H3[Self Energizing]
    H --> H4[Self De Energizing]
    
    D3 -.Uses Formula.-> F1X[T = 2 by 3 mu W R3 minus r3 over R2 minus r2]
    D4 -.Uses Formula.-> F2X[T = 1 by 2 mu W R plus r]
    E2 -.Uses Formula.-> F3X[T = mu W R plus r over 2 sin alpha]
    F1 -.Uses Formula.-> F4X[T = mu N effective R]
    G1 -.Uses Formula.-> F5X[T1 over T2 = e power mu theta]
    H1 -.Uses Formula.-> F6X[T = mu N R]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Axial Clutch Force Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Axial Engaging Force W] --> B[Pressure Distribution on Annular Face]
    B --> C{Design Theory}
    C -->|Uniform Pressure| D[p = constant over area]
    C -->|Uniform Wear| E[pr = constant C]
    
    D --> F[Friction Force Element dFf = mu p 2 pi x dx]
    E --> G[Friction Force Element dFf = 2 pi mu C dx]
    
    F --> H[Torque Integral from r to R]
    G --> H
    
    H --> I[T = function of mu W R r]
    I --> J[Output Torque in Newton meter]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cone Clutch Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Axial Force W] --> B[Decompose along Cone Surface]
    B --> C[Normal Component W sin alpha]
    C --> D[Acting on Slant Annular Area]
    
    D --> E[Friction Force mu N]
    E --> F[Torque Arm is Radius x]
    
    F --> G[Integrate over r to R]
    G --> H[T cone = mu W R plus r over 2 sin alpha]
    
    H --> I[Advantage Factor 1 over sin alpha]
    I --> J[Reduced Axial Force Required]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Internal Expanding Rim Clutch

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Shaft Rotation omega] --> B[Centrifugal Force Fc on Shoe]
    A1[Spring Force Ns] --> C[Net Outward Force N effective]
    B --> C
    
    C -->|Positive| D[Shoe Presses Against Drum Lining]
    C -->|Negative| E[Shoe Disengages]
    
    D --> F[Friction Force mu N effective]
    F --> G[Torque = mu N effective R mean]
    G --> H[Power Transmission Achieved]
    
    E --> I[No Power Transmission]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram — Band Brake Force Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Operating Force P] --> B[Lever L]
    B --> C[Tight Side Tension T1]
    B --> D[Slack Side Tension T2]
    
    C --> E[Capstan Equation T1 = T2 e power mu theta]
    E --> F[Drum Friction Force]
    F --> G[Braking Torque T equals T1 minus T2 times R]
    
    D --> C
```

## 4.6 Mermaid Block Diagram — Block Brake Self-Energizing Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pivoted Block Geometry] --> B[Coordinates x and y from Pivot]
    B --> C{Friction Force Direction}
    
    C -->|Helps Press| D[SELF ENERGIZING]
    C -->|Opposes Press| E[SELF DE ENERGIZING]
    
    D --> F[Condition mu y greater than x]
    E --> G[Condition mu y less than x]
    
    F --> H[Smaller P required]
    G --> I[Larger P required]
    
    H --> J[Advantage: Mechanical Leverage]
    I --> K[Disadvantage: Higher Force Needed]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Distinguish between Uniform Pressure Theory and Uniform Wear Theory used in the design of friction clutches.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Uniform Pressure Theory | Uniform Wear Theory |
|--------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Assumption | Pressure *p* is constant over contact surface | Product *p × r* is constant (wear is uniform) |
| Condition | New/unworn clutch | Worn clutch (steady-state design) |
| Use | Theoretical analysis only | **Preferred design basis (KTU)** |
| Torque form | $T = \frac{2}{3}\mu W \frac{R^3-r^3}{R^2-r^2}$ | $T = \frac{1}{2}\mu W(R+r)$ |

The Uniform Wear Theory is preferred because in actual service the pressure adjusts itself so that wear is uniform throughout the contact surface.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define Semi-cone angle in a cone clutch and state its effect on axial force required.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **semi-cone angle (α)** is the angle between the slant surface of the cone and its axis of rotation.

**Effect on Axial Force:**
- Normal force on cone = **W sin α**
- For the same torque, axial force required: $W = \frac{2T\sin\alpha}{\mu(R+r)}$
- Smaller α → smaller W → **greater mechanical advantage**
- Standard α used: **8° to 15°** (very small to prevent self-locking)

If α → 0°, the cone becomes a flat disc (axial clutch), and there is no wedging advantage. If α is too large, the cone tends to **self-lock** (jam).

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students often lose marks by:
> 1. **Forgetting the factor sin α** in cone clutch problems — partial credit only
> 2. **Using UPT instead of UWT** — UWT is the **default design assumption** unless the problem explicitly says "uniform pressure"
> 3. **Unit mismatch** — Use **SI units** throughout (N, m, N·m)
> 4. **Wrap angle in degrees** — Capstan equation requires **radians**
> 5. **Self-energizing sign error** — Always re-check geometry diagram

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**A multi-plate disc clutch has 5 plates (3 driving + 2 driven). The effective outer diameter is 240 mm and inner diameter is 160 mm. The axial force applied is 25 kN and coefficient of friction is 0.3. Determine:**

**(a)** The torque transmitted using **uniform wear theory**. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** The maximum pressure intensity on the contact surface. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution:

**Given:**
- Number of friction surfaces, n = (5 − 1) = 4 ... *Wait! For n plates, friction surfaces = n−1, but for 5 plates with alternating driving/driven = 4 surfaces*

Re-checking: 3 driving + 2 driven = **4 friction surfaces**, so n = 4.

- Outer diameter = 240 mm → **R = 0.120 m**
- Inner diameter = 160 mm → **r = 0.080 m**
- W = 25,000 N (total axial force on clutch pack)
- μ = 0.3

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** In multi-plate clutches, W is the **total** axial force on the entire stack. Force per surface = W/n.

---

**Part (a) — Torque by Uniform Wear Theory:**

$$T_{UWT} = \frac{n \cdot \mu \cdot W}{2}(R + r)$$

$$T_{UWT} = \frac{4 \cdot 0.3 \cdot 25000}{2} \cdot (0.120 + 0.080)$$

$$T_{UWT} = \frac{30000}{2} \cdot 0.200 = 15000 \cdot 0.200$$

$$\boxed{T_{UWT} = 3000 \text{ N·m}}$$

---

**Part (b) — Maximum Pressure:**

Under uniform wear, $p \cdot r = C$ where $r$ is the **inner** radius (max pressure occurs at smallest radius):

$$C = \frac{W}{2\pi n (R - r)} = \frac{25000}{2\pi \cdot 4 \cdot (0.120 - 0.080)}$$

$$C = \frac{25000}{2\pi \cdot 4 \cdot 0.040} = \frac{25000}{1.0053}$$

$$C = 24868 \text{ Pa·m}$$

Maximum pressure at inner radius:
$$p_{max} = \frac{C}{r} = \frac{24868}{0.080}$$

$$\boxed{p_{max} = 3.11 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 0.311 \text{ MPa}}$$

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**A cone clutch has an effective mean diameter of 320 mm, a face width of 80 mm, and a semi-cone angle of 12.5°. The coefficient of friction is 0.25 and the axial spring force is 600 N. Determine:**

**(a)** The torque transmitted by the clutch using **uniform pressure theory**. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Compare this with the torque obtained by **uniform wear theory** and comment. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution:

**Given:**
- Mean diameter = 320 mm → **Mean radius R_m = 0.160 m**
- Face width *b* = 80 mm = 0.080 m
- Semi-cone angle α = 12.5° → sin α = sin(12.5°) = **0.2164**
- μ = 0.25
- W = 600 N

**Step 1 — Find R and r from mean radius and width:**

$$R_m = \frac{R+r}{2} = 0.160 \Rightarrow R + r = 0.320 \text{ m}$$

Width relation: $b = \frac{R-r}{\sin\alpha}$

$$R - r = b \sin\alpha = 0.080 \cdot 0.2164 = 0.01731 \text{ m}$$

Solving:
$$R = \frac{0.320 + 0.01731}{2} = 0.16866 \text{ m}$$

$$r = \frac{0.320 - 0.01731}{2} = 0.15134 \text{ m}$$

---

**Part (a) — Torque by Uniform Pressure Theory:**

$$T_{UPT} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{\mu W}{\sin\alpha} \cdot \frac{R^3 - r^3}{R^2 - r^2}$$

Calculate $R^3 - r^3$:
$$R^3 = (0.16866)^3 = 4.797 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$
$$r^3 = (0.15134)^3 = 3.467 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$
$$R^3 - r^3 = 1.330 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

Calculate $R^2 - r^2$:
$$R^2 = 0.02845 \text{ m}^2$$
$$r^2 = 0.02291 \text{ m}^2$$
$$R^2 - r^2 = 0.00554 \text{ m}^2$$

Ratio:
$$\frac{R^3 - r^3}{R^2 - r^2} = \frac{1.330 \times 10^{-3}}{0.00554} = 0.2401 \text{ m}$$

Now:
$$T_{UPT} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{0.25 \cdot 600}{0.2164} \cdot 0.2401$$

$$T_{UPT} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{150}{0.2164} \cdot 0.2401 = \frac{2}{3} \cdot 693.16 \cdot 0.2401$$

$$\boxed{T_{UPT} \approx 110.96 \text{ N·m}}$$

---

**Part (b) — Torque by Uniform Wear Theory + Comparison:**

$$T_{UWT} = \frac{\mu W (R+r)}{2\sin\alpha} = \frac{0.25 \cdot 600 \cdot 0.320}{2 \cdot 0.2164}$$

$$T_{UWT} = \frac{48}{0.4328}$$

$$\boxed{T_{UWT} \approx 110.91 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Comparison Comment:**
- The two torques are nearly equal (difference ≈ 0.05 N·m, negligible).
- This is because the radial dimension (R − r) is small compared to R+r, so both theories converge.
- However, **UWT is preferred** for design because it represents the actual worn condition.
- The cone clutch requires only **W sin α = 600 × 0.2164 = 130 N** of effective normal force, demonstrating its mechanical advantage over a disc clutch.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section — it covers the essentials for last-minute KTU exam revision.**

### ✅ Clutches — Key Takeaways

- [ ] **Uniform Pressure Theory (UPT):** $T = \frac{2}{3}\mu W \frac{R^3-r^3}{R^2-r^2}$
- [ ] **Uniform Wear Theory (UWT):** $T = \frac{1}{2}\mu W(R+r)$ ← **DEFAULT in KTU**
- [ ] **Multi-plate Clutch:** Multiply axial torque by number of friction surfaces *n*
- [ ] **Max pressure in UWT** occurs at the **inner radius**: $p_{max} = \frac{W}{2\pi n r (R-r)}$
- [ ] **Cone Clutch** reduces required W by factor **sin α** (α = semi-cone angle)
- [ ] **Cone Clutch UWT:** $T = \frac{\mu W (R+r)}{2\sin\alpha}$
- [ ] **Standard semi-cone angle:** α = 8° to 15° (smaller = greater advantage, but risk of self-locking)
- [ ] **Internal expanding clutch:** Centrifugal force = $m\omega^2 R_c$; subtract from spring force to get N_eff

### ✅ Brakes — Key Takeaways

- [ ] **Band Brake — Capstan Equation:** $\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu\theta}$ (θ in **radians**)
- [ ] **Braking torque:** $T = (T_1 - T_2)R$
- [ ] **Differential Band Brake:** Self-locking when $\frac{a}{b} \leq e^{\mu\theta}$ (in same-direction tensions)
- [ ] **Block Brake:** Torque $T = \mu N R$
- [ ] **Pivoted Block — Moment eq:** $Pl = Nx \pm \mu N y$
- [ ] **Self-energizing:** When friction *helps* press the block (μy > x)
- [ ] **Self-de-energizing:** When friction *opposes* pressing (μy < x)

### ✅ Quick Decision Guide — Which Formula to Use?

| Problem Statement | Use |
|-------------------|-----|
| "Find torque of disc clutch" (no theory specified) | **UWT** |
| "Find torque assuming uniform pressure" | **UPT** |
| "Find torque of cone clutch" | **UWT with sin α in denominator** |
| "Multi-plate clutch" | Multiply axial result by *n* |
| "Band brake" | Capstan $T_1 = T_2 e^{\mu\theta}$ |
| "Self-energizing?" | Check μy vs x |
| "Centrifugal clutch" | Subtract $m\omega^2 R_c$ from spring force |

### ✅ Common Numerical Pitfalls

1. ⚠️ Forgetting to convert **wrap angle θ from degrees to radians**
2. ⚠️ Using **W directly instead of W sin α** in cone clutches
3. ⚠️ Using **outer radius R alone** instead of mean radius $(R+r)/2$
4. ⚠️ Mixing **per-surface force with total force** in multi-plate clutches
5. ⚠️ **Sign error in self-energizing** — always draw free-body diagram first

---

> 🎯 **KTU 2024 Final Tip:** In every clutch/brake problem, **state your assumption clearly** (UWT/UPT), **draw a free-body diagram**, and **maintain SI units** throughout. Examiners award partial marks for correct methodology even if the final answer has arithmetic errors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT521 - DESIGN OF TRANSMISSION ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** External Shoe Brakes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **External Shoe Brake (Block Brake):** A friction brake in which a stationary friction element (the **shoe** or **block**) is pressed against the **outer cylindrical surface** of a rotating drum or pulley to generate a braking torque through dry friction. The shoe does not enclose the drum; instead, it acts tangentially on the drum's exterior surface.

In the KTU 2024 scheme (PERAT521), an **external shoe brake** is classified as a **non-enveloping, friction-type, energy-absorbing (dissipative) brake**. It is a *single-faced* contact device, unlike internal expanding shoe brakes where contact occurs on the inner periphery.

### Formal Classification under KTU PERAT521

| Classification Axis | Category |
|---|---|
| Energy conversion | **Dissipative (frictional)** brake |
| Mode of actuation | **Mechanical / Pneumatic / Hydraulic** |
| Geometry of contact | **External (non-enveloping)** |
| Number of friction elements | **Single shoe (block) or Double shoe** |
| Pivot arrangement | **Pivoted or Non-pivoted** |
| Wear law | **Uniform pressure** or **Uniform wear** |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine you are riding a bicycle and you press the brake lever. The brake pad (shoe) is squeezed **against the outer rim** of the wheel. The harder you press, the stronger the gripping force, and the wheel slows down. The pad is hinged (pivoted) at one end so that it self-aligns with the wheel's curvature. This is exactly how an **external pivoted shoe brake** works.

**Plain-English Explanation:**
1. A **drum** is mounted on the rotating shaft (like a flywheel or pulley).
2. A **rigid block (shoe)** lined with friction material (asbestos, sintered bronze, composite) is positioned just outside the drum.
3. A **lever mechanism** pushes the shoe inward, generating a **normal force N** on the drum surface.
4. As the drum rotates, the friction force **F = μN** acts at the contact interface, opposing rotation.
5. The **friction torque T = F × r = μN × r** decelerates the drum, dissipating kinetic energy as heat.

> [!TIP]
> **Key insight:** Because the shoe is *outside* the drum, the entire drum surface is accessible for cooling — making external brakes excellent for high heat dissipation in industrial applications (cranes, conveyors, hoists).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> **Standard friction coefficient values (μ)** for shoe-drum pairs (bolded for emphasis):
> - **Cast iron shoe on cast iron drum:** μ ≈ **0.30 – 0.40**
> - **Sintered bronze shoe on steel drum:** μ ≈ **0.35 – 0.45**
> - **Wooden shoe on metal drum:** μ ≈ **0.20 – 0.30**
> - **Cork-based lining on steel:** μ ≈ **0.30 – 0.45**
> - **Semimetallic / organic composites:** μ ≈ **0.35 – 0.50**

- **Maximum allowable pressure (p_max)** for shoe lining: **0.35 – 1.05 MPa** (typical)
- **Operating temperature range:** Up to **300 °C** for organic linings
- **Standard drum surface speed:** Up to **10 m/s** (single-block); higher for multi-block
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Features of an External Shoe Brake

The principal components are:

1. **Rotating drum** — rigidly keyed to the shaft; carries kinetic energy.
2. **Brake shoe (block)** — a rigid block faced with friction lining; may be **pivoted** or **non-pivoted** at its lower end.
3. **Lever / Toggle mechanism** — transmits the actuating force **P** to the shoe.
4. **Pivot pin** — hinge point about which the lever (or shoe) rotates.
5. **Friction lining** — high-μ material bonded or riveted to the shoe face.
6. **Stop / Adjustable screw** — limits shoe travel and sets initial clearance.
7. **Actuator** — manual lever, hydraulic cylinder, or pneumatic piston providing force **P**.

---

## 2.2 Types of External Shoe Brakes (KTU Syllabus Coverage)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 PERAT521 syllabus explicitly requires study of the following configurations:

### Type A: Non-Pivoted (Sliding) Block Brake
- The shoe translates radially without rotation.
- Simpler construction; **uniform pressure** assumed.
- Used in low-cost applications (hand brakes on trolleys).

### Type B: Single Pivoted Block Brake
- Shoe pivots about a hinge so it **self-aligns** with the drum curvature.
- Two sub-cases:
  - **(i)** Line of action of applied force **P** passes **through the centre of the drum** → *unaffected by friction at the pivot*.
  - **(ii)** Line of action of P is **offset** from the drum centre → friction force creates a moment about the pivot → **self-energizing** or **self-de-energizing** behaviour.

### Type C: Double Shoe (External) Brake
- Two symmetrically placed shoes act on the same drum.
- **Counter-lever** arrangement: a single actuating force presses both shoes simultaneously.
- Used in **hoists, winches, and crane mechanisms**.

---

## 2.3 Wear Theories — Two Fundamental Assumptions

The contact pressure distribution between shoe and drum is governed by one of two hypotheses:

### Theory 1: Uniform Pressure (New Shoe)
> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption:** The shoe is new and rigid; the lining is stiff; the drum is perfectly circular. Therefore, the **contact pressure p is constant** over the entire contact area.

$$p = \text{constant} = p_{max}$$

### Theory 2: Uniform Wear (Worn Shoe / Long Service Life)
> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption:** After extended use, the lining wears in proportion to the **sliding distance** (Archard's wear law). The linear wear depth **w = k · p · s** (where *s* is sliding distance, *k* is wear coefficient). Since *s* is proportional to radius *r*, we get **p × r = constant** for steady-state uniform wear.

$$p \cdot r = k = \text{constant}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Memorize the following table — these are the **high-yield equations** for KTU ESE questions on this topic. Pay close attention to the **mean radius** distinction between the two wear theories.

| # | Quantity | Uniform Pressure (p = const) | Uniform Wear (p·r = const) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Max pressure | $p_{max} = p$ | $p_{max} = k/r$ (at smallest *r*) |
| 2 | Normal force (block of width *b*, contact angle *2α*) | $N = p \cdot b \cdot 2r\alpha$ | $N = k \cdot b \cdot 2\alpha$ |
| 3 | Resultant location (from drum centre) | $r$ (at full radius) | $r/2$ (mean radius) |
| 4 | Friction force | $F = \mu N$ | $F = \mu N$ |
| 5 | **Friction (braking) torque** | $T = \mu N \cdot r$ | $T = \mu N \cdot (r/2)$ |
| 6 | Energy dissipated per revolution | $W = T \cdot 2\pi$ | $W = T \cdot 2\pi$ |
| 7 | Heat generated | $Q = W / t$ (W) | $Q = W / t$ (W) |

### Force Equations for Pivoted Block Brake

Let the following geometry be defined (standard KTU notation):
- **P** = applied force at the end of the lever
- **L** = perpendicular distance of P from pivot
- **a** = perpendicular distance of line of action of normal reaction N from pivot
- **b** = offset distance of friction force F = μN from pivot (perpendicular to F)
- **x** = horizontal offset of pivot from drum centre
- **y** = vertical offset of pivot from drum centre

| Condition | Normal force equation | Braking torque |
|---|---|---|
| **P-line through drum centre** (b = 0) | $P \cdot L = N \cdot a$ | $T = \mu N \cdot r$ |
| **P-line offset — Self-energizing** (rotation drags shoe *into* drum) | $P \cdot L = N \cdot a - \mu N \cdot b$ | $T = \mu N \cdot r$ |
| **P-line offset — Self-de-energizing** (rotation pushes shoe *away* from drum) | $P \cdot L = N \cdot a + \mu N \cdot b$ | $T = \mu N \cdot r$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign convention trap:** In the self-energizing case, the term **μN·b** *reduces* the required P; in the self-de-energizing case it *adds*. Examiners frequently test this sign carefully.

### Brake Efficiency & Self-Energization Ratio

$$\eta_{brake} = \frac{T_{actual}}{T_{without\ friction}} = \frac{N \cdot a - \mu N \cdot b}{N \cdot a} = 1 - \mu \frac{b}{a}$$

For **self-energizing** behaviour: $\eta_{brake} > 1$ (theoretically, before lock-up). For **self-de-energizing**: $\eta_{brake} < 1$. In practical design, $\eta_{brake}$ is kept between **0.6 and 1.0** to prevent runaway self-locking.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

External shoe brakes are the **workhorse** of low-to-medium speed heavy machinery:

- **Cranes & hoists:** Double-shoe external brakes on hoist drums for load holding.
- **Conveyor belts:** Single-block brakes for emergency stopping.
- **Agricultural machinery:** Trailer hand-brakes, tractor PTO brakes.
- **Elevators (older systems):** Drum-type external brakes as secondary safety.
- **Rolling mills:** Massive external shoe brakes absorb enormous kinetic energy during emergency stops.
- **Locomotive parking brakes:** External shoe on the wheel tread.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Normal Force & Friction Torque (Uniform Pressure Theory)

### Problem Setup
A block of width **b** presses against a drum of radius **r**. The contact spans an angle **2α** symmetric about the vertical. Pressure is **uniform** at value **p**.

### Step 1 — Elemental Area

Consider an infinitesimal element on the drum surface at angular position θ:

$$dA = (r \, d\theta) \cdot b$$

### Step 2 — Elemental Normal Force

The elemental normal force on this area is:

$$dN = p \cdot dA = p \cdot b \cdot r \, d\theta$$

### Step 3 — Total Normal Force (Integrate over contact angle 2α)

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \int_{-\alpha}^{+\alpha} p \cdot b \cdot r \, d\theta \\[4pt]
&= p \cdot b \cdot r \cdot \big[\theta\big]_{-\alpha}^{+\alpha} \\[4pt]
&= p \cdot b \cdot r \cdot (2\alpha) \\[4pt]
\therefore N &= 2 \, p \, b \, r \, \alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Friction Force on the Element

$$dF = \mu \cdot dN = \mu p b r \, d\theta$$

### Step 5 — Friction Torque (each elemental force acts at radius r)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \int_{-\alpha}^{+\alpha} dF \cdot r = \int_{-\alpha}^{+\alpha} (\mu p b r \, d\theta) \cdot r \\[4pt]
T &= \mu p b r^{2} \int_{-\alpha}^{+\alpha} d\theta = \mu p b r^{2} \cdot 2\alpha \\[4pt]
\therefore T &= 2 \, \mu \, p \, b \, r^{2} \, \alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Verification: T = μN · r

$$T = \mu \cdot (2p b r \alpha) \cdot r = 2 \mu p b r^{2} \alpha \quad \checkmark$$

> **Engineering meaning:** Under uniform pressure, the **resultant normal force acts at the geometric centre of the contact arc** (radius *r*). Hence the lever arm is the full radius.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Normal Force & Friction Torque (Uniform Wear Theory)

### Step 1 — Pressure-Velocity Relation (Archard)

For uniform wear, $p \cdot v = \text{constant}$ where $v = r\omega$. Since $\omega$ is constant, $p \cdot r = k$ (constant).

$$p(\theta) = \frac{k}{r(\theta)} = \frac{k}{r} = \text{constant along arc of constant }r$$

### Step 2 — Elemental Normal Force

$$dN = p \cdot dA = \left(\frac{k}{r}\right)(r \, d\theta)(b) = k \cdot b \, d\theta$$

Notice that **r cancels** — the elemental force is independent of radius in this idealization.

### Step 3 — Total Normal Force

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \int_{-\alpha}^{+\alpha} k b \, d\theta = k b \cdot 2\alpha \\[4pt]
\therefore N &= 2 \, k \, b \, \alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Friction Torque

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \int_{-\alpha}^{+\alpha} \mu \, dN \cdot r = \int_{-\alpha}^{+\alpha} \mu k b \, d\theta \cdot r \\[4pt]
T &= \mu k b r \cdot 2\alpha \\[4pt]
\therefore T &= 2 \, \mu \, k \, b \, r \, \alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Effective Friction Radius

Substituting $N = 2kb\alpha$ into T:

$$T = \mu N \cdot r \quad \text{...Wait, this gives } T = \mu(2kb\alpha)(r) = 2\mu k b r \alpha \checkmark$$

But comparing with uniform pressure, the **friction torque is halved** when expressed per unit N:

| Theory | $T/N$ |
|---|---|
| Uniform pressure | $T = \mu N r$ |
| Uniform wear | $T = \mu N (r/2)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Under uniform wear, the resultant **N** acts at a **mean radius r/2** (centroid of the linearly decreasing pressure distribution), not at the full radius *r*. This is the crucial distinction.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Pivoted Block Brake — Self-Energizing Analysis

### Geometry

- Drum of radius **r** rotating **clockwise**.
- Shoe pivoted at point **O** located at horizontal offset **x** and vertical offset **y** from drum centre **C**.
- Applied force **P** acts vertically at the end of a lever of arm **L** (from O).
- Normal reaction **N** is horizontal, friction force **F = μN** is vertical (tangent to drum).

### Free Body Diagram of the Shoe

Forces on the shoe:
1. Applied force **P** (downward, say) at distance L from O.
2. Reaction from drum on shoe: **N** (horizontal, away from drum) and **F** (vertical, opposing rotation).
3. Pivot reaction at O: $R_x$ and $R_y$.

### Step 1 — Geometry of N and F about O

The line of action of **N** (perpendicular to drum surface at contact point) passes at perpendicular distance:

$$\text{arm of N about O} = x$$

The line of action of **F** (= μN, tangent to drum at contact point) passes at perpendicular distance:

$$\text{arm of F about O} = y - r \quad \text{(if contact point is at top of drum)}$$

Let us denote these simply as:
- $a$ = perpendicular distance of N from O
- $b$ = perpendicular distance of F from O

### Step 2 — Moment Equilibrium about Pivot O

Sum of moments about O = 0. With **clockwise drum rotation**, the friction force on the shoe (by Newton's 3rd law of drum-on-shoe) acts **upward** at the contact (this is the direction creating a self-energizing effect if applied at the right location).

**Self-energizing configuration (rotation drags shoe against drum):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P \cdot L + F \cdot b - N \cdot a &= 0 \quad \text{(if F aids the lever)} \\[4pt]
P \cdot L &= N \cdot a - F \cdot b \\[4pt]
P \cdot L &= N \cdot a - \mu N \cdot b \\[4pt]
P \cdot L &= N(a - \mu b) \\[4pt]
\therefore N &= \frac{P \cdot L}{a - \mu b}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Self-de-energizing configuration (rotation pushes shoe away from drum):**

$$P \cdot L = N \cdot a + \mu N \cdot b \quad \Rightarrow \quad N = \frac{P \cdot L}{a + \mu b}$$

### Step 3 — Self-Energization Limit (Lock-up Condition)

The brake becomes **self-locking** when the denominator approaches zero:

$$a - \mu b = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mu = \frac{a}{b}$$

If $\mu > a/b$, the brake locks up — the friction generates enough moment to press the shoe harder **without any external P**. This is a **runaway condition** that must be prevented by design (limit μ via material choice, or place pivot carefully).

### Step 4 — Braking Torque

The braking torque transmitted to the drum equals the friction force times the drum radius:

$$T = F \cdot r = \mu N \cdot r$$

Substituting N from the self-energizing case:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \mu \cdot \frac{P \cdot L}{a - \mu b} \cdot r \\[4pt]
T &= \frac{\mu P L r}{a - \mu b}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the **self-de-energizing** case:

$$T = \frac{\mu P L r}{a + \mu b}$$

> [!TIP]
> The ratio $\mu b / a$ governs whether the brake is self-energizing (< 1) or approaching lock-up (= 1). Keep this ratio in the range **0.3 – 0.7** for safe, controllable braking.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Double External Shoe Brake — Symmetric Analysis

### Configuration

Two identical shoes placed diametrically opposite (or symmetrically about a horizontal axis). A single lever pivoted at its centre applies equal and opposite forces to both shoes.

### Key Property: Force Cancellation at Pivot

Let the left shoe have **self-energizing** geometry (friction force tends to push shoe *into* the drum), and the right shoe have **self-de-energizing** geometry. The friction forces at the two shoes act in **opposite tangential directions** on the central pivot pin, so their net moment about the central pivot **cancels** to first order.

### Effective Total Torque

The total braking torque is the sum of contributions from both shoes:

$$T_{total} = T_1 + T_2 = \mu N_1 r + \mu N_2 r = \mu r (N_1 + N_2)$$

If the lever arms are equal and P is applied at the centre:

$$N_1 = N_2 = \frac{P \cdot L}{a}$$

(neglecting the small self-energization correction in well-designed symmetric brakes)

$$\therefore T_{total} = \frac{2 \mu P L r}{a}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symmetric double-shoe brakes are self-balancing** — the friction-induced side load on the central pivot is minimized, eliminating brake chatter and uneven wear. This is why they dominate in **hoist and winch** applications.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Design Equations Summary

| Design Parameter | Formula | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Braking torque (uniform pressure) | $T = \mu p b r^{2} (2\alpha)$ | New-shoe design |
| Braking torque (uniform wear) | $T = \mu k b r (2\alpha)$ | Worn-shoe / steady-state design |
| Required actuating force (pivoted, simple) | $P = N a / L$ | Pivot-through-centre case |
| Required actuating force (self-energizing) | $P = N(a - \mu b)/L$ | Designing for reduced P |
| Required actuating force (self-de-energizing) | $P = N(a + \mu b)/L$ | Fail-safe design |
| Heat dissipation rate | $Q = T \cdot \omega = T \cdot (2\pi N/60)$ | Cooling design |
| Power absorbed | $P_{abs} = T \cdot \omega$ (W) | Energy balance |
| Energy per stop | $E = \frac{1}{2}I(\omega_1^{2} - \omega_2^{2})$ | Stopping time / distance |
| Lining area | $A = b \cdot 2r\alpha$ | Wear life calculation |
| Max lining pressure | $p_{max} = N/A$ | Material selection |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a complete external shoe brake assembly, including actuation, friction interface, and heat dissipation paths.

```mermaid
    nodeB --> nodeC[Rotating Drum Surface]:::contact

    nodeE[Actuator Hydraulic Pneumatic Manual]:::actuator --> nodeF[Lever Mechanism]:::lever

    nodeG --> nodeD
    
    nodeD --> nodeH[Normal Force N on Drum]:::force

    nodeJ --> nodeB
    
    nodeK[Heat Generation]:::heat --> nodeL[Drum Surface Conduction]:::heat
    nodeL --> nodeM[Air Convection and Radiation]:::heat

    nodeO --> nodeP[Uniform Wear Steady State]:::wear
```

## 4.3 Self-Energizing vs Self-De-Energizing — Conceptual Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    nodeB --> nodeC[Pivot is on Same Side as Friction Force Action]:::case

    nodeC --> nodeE[Friction Moment Aids Applied Force]:::result
    nodeE --> nodeF[Required P DECREASES]:::result

    nodeG --> nodeH[Risk of Lock Up if mu b approaches a]:::warn
    
    nodeD --> nodeI[Friction Moment Opposes Applied Force]:::result
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Required P INCREASES]:::result
```

For exam answer sheets, draw the following layout:
