# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**Q: Differentiate between self-energizing and self-de-energizing brakes with a suitable sketch.**

**Model Answer:**

A **self-energizing brake** is one in which the frictional force developed at the shoe-drum interface creates a moment about the pivot that **assists** the applied actuating force. As a result, a **smaller applied force P** produces a given braking torque. The geometry is arranged so that the line of action of friction force passes on the *same side* of the pivot as the moment due to P.

Mathematically, for a pivoted shoe:

$$N = \frac{P \cdot L}{a - \mu b}$$

A **self-de-energizing brake** has the *opposite* geometry: the frictional moment **opposes** the applied moment, requiring a *larger* P for the same torque. Such brakes are **fail-safe** — when P is removed, friction actively pulls the shoe away from the drum.

$$N = \frac{P \cdot L}{a + \mu b}$$

> Self-energizing brakes risk **self-locking** if $\mu b \geq a$; self-de-energizing brakes are preferred in lifts, cranes, and hoists where brake release on power loss is critical.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**Q: State and explain the two fundamental theories of wear used in the design of external shoe brakes.**

**Model Answer:**

The two wear theories used in shoe brake design are:

**(1) Uniform Pressure Theory (p = constant):**
This is applicable when the shoe is *new*, the friction lining is rigid, and the drum surface is perfectly circular. The contact pressure is assumed uniform across the entire contact area. The resultant normal force acts at the **full radius r** of the drum, so the braking torque is $T = \mu N r$.

**(2) Uniform Wear Theory (p · r = constant):**
After prolonged service, the lining wears proportionally to the *sliding distance* (which is proportional to the radius r). To equalize the wear rate over the contact area, the pressure must vary inversely with radius: $p \cdot r = k$. The resultant normal force then acts at the **mean radius r/2**, giving $T = \mu N (r/2)$.

| Theory | Best suited for |
|---|---|
| Uniform pressure | Short life, rigid lining, lightly loaded brakes |
| Uniform wear | Long life, resilient lining, industrial duty cycles |

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before Attempting:**
> 1. **Forget to specify the wear theory assumed.** You must state *“Assuming uniform wear theory”* or *“Assuming uniform pressure”* explicitly. A bare answer without this assumption will lose **2 marks**.
> 2. **Sign errors in self-energizing moment equations.** Re-check whether the friction moment should add to or subtract from the applied moment. Wrong sign = full marks lost on the sub-part.
> 3. **Units inconsistency.** Pressures in MPa, torques in N·m, forces in N. Mixing units within one equation is a **common 1-mark deduction**.
> 4. **Forgetting to verify the self-locking condition** ($\mu b < a$). Examiners allot marks specifically for this safety check.
> 5. **Drawing no FBD.** A labelled free-body diagram of the shoe is **mandatory** for 14-mark problems. Missing FBD = –2 marks.
> 6. **Confusing internal vs external shoe brakes.** The KTU 2024 scheme treats them as *separate topics*. The formulae and pivoting logic differ.

---

### PART B — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Past Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

> A single pivoted external shoe brake has the following data:
> - Drum diameter: **D = 300 mm**, hence **r = 150 mm**
> - Coefficient of friction: **μ = 0.30**
> - Block width (axial): **b = 60 mm**
> - Contact angle: **2α = 60°**, hence **α = 30° = π/6 rad**
> - Lever arm of applied force: **L = 400 mm**
> - Perpendicular distance of N from pivot: **a = 200 mm**
> - Perpendicular distance of F from pivot: **b' = 100 mm** (using b' to avoid conflict with block width)
> - Applied force: **P = 500 N**
> - Rotation: such that the brake is **self-energizing**
>
> **Assumption:** Uniform pressure theory.
>
> **Calculate:**
> **(a)** The normal reaction N on the shoe, the friction force F, and the braking torque T. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** The maximum allowable drum speed for the lining pressure not to exceed 1 MPa, and the heat generated per minute at this speed. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION

**Part (a): Normal force, friction force, braking torque**

Since the brake is **self-energizing**, the moment equilibrium about the pivot O gives:

$$P \cdot L = N \cdot a - \mu N \cdot b'$$

Substituting values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
500 \times 400 &= N \times 200 - 0.30 \times N \times 100 \\[4pt]
200{,}000 &= 200N - 30N \\[4pt]
200{,}000 &= 170 N \\[4pt]
N &= \frac{200{,}000}{170} = 1176.47 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Friction force:**

$$F = \mu N = 0.30 \times 1176.47 = 352.94 \text{ N}$$

**Braking torque (uniform pressure, T = μN·r):**

$$T = \mu N r = 0.30 \times 1176.47 \times 0.150 = 52.94 \text{ N·m}$$

> **Result for (a):** N = **1176.5 N**, F = **353 N**, T = **52.94 N·m**

---

**Part (b): Max drum speed and heat generation**

The lining area (contact area) is:

$$A = b_{block} \times 2 r \alpha = 0.060 \times 2 \times 0.150 \times \frac{\pi}{6}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 0.060 \times 0.300 \times 0.5236 \\[4pt]
A &= 0.009425 \text{ m}^{2} = 9425 \text{ mm}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

For pressure not to exceed 1 MPa:

$$p = \frac{N}{A} \leq 1 \text{ MPa}$$

The actual pressure with N = 1176.47 N is:

$$p_{actual} = \frac{1176.47}{9425 \times 10^{-6}} = 124{,}823 \text{ Pa} = 0.125 \text{ MPa}$$

Since 0.125 MPa < 1 MPa, **the pressure criterion is satisfied at any speed**. (Speed does not affect contact pressure in the static analysis; it affects only the *rate* of heat generation.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Note to examiner:** In this design, the normal force is fixed by P and the geometry — it does not vary with speed. So the max speed is governed by **heat dissipation capacity**, not by pressure. We compute the heat generated at a chosen design speed.

**Choose a design speed:** For external shoe brakes, a typical industrial design surface speed is 6 m/s.

Drum surface speed: $v = \pi D N_{rpm}/60$. Setting v = 6 m/s:

$$N_{rpm} = \frac{60 \times v}{\pi D} = \frac{60 \times 6}{\pi \times 0.300} = 381.97 \text{ rpm} \approx 382 \text{ rpm}$$

Angular velocity:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 382}{60} = 40.0 \text{ rad/s}$$

Heat generated per second (= power absorbed):

$$P_{heat} = T \cdot \omega = 52.94 \times 40.0 = 2117.6 \text{ W} \approx 2.12 \text{ kW}$$

Heat generated per minute:

$$Q = P_{heat} \times 60 = 2117.6 \times 60 = 127{,}058 \text{ J/min} \approx 127.06 \text{ kJ/min}$$

> **Result for (b):** Pressure = 0.125 MPa (well below 1 MPa limit); at 382 rpm, heat generated = **127 kJ/min (≈ 2.12 kW)**.

---

### PART B — Question B (14 Marks) — *ALTERNATIVE CHOICE*

**[KTU University Exam – Past Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

> A **double external shoe brake** is fitted to the drum of a hoist. The following data are given:
> - Drum diameter: **D = 400 mm**, hence **r = 200 mm**
> - Drum speed: **N = 200 rpm**
> - Coefficient of friction: **μ = 0.35**
> - Brake lining material: Sintered bronze; allowable pressure **p = 0.8 MPa**
> - Block width (each shoe): **b = 80 mm**
> - Contact angle per shoe: **2α = 90° = π/2 rad**
> - **Assumption:** Uniform wear theory.
> - The hoist must develop a braking torque of **T_req = 1500 N·m** to arrest the load safely.
>
> **Calculate:**
> **(a)** The required normal force per shoe, and the actuating force P at the lever (assume lever arm L = 500 mm, perpendicular distance a = 250 mm; neglect self-energization correction for this symmetric design). **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** The actual pressure developed, the friction force per shoe, and verify whether the design meets the safe-pressure criterion. If not, suggest a corrective measure. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION

**Part (a): Required normal force and actuating force**

Each shoe contributes half the total braking torque (by symmetry):

$$T_{per\ shoe} = \frac{T_{req}}{2} = \frac{1500}{2} = 750 \text{ N·m}$$

Under **uniform wear theory**, the friction torque per shoe is:

$$T_{per\ shoe} = \mu N \cdot \frac{r}{2}$$

Solving for N:

$$
\begin{aligned}
750 &= 0.35 \times N \times \frac{0.200}{2} \\[4pt]
750 &= 0.35 \times N \times 0.100 \\[4pt]
750 &= 0.035 \, N \\[4pt]
N &= \frac{750}{0.035} = 21{,}428.6 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Normal force per shoe: N ≈ 21,429 N (≈ 21.43 kN)**

**Actuating force** (symmetric double-shoe, neglecting self-energization correction):

$$P = \frac{N \cdot a}{L} = \frac{21{,}428.6 \times 0.250}{0.500}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P = \frac{5357.15}{0.500} = 10{,}714.3 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Actuating force: P ≈ 10,714 N (≈ 10.71 kN)**

---

**Part (b): Actual pressure, friction force, safety check**

**Lining contact area per shoe** (rectangular approximation; for an arc, the axial length times the arc length is used):

$$A = b \times 2r\alpha = 0.080 \times 2 \times 0.200 \times \frac{\pi}{4}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 0.080 \times 0.400 \times 0.7854 \\[4pt]
A &= 0.02513 \text{ m}^{2} = 25{,}133 \text{ mm}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Actual pressure developed:**

$$p_{actual} = \frac{N}{A} = \frac{21{,}428.6}{0.02513} = 852{,}742 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.853 \text{ MPa}$$

**Friction force per shoe:**

$$F = \mu N = 0.35 \times 21{,}428.6 = 7500 \text{ N} = 7.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Safety check:**

$$p_{actual} = 0.853 \text{ MPa} > p_{allowable} = 0.8 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{✗ UNSAFE}$$

The design **fails the pressure criterion** by about 6.6%.

**Corrective measures (any one is acceptable):**
1. **Increase the block width** *b*. Required b for safe pressure:

$$b_{new} = \frac{N}{p_{allow} \times 2r\alpha} = \frac{21{,}428.6}{0.8 \times 10^{6} \times 0.3142} = 0.0853 \text{ m} \approx 86 \text{ mm}$$

Increase from 80 mm to **at least 86 mm** (≈ 7.5% increase).

2. **Increase the contact angle 2α** (wider shoe) — same effect.
3. **Switch to a higher-μ lining** material — but this changes the friction torque and may cause self-locking.
4. **Reduce the required braking torque** by re-checking the deceleration requirement (may be over-designed).

> **Recommended correction:** Use a block of **b = 90 mm** (with 10% safety margin) — this gives $p = 0.853 \times (80/90) = 0.758$ MPa, comfortably below 0.8 MPa.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this on your wall before the exam):**

- [x] **Definition:** External shoe brake = friction shoe pressing on the *outer* surface of a rotating drum.
- [x] **Two wear theories:** Uniform pressure (p = const, T = μNr) and Uniform wear (pr = const, T = μNr/2).
- [x] **Mean radius:** Uniform pressure → resultant N at full radius *r*; Uniform wear → resultant N at *r/2*.
- [x] **Pivoted block brake moment equation:** $P \cdot L = N(a \mp \mu b)$ where the *minus* is **self-energizing** and the *plus* is **self-de-energizing**.
- [x] **Self-locking condition:** $a = \mu b$ — beyond this, the brake locks up *without* applied P. **Avoid** in design.
- [x] **Double external shoe brake:** Symmetric, self-balancing; total torque = 2 × single-shoe torque; pivot side load is minimized.
- [x] **Real-world use:** Cranes, hoists, winches, conveyor belts, agricultural machinery, locomotive parking brakes.
- [x] **Standard μ values:** Cast iron ≈ 0.30–0.40; Sintered bronze ≈ 0.35–0.45; Wood ≈ 0.20–0.30.
- [x] **Typical p_max:** 0.35 – 1.05 MPa for shoe lining.
- [x] **Power dissipated:** $P = T \cdot \omega = T \cdot (2\pi N/60)$ in watts.
- [x] **Energy per stop:** $E = \frac{1}{2} I (\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2)$ where I is the moment of inertia of the rotating mass.
- [x] **Always state** the assumed wear theory explicitly in your answer (else –2 marks).
- [x] **Always draw** the FBD of the shoe and **label rotation direction** of the drum.
- [x] **Always verify** $\mu b < a$ for self-energizing brakes to prevent runaway lock-up.
- [x] **For double-shoe:** If symmetric, side load on pivot is ~ zero → smoother braking, no brake chatter.
- [x] **For unsymmetric double-shoe** (one self-energizing, one self-de-energizing): the side load does *not* fully cancel, but the design is fail-safe.
- [x] **Heat dissipation** is the limiting factor in continuous-duty applications; intermittent duty allows higher torques.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute tip:** In any KTU 14-mark question on shoe brakes, follow this order: **(1)** State assumption (uniform pressure or wear), **(2)** Draw FBD with rotation arrow, **(3)** Write the moment equation about the pivot, **(4)** Solve for N, then F = μN, then T = F·r (or F·r/2 for uniform wear), **(5)** Check self-locking condition, **(6)** Verify pressure limit, **(7)** Compute heat generation. This 7-step method captures **all** the marks in any typical KTU ESE problem on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Internal Expanding Shoe Brake
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT521 - DESIGN OF TRANSMISSION ELEMENTS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Internal Expanding Shoe Brake # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal KTU-Compliant Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Internal Expanding Shoe Brake (per KTU PERAT521 Syllabus, Module 3):** > An *Internal Expanding Shoe Brake* is a friction-type brake in which one or more curved friction shoes are forced **outward radially** against the **inner cylindrical surface** of a rotating drum or shell. The friction between the shoe lining and the drum inner surface develops the retarding torque that absorbs kinetic energy of the moving system. The actuation of the shoes can be performed by: - **Mechanical linkage** (lever and cam) - **Hydraulic pressure** (master–slave cylinder arrangement) - **Pneumatic pressure** (air-actuated bellows or diaphragm) - **Electrical actuation** (solenoid-driven cam) The direction of rotation of the drum determines whether a particular shoe is **self-energizing** (friction torque assists the actuation) or **self-de-energizing** (friction torque opposes the actuation). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Tag:** This topic falls under *Module 3 — Brakes, Clutches and Friction Drives*. The expected outcome is the ability to *"design and analyze single and double internal expanding shoe brakes for given braking torque and actuation constraints."* --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Imagine holding a **bicycle tyre** in your hands with the inside of the tube facing you. Now press your **two palms outward** against the inner rubber wall. As the tube spins, the friction between your palms and the rubber wall resists rotation. **Your palms are the brake shoes**, the **inner wall is the rotating drum**, and the **outward push is the actuation force**. Now think about a *real-life drum brake on the rear wheel of a small motorcycle or a Maruti 800 car*. Two curved brake shoes sit inside a hollow cylindrical drum bolted to the wheel. When you press the brake pedal, hydraulic oil pushes a piston that **forces the shoes outward** to rub against the drum's inside surface. The faster the wheel tries to spin, the more the drum drags on the shoes — and the vehicle decelerates. **Why is this design so common?** - It is **compact** (shoes fit *inside* the drum, no external clutter). - It **protects the friction surface** from water, dust, and mud because the drum acts as a shield. - It can be **self-energizing** — meaning a small pedal force can produce a large braking torque because friction itself helps push the shoe harder against the drum. --- ## 1.3 Classification of Internal Expanding Shoe Brakes The KTU syllabus specifically requires analysis of **single-shoe** and **double-shoe** configurations. The following classification is essential: | Type | Pivot Location | Behaviour | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Single Shoe Brake** | One shoe only, pivoted at heel or toe | Simple analysis, rarely used alone | | **Double Shoe Brake (Symmetric)** | Both shoes pivoted at the **top** | Both shoes **self-energizing** for downward rotation | | **Double Shoe Brake (Asymmetric — Leading/Trailing)** | One shoe pivoted, other floats | One shoe self-energizing (leading), the other self-de-energizing (trailing) | | **Double Shoe Brake (Twin-Acting / Two-Way)** | Both shoes actuated independently in both directions | Equal braking in forward and reverse (used in elevators, hoists) | > [!NOTE] > **Self-Energizing Brake:** A brake in which the friction force *assists* the applied actuation force, producing a higher braking torque for the same input. The **leading shoe** of an automobile drum brake is a classic example. > > **Self-De-Energizing Brake:** A brake in which the friction force *opposes* the applied actuation force, requiring a *larger* actuation force for the same braking torque. The **trailing shoe** of an asymmetric drum brake is this type. --- ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Coefficient of friction (μ)** for shoe-on-cast-iron/drum: typically **μ = 0.25 to 0.40** (dry), **μ = 0.10 to 0.20** (wet). - **Maximum allowable pressure** on the lining: **0.5 MPa to 1.0 MPa** for organic linings; up to **2.0 MPa** for sintered metallic linings. - **Drum surface temperature limit:** generally **≤ 300 °C** for organic linings. - **Standard KTU assumed values for design problems:** *μ = 0.30*, *p_allow = 0.7 MPa* (unless otherwise specified). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic The operational sequence of an internal expanding shoe brake is best understood through the following structured logic: **Step 1 — Actuation Initiation** - The driver or operator applies an input force through a mechanical lever, hydraulic piston, or pneumatic actuator. - This input force is transmitted to the brake shoe via an actuation mechanism (link, cam, or piston). **Step 2 — Radial Expansion** - The shoe is mechanically forced to move radially **outward** until its friction lining contacts the inner surface of the drum. - The contact is initially light (small contact area); as actuation force grows, the contact area enlarges. **Step 3 — Pressure Build-Up** - The lining develops a normal pressure **p** (force per unit area) on the drum's inner surface. - Two classical theories describe this pressure distribution: - **Uniform Pressure Theory:** $p = \text{constant}$ over the entire lining area. - **Uniform Wear Theory:** $p \cdot r = \text{constant}$ (where $r$ is the radial distance from drum centre). This is the more realistic assumption because worn linings develop wear proportional to sliding distance, which is proportional to radius. **Step 4 — Friction Force Generation** - The tangential friction force on every elemental area is $dF_f = \mu \cdot p \cdot dA$. - The resultant friction force $\mu N$ acts tangentially to the drum, in the direction opposing rotation. **Step 5 — Braking Torque** - The friction force, acting at radius $R$, produces a braking torque: $$ T_b = F_f \cdot R = \mu N \cdot R $$ - This torque decelerates the drum and the connected shaft. **Step 6 — Moment Equilibrium of the Shoe** - For the shoe to remain in static equilibrium, the moment of the actuation force about the pivot must balance the moment of the normal and friction reactions about the pivot. - This gives the design equation for the required actuation force $F_a$. --- ## 2.2 The Two Pressure Distribution Theories ### 2.2.1 Uniform Pressure Theory > **Assumption:** The normal pressure $p$ is uniform across the entire shoe-drum contact area. For a shoe of angular extent $2\theta_s$ (in radians), width $b$, and drum radius $R$: - **Contact area** $A = b \cdot R \cdot 2\theta_s$ - **Total normal force** $N = p \cdot b \cdot R \cdot 2\theta_s$ ### 2.2.2 Uniform Wear Theory > **Assumption:** The product $p \cdot r$ is constant across the contact surface, i.e., $p \cdot r = C$ (constant). This implies $p = C / r$ — the pressure is **highest at the smallest radius** and **lowest at the largest radius**. - **Total normal force** $N = \int p \, dA = \int_{R_1}^{R_2} \frac{C}{r} \cdot b \cdot 2\theta_s \cdot r \cdot dr = C \cdot b \cdot 2\theta_s \cdot (R_2 - R_1)$ where $R_1$ and $R_2$ are the inner and outer radii of the lining band. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Tip:** When the lining is *new* and unworn, the *uniform pressure* assumption gives more accurate predictions. As the lining wears and conforms to the drum, the *uniform wear* assumption becomes more realistic. **Most KTU problems default to uniform wear** unless explicitly stated otherwise. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The table below contains every high-yield formula required to solve KTU Part B (14-mark) numericals on Internal Expanding Shoe Brakes. **Memorize the geometry of moment arms before the algebra.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Braking torque | $T_b = \mu N R$ | N·m | | 2 | Normal force — Uniform pressure | $N = p \, b \, R \, 2\theta_s$ | N | | 3 | Normal force — Uniform wear | $N = p_{\max} \, b \, R \, \theta_s$ | $p_{\max}$ is at radius $R$ | | 4 | Friction force on shoe | $F_f = \mu N$ | N | | 5 | Actuation force (generic) | $F_a = \dfrac{N \cdot c \; \mp \; \mu N \cdot d}{L}$ | N; minus sign = self-energizing | | 6 | Self-energizing condition | $\mu \geq \dfrac{c}{d}$ (lock-up threshold) | Dimensionless | | 7 | Brake factor | $BF = \dfrac{\mu N R}{F_a \cdot R_{\text{eff}}} = \dfrac{\mu N}{F_a}$ | Dimensionless | | 8 | Self-energizing ratio | $S = \dfrac{N \cdot c - \mu N \cdot d}{N \cdot c + \mu N \cdot d}$ | Used to compare shoe efficiency | | 9 | Pivot moment — short shoe | $M_{\text{pivot}} = N(c) \mp \mu N(d)$ | N·m | | 10 | Equivalent moment arm of N | $c = \dfrac{a_1 \sin\alpha_2 - a_2 \sin\alpha_1}{\sin(\alpha_2 - \alpha_1)}$ | m (geometry dependent) | | 11 | Energy absorbed per stop | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ | J | | 12 | Power dissipation | $P = T_b \cdot \omega$ | W | | 13 | Heat dissipation rate | $\dot{Q} = m \, c_p \, \Delta T / \Delta t$ | W | > [!WARNING] > **Convention for signs in the actuation force equation:** > - **Minus sign ($-$) → Self-energizing direction** (friction torque helps actuation) > - **Plus sign ($+$) → Self-de-energizing direction** (friction torque opposes actuation) > - Getting this sign convention wrong is the **#1 cause of marks lost** in KTU Part B problems. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Internal expanding shoe brakes are the **workhorses of mechanical braking** in the following applications: | Application | Why Internal Shoe is Chosen | | :--- | :--- | | **Passenger cars** (rear wheels) | Self-energizing action reduces pedal effort; enclosed drum shields from water | | **Trucks and buses** | Large braking torque from compact dual-shoe design | | **Cranes, hoists, winches** | Twin-acting configuration gives bidirectional braking for safe load lowering | | **Conveyor drives** | Long-life enclosed design suits dusty industrial environments | | **Railway wagon handbrakes** | Mechanical linkage with high mechanical advantage | | **Elevator drum brakes** | Fail-safe: spring-applied, hydraulically released | > The *self-energizing* effect is what makes drum brakes feel "grabbier" than disc brakes in older cars — and why KTU examiners frequently ask students to compare the **two shoe configurations** and identify which shoe carries more braking torque. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Normal Force Under Uniform Wear Theory ### Setup Consider a thin elemental ring of the lining at radius $r$, of thickness $dr$, and angular extent $2\theta_s$ (where $\theta_s$ is the half-angle subtended by the shoe at the drum centre). ### Elemental Area The elemental arc length at radius $r$ is $r \cdot d\theta$. But because the ring is full $2\theta_s$ wide, the elemental area is: $$ dA = b \cdot (r \, d\theta) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{total elemental ring} \quad dA = b \cdot r \cdot 2\theta_s \cdot \frac{dr}{?} $$ Wait — let me redo this carefully. A differential ring at radius $r$ of thickness $dr$ has circumference $2\pi r$, but only a fraction $2\theta_s / 2\pi$ of it is covered by the shoe. So the elemental area is: $$ dA = b \cdot (2\theta_s) \cdot dr $$ ### Uniform Wear Condition The wear theory postulates that the rate of wear at every point of the lining is the same. The wear rate per unit time is proportional to (sliding velocity) × (pressure). The sliding velocity at radius $r$ is $v = r\omega$. For uniform wear: $$ p \cdot v = \text{constant} \quad \Rightarrow \quad p \cdot r\omega = \text{constant} \quad \Rightarrow \quad p \cdot r = C $$ Therefore, at any radius $r$: $$ p = \dfrac{C}{r} $$ ### Total Normal Force $$ N = \int p \, dA = \int_{R_i}^{R_o} \dfrac{C}{r} \cdot b \cdot 2\theta_s \cdot dr = 2 \theta_s \, b \, C \int_{R_i}^{R_o} \dfrac{dr}{r} $$ Integrating: $$ N = 2 \theta_s \, b \, C \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{R_o}{R_i}\right) $$ **But**, when the lining is **thin** relative to the drum radius (which is the standard assumption for shoe brakes), $R_o \approx R_i \approx R$, and $\ln(R_o/R_i) \approx (R_o - R_i)/R = t/R$ where $t$ is lining thickness. Hence the classical simplified result: $$ \boxed{\,N = p_{\max} \, b \, R \, \theta_s \,} $$ where $p_{\max} = C/R$ is the **maximum pressure** occurring at the smallest radius (heel of shoe). > **Conversion logic:** The integral of $p \cdot dA$ over the lining surface gives total normal force. Under uniform wear, $p = C/r$, so $C$ drops out and we get the compact expression above. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Actuation Force for a Single Pivoted Shoe ### Geometry Consider a single internal shoe pivoted at point $O$. The drum centre is $C$. The actuating force $F_a$ is applied perpendicular to the lever arm at distance $L$ from the pivot. The shoe-drum contact is "short," meaning we can represent the entire contact pressure as a single resultant **normal force $N$** acting at the geometric centre of the shoe, and a single resultant **friction force $\mu N$** acting tangentially. Let: - $c$ = perpendicular distance from pivot $O$ to the **line of action of $N$** (which passes through drum centre $C$) - $d$ = perpendicular distance from pivot $O$ to the **line of action of $\mu N$** (tangent to drum) - $L$ = perpendicular distance from pivot $O$ to the **line of action of $F_a$** - The drum rotates **clockwise** (or counterclockwise — to be specified per case). ### Free Body Diagram of the Shoe Three forces act on the shoe: 1. $F_a$ — applied actuation force (known direction) 2. $N$ — normal reaction of drum on shoe (radially inward, i.e., pointing from contact point toward drum centre $C$) 3. $\mu N$ — friction reaction of drum on shoe (tangent to drum, opposing drum's surface motion) ### Moment Equilibrium About Pivot O Taking moments about $O$ (eliminating pivot reaction): $$ F_a \cdot L \; = \; N \cdot c \; \mp \; \mu N \cdot d $$ - **Minus sign** when friction moment **assists** $F_a$ (self-energizing) - **Plus sign** when friction moment **opposes** $F_a$ (self-de-energizing) ### Solving for Actuation Force $$ \boxed{\,F_a = \dfrac{N \cdot c \; \mp \; \mu N \cdot d}{L} \,} $$ ### Braking Torque The friction force acts at the drum surface radius $R$: $$ T_b = \mu N \cdot R $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Equivalent Moment Arms ($c$ and $d$) for a Short Shoe For a short shoe, the contact between shoe and drum is approximated as a single point at the shoe's geometric centre. Let: - $R$ = drum inner radius - $a_1$ = distance from pivot $O$ to the **drum centre** $C$ - $\alpha_1$ = angle between $OC$ and the **vertical reference** - $\alpha_2$ = angle between $OC$ and the line joining pivot to the **shoe-centre contact point** The contact point lies on the drum, at distance $R$ from $C$, along the line making angle $\alpha_2$ from $OC$. ### Distance from Pivot to Drum Centre $$ a_1 = \text{(given geometry)} $$ ### Distance from Pivot to Contact Point By the law of cosines in triangle $O$-Centre-$P_{\text{contact}}$: $$ a_2 = \sqrt{a_1^2 + R^2 - 2 a_1 R \cos\alpha_2} $$ ### Equivalent Moment Arm of Normal Force N The normal force $N$ acts along the line joining the contact point to the drum centre $C$ (radially). The perpendicular distance from $O$ to this line is: $$ c = a_2 \sin(\angle \text{between } OC \text{ and } a_2) $$ Using trigonometric identities: $$ \boxed{\,c = \dfrac{a_1 \sin\alpha_2 - a_2 \sin\alpha_1}{\sin(\alpha_2 - \alpha_1)} \,} $$ ### Equivalent Moment Arm of Friction Force $\mu N$ The friction force is tangent to the drum at the contact point. By construction, the perpendicular distance from $O$ to this tangent equals the distance from $O$ to $C$ (a well-known geometric property: the tangent at $P$ to a circle centred at $C$ has perpendicular distance to $O$ equal to $|OC|$ *only* if $OP$ is perpendicular to $CP$ — which is true since $CP$ is radial). More precisely: $$ \boxed{\,d = a_1 \sin\!\left(\alpha_2 - \dfrac{\pi}{2}\right) \quad \text{or} \quad d = a_1 \cos\alpha_2 \,} $$ depending on the chosen reference axis. **Always derive this from the geometry of the specific problem.** --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Double Shoe Brake (Symmetric, Pivot at Top) Two identical shoes are pivoted at the top of the drum, each actuated by force $F_a$ directed horizontally. For a given direction of drum rotation: - **One shoe is self-energizing** (the one on the side where friction assists actuation) - **The other shoe is self-de-energizing** The total braking torque is the **sum** of both shoes' contributions: $$ T_b = T_{b,\text{leading}} + T_{b,\text{trailing}} $$ $$ T_b = \mu N_L R + \mu N_T R $$ where $N_L$ and $N_T$ are the normal forces on the leading and trailing shoes, respectively. Since both shoes share the same actuation force $F_a$ but have different signs for the friction term: **Leading shoe:** $$ F_a L_L = N_L c_L - \mu N_L d_L \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_L = \dfrac{F_a L_L}{c_L - \mu d_L} $$ **Trailing shoe:** $$ F_a L_T = N_T c_T + \mu N_T d_T \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_T = \dfrac{F_a L_T}{c_T + \mu d_T} $$ **Total braking torque:** $$ \boxed{\,T_b = \mu R \left[ \dfrac{F_a L_L}{c_L - \mu d_L} + \dfrac{F_a L_T}{c_T + \mu d_T} \right] \,} $$ > **Conversion logic:** This formula combines two independent shoe analyses. Each shoe has its own geometry ($c, d, L$), but they share the same actuation force $F_a$ when mechanically linked. The leading shoe produces *more* normal force (and therefore more friction torque) than the trailing shoe — this is the asymmetry that KTU examiners love to test. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Shoe Classification by Geometry | Pivot Location | Actuation Direction | Rotation Direction | Self-Energizing? | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Pivoted at **heel**, actuated at **toe** | Horizontal force at toe | Drum rotates such that shoe is pushed toward drum | **YES** (leading) | | Pivoted at **heel**, actuated at **toe** | Horizontal force at toe | Drum rotates in opposite sense | **NO** (trailing) | | Pivoted at **top**, both shoes below | Both shoes actuated symmetrically | Drum rotates clockwise | **Right shoe** = leading; **Left shoe** = trailing | | Twin-acting (shoes pivoted, actuated from inside) | Hydraulic pressure pushes shoes outward equally | Either direction | **Both shoes equally energized** (bidirectional) | --- ## 3.6 Derivation 5 — Self-Locking Condition A **self-locking (lock-up) brake** occurs when the actuation force required drops to zero — the shoe drags itself into engagement purely from friction. From the moment equation: $$ F_a = \dfrac{N \cdot c - \mu N \cdot d}{L} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N \cdot c = \mu N \cdot d $$ $$ \boxed{\,\mu_{\text{lock}} = \dfrac{c}{d} \,} $$ If the actual $\mu > c/d$, the shoe is **over-energized** and locks up uncontrollably. KTU problems often ask: *"For what range of $\mu$ is the shoe self-energizing but not self-locking?"* Answer: $0 < \mu < c/d$. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Internal Expanding Shoe Brake System The following Mermaid block diagram maps the *functional interactions* within an internal expanding shoe brake assembly, since a physical drawing of the shoe-drum geometry would require complex curved shapes that are difficult to render in pure Mermaid syntax. ```mermaid flowchart TB node1["Driver Input"] --> node2["Actuation Mechanism
Mechanical Lever or Hydraulic Piston"] node2 --> node3["Force Amplifier Linkage
Increases Applied Force"] node3 --> node4["Brake Shoe A
Leading Shoe Self-Energizing"] node3 --> node5["Brake Shoe B
Trailing Shoe Self-De-Energizing"] node4 --> node6["Friction Lining Surface
Pressure p and Friction mu N"] node5 --> node6 node6 --> node7["Rotating Drum Inner Surface
Diameter D Radius R"] node7 --> node8["Tangential Friction Force

Angular Decay omega dot"] node10 --> node11["Heat Generation

Friction Assists Actuation"] node5 -.Feedback.-> node13["Self De Energizing Loss
Friction Opposes Actuation"] node12 --> node2 node13 --> node2 ``` > **Block Diagram Interpretation:** > - **Solid arrows** indicate direct force or energy flow. > - **Dashed feedback arrows** represent the frictional interaction that either *aids* (leading shoe) or *hinders* (trailing shoe) the actuation force. > - This dual feedback is the **essence** of why double internal shoe brakes are self-energizing for one direction of rotation and self-de-energizing for the other. --- ## 4.2 Free Body Diagram of a Single Pivoted Shoe — Force Layout Below is a simplified Mermaid representation of the *spatial arrangement* of forces on a single shoe pivoted at the top. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph shoeAssembly["Single Internal Shoe Assembly"] pivot["Pivot Point O
