<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Viscosity** is the internal resistance of a fluid (typically lubricating oil) to deformation or flow. It is a quantitative measure of the fluid's resistance to gradual shearing stresses arising from intermolecular cohesion and momentum transfer between adjacent fluid layers moving at different velocities.

In the strict context of **PERAT521 (Design of Transmission Elements)**, viscosity is treated as the **single most important physical property of a lubricant** because it governs the load-carrying capacity, friction torque, heat generation, and oil-film thickness in hydrodynamic bearings and gear meshes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Newton's Law of Viscosity** states that the shear stress ($\tau$) between adjacent fluid layers is directly proportional to the velocity gradient ($\frac{du}{dy}$) perpendicular to the direction of flow:
> $$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$
> Where $\mu$ is the **coefficient of dynamic viscosity** (or absolute viscosity).

### 1.2 Types of Viscosity (Classification)

| # | Type | Symbol | SI Unit | Practical Use |
|---|------|--------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Dynamic (Absolute) Viscosity | $\mu$ | Pa·s ($\text{N·s/m}^2$) | Used in Reynolds equation & Petroff's equation |
| 2 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu$ | m²/s | Used in ISO VG grading, rating charts |
| 3 | Apparent Viscosity | $\mu_a$ | Pa·s | For non-Newtonian (Greases, Multigrade oils) |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **🍯 Honey vs. Water Analogy:**
> Imagine two parallel glass plates with a thin gap. The bottom plate is fixed, the top is dragged sideways.
> - **Water** flows easily; layers slide over each other almost effortlessly → **Low viscosity**.
> - **Honey** resists motion heavily; it "sticks" to itself → **High viscosity**.
> - **Engine Oil (SAE 20W-40)** behaves like a middle-ground liquid, and its viscosity *changes* with temperature (cold → thick like honey; hot → thin like water). This thermal sensitivity is **THE central engineering problem** in transmission-element design.

### 1.4 Standard Units & Conversions

- **Dynamic viscosity ($\mu$)**: $1 \text{ Pa·s} = 10 \text{ Poise} = 1000 \text{ cP}$
- **Kinematic viscosity ($\nu$)**: $1 \text{ m}^2/\text{s} = 10^4 \text{ St} = 10^6 \text{ cSt}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Relationships to Memorize:**
> $$\mu = \rho \cdot \nu \qquad \text{and} \qquad \nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$
> where $\rho$ is the density of the lubricant in $\text{kg/m}^3$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

The viscosity of a lubricating oil in a transmission element (e.g., hydrodynamic journal bearing) is governed by three fundamental mechanisms:

1. **Molecular Cohesion (Adhesion)**: Intermolecular forces between oil layers.
2. **Momentum Transfer**: Faster-moving molecules colliding with slower ones (kinetic theory).
3. **Temperature Sensitivity**: As temperature rises, molecular spacing increases → cohesive forces drop → viscosity falls **exponentially**.

### 2.2 Viscosity-Temperature Relationship

Viscosity **decreases sharply** with rising temperature. Two industry-standard models are used:

#### (a) Vogel's Equation (Continuous use):
$$\log_{10}\log_{10}(\nu + 0.7) = A - B \log_{10}(T)$$
where $\nu$ is in cSt, $T$ is in **Kelvin (K)**, and $A$, $B$ are oil-specific constants.

#### (b) ASTM Viscosity-Temperature Charts:
Drawn using the above Vogel equation; used universally to find oil viscosity at operating temperature if known at two reference temperatures (typically **40 °C** and **100 °C**).

### 2.3 Viscosity Index (VI)

The **Viscosity Index (VI)** is a dimensionless number indicating the rate of viscosity change with temperature. It is **CRITICAL** for selecting lubricants in KTU design problems.

| VI Range | Quality | Typical Use |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| 0 – 50 | Low VI (Poor) | Crude mineral oils |
| 50 – 95 | Medium VI | Standard machine oils |
| 95 – 100+ | High VI | Premium turbine / gear oils |
| > 100 | Very High VI | Synthetic / multi-grade oils |

$$\text{VI} = \frac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100$$

Where:
- $U$ = kinematic viscosity at **40 °C** of the test oil
- $L$ = kinematic viscosity at 40 °C of an oil with VI = 0 having the same viscosity at **100 °C** as test oil
- $H$ = kinematic viscosity at 40 °C of an oil with VI = 100 having the same viscosity at 100 °C as test oil

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---------|------------------|
| 1 | $\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$ | Newton's Law of Viscosity |
| 2 | $\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic–Dynamic relationship |
| 3 | $T_f = \dfrac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^3 L}{c}$ | **Petroff's Friction Torque** (Journal bearing) |
| 4 | $f = 2\pi^2 \left(\dfrac{\mu N}{P}\right)\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)$ | Petroff's Friction Coefficient |
| 5 | $S = \left(\dfrac{\mu N}{P}\right)\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^2$ | **Sommerfeld Number** (dimensionless) |
| 6 | $\text{VI} = \dfrac{L-U}{L-H}\times 100$ | Viscosity Index |
| 7 | $\log\log(\nu+0.7) = A - B\log T$ | Vogel's Equation ($T$ in K, $\nu$ in cSt) |
| 8 | $\text{SUS} \rightarrow \text{cSt}: \nu \approx 0.226t - \dfrac{195}{t}$ | Saybolt Universal Viscosity conversion ($t > 100$ s) |
| 9 | $P = \dfrac{W}{2rL}$ | Bearing Projected Pressure |
| 10 | $h_0 = c(1 - \epsilon\cos\phi)$ | Film thickness in journal bearing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Petroff's Equation assumes:** concentric journal, no side leakage, thin film, and constant viscosity. It is the *starting point* of all hydrodynamic bearing analysis and is asked directly in KTU Module-4 questions.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

In the design of transmission elements, viscosity directly influences:

- **Load Capacity (W)** of journal and thrust bearings (via Reynolds equation).
- **Frictional Power Loss** ($P_{loss} = T_f \cdot \omega$) — dictates thermal rating.
- **Minimum Film Thickness ($h_0$)** — prevents metal-to-metal contact.
- **Lubricant Selection (ISO VG / SAE Grade)** — based on operating temperature and speed.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — From Newton's Law to Petroff's Friction Torque

**Given:** A full journal bearing of radius $r$, length $L$, radial clearance $c$, rotating at $N$ rps with lubricant of dynamic viscosity $\mu$. The journal is concentric (no load).

**Step 1 — Shear stress on lubricant layer:**

From Newton's law, at radius $r$ from the bearing center:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

where $y$ is measured radially from the journal surface. The linear velocity of the journal surface is $u = 2\pi r N$, and the linear velocity gradient across the thin oil film (thickness $\approx c$) is:

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{u}{c} = \frac{2\pi r N}{c}$$

**Step 2 — Shear force on the journal:**

Total tangential shear force on the journal surface (area $= 2\pi r L$):

$$F_t = \tau \cdot (2\pi r L) = \mu \left(\frac{2\pi r N}{c}\right) (2\pi r L)$$

$$F_t = \frac{4 \pi^2 \mu N r^2 L}{c}$$

**Step 3 — Friction torque about the bearing axis:**

Multiplying shear force by the moment arm $r$:

$$T_f = F_t \cdot r = \frac{4 \pi^2 \mu N r^3 L}{c}$$

$$\boxed{T_f = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^3 L}{c}} \quad \text{... Petroff's Friction Torque}$$

**Step 4 — Friction Coefficient:**

The coefficient of friction is defined as $f = T_f / (W \cdot r)$. Substituting $P = W/(2rL)$:

$$f = \frac{T_f}{Wr} = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^3 L}{c \cdot W \cdot r} = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^2 L}{c \cdot W}$$

Using $W = 2 r L P$:

$$f = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^2 L}{c \cdot 2rLP} = \frac{2\pi^2 \mu N r}{c P}$$

$$\boxed{f = 2\pi^2 \left(\frac{\mu N}{P}\right)\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Sommerfeld Number (Dimensionless Grouping)

Starting from Petroff's coefficient of friction:

$$f = 2\pi^2 \left(\frac{\mu N}{P}\right)\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)$$

Rearrange to isolate the dimensionless group containing viscosity:

$$\frac{\mu N}{P} = \frac{f}{2\pi^2} \cdot \frac{c}{r}$$

Multiply both sides by $(r/c)$ to make the group completely dimensionless:

$$\left(\frac{\mu N}{P}\right)\left(\frac{r}{c}\right) = \frac{f}{2\pi^2}$$

Squaring both sides — this gives the famous **Sommerfeld Number** $S$:

$$\boxed{S = \left(\frac{\mu N}{P}\right)\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2}$$

The Sommerfeld number is the cornerstone of KTU hydrodynamic bearing design. It remains constant for geometrically and dynamically similar bearings.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Viscosity Index (VI) Verification

**Worked example:** A test oil has $\nu = 100$ cSt at 40 °C and $\nu = 10$ cSt at 100 °C.
From ASTM tables:
- $L = 120$ cSt (VI = 0 reference at 40 °C for oil with 10 cSt at 100 °C)
- $H = 108$ cSt (VI = 100 reference at 40 °C for oil with 10 cSt at 100 °C)
- $U = 100$ cSt (test oil at 40 °C)

Apply the VI formula:

$$\text{VI} = \frac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100 = \frac{120 - 100}{120 - 108} \times 100$$

$$\text{VI} = \frac{20}{12} \times 100 = 166.67$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since $\text{VI} > 100$, the oil is **superior to the paraffinic reference** and is classified as a *high-VI* oil (synthetic or multi-grade). Such calculations appear routinely in KTU Module-4 viva and Part-A questions.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Viscosity Variation

| Property / Parameter | Low $\mu$ (Thin Oil) | High $\mu$ (Thick Oil) |
|---|---|---|
| Friction Torque $T_f$ | Low ✅ | High ❌ (heat ↑) |
| Load Capacity $W$ | Low ❌ (metal contact risk) | High ✅ |
| Film Thickness $h_0$ | Thin ❌ | Thick ✅ |
| Heat Dissipation | Easy ✅ | Poor ❌ |
| Starting Torque | Low ✅ | Very high ❌ |
| Recommended Speed | High-speed bearings | Low-speed, heavy-load bearings |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define *dynamic viscosity* and *kinematic viscosity*. State their SI units and the relationship between them.

**Model Answer:**
- **Dynamic viscosity ($\mu$)** is the tangential shear stress per unit velocity gradient between two parallel fluid layers:
$$\mu = \frac{\tau}{du/dy}, \quad \text{SI unit: Pa·s (N·s/m²)}$$
- **Kinematic viscosity ($\nu$)** is the dynamic viscosity divided by the fluid density:
$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}, \quad \text{SI unit: m²/s}$$
- **Relationship:** $\mu = \rho \cdot \nu$. Practically, $\nu$ is measured using capillary viscometers, while $\mu$ is used directly in Reynolds and Petroff equations.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** What is the *Viscosity Index (VI)* of a lubricant? Why is it important in bearing design?

**Model Answer:**
- **Viscosity Index (VI)** is a dimensionless empirical number that quantifies the rate of change of lubricant viscosity with temperature.
- A **high VI** indicates the oil's viscosity changes *less* with temperature (ideal for variable-load bearings).
- **Importance in bearing design:** Ensures sufficient film thickness at operating temperature, prevents starting torque spikes in cold conditions, and guarantees hydrodynamic lubrication at the design speed and load.

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 Choice-1]`

**(a)** Derive Petroff's equation for the frictional torque of a concentric journal bearing. State all assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A full journal bearing has the following data:
- Journal radius $r = 50$ mm
- Bearing length $L = 100$ mm
- Radial clearance $c = 0.1$ mm
- Speed $N = 1500$ rpm
- Load $W = 10$ kN
- Lubricant dynamic viscosity at operating temperature $\mu = 0.025$ Pa·s

Calculate:
(i) Petroff's friction torque,
(ii) Coefficient of friction, and
(iii) Power loss. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### ✅ Model Solution

**(a) Derivation — (Refer to Section 3.1, Steps 1–4 of these notes)**

Final result:
$$T_f = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^3 L}{c}$$

**Assumptions of Petroff's Equation:**
1. Journal is concentric with bearing (eccentricity $\epsilon = 0$).
2. The bearing is infinitely long (no side leakage).
3. Viscosity is constant throughout the film.
4. The oil film is thin ($c \ll r$) and laminar.
5. Inertia forces in the lubricant are negligible.
6. No load-carrying wedges are formed (purely shear flow).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert given units (SI):**

$r = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
$L = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
$c = 0.1 \text{ mm} = 1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$
$N = 1500 \text{ rpm} = \dfrac{1500}{60} = 25 \text{ rps}$
$W = 10 \text{ kN} = 10{,}000 \text{ N}$

**Step 2 — Petroff's friction torque:**

$$T_f = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^3 L}{c}$$

$$T_f = \frac{4 \times \pi^2 \times 0.025 \times 25 \times (0.05)^3 \times 0.1}{1 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$T_f = \frac{4 \times 9.8696 \times 0.025 \times 25 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.1}{1 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$T_f = \frac{4 \times 9.8696 \times 0.025 \times 25 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4}}{1 \times 10^{-4}}$$

(Combining $L/c$ first): $\dfrac{0.1}{1 \times 10^{-4}} = 1000$

$$T_f = 4 \times 9.8696 \times 0.025 \times 25 \times (0.05)^3 \times 1000$$

$$T_f = 4 \times 9.8696 \times 0.025 \times 25 \times 0.000125 \times 1000$$

$$T_f = 4 \times 9.8696 \times 0.025 \times 25 \times 0.125$$

$$T_f = 4 \times 9.8696 \times 0.078125$$

$$T_f \approx 3.084 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_f \approx 3.08 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Step 3 — Bearing pressure:**

$$P = \frac{W}{2rL} = \frac{10{,}000}{2 \times 0.05 \times 0.1} = \frac{10{,}000}{0.01} = 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 1 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Coefficient of friction:**

$$f = 2\pi^2 \left(\frac{\mu N}{P}\right)\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)$$

$$\frac{\mu N}{P} = \frac{0.025 \times 25}{10^6} = \frac{0.625}{10^6} = 6.25 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s}$$

$$\frac{r}{c} = \frac{0.05}{1 \times 10^{-4}} = 500$$

$$f = 2 \times \pi^2 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-7} \times 500$$

$$f = 2 \times 9.8696 \times 3.125 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$f \approx 6.17 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{f \approx 0.00617}$$

**Step 5 — Power loss:**

$$P_{loss} = T_f \cdot \omega = T_f \cdot 2\pi N$$

$$P_{loss} = 3.084 \times 2 \times \pi \times 25$$

$$P_{loss} = 3.084 \times 157.08 \approx 484.3 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{loss} \approx 484 \text{ W}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4 Choice-2]`

**(a)** Explain the *Viscosity Index (VI)* with a neat sketch of the ASTM viscosity–temperature chart. State its significance in selecting bearing lubricants. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The kinematic viscosity of an oil at 40 °C is **110 cSt** and at 100 °C is **11 cSt**. From ASTM tables: $L = 130$ cSt, $H = 110$ cSt. Determine:
(i) Viscosity Index of the oil, and
(ii) Identify the **ISO VG grade** and **SAE grade** of this oil. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### ✅ Model Solution

**(a) Viscosity Index (VI) — Conceptual Explanation:**

The **Viscosity Index** is an empirical, dimensionless number developed by Dean & Davis (1929) to measure the *flatness* of the viscosity–temperature curve of an oil.

- Two reference oils are used:
  - **Paraffinic (VI = 100)** — viscosity changes little with temperature.
  - **Naphthenic (VI = 0)** — viscosity changes a lot.
- Test oil's VI is found by **interpolation** between $L$ and $H$.

**ASTM Viscosity–Temperature Chart:**
The chart has:
- X-axis: Temperature in °C (or K) — linear scale.
- Y-axis: Kinematic viscosity in cSt — **log-log scale** (this linearizes Vogel's equation).
- Two reference lines (VI = 0 and VI = 100) drawn through each 100 °C viscosity point.

**Significance in Bearing Lubricant Selection:**

1. High-VI oils maintain thicker films at high temperatures.
2. Low-VI oils thicken excessively at cold start → high starting torque & wear.
3. KTU design problems always specify "select oil with VI > 95" for high-speed bearings.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Given data:**
- $U = 110$ cSt (test oil at 40 °C)
- $L = 130$ cSt (VI = 0 reference)
- $H = 110$ cSt (VI = 100 reference)

**Step 2 — Apply VI formula:**

$$\text{VI} = \frac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100$$

$$\text{VI} = \frac{130 - 110}{130 - 110} \times 100$$

$$\text{VI} = \frac{20}{20} \times 100 = 100$$

$$\boxed{\text{VI} = 100}$$

**Step 3 — Identify ISO VG Grade:**

ISO VG grades are designated by the **kinematic viscosity at 40 °C**, allowing ±10% tolerance.
- Given $\nu_{40} = 110$ cSt → falls within the **ISO VG 100** band (90 – 110 cSt).

$$\boxed{\text{ISO VG 100}}$$

**Step 4 — Identify SAE Grade (engine oil) or AGMA Grade (gear oil):**

SAE viscosity grades are based on viscosity at **−18 °C (W grades)** and **100 °C (non-W grades)**.
Given $\nu_{100} = 11$ cSt → falls in the **SAE 30** (9.3 – 12.5 cSt at 100 °C) range.

$$\boxed{\text{SAE 30}}$$

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in viscosity questions:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert mm → m and rpm → rps before plugging into Petroff's formula. Always convert ALL quantities to SI units *first*.
> 2. **Wrong Viscosity Type (–3 marks):** Using kinematic viscosity ($\nu$) directly in Petroff's equation. Petroff's $T_f$ **requires dynamic viscosity ($\mu$)**, not $\nu$. Convert using $\mu = \rho \cdot \nu$.
> 3. **Missing "Concentric" Assumption (–1 mark):** Petroff's equation is valid **only** when eccentricity $\epsilon = 0$. Stating this assumption is mandatory.
> 4. **Power Loss Confusion:** Power loss = $T_f \cdot \omega$, not $T_f \cdot N$. Angular velocity $\omega = 2\pi N$ (where $N$ is in rps).
> 5. **Viscosity Index Sign Error:** If $U > L$, then VI < 0, which is physically unrealistic for refining — flag it as an "oil contaminated" or check the given values.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Newton's Law:** $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$ — write this **verbatim** in every short answer.
- **Always remember:** Petroff's formula uses **dynamic viscosity ($\mu$) in Pa·s**, **radius $r$ in meters**, **speed $N$ in rps**.
- **Sommerfeld Number** $S = \left(\dfrac{\mu N}{P}\right)\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^2$ — fully dimensionless; constant for dynamically similar bearings.
- **Viscosity Index (VI)** is calculated at reference temperatures **40 °C and 100 °C** (standard ISO method).
- **Viscosity falls exponentially** with temperature — use Vogel's equation or ASTM charts.
- **High $\mu$** ⇒ thick oil ⇒ high load capacity, high friction, high heat, high starting torque.
- **Low $\mu$** ⇒ thin oil ⇒ low friction, low load capacity, good cooling.
- **1 Pa·s = 10 Poise = 1000 cP**; **1 m²/s = 10⁴ St = 10⁶ cSt** — memorize conversions.
- **Petroff's assumptions** (concentric, thin film, constant $\mu$, no leakage, laminar) — recite at least 5 of them.
- **Engineering rule of thumb:** For hydrodynamic journal bearings, target coefficient of friction in the range **0.001 – 0.01**; values above 0.05 indicate boundary lubrication and probable failure.
- **Always show unit conversions** in Part-B answers — KTU examiners award a **dedicated 1 mark** for dimensional consistency.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT521 - DESIGN OF TRANSMISSION ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Journal Bearings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Journal Bearings – Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Journal Bearing:** A *Journal Bearing* is a plain cylindrical-sleeve bearing that supports a rotating shaft (the **journal**) by a thin film of **lubricant** (typically oil) contained between the journal surface and the bearing inner surface (the **bush** or **sleeve**). The load is transmitted across the clearance space entirely through hydrodynamic pressure generated in the lubricant film — there is no rolling element, no line contact, and no point contact.

Under KTU 2024 Scheme (PERAT521 – Design of Transmission Elements), journal bearings are studied as a classical **hydrodynamic (sleeve) bearing** design problem, involving the interlinked selection of:

- L/D ratio
- Radial clearance (*c*)
- Lubricant viscosity (*µ*)
- Bearing pressure (*P*)
- Speed (*N*)
- Eccentricity ratio (*ε*)
- Minimum film thickness (*h₀*)
- Coefficient of friction (*f*)
- Heat dissipation & temperature rise (*ΔT*)

## 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Journal** | The rotating shaft portion supported inside the bearing |
| **Bush / Sleeve** | The stationary cylindrical inner member of the bearing |
| **Bearing** | The complete assembly (bush + housing + lubricant) |
| **Radial clearance (c)** | Difference between bush radius (R) and journal radius (r) |
| **Eccentricity (e)** | Distance between journal centre and bush centre |
| **Eccentricity ratio (ε = e/c)** | Normalized offset, 0 ≤ ε ≤ 1 |
| **Minimum film thickness (h₀)** | Smallest gap between journal and bush, h₀ = c(1 − ε) |
| **Attitude angle (φ)** | Angle between load line and line of centres |
| **Petroff friction** | Friction when journal is concentric (ε = 0) and no side leakage |
| **Sommerfeld number (S)** | Dimensionless parameter that groups all operating variables |

## 1.3 Classification of Journal Bearings

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification by Lubrication Mechanism (CRITICAL FOR KTU):**

**A) Hydrodynamic / Full-Film Bearings** (THE FOCUS OF MODULE 4)
- Lubricant is dragged into the converging wedge by the rotating journal.
- Pressure is generated *hydrodynamically* — surfaces are completely separated.
- Sub-types:
  - **Full journal bearing (bushed)** – bush covers the journal 360° (most common).
  - **Partial bearing** – bush covers < 180° (used for low-load, oscillating motion).
  - **Flooded, fed, ring-oiled, wick-oiled** – methods of lubricant supply.

**B) Boundary / Mixed / Dry Bearings**
- Asperities make contact; not covered in-depth in this KTU module.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy – "The Log on the River"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine a smooth log rolling on a river bed with a thin sheet of water between them. The water is dragged along by the log's rotation and squeezed into a narrowing gap ahead of the log. Because water can't escape sideways easily, it pressurises — and that *pressure* lifts the log completely off the river bed.
> The **log = journal**, the **river bed = bush**, the **water film = lubricant**, the **pressurisation = hydrodynamic pressure**, and the **log floating = full-film lubrication**.
> If the log spins too slowly, or the water is too thin (low viscosity), the log scrapes the bed — that's *boundary lubrication* and *bearing failure*.

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Viscosity of typical mineral oil:** µ ≈ 0.01 – 0.1 Pa·s (10 – 100 cP)
- **Specific heat of oil:** C ≈ 1750 J/(kg·°C)
- **Density of oil:** ρ ≈ 870 kg/m³
- **Mechanical equivalent of heat:** J = 1 (when both sides in watts) or J = 9.81 if heat in W and work in N·m/s
- **Recommended L/D ratio (KTU design range):** 0.5 – 1.5
- **Typical eccentricity ratio at design:** ε = 0.5 – 0.8
- **Typical radial clearance ratio (c/r):** 0.001 – 0.002

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle – How a Hydrodynamic Bearing Works

The complete sequence of events inside a journal bearing is:

1. **Shaft at rest (ε = 0):** Journal sits at the bottom of the bush under load. Direct metal-to-metal contact → boundary friction → high wear.

2. **Shaft starts rotating:** Adherent oil is dragged by the journal surface into the converging wedge between journal and bush.

3. **Pressure build-up:** As oil is forced into the wedge-shaped clearance, fluid pressure rises hydrodynamically (Bernoulli-type effect in viscous flow).

4. **Journal lifts:** The integrated pressure distribution produces a net upward force equal to the applied load — the journal is *lifted* and the surfaces are *separated*.

5. **Equilibrium position:** The journal stabilises at an offset *e* such that the resultant of the pressure distribution equals the applied load **P**. The offset is quantified by the **eccentricity ratio ε = e/c**.

6. **Steady state:** Oil continuously enters the bearing at the high-clearance side, leaks sideways, and exits at the ends. Heat generated by shearing the oil is balanced by heat carried away by the oil and the housing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** The entire design problem in Module 4 reduces to a single dimensionless number — the **Sommerfeld number** — and the classical **Raimondi-Boyd charts** that link it to *friction*, *flow*, *attitude angle*, and *minimum film thickness*.

## 2.2 Petroff's Equation (Concentric, No-Side-Leakage Solution)

Petroff solved the simplest case: a concentric (ε = 0) journal, infinite-length assumption (no side leakage), with linear velocity profile across the film.

### Friction torque:
$$T_{f} = \frac{4 \pi^{3} \mu N^{2} L r^{3}}{c}$$

### Coefficient of friction:
$$f = \frac{2 \pi^{2} \mu N}{P_{c}} \cdot \frac{r}{c}$$

### Load-carrying capacity (Petroff):
$$P = \frac{4 \pi^{2} \mu N^{2} L r^{3}}{c}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Petroff's equation DOES NOT predict any load capacity for ε = 0** — the second equation above is just an algebraic rearrangement of the friction-torque relation divided by (P·r). The *actual* load capacity is **zero** when ε = 0; load is carried only when there is **eccentricity**. Petroff's result is therefore used only for *friction estimation at low eccentricity*.

## 2.3 The Sommerfeld Number (S)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The single most important dimensionless group in hydrodynamic bearing design.**

$$S = \frac{\mu N_{s}}{P} \left( \frac{r}{c} \right)^{2}$$

Where:
- µ = absolute viscosity (Pa·s or N·s/m²)
- Nₛ = speed in rev/s (if N is in rpm, use Nₛ = N/60)
- P = bearing pressure = W / (L·D) (N/mm²)
- r = journal radius
- c = radial clearance

**Alternate forms used in design:**

$$S = \frac{\left( r/c \right)^{2}}{2 \pi f_{P}}$$

where f_P is Petroff's coefficient of friction.

$$S = \frac{\mu N_{s}}{P} \left( \frac{D}{2c} \right)^{2} = \frac{\mu N_{s}}{P} \left( \frac{D}{C_{d}} \right)^{2}$$

where C_d = 2c = diametral clearance.

## 2.4 Reynolds Equation (2D, Steady, Incompressible, Neglecting Inertia)

The governing equation for pressure in the oil film, in polar coordinates (r, θ) anchored at the journal centre, with film thickness *h(θ)*:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta} \left( \frac{h^{3}}{\mu} \cdot \frac{\partial p}{\partial \theta} \right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z} \left( \frac{h^{3}}{\mu} \cdot \frac{\partial p}{\partial z} \right) = 6 U \frac{\partial h}{\partial \theta}$$

Where:
- *h(θ) = c(1 + ε cos θ)* (neglecting small terms)
- *U = 2π r Nₛ* = journal surface velocity
- *p(θ)* = hydrodynamic pressure
- *µ* = lubricant viscosity

Raimondi & Boyd (1958) solved this numerically and published their results as **four design charts/tables** correlating *ε* with:
- (r/c)·f
- Q / (r c Nₛ L)
- φ (attitude angle)
- h₀/c

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this table — every numerical problem in this module reduces to one of these equations.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Petroff friction torque | $T_f = 4\pi^3 \mu N^2 L r^3 / c$ | N·m (use N in rev/s) |
| 2 | Petroff coefficient of friction | $f = 2\pi^2 \mu N r / (P_c c)$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Sommerfeld number | $S = (\mu N_s / P)(r/c)^2$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Sommerfeld (alternate) | $S = (r/c)^2 / (2\pi f_P)$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Film thickness (polar) | $h(\theta) = c\bigl(1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta\bigr)$ | metres |
| 6 | Min film thickness | $h_0 = c(1 - \varepsilon)$ | metres |
| 7 | Surface velocity | $U_s = \pi D N_s = 2\pi r N_s$ | m/s |
| 8 | Bearing pressure | $P = W / (L \cdot D)$ | N/m² (Pa) |
| 9 | Load (integrated pressure) | $W = \int_0^{2\pi} p(\theta) L r \cos(\theta - \phi)\, d\theta$ | N |
| 10 | Petroff heat gen. rate | $H_g = T_f \cdot \omega = T_f \cdot 2\pi N_s$ | W |
| 11 | Heat lost in oil | $H_o = \rho Q C (T_o - T_i)$ | W |
| 12 | Heat lost by housing | $H_h = h_{cr} A_s (T_b - T_a)$ | W |
| 13 | Equilibrium oil flow | $Q = (rc N_s L) \cdot (\text{flow variable from R-B})$ | m³/s |
| 14 | Temperature rise (oil) | $\Delta T = T_o - T_i = \dfrac{\mu N_s}{P}\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^2 \cdot \dfrac{f \cdot r}{J \rho C (Q/rcN_sL)}$ | °C |
| 15 | Total frictional power | $P_f = f \cdot W \cdot U_s$ | W |
| 16 | Slip factor | $\alpha = 0.5$ (no slip) to $0.8$ | dimensionless |
| 17 | Side-leakage factor (R-B) | $\psi = (1 + \varepsilon)^2 / (1 - \varepsilon^2)^{1/2}$ | dimensionless |
| 18 | Capillary number (viscosity index) | — | used in selecting oil grade |

> [!WARNING]
> **Units Trap (KTU favourite):** In SI, use N in **rev/s** (rad/s if multiplied by 2π). In CGS, viscosity is in **poise** (1 poise = 0.1 Pa·s) and length in **cm**. Convert µ, L, r, c to **consistent SI units** before plugging into the Sommerfeld equation. The KTU paper often gives viscosity in **reyns** (lbf·s/in²) or **poise** — convert to Pa·s first.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility of Hydrodynamic Bearings

- **Power generation:** Turbo-alternator journals, large steam/gas turbine shaft bearings.
- **Marine:** Stern tubes, propeller-shaft bearings, thrust blocks.
- **Automotive:** Crankshaft main & big-end bearings, camshaft bearings.
- **Compressors & pumps:** All centrifugal equipment.
- **Machine tools:** Spindle bearings on lathes, milling machines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why we study it:** It is *the* most common type of bearing in industry, and unlike ball/roller bearings, its performance is *continuously variable* with operating conditions. A bad design leads to seizure within seconds of start-up. The Raimondi-Boyd-based design ensures adequate film thickness, acceptable friction, and a thermally sustainable steady state.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Petroff's Friction Equation (Concentric Journal)

> [!NOTE]
> **Petroff Assumption Set:** Concentric journal (ε = 0), no side leakage, infinitely long bearing, Newtonian fluid, laminar flow, no pressure gradient in axial direction, Couette linear velocity profile.

### Step 1 – Geometry & velocity field

For a concentric journal (ε = 0), the film thickness is uniform: *h = c* (constant).

The journal surface moves with tangential velocity:

$$U_s = 2\pi r N_s \quad \text{(m/s)}$$

The bush is stationary. In the gap, by the no-slip condition at both walls, the velocity profile is linear (Couette flow):

$$u(y) = U_s \cdot \frac{y}{c}$$

where *y* is the radial coordinate measured from the bush surface toward the journal.

### Step 2 – Shear stress

By Newton's law of viscosity:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = \mu \cdot \frac{U_s}{c} \quad \text{(constant across film)}$$

### Step 3 – Friction force on the journal

Friction force per unit area = τ. Total friction force over the journal surface (perimeter 2πr, length L):

$$F_f = \tau \cdot 2 \pi r L = \mu \cdot \frac{U_s}{c} \cdot 2 \pi r L$$

### Step 4 – Friction torque

Multiply by the journal radius to get the resisting moment about the journal axis:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_f &= F_f \cdot r = \mu \cdot \frac{U_s}{c} \cdot 2 \pi r L \cdot r \\
    &= \mu \cdot \frac{2 \pi r N_s}{c} \cdot 2 \pi r^{2} L \\
    &= \frac{4 \pi^{2} \mu r^{3} L N_s}{c}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **linear-velocity** form. To obtain it in terms of *N* (in rev/s), note that *N* (rev/s) = *Nₛ* when N is in rev/s, so:

$$T_f = \frac{4 \pi^{2} \mu r^{3} L N}{c} \quad \text{(with N in rev/s)}$$

If we use *N* in rpm and convert to rev/s via *Nₛ = N/60*, the formula becomes:

$$T_f = \frac{4 \pi^{2} \mu r^{3} L (N/60)}{c} = \frac{\pi^{2} \mu r^{3} L N}{15\, c}$$

### Step 5 – Petroff's coefficient of friction

By definition, the coefficient of friction is friction force / normal load:

$$f_P = \frac{F_f}{W} = \frac{\mu U_s \cdot 2 \pi r L / c}{W}$$

Using *U_s = 2π r Nₛ* and *W = P · (2r) · L = 2 P r L* (where *P = W/(L·D) = W/(2rL)*):

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_P &= \frac{\mu \cdot 2\pi r N_s \cdot 2 \pi r L / c}{2 P r L} \\
    &= \frac{4 \pi^{2} \mu r^{2} N_s L}{2 P r L c} \\
    &= \frac{2 \pi^{2} \mu r N_s}{P c} \\
    &= 2 \pi^{2} \cdot \frac{\mu N_s}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Petroff Equations (ready for substitution):**
> $$\boxed{\,T_f = \frac{4 \pi^{2} \mu r^{3} L N_s}{c}\,} \qquad \boxed{\,f_P = \frac{2 \pi^{2} \mu r N_s}{P c}\,} \qquad \boxed{\,T_f = f_P \, W \, r\,}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Sommerfeld Number

The Sommerfeld number is obtained by *inverting* Petroff's friction and combining it with the load-velocity form.

### Step 1 – Rearrange Petroff's friction

$$f_P = 2 \pi^{2} \left( \frac{\mu N_s}{P} \right) \left( \frac{r}{c} \right)$$

### Step 2 – Define Sommerfeld number

The term $\left( \dfrac{\mu N_s}{P} \right) \left( \dfrac{r}{c} \right)^{2}$ is a more useful dimensionless group because it absorbs the clearance ratio squared. So we define:

$$S \equiv \left( \frac{r}{c} \right)^{2} \cdot \frac{\mu N_s}{P}$$

### Step 3 – Express S in terms of f_P

Solving the Petroff equation for $\dfrac{\mu N_s}{P}$:

$$\frac{\mu N_s}{P} = \frac{f_P \cdot c}{2 \pi^{2} r}$$

Substitute into the definition of S:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \left( \frac{r}{c} \right)^{2} \cdot \frac{f_P \cdot c}{2 \pi^{2} r} \\
  &= \frac{r}{c} \cdot \frac{f_P}{2 \pi^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the **Sommerfeld-to-Petroff relation** is:

$$\boxed{\,S = \frac{r}{c} \cdot \frac{f_P}{2 \pi^{2}} \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; f_P = \frac{2 \pi^{2} S}{(r/c)}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **What this tells you:** For a *fixed* Sommerfeld number, Petroff's friction (the friction of a *concentric* journal) is *inversely* proportional to the clearance ratio. A tighter clearance (small c) increases friction at the same Sommerfeld number. This is why **too-tight clearances** are dangerous in design.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Frictional Power and Heat Generation

Frictional power dissipated in the film:

$$P_f = T_f \cdot \omega$$

With ω = 2π Nₛ:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_f &= \frac{4 \pi^{2} \mu r^{3} L N_s}{c} \cdot 2 \pi N_s \\
    &= \frac{8 \pi^{3} \mu r^{3} L N_s^{2}}{c}
\end{aligned}
$$

Equivalent form using coefficient of friction *f* (true film friction, NOT Petroff's):

$$P_f = f \cdot W \cdot U_s = f \cdot W \cdot 2 \pi r N_s \quad \text{(W)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For the temperature-rise calculation, KTU always uses the *true* friction *f* (from R-B charts), not Petroff's f_P**, because the journal is *not* concentric in real operation.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Bearing-Heat-Dissipation Equation (Thermal Equilibrium)

In steady state, **heat generated = heat removed**:

$$H_g = H_{\text{oil}} + H_{\text{case}}$$

### Component 1 – Heat carried away by the oil (by far the dominant term)

The oil enters at temperature *T_i* and leaves at *T_o*. The mass flow rate is *ρ Q*, where *Q* is the volumetric flow rate. With specific heat *C*:

$$H_{\text{oil}} = \rho \, Q \, C \, (T_o - T_i) \quad \text{(W)}$$

### Component 2 – Heat lost through the bearing housing (convection + radiation)

$$H_{\text{case}} = h_{cr} A_s (T_b - T_a)$$

where *h_cr* is the combined heat-transfer coefficient (≈ 17–35 W/m²·°C for natural convection in still air), *A_s* is the housing surface area, *T_b* is the bearing outer surface temperature, and *T_a* is the ambient.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU exam simplification:** The housing heat loss is often **neglected**, so:
> $$H_g \approx \rho Q C (T_o - T_i) = \rho Q C \, \Delta T$$

### Step 3 – Solve for ΔT

Equate frictional power to oil-cooling:

$$f W U_s = \rho Q C \, \Delta T$$

Rearrange:

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta T = \dfrac{f W U_s}{\rho Q C} = \dfrac{2 \pi r f W N_s}{\rho Q C}\;}
$$

Substitute *W = P · 2r · L* and *Q = (rc N_s L) · \bar{Q}*, where $\bar{Q} = Q/(rc N_s L)$ is the dimensionless **flow variable from R-B charts**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T &= \frac{2 \pi r f (2 P r L) N_s}{\rho \cdot (r c N_s L) \bar{Q} \cdot C} \\
        &= \frac{4 \pi P r^{2} f}{\rho c \bar{Q} C} \\
        &= \frac{4 \pi}{C \rho} \cdot \frac{\mu N_s}{P} \cdot \left( \frac{r}{c} \right)^{2} \cdot \frac{f \cdot P^{2} c}{\mu N_s r^{2} \bar{Q}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final, exam-ready form of the temperature-rise equation:**
> $$\boxed{\;\Delta T = \frac{1}{J} \cdot \left[ \frac{\mu N_s}{P} \left( \frac{r}{c} \right)^{2} \right] \cdot \frac{f \cdot (r/c)}{C \rho \bar{Q}} \cdot \frac{P}{1}\;}$$
>
> where the bracketed group is **the Sommerfeld number S**, and *J* is the mechanical equivalent of heat (= 1 in pure SI, 9.81 if heat in W and work in kgf·m/s).

**Simplified for KTU use (SI, J = 1):**

$$\Delta T = \frac{8.30 \cdot S \cdot f \cdot (r/c)}{\bar{Q}} \quad \text{with } \rho C \approx 1522 \text{ kJ/m}^{3}\text{°C for typical oil}$$

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (Analytical Matrix Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU-style):** A journal bearing has the following data: Load *W = 50 kN*, diameter *D = 100 mm*, speed *N = 1440 rpm*, L/D = 1, radial clearance ratio *c/r = 0.001*, viscosity at operating temperature *µ = 0.020 Pa·s*. Find: (i) Petroff's friction, (ii) Sommerfeld number, (iii) expected coefficient of friction from Raimondi–Boyd charts, (iv) power loss, (v) temperature rise for an oil flow of *Q = 1.5 L/min* per bearing. (Use ρC = 1750 × 870 ≈ 1.52 × 10⁶ J/m³·°C, J = 1.)

### (i) Petroff's coefficient of friction

Given:
- *W* = 50,000 N, *D* = 0.1 m, *L* = 0.1 m
- *N* = 1440 rpm = 24 rev/s → Nₛ = 24
- *r* = 0.05 m, *c* = 0.001 × 0.05 = 5 × 10⁻⁵ m
- *P* = *W/(L·D)* = 50000/(0.1 × 0.1) = 5 × 10⁶ Pa = **5 MPa**
- *µ* = 0.020 Pa·s

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_P &= \frac{2 \pi^{2} \mu r N_s}{P c} \\
    &= \frac{2 \pi^{2} (0.020)(0.05)(24)}{(5\times 10^{6})(5 \times 10^{-5})} \\
    &= \frac{2 \times 9.8696 \times 0.024}{250} \\
    &= \frac{0.4737}{250} \\
    &= 1.895 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✓ **f_P ≈ 0.00190 (dimensionless)**

### (ii) Sommerfeld number

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{\mu N_s}{P} \left( \frac{r}{c} \right)^{2} \\
  &= \frac{0.020 \times 24}{5\times 10^{6}} \times (0.05/5\times 10^{-5})^{2} \\
  &= 9.6\times 10^{-8} \times (1000)^{2} \\
  &= 0.096
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✓ **S ≈ 0.096**

### (iii) Coefficient of friction from Raimondi–Boyd

For *S = 0.096*, the R-B chart gives (interpolating):
- (r/c)·f ≈ 3.32
- So f = 3.32 / 1000 = **0.00332**
- ε ≈ 0.40
- h₀/c ≈ 0.60
- φ ≈ 70°

> ✓ **f ≈ 0.0033** (real film friction — higher than Petroff because of side leakage and shearing in the converging wedge).

### (iv) Frictional power

$$
P_f = f \cdot W \cdot U_s = f \cdot W \cdot 2\pi r N_s
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_f &= 0.0033 \times 50{,}000 \times 2 \pi (0.05)(24) \\
    &= 0.0033 \times 50{,}000 \times 7.5398 \\
    &= 1244 \text{ W} \approx 1.24 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

### (v) Temperature rise

Given *Q* = 1.5 L/min = 1.5 × 10⁻³ / 60 = **2.5 × 10⁻⁵ m³/s**.

$$
\Delta T = \frac{P_f}{\rho C Q} = \frac{1244}{(1.52 \times 10^{6})(2.5\times 10^{-5})}
$$

$$
\Delta T = \frac{1244}{38.0} = 32.7 \text{ °C}
$$

> ✓ **ΔT ≈ 32.7 °C** — well within the typical 40 °C limit for hydrodynamic bearings.

---

## 3.6 Summary of Standard Design Procedure (Analytical Matrix)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU-codified design procedure for a journal bearing is:**

**Step 1** — Compute the *bearing pressure*:
$$P = \frac{W}{L \cdot D}$$

**Step 2** — Choose the *L/D* ratio (KTU: 0.5 to 1.5; common: 1.0).

**Step 3** — Choose a *trial viscosity* µ at the *expected operating temperature* (typically T_op = 75 – 90 °C).

**Step 4** — Assume a *clearance ratio c/r* (typical: 0.001).

**Step 5** — Compute the *Sommerfeld number*:
$$S = \frac{\mu N_s}{P} \left( \frac{r}{c} \right)^{2}$$

**Step 6** — Read the **four Raimondi–Boyd variables** (f, ε, φ, h₀/c) from the design charts at this *S*.

**Step 7** — Check the **minimum film thickness**:
$$h_0 = c(1 - \varepsilon) \ge 2.5 \text{ to } 5 \,\mu\text{m (rule of thumb)}$$

**Step 8** — Compute the **frictional power loss**:
$$P_f = f \, W \, U_s$$

**Step 9** — Compute the **oil flow** needed (from R-B chart) and the **temperature rise**:
$$\Delta T = \frac{P_f}{\rho \, C \, Q}$$

**Step 10** — If ΔT > 40 °C, **reduce viscosity** (use a lighter oil), **increase oil flow**, or **increase clearance** and **re-iterate from Step 3**.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bearing Geometry – Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["APPLIED LOAD W (downward)"] --> nodeB["BUSH CENTER Ob"]
    nodeC["JOURNAL CENTER Oj (offset by e)"] --> nodeD["FILM PRESSURE DISTRIBUTION p theta"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["INTEGRATED LOAD = W"]
    nodeB --> nodeF["ATTITUDE ANGLE phi"]
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG["LINE OF CENTERS"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["MIN FILM THICKNESS h0"]
    nodeI["JOURNAL ROTATION Ns"] --> nodeJ["WEDGE ACTION"]
    nodeJ --> nodeD
    nodeH --> nodeK["CHECK h0 = c 1 minus epsilon"]
    nodeL["LUBRICANT INLET Ti"] --> nodeM["OIL LEAKS SIDEWAYS"]
    nodeM --> nodeN["OIL OUTLET To"]
    nodeN --> nodeO["TEMPERATURE RISE DELTA T"]
    nodeD --> nodeP["FRICTION f W Us"]
    nodeP --> nodeO
    nodeO --> nodeQ{"DELTA T within 40 C"}
    nodeQ -->|yes| nodeR["DESIGN ACCEPTABLE"]
    nodeQ -->|no| nodeS["REDUCE MU OR INCREASE Q"]
    nodeS --> nodeJ
```

## 4.2 Pressure Distribution Around the Journal

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["theta 0 deg  Max pressure zone"] --> nodeB["theta 90 deg  Pressure falls"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["theta 180 deg  Film diverges, sub atmospheric pressure"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["theta 270 deg  Cavitation region"]
    nodeD --> nodeA
    nodeA --> nodeE["phi  Attitude angle  Load line to line of centers"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Positive pressure zone covers 180 deg plus phi"]
```

## 4.3 Design Flow – Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sA["INPUT  W  N  D"] --> sB["SELECT L over D"]
    sB --> sC["COMPUTE P  W over L D"]
    sC --> sD["ASSUME c over r and mu at Top"]
    sD --> sE["COMPUTE SOMMERFELD S"]
    sE --> sF["RAIMONDI BOYD CHARTS  read f  epsilon  phi  h0 over c"]
    sF --> sG{"h0 above 2.5 micron"}
    sG -->|yes| sH["COMPUTE POWER LOSS Pf"]
    sG -->|no| sI["REDUCE c over r and REPEAT"]
    sH --> sJ["COMPUTE DELTA T"]
    sJ --> sK{"DELTA T below 40 C"}
    sK -->|yes| sL["DESIGN FINAL"]
    sK -->|no| sM["REDUCE mu or INCREASE Q and REPEAT"]
    sI --> sE
    sM --> sE
```

```mermaid
        nodeX["Inner radius R Bush center Ob"]
    end

        nodeY["Outer radius r Journal center Oj offset by e from Ob"]
    end

        nodeZ["Lubricant film h theta  c times 1 plus epsilon cos theta"]
    end

    FILM --- nodeY

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Dec 2023 style]**

### Q1. Define a journal bearing. List its types based on the lubrication mechanism.

**Model Answer:**

> A *journal bearing* is a **plain cylindrical-sleeve bearing** in which a rotating shaft (journal) is supported by a thin **lubricant film** contained in the clearance space between the journal and the stationary bush. The load is transmitted hydrodynamically through the pressurised oil film with no direct metal-to-metal contact.
>
> **Types based on lubrication mechanism:**
> 1. **Hydrodynamic (full-film) bearings** – pressure generated by the motion of the journal drawing oil into a converging wedge. These include *full*, *partial*, *flooded*, *ring-oiled*, and *wick-oiled* variants.
> 2. **Boundary-lubricated bearings** – oil film discontinuous; surfaces separated by adsorbed molecular layers.
> 3. **Mixed-film bearings** – combination of hydrodynamic and boundary regimes.
> 4. **Dry / solid-lubricated bearings** – for high-temperature or vacuum service.
>
> For KTU Module 4, the focus is on **hydrodynamic full journal bearings**.

### Q2. What is the Sommerfeld number? Why is it important in hydrodynamic bearing design?

**Model Answer:**

> The **Sommerfeld number (S)** is a *dimensionless parameter* that groups all the principal operating variables of a hydrodynamic journal bearing into a single number:
>
> $$S = \frac{\mu N_{s}}{P} \left( \frac{r}{c} \right)^{2}$$
>
> where µ is lubricant viscosity, Nₛ is the journal speed in rev/s, P is the bearing pressure, r is the journal radius, and c is the radial clearance.
>
> **Importance:**
> - It links the *frictional*, *load-carrying*, *flow*, and *attitude-angle* behaviour of the bearing through the **Raimondi–Boyd design charts**.
> - For a given *S*, the eccentricity ratio, coefficient of friction, attitude angle, and oil flow can be read off the charts without solving the Reynolds equation each time.
> - It enables **direct comparison of bearings** of different sizes, speeds, and lubricants on a common scale.
> - It is the *single governing parameter* of the entire design — change µ, N, P, or c, and S changes; the bearing's behaviour changes accordingly.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module 4 Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B questions on journal bearings carry 14 marks, typically split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. The two alternatives below are mutually independent — answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**A 100 mm diameter shaft runs in a full journal bearing at 1500 rpm. The bearing length is 100 mm, the radial clearance is 0.05 mm, and the lubricant viscosity at operating temperature is 0.020 Pa·s. The bearing carries a load of 30 kN.**

**(a)** Calculate Petroff's coefficient of friction and the Sommerfeld number. Comment on the operating eccentricity ratio and the minimum film thickness. (7 marks)

**(b)** Using the Raimondi–Boyd design data for the calculated Sommerfeld number (given in the data table below), determine the **true coefficient of friction**, the **attitude angle**, the **oil flow rate**, and the **steady-state temperature rise** of the oil. State the assumptions made. (7 marks)

**Raimondi–Boyd design data (use if standard charts/tables are not provided in the paper):**

| S | (r/c)·f | ε | φ (deg) | h₀/c | Q/(rcNₛL) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.06 | 2.60 | 0.40 | 70.2 | 0.60 | 3.34 |
| 0.08 | 2.83 | 0.43 | 69.4 | 0.57 | 3.51 |
| **0.10** | **3.04** | **0.46** | **68.6** | **0.54** | **3.66** |
| 0.12 | 3.24 | 0.48 | 67.8 | 0.52 | 3.79 |
| 0.14 | 3.43 | 0.50 | 67.0 | 0.50 | 3.92 |

(Assume ρC = 1.52 × 10⁶ J/m³·°C and J = 1.)

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**Given data:**

- D = 0.1 m, r = 0.05 m, L = 0.1 m
- c = 0.05 mm = 5 × 10⁻⁵ m
- N = 1500 rpm → Nₛ = 25 rev/s
- W = 30,000 N, µ = 0.020 Pa·s
- Bearing pressure: P = W/(L·D) = 30,000/(0.1 × 0.1) = **3 × 10⁶ Pa = 3 MPa**

### (a) Petroff's friction and Sommerfeld number

**Petroff's coefficient of friction:**

$$
f_P = \frac{2 \pi^{2} \mu r N_s}{P c} = \frac{2 \pi^{2} (0.020)(0.05)(25)}{(3 \times 10^{6})(5 \times 10^{-5})}
$$

$$
f_P = \frac{2 \times 9.8696 \times 0.025}{150} = \frac{0.4935}{150} = 3.29 \times 10^{-3}
$$

> ✓ **f_P ≈ 0.00329**

**Sommerfeld number:**

$$
S = \frac{\mu N_s}{P} \left( \frac{r}{c} \right)^{2} = \frac{0.020 \times 25}{3 \times 10^{6}} \times \left( \frac{0.05}{5 \times 10^{-5}} \right)^{2}
$$

$$
S = 1.667 \times 10^{-7} \times (1000)^{2} = 0.1667
$$

> ✓ **S ≈ 0.167** — lies between the 0.14 and 0.20 entries of the R-B table (interpolate).

**Eccentricity ratio and minimum film thickness** (interpolating between S = 0.14 and S = 0.20):
- For S = 0.14: ε = 0.50, h₀/c = 0.50
- For S = 0.20: ε ≈ 0.53, h₀/c ≈ 0.47 (approximate)

Linear interpolation at S = 0.167:

$$
\varepsilon = 0.50 + \frac{(0.167 - 0.14)}{0.20 - 0.14} \times (0.53 - 0.50) = 0.50 + 0.45 \times 0.03 = 0.514
$$

$$
h_0 = c (1 - \varepsilon) = (5 \times 10^{-5})(1 - 0.514) = 2.43 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 24.3 \; \mu\text{m}
$$

> ✓ **ε ≈ 0.51, h₀ ≈ 24 µm** — well above the 2.5–5 µm minimum-film-thickness rule of thumb, so the bearing is **safely operating in the full-film regime**.

### (b) True friction, attitude angle, flow, and temperature rise

**True coefficient of friction** (interpolating the (r/c)·f column at S = 0.167):

$$
(r/c) \cdot f = 3.43 + \frac{0.027}{0.06} \times (3.62 - 3.43) \approx 3.43 + 0.45 \times 0.19 = 3.52
$$

(Using r/c = 1000 here, so): $f = 3.52/1000 = $ **0.00352**

> ✓ **f ≈ 0.00352**

**Attitude angle** (interpolating at S = 0.167):
$$
\phi = 67.0 + 0.45 \times (66.2 - 67.0) = 67.0 - 0.36 = 66.6^{\circ}
$$

> ✓ **φ ≈ 66.6°**

**Oil flow rate:**

$$
Q = (r c N_s L) \times \bar{Q} = (0.05)(5\times 10^{-5})(25)(0.1) \times \bar{Q}
$$

Interpolating the flow variable: $\bar{Q} = 3.92 + 0.45 \times (4.05 - 3.92) = 3.98$

$$
Q = (6.25 \times 10^{-6}) \times 3.98 = 2.49 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{3}\text{/s} = 1.49 \text{ L/min}
$$

> ✓ **Q ≈ 1.49 L/min**

**Frictional power loss:**

$$
P_f = f \, W \, U_s = f \, W \, 2 \pi r N_s = 0.00352 \times 30{,}000 \times 2\pi (0.05)(25)
$$

$$
P_f = 0.00352 \times 30{,}000 \times 7.854 = 829 \text{ W} \approx 0.83 \text{ kW}
$$

**Temperature rise:**

$$
\Delta T = \frac{P_f}{\rho C Q} = \frac{829}{(1.52 \times 10^{6})(2.49 \times 10^{-5})} = \frac{829}{37.85} = 21.9 \text{ °C}
$$

> ✓ **ΔT ≈ 21.9 °C** — comfortably below 40 °C. The bearing is **thermally safe**.

**Assumptions made:**
1. Steady-state, isoviscous (constant µ) analysis.
2. Neglect housing heat loss.
3. Laminar flow, no turbulence, Newtonian lubricant.
4. Inertia terms neglected (valid for *N < 5000 rpm*).
5. No slip at the journal and bush surfaces.
6. Standard L/D = 1.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**A journal bearing has the following data:** Diameter *D = 75 mm*, length *L = 75 mm*, speed *N = 1800 rpm*, radial clearance *c = 0.0375 mm*, viscosity *µ = 0.018 Pa·s*, load *W = 20 kN*, and the oil density–specific-heat product is *ρC = 1.6 × 10⁶ J/m³·°C*.

**(a)** Determine the **Sommerfeld number** and hence read off the **Raimondi–Boyd design variables** (ε, φ, h₀/c, f, flow variable). Comment on the design's safety with respect to **minimum film thickness**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Calculate the **actual volumetric flow rate** of oil, the **frictional power loss**, the **temperature rise**, and hence verify the thermal design. Suggest a modification if ΔT exceeds 30 °C. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**Given:**
- D = 0.075 m, r = 0.0375 m, L = 0.075 m
- c = 0.0375 mm = 3.75 × 10⁻⁵ m
- N = 1800 rpm → Nₛ = 30 rev/s
- W = 20,000 N, µ = 0.018 Pa·s
- Bearing pressure: P = W/(L·D) = 20,000/(0.075 × 0.075) = **3.556 × 10⁶ Pa**

### (a) Sommerfeld number and design variables

$$
S = \frac{\mu N_s}{P} \left( \frac{r}{c} \right)^{2} = \frac{0.018 \times 30}{3.556 \times 10^{6}} \times \left( \frac{0.0375}{3.75 \times 10^{-5}} \right)^{2}
$$

$$
S = 1.519 \times 10^{-7} \times (1000)^{2} = 0.152
$$

> ✓ **S ≈ 0.152**

Reading the R-B charts at S = 0.152 (interpolating between the S = 0.14 and S = 0.16 rows of any standard R-B table):

| Variable | Value |
|---|---|
| (r/c)·f | ≈ 3.55 |
| ε | ≈ 0.51 |
| φ | ≈ 66.5° |
| h₀/c | ≈ 0.49 |
| $\bar{Q} = Q/(rcN_sL)$ | ≈ 4.00 |

**True coefficient of friction:**

$$
f = \frac{(r/c)\cdot f}{r/c} = \frac{3.55}{1000} = 0.00355
$$

**Minimum film thickness:**

$$
h_0 = c(1 - \varepsilon) = (3.75 \times 10^{-5})(1 - 0.51) = 1.84 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 18.4 \; \mu\text{m}
$$

> ✓ **h₀ ≈ 18.4 µm** — much greater than the 2.5–5 µm rule-of-thumb. The bearing is **safely in the full-film regime**.

### (b) Oil flow, friction loss, temperature rise, design check

**Oil flow rate:**

$$
Q = (r c N_s L) \bar{Q} = (0.0375)(3.75 \times 10^{-5})(30)(0.075)(4.00)
$$

$$
Q = (3.164 \times 10^{-6}) \times 4.00 = 1.266 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{3}\text{/s} = 0.76 \text{ L/min}
$$

> ✓ **Q ≈ 0.76 L/min**

**Frictional power:**

$$
P_f = f \, W \, 2 \pi r N_s = 0.00355 \times 20{,}000 \times 2 \pi (0.0375)(30)
$$

$$
P_f = 0.00355 \times 20{,}000 \times 7.069 = 502 \text{ W} \approx 0.50 \text{ kW}
$$

> ✓ **P_f ≈ 0.50 kW**

**Temperature rise:**

$$
\Delta T = \frac{P_f}{\rho C Q} = \frac{502}{(1.6 \times 10^{6})(1.266 \times 10^{-5})} = \frac{502}{20.25} = 24.8 \text{ °C}
$$

> ✓ **ΔT ≈ 24.8 °C** — well below the 30 °C threshold. The thermal design is **acceptable**.

**Modifications if ΔT > 30 °C** *(for completeness, even though not required here):*
- Use an oil with **lower viscosity** at operating temperature (lighter grade).
- **Increase oil supply rate** Q by enlarging the oil-feed groove or using forced circulation.
- **Reduce L/D** slightly (decreases friction surface) or **increase L** to lower pressure (must check ε simultaneously).
- **Increase clearance** *c* by re-choosing bush diameter — but watch for the resulting drop in *S* and rise in eccentricity.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks on journal-bearing problems:**
>
> 1. **Speed units confusion (–2 marks):** KTU papers often give N in **rpm** but the formula needs **rev/s** or **rad/s**. Forgetting to divide by 60 is the most common error.
>
> 2. **Using Petroff's friction instead of true friction in the temperature-rise equation (–2 marks):** Petroff's f_P is the *concentric, no-side-leakage* approximation. The *true* friction f (from R-B charts) is **always higher** for the same S, and that is the value to use in $\Delta T = fWU_s/(\rho C Q)$.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the r/c squared term in S (–1 mark):** $S = (\mu N_s/P)(r/c)^2$ — missing the *square* on the clearance ratio gives wrong S, wrong ε, wrong f, wrong ΔT.
>
> 4. **Mixing up radial and diametral clearance (–1 mark):** *c* is the **radial** clearance. The diametral clearance is *C_d = 2c*. Some texts use diametral — read the question carefully.
>
> 5. **Neglecting to state units or to convert viscosity (–1 mark):** If the problem gives µ in poise, convert to Pa·s (× 0.1) before plugging into the Sommerfeld equation.
>
> 6. **No final check on h₀ and ΔT (–1 mark):** Even if the numbers come out "right", the examiner expects an explicit *comment* on whether the design is safe (h₀ > 2.5 µm, ΔT < 40 °C). Missing the comment = losing at least one mark.
>
> 7. **Skipping the assumption list (–1 mark):** KTU 14-mark questions award 1 mark for *clearly stating assumptions* (steady state, isoviscous, laminar, housing heat loss neglected, etc.).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this 5 minutes before the exam. Memorise the boxed equations.**

### Core vocabulary
- [ ] **Journal** = shaft; **Bush / Sleeve** = stationary outer member; **Bearing** = the complete assembly.
- [ ] **Radial clearance c** = R − r; **Diameter clearance C_d = 2c**.
- [ ] **Eccentricity e** = distance between journal and bush centres.
- [ ] **Eccentricity ratio ε = e/c**, 0 ≤ ε ≤ 1.
- [ ] **Min film thickness h₀ = c(1 − ε)**.
- [ ] **Attitude angle φ** = angle between load line and line of centres.

### Petroff essentials
- [ ] Petroff is valid only for **ε = 0, infinitely long bearing, no side leakage**.
- [ ] $T_f = 4\pi^2 \mu r^3 L N_s / c$.
- [ ] $f_P = 2\pi^2 \mu r N_s / (P c)$.
- [ ] Petroff's load capacity is *zero*; it is used only to estimate friction at low ε.

### Sommerfeld number
- [ ] $S = (\mu N_s / P)(r/c)^2 = (r/c)\cdot f_P/(2\pi^2)$.
- [ ] S is the **only** parameter that links all operating variables.
- [ ] High S → high viscosity or low load or small clearance → high load capacity, low ε, large h₀.

### Raimondi–Boyd chart read-off (the four variables at a given S)
- [ ] (r/c)·f → true coefficient of friction f
- [ ] ε → operating eccentricity ratio
- [ ] φ → attitude angle (in degrees)
- [ ] h₀/c → minimum film thickness ratio
- [ ] $\bar{Q} = Q/(rcN_sL)$ → dimensionless flow variable

### Thermal design
- [ ] $P_f = f W U_s = f W \cdot 2\pi r N_s$ (use TRUE f, not f_P).
- [ ] $H_g = H_{oil} + H_{case} \approx H_{oil}$.
- [ ] $\Delta T = P_f / (\rho C Q)$.
- [ ] Design is **safe** if ΔT ≤ 40 °C and h₀ ≥ 2.5–5 µm.

### Design procedure
- [ ] **Step 1:** W, N, D given. **Step 2:** Choose L/D. **Step 3:** P = W/(LD). **Step 4:** Assume µ and c/r. **Step 5:** Compute S. **Step 6:** Read R-B. **Step 7:** Check h₀. **Step 8:** Compute P_f. **Step 9:** Compute ΔT. **Step 10:** Iterate if ΔT > 40 °C.

### Common KTU exam-ready constants
- [ ] ρC for typical oil ≈ **1.52 × 10⁶ J/m³·°C** (ρ = 870 kg/m³, C = 1750 J/kg·°C).
- [ ] J = 1 in pure SI; J = 9.81 in mixed SI/CGS.
- [ ] Recommended L/D: **0.5 to 1.5**, common value 1.0.
- [ ] Typical c/r: **0.001 to 0.002**.
- [ ] Safe h₀: **≥ 2.5 to 5 µm** depending on surface finish.

### Critical exam pitfalls (already covered above) — re-read before submission.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Mantra for the KTU board exam:** *Petroff for friction estimate at ε ≈ 0; **Sommerfeld to design; Raimondi–Boyd to read; f·W·U_s for power; ΔT ≤ 40 °C; h₀ ≥ 2.5 µm.** Repeat until fluent.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrodynamic Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT521 - DESIGN OF TRANSMISSION ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hydrodynamic Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hydrodynamic Theory** of lubrication is the foundational theoretical framework that explains the generation of a continuous, pressurized fluid film between two relatively moving surfaces (typically a journal rotating inside a bearing), without any direct surface-to-surface contact. The pressure required to support the applied load is generated entirely by the **viscous shearing** of the lubricant as it is dragged into the converging clearance space by the motion of the moving surface.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Design of Transmission Elements*, Hydrodynamic Theory is treated as the analytical backbone used to predict:
- **Pressure distribution** within the lubricant film
- **Load-carrying capacity** of the bearing
- **Coefficient of friction** and power loss
- **Film thickness**, **eccentricity ratio**, and **attitude angle**

The governing mathematical relationship is the famous **Reynolds Equation** (1886), derived from the Navier–Stokes equations combined with the continuity equation for an incompressible, viscous lubricant film.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Hydrodynamic Theory is the prerequisite for the design of journal bearings, slider bearings, and thrust bearings. Numerical problems based on the **Sommerfeld Number** and **Petroff's Equation** are compulsory in Part B (14-mark) questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Water Skater**
> Imagine pushing a flat-bottomed boat across still water with one hand. If you push *very slowly*, water escapes from underneath and the hull scrapes the lakebed (boundary lubrication / mixed lubrication — wear occurs). But if you push *fast enough*, the boat rides up on a wedge of water and *skates* across the surface without ever touching the bottom. That wedge of water is your hydrodynamic film. The faster you move, the more water gets dragged in (just like oil), and the higher the boat is lifted.

In a **journal bearing**, the rotating shaft drags oil into the converging wedge-shaped gap (where clearance $c$ decreases from maximum to minimum). Because oil cannot instantaneously squeeze out, a pressure builds up — this pressure acts like an invisible "water bed" that lifts the shaft, prevents metal-to-metal contact, and carries the radial load.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Metrics (KTU Standard)

- **Dynamic viscosity of oil, μ** — typically expressed in **Pa·s** (or N·s/m²) at operating temperature.
- **Kinematic viscosity, ν = μ/ρ** — usually given in **cSt** (centistokes) or **mm²/s** at a reference temperature (often **40 °C** or **50 °C**).
- **Specific gravity of oil** ≈ **0.85–0.95** (dimensionless).
- **Radial clearance, c** = $D - d$ over **2** = $R - r$ (in mm).
- **Clearance ratio, ψ** = $c/r$ — typically **0.001** to **0.002** for hydrodynamic bearings.
- **Absolute viscosity, μ** in **reyns** (1 reyn = 6895 Pa·s) is sometimes used in older textbooks; standard SI units are recommended by KTU.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Foundational Hypotheses of Hydrodynamic Theory

For a thin lubricant film in a journal bearing, the following assumptions are universally adopted:

1. **Laminar flow** — No turbulence (Reynolds number of flow < 2000 inside the film).
2. **Negligible inertial forces** — Viscous forces dominate; body forces (gravity) and inertia are small compared to the viscous shear stresses.
3. **No-slip condition** — The oil adheres to both the journal surface and the bearing surface (Continuum assumption holds; film is continuous).
4. **Constant viscosity** — The lubricant is treated as **isoviscous** (Newtonian) throughout the film in classical theory. (Thermal effects are neglected.)
5. **Thin-film approximation** — Film thickness $h$ is negligibly small compared to the bearing radius $R$; thus, curvature is ignored and pressure is assumed constant across the film thickness.

### 2.2 Film Geometry — The Heart of Hydrodynamic Theory

Let:
- $R$ = radius of the bearing (m)
- $r$ = radius of the journal (shaft)
- $c = R - r$ = radial clearance (m)
- $e$ = eccentricity (distance between bearing center $O_b$ and journal center $O_j$)
- $\theta$ = angular position measured from the line of centers (from maximum film thickness point)
- $\phi$ = attitude angle (angle between the line of action of load $W$ and the line of centers)

The **film thickness** at any angular position $\theta$ (measured from the position of *maximum film thickness*) is:

$$h = c + e \cos\theta$$

Or equivalently, if measured from the position of *minimum film thickness* (most common convention):

$$h = c \left(1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta\right)$$

where $\varepsilon = e/c$ is the **eccentricity ratio** ($0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$).

- $\varepsilon = 0$ → perfectly concentric (no load capacity).
- $\varepsilon = 1$ → journal touches bearing (boundary condition).

### 2.3 Petroff's Equation — The Simplest Hydrodynamic Model

Petroff (1883) assumed a **completely concentric** shaft (no load eccentricity) but with the shaft rotating. The friction drag on the bearing surface due to viscous shear gives:

$$F_f = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N L r^3}{c}$$

$$T_f = F_f \cdot r = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N L r^4}{c}$$

The **coefficient of friction** from Petroff's equation is:

$$f = \frac{2\pi^2 \mu N r}{P} \cdot \left(\frac{r}{c}\right) = 2\pi^2 \left(\frac{\mu N}{P}\right) \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)$$

where $P = W / (2rL)$ is the **bearing pressure** projected on the projected area $2rL$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Petroff's equation underestimates the actual friction because it ignores the load-carrying wedge effect (no eccentricity). However, it is the **first step** in KTU problems and is used to verify friction values at low eccentricity ratios.

### 2.4 Reynolds Equation (1886) — The Master Equation

For a journal bearing with the thin-film assumptions, Reynolds derived:

$$\frac{dp}{d\theta} = \frac{6 \mu \omega r}{h^3}\left(\int_0^{\theta} \frac{\partial h}{\partial \theta}\, d\theta - \text{average flow term}\right)$$

The integrated, **classical form** of the Reynolds equation for a journal bearing is:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{h^3}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(\frac{h^3}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\right) = 6 U \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$$

For a journal bearing (1D simplification along the circumferential direction $\theta$):

$$\frac{d}{d\theta}\left(h^3 \frac{dp}{d\theta}\right) = -6 \mu \omega r^2 \frac{dh}{d\theta}$$

After substituting $h = c(1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta)$ and applying boundary conditions ($p = 0$ at film termination angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$), one arrives at:

$$p(\theta) = \frac{6 \mu \omega r^2 \varepsilon \sin\theta}{c^2 (1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta)^2} \cdot \left[\text{correction factor}\right]$$

The **exact pressure distribution** (Hays, 1959 — used in modern textbooks):

$$p = \frac{6 \mu \omega r^2}{c^2} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon \sin\theta \left(2 + \varepsilon \cos\theta\right)}{(2 + \varepsilon^2)(1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta)^2}$$

This equation is **the starting point** for every Sommerfeld-based KTU problem.

### 2.5 Sommerfeld Number — The Dimensionless Load Parameter

The **Sommerfeld Number** is a dimensionless number that groups bearing geometry, viscosity, speed, and load. It is the most important design parameter in hydrodynamic journal bearing design:

$$S = \frac{r^2 N \mu}{W} \cdot \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 \cdot 2 \quad \text{(varies by convention)}$$

The most common KTU textbook form (Khurmi & Gupta / V.B. Bhandari convention):

$$S_o = \frac{\mu N}{P} \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 = \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{1}{\psi^2}$$

where $P = W/(2rL)$ = average bearing pressure, and $\psi = c/r$ = clearance ratio.

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **SI Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Radial clearance | $c$ | $R - r$ | m |
| Clearance ratio | $\psi$ | $c / r$ | dimensionless |
| Eccentricity ratio | $\varepsilon$ | $e / c$ | dimensionless |
| Film thickness | $h$ | $c(1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta)$ | m |
| Petroff friction torque | $T_f$ | $\dfrac{4\pi^2 \mu N L r^4}{c}$ | N·m |
| Petroff coefficient of friction | $f$ | $\dfrac{2\pi^2 \mu N}{P} \cdot \dfrac{r}{c}$ | dimensionless |
| Pressure distribution | $p(\theta)$ | $\dfrac{6\mu \omega r^2}{c^2}\dfrac{\varepsilon\sin\theta(2+\varepsilon\cos\theta)}{(2+\varepsilon^2)(1+\varepsilon\cos\theta)^2}$ | Pa |
| Sommerfeld number (Bhandari) | $S_o$ | $\dfrac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\psi^2}$ | dimensionless |
| Sommerfeld number (Shigley) | $S$ | $\dfrac{r^2 N \mu}{P} \cdot 2$ (with $\psi$) | dimensionless |
| Load capacity (integrated) | $W$ | $\dfrac{6 \mu \omega r^3 L}{c^2} \cdot \dfrac{\varepsilon \pi \sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^2}}{(2 + \varepsilon^2)\sqrt{1}}$ | N |
| Coefficient of friction (approx.) | $f$ | $\dfrac{r}{c}(2\pi^2 \mu N / W)(r) + 0.5 \cdot \varepsilon \cdot \psi$ | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Conversion:** The coefficient of friction in hydrodynamic bearings is often given as: $f \approx \dfrac{r}{c}(2\pi^2 \mu N / W) + 0.5\varepsilon\psi$ (Petroff + wedge correction). For purely Petroff friction, use only the first term. **Read the question carefully!**

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Turbochargers and IC engine crankshafts** — operate entirely on hydrodynamic films.
- **Electric motor rotors** — hydrodynamic sleeve bearings are replacing rolling element bearings for noise reduction.
- **Hydroelectric turbines** — massive hydrodynamic thrust bearings carry vertical loads of thousands of tonnes.
- **Precision machine tool spindles** — controlled hydrodynamic film thickness governs machining accuracy.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Petroff's Friction Torque

**Step 1 — Geometry of concentric journal.**

Consider a journal of radius $r$ rotating concentrically inside a bearing of radius $R$, separated by a thin lubricant film of constant thickness $c = R - r$. The journal rotates at $N$ rps (rev/s). The angular velocity is $\omega = 2\pi N$ rad/s.

**Step 2 — Velocity profile.**

For a thin Newtonian film with no pressure gradient along the circumferential direction (concentric assumption), the Couette velocity profile is linear. The velocity at any radial distance $y$ from the bearing surface is:

$$u(y) = U \left(1 - \frac{y}{c}\right)$$

where $U = \omega r$ is the surface velocity of the journal.

**Step 3 — Shear stress in the film.**

By Newton's law of viscosity:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = \mu \cdot \left(-\frac{U}{c}\right)$$

The magnitude of shear stress on the bearing surface (at $y = 0$):

$$\vert \tau \vert = \frac{\mu \omega r}{c}$$

**Step 4 — Friction force.**

Integrating the shear stress over the entire bearing surface area $A = 2\pi r L$:

$$F_f = \tau \cdot A = \frac{\mu \omega r}{c} \cdot (2\pi r L) = \frac{2\pi \mu \omega r^2 L}{c}$$

Substituting $\omega = 2\pi N$:

$$F_f = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^2 L}{c}$$

**Step 5 — Friction torque.**

The friction torque acts at the journal surface of radius $r$:

$$T_f = F_f \cdot r = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^3 L \cdot r}{c} = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N L r^4}{c} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 6 — Coefficient of friction.**

Using projected bearing area $A_p = 2rL$ and bearing pressure $P = W / (2rL)$:

$$f = \frac{F_f}{W} = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^2 L / c}{P \cdot 2rL} = \frac{2\pi^2 \mu N r}{P \cdot c}$$

Rearranging:

$$f = 2\pi^2 \left(\frac{\mu N}{P}\right) \left(\frac{r}{c}\right) \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Sommerfeld Number from the Reynolds Equation

**Step 1 — Set up the geometry.**

With $\varepsilon = e/c$, $h = c(1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta)$. The journal surface velocity is $U = \omega r$.

**Step 2 — Start with the Reynolds equation in 1D.**

$$\frac{d}{d\theta}\left(h^3 \frac{dp}{d\theta}\right) = -6 \mu \omega r^2 \frac{dh}{d\theta}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $h = c(1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta)$.**

Let $\beta = 1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta$. Then $h = c\beta$ and $dh/d\theta = -c\varepsilon \sin\theta$.

The equation becomes:

$$\frac{d}{d\theta}\left(c^3 \beta^3 \frac{dp}{d\theta}\right) = 6 \mu \omega r^2 \cdot c \varepsilon \sin\theta$$

Dividing both sides by $c^3$:

$$\frac{d}{d\theta}\left(\beta^3 \frac{dp}{d\theta}\right) = \frac{6 \mu \omega r^2 \varepsilon \sin\theta}{c^2}$$

**Step 4 — First integration.**

Integrating from $0$ to $\theta$:

$$\beta^3 \frac{dp}{d\theta} = -\frac{6 \mu \omega r^2 \varepsilon \cos\theta}{c^2} + C_1$$

**Step 5 — Apply boundary conditions.**

Boundary condition 1: pressure is finite, so $dp/d\theta$ is finite everywhere.
Boundary condition 2: $p = 0$ at film start ($\theta_1$) and end ($\theta_2$).

For symmetry around the maximum pressure position (which occurs roughly at $\theta = \pi/2$ in the converging zone), integrating the equation across one full cycle gives the constant $C_1$:

$$C_1 = \frac{6 \mu \omega r^2 \varepsilon}{c^2(2 + \varepsilon^2)}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $p(\theta)$.**

After substituting back and integrating once more, we obtain the **Hays pressure distribution**:

$$p = \frac{6 \mu \omega r^2}{c^2} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon \sin\theta(2 + \varepsilon \cos\theta)}{(2 + \varepsilon^2)(1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta)^2} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 7 — Integrate pressure to obtain load.**

The vertical component of the pressure force balances the load $W$. The total load on the upper half of the bearing (length $L$, integral of $p \cos(\theta - \phi) d\theta dL$):

$$W = \frac{6 \mu \omega r^3 L}{c^2} \cdot \frac{\pi \varepsilon}{(2 + \varepsilon^2)\sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^2}}$$

(The exact integral form of the Sommerfeld relationship.)

**Step 8 — Define the Sommerfeld number.**

Dividing both sides by $P \cdot 2rL$ (where $P = W/2rL$) and rearranging:

$$S_o = \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 = \frac{(2 + \varepsilon^2)\sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^2}}{3\pi \varepsilon} \quad \blacksquare$$

This is the **inverse relationship**: a known load and geometry give $\varepsilon$; conversely, knowing $S_o$ allows us to solve for $\varepsilon$ by trial and error (or iteration).

### 3.3 KTU Analytical Matrix — Common Problem Setups

| **Given** | **To Find** | **Approach** |
|---|---|---|
| $W, N, r, c, L, \mu$ | Load capacity ratio $\varepsilon$ | Compute $S_o$, solve $\dfrac{(2+\varepsilon^2)\sqrt{1-\varepsilon^2}}{3\pi\varepsilon} = S_o$ iteratively |
| $S_o, \varepsilon, \psi$ | Coefficient of friction $f$ | Use $f = \dfrac{r}{c}\left(\dfrac{2\pi^2 \mu N}{W}\right) + 0.5\varepsilon\psi$ |
| Petroff model parameters | Torque $T_f$ | Direct: $T_f = \dfrac{4\pi^2 \mu N L r^4}{c}$ |
| Power loss $P_{loss}$ | Heat dissipation | $P_{loss} = T_f \cdot \omega = 2\pi N T_f$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hydrodynamic Film Formation — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Journal Rotating at Angular Velocity omega] --> B[Oil Adheres to Journal Surface - No Slip Condition]
    B --> C[Oil Dragged into Converging Wedge Gap h theta]
    C --> D[Pressure Built Up Due to Viscosity and Continuity Constraint]
    D --> E{Reynolds Equation Solved p theta}
    E --> F[Pressure Distribution Computed]
    F --> G[Load Carrying Capacity W Calculated]
    F --> H[Film Thickness h epsilon theta Computed]
    G --> I[Bearing Performance Validated]
    H --> I
    I --> J{Is epsilon within 0.3 to 0.8}
    J -->|Yes| K[Safe Hydrodynamic Operation]
    J -->|No| L[Redesign Clearance or Viscosity]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of Hydrodynamic Bearing Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[Input Bearing Geometry L D d] --> PROC1
    IN2[Input Operating Conditions W N T] --> PROC1
    IN3[Input Lubricant Properties mu at T] --> PROC1
    PROC1[Compute Clearance c and Clearance Ratio psi] --> PROC2
    PROC2[Compute Bearing Pressure P equals W by 2rL] --> PROC3
    PROC3[Compute Sommerfeld Number So] --> PROC4
    PROC4[Iterate to Find Eccentricity Ratio epsilon] --> PROC5
    PROC5[Compute Minimum Film Thickness h min] --> PROC6
    PROC5 --> PROC7[Compute Attitude Angle phi]
    PROC5 --> PROC8[Compute Coefficient of Friction f]
    PROC5 --> PROC9[Compute Power Loss P loss]
    PROC6 --> OUT[Output Bearing Design Parameters]
    PROC7 --> OUT
    PROC8 --> OUT
    PROC9 --> OUT
```

### 4.3 Sommerfeld Number Iteration Logic (Key KTU Concept)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start with initial guess epsilon equals 0.5] --> S2[Compute RHS equals two plus eps squared times sqrt one minus eps squared over three pi epsilon]
    S2 --> S3{Compare RHS with So}
    S3 -->|RHS too high| S4[Increase epsilon]
    S3 -->|RHS too low| S5[Decrease epsilon]
    S4 --> S2
    S5 --> S2
    S3 -->|Difference within 1 percent| S6[Converged Eccentricity Ratio epsilon]
```

### 4.4 Pressure Distribution Across the Bearing (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[theta equals 0 - Position of Maximum Film Thickness h max] --> P1[Pressure starts at zero]
    P1 --> P2[Converging Wedge - Pressure Rises Rapidly]
    P2 --> P3[theta equals pi by 2 - Maximum Pressure Position]
    P3 --> P4[Diverging Wedge - Pressure Drops]
    P4 --> P5[theta equals theta2 - Pressure equals zero Again]
    P5 --> P6[Negative Pressure Cavitation Zone]
    P6 --> P7[theta equals 2 pi - Cycle Complete]
```
