## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**
*Define the term "Hydrodynamic Lubrication" and state the conditions necessary for it to occur.*

**Model Answer:**

Hydrodynamic lubrication is a regime of lubrication in which the load between two relatively moving surfaces is supported entirely by a self-generated pressurized fluid film, with no direct solid-to-solid contact.

**Conditions necessary:**
1. There must be **relative motion** between the surfaces (rotation or sliding).
2. The lubricant must be **viscous** enough to resist being squeezed out.
3. The geometry must create a **converging wedge** (e.g., eccentric journal in a bearing).
4. The **speed of motion** must be sufficiently high to drag fluid into the wedge faster than it can escape.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]**
*Define the "Sommerfeld Number" and explain its physical significance.*

**Model Answer:**

The Sommerfeld Number ($S_o$) is a dimensionless parameter used in hydrodynamic journal bearing design, defined as:

$$S_o = \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 = \frac{\mu N}{P \psi^2}$$

**Physical Significance:**
- It is a **similarity parameter** — bearings with the same Sommerfeld number have the same operating characteristics (same eccentricity ratio, same coefficient of friction).
- It governs the **load capacity vs. speed trade-off**: a high $S_o$ corresponds to low load or high speed (small eccentricity); a low $S_o$ corresponds to high load (large eccentricity).
- It allows **scale-up** from model tests to full-size bearings.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Petroff vs. Hydrodynamic Confusion:**
> Students commonly **mix up** Petroff's friction equation with the Sommerfeld-derived friction. Petroff assumes **concentric** (ε = 0) — friction is purely due to viscous shear with no load support. In real bearings, **ε ≠ 0** and the friction is **higher** than Petroff's value. If the question says "compute coefficient of friction using Petroff's equation," do **NOT** include the wedge correction term +0.5εψ. If it says "compute actual coefficient of friction," include it.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Focus: Petroff's Equation*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 4, Part B]**

A full journal bearing has the following specifications:
- Diameter of journal, $d = 100$ mm
- Length of bearing, $L = 200$ mm
- Radial clearance, $c = 0.1$ mm
- Speed of journal, $N = 1500$ rpm
- Viscosity of lubricant, $\mu = 0.04$ Pa·s

**Compute:**
**(a)** The friction torque using Petroff's equation. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The coefficient of friction and power loss in the bearing. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $r = d/2 = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m
- $L = 0.2$ m
- $c = 0.1$ mm $= 0.0001$ m
- $N = 1500/60 = 25$ rps
- $\mu = 0.04$ Pa·s

**(a) Friction Torque (Petroff's Equation):**

$$T_f = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N L r^4}{c}$$

Substituting values:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_f &= \frac{4 \times \pi^2 \times 0.04 \times 25 \times 0.2 \times (0.05)^4}{0.0001} \\
&= \frac{4 \times 9.8696 \times 0.04 \times 25 \times 0.2 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-6}}{10^{-4}} \\
&= \frac{4 \times 9.8696 \times 0.04 \times 25 \times 0.2 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-6}}{10^{-4}}
\end{aligned}$$

Calculating the numerator step by step:

$$\begin{aligned}
4 \times 9.8696 &= 39.4784 \\
39.4784 \times 0.04 &= 1.57914 \\
1.57914 \times 25 &= 39.4784 \\
39.4784 \times 0.2 &= 7.89568 \\
7.89568 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-6} &= 4.9348 \times 10^{-5}
\end{aligned}$$

Dividing by $10^{-4}$:

$$T_f = \frac{4.9348 \times 10^{-5}}{10^{-4}} = 0.49348 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_f \approx 0.4935 \text{ N·m}}$$

**(b) Coefficient of Friction and Power Loss:**

The friction force is:

$$F_f = \frac{T_f}{r} = \frac{0.4935}{0.05} = 9.87 \text{ N}$$

The bearing pressure requires the load $W$. However, since only Petroff is asked (concentric, no load specified), we use the **direct Petroff coefficient of friction formula**:

$$f = \frac{2\pi^2 \mu N r}{P \cdot c}$$

We can also express $f$ in terms of **specific load**, but here $W$ is not given. Therefore, we use the alternative — Petroff's coefficient of friction in terms of geometry:

$$f = \frac{F_f}{W}$$

Without $W$ given, we present the formula and **note** that $P = W/(2rL)$ is the bearing pressure. Using Petroff's standard form:

$$\begin{aligned}
f &= \frac{F_f}{W} = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^2 L / c}{W} \\
&= \frac{4 \times 9.8696 \times 0.04 \times 25 \times (0.05)^2 \times 0.2}{0.0001 \times W} \\
&= \frac{0.7895}{10^{-4} \times W} \\
&= \frac{7895}{W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Power Loss:**

$$P_{loss} = T_f \cdot \omega = T_f \cdot 2\pi N$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{loss} &= 0.4935 \times 2 \times \pi \times 25 \\
&= 0.4935 \times 157.08 \\
&\approx 77.52 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{P_{loss} \approx 77.52 \text{ W}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Focus: Sommerfeld Number & Eccentricity*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Module 4, Part B]**

A short journal bearing has the following data:
- Journal diameter, $d = 80$ mm
- Bearing length, $L = 80$ mm (so $L/d = 1$)
- Clearance ratio, $\psi = 0.001$
- Speed, $N = 1440$ rpm
- Load on bearing, $W = 12$ kN
- Lubricant viscosity, $\mu = 0.025$ Pa·s

**Compute:**
**(a)** The Sommerfeld Number and the corresponding eccentricity ratio. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The minimum film thickness, attitude angle, and coefficient of friction. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $r = 0.04$ m
- $L = 0.08$ m
- $c = \psi \cdot r = 0.001 \times 0.04 = 4 \times 10^{-5}$ m
- $N = 1440/60 = 24$ rps
- $W = 12000$ N
- $\mu = 0.025$ Pa·s

**(a) Sommerfeld Number and Eccentricity:**

Average bearing pressure:

$$P = \frac{W}{2rL} = \frac{12000}{2 \times 0.04 \times 0.08} = \frac{12000}{0.0064} = 1.875 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

Sommerfeld Number (Bhandari convention):

$$S_o = \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{1}{\psi^2}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
S_o &= \frac{0.025 \times 24}{1.875 \times 10^6} \times \frac{1}{(0.001)^2} \\
&= \frac{0.6}{1.875 \times 10^6} \times 10^6 \\
&= \frac{0.6 \times 10^6}{1.875 \times 10^6} \\
&= 0.32
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{S_o = 0.32}$$

To find $\varepsilon$, use the inverse Sommerfeld relationship:

$$S_o = \frac{(2 + \varepsilon^2)\sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^2}}{3\pi \varepsilon}$$

Substituting $S_o = 0.32$:

$$0.32 = \frac{(2 + \varepsilon^2)\sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^2}}{3\pi \varepsilon}$$

Multiplying both sides by $3\pi \varepsilon$:

$$0.32 \times 3\pi \varepsilon = (2 + \varepsilon^2)\sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^2}$$

$$3.0159 \varepsilon = (2 + \varepsilon^2)\sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^2}$$

**Trial and error:**

| $\varepsilon$ | LHS $= 3.0159\varepsilon$ | RHS $= (2+\varepsilon^2)\sqrt{1-\varepsilon^2}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0.5 | 1.508 | $2.25 \times 0.866 = 1.949$ |
| 0.6 | 1.810 | $2.36 \times 0.8 = 1.888$ |
| 0.65 | 1.960 | $2.4225 \times 0.7599 = 1.841$ |
| 0.62 | 1.870 | $2.3844 \times 0.7846 = 1.871$ |

$$\boxed{\varepsilon \approx 0.62}$$

**(b) Minimum Film Thickness, Attitude Angle, Coefficient of Friction:**

Minimum film thickness:

$$h_{min} = c(1 - \varepsilon) = 4 \times 10^{-5} \times (1 - 0.62) = 4 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.38$$

$$\boxed{h_{min} = 1.52 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 15.2 \text{ μm}}$$

Attitude angle (from standard charts/equations):

$$\tan\phi = \frac{\pi \sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^2}}{2 + \varepsilon^2}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\tan\phi &= \frac{\pi \times 0.7846}{2 + 0.3844} = \frac{2.4647}{2.3844} = 1.0338 \\
\phi &= \arctan(1.0338) \approx 45.94°
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\phi \approx 45.9°}$$

Coefficient of friction (Petroff + wedge correction):

$$f = \frac{r}{c}\left(\frac{2\pi^2 \mu N}{W}\right) + 0.5\varepsilon\psi$$

First term (Petroff component):

$$\frac{r}{c} \cdot \frac{2\pi^2 \mu N}{W} = \frac{0.04}{4 \times 10^{-5}} \cdot \frac{2 \times 9.8696 \times 0.025 \times 24}{12000}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
&= 1000 \times \frac{11.8435}{12000} \\
&= 1000 \times 9.8696 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 0.9870
\end{aligned}$$

Wait — let me recompute the Petroff part. The Petroff friction force for a concentric bearing with $W$ as the load is:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{f,\text{Petroff}} &= \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^2 L}{c} \\
&= \frac{4 \times 9.8696 \times 0.025 \times 24 \times (0.04)^2 \times 0.08}{4 \times 10^{-5}} \\
&= \frac{4 \times 9.8696 \times 0.025 \times 24 \times 0.0016 \times 0.08}{4 \times 10^{-5}} \\
&= \frac{0.003031}{4 \times 10^{-5}} \\
&= 75.78 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

So Petroff coefficient:

$$f_{\text{Petroff}} = \frac{F_f}{W} = \frac{75.78}{12000} = 6.32 \times 10^{-3}$$

Adding the wedge correction:

$$f = 6.32 \times 10^{-3} + 0.5 \times 0.62 \times 0.001 = 6.32 \times 10^{-3} + 3.1 \times 10^{-4} = 6.63 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{f \approx 0.00663}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall #2 — Attitude Angle Sign Error:** The attitude angle $\phi$ is measured from the **maximum film thickness position** to the **line of action of the load**. A common student error is to take $\phi$ from the minimum film thickness. Always check your textbook's convention; in Bhandari/Khurmi, $\phi$ is from the line of centers to the load line, and it lies in the converging zone.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Hydrodynamic lubrication** requires a wedge, relative motion, and viscous fluid — the Reynolds equation governs the pressure.
- ✅ **Petroff's equation** assumes **ε = 0** (concentric) — gives the **lower bound** of friction torque.
- ✅ **Reynolds equation** (1D, thin film) is the **master equation** for journal bearing pressure analysis.
- ✅ **Sommerfeld Number** $S_o = \dfrac{\mu N}{P \psi^2}$ — **inverse** relationship: large $S_o$ ↔ small $\varepsilon$ (lightly loaded); small $S_o$ ↔ large $\varepsilon$ (heavily loaded).
- ✅ **Eccentricity ratio ε** is found by **iterative trial-and-error** of the equation $S_o = \dfrac{(2 + \varepsilon^2)\sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^2}}{3\pi \varepsilon}$.
- ✅ **Minimum film thickness** $h_{min} = c(1 - \varepsilon)$ — must be ≥ **15 μm** (typical KTU minimum).
- ✅ **Attitude angle** $\phi$ from $\tan\phi = \dfrac{\pi\sqrt{1-\varepsilon^2}}{2+\varepsilon^2}$ — increases with $\varepsilon$.
- ✅ **Coefficient of friction** in real bearings: $f = f_{\text{Petroff}} + 0.5\varepsilon\psi$.
- ✅ **Power loss** = $2\pi N \cdot T_f$ — for thermal design, dissipated as heat in lubricant.
- ✅ **Conventions differ** between textbooks (Shigley vs. Khurmi vs. Bhandari) — **always verify** the Sommerfeld formula being used in your numerical problem.
- ✅ **Boundary conditions** for Reynolds equation: $p = 0$ at the start and end of the oil film (cavitation zones ignored in classical theory).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Petroff's Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT521 - DESIGN OF TRANSMISSION ELEMENTS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Petroff's Equation # Petroff's Equation — The Genesis of Journal Bearing Tribology ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Petroff's Equation** is the fundamental analytical expression used in the field of **Machine Design** to compute the **frictional torque generated in a complete journal bearing operating under hydrodynamic full-film lubrication, assuming a perfectly concentric (zero-eccentricity) journal position.** It is mathematically expressed as: $$T = \frac{2 \pi^{2} \, \mu \, N \, L \, r^{3}}{c}$$ where every term has strict SI / CGS units as defined in classical tribological literature. The equation was originally derived by **Nikolai Petroff** in **1883** and represents the *limiting case* of journal bearing analysis — the case in which the load is zero, eccentricity ratio ($\varepsilon$) is zero, and the journal rotates centrally without any whipping motion. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Petroff's equation is a **Module 4 (Sliding Contact Bearings)** cornerstone. It is the **theoretical upper bound** of friction in a bearing and serves as the **bench-mark model** against which all real eccentric bearings (using the Sommerfeld number) are validated. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine a **perfectly round log (journal)** floating in the centre of a **perfectly circular bucket of honey (bearing shell)**. The log is spinning on its own central axis but **not touching the bucket walls** because there is a thin film of honey everywhere around it. - The honey is the **lubricant**. - The log spinning in honey experiences a **viscous drag**, similar to how stirring tea with a spoon makes you feel resistance. - Petroff's equation calculates **exactly how much torque you need to keep stirring** (and how much heat you generate). If you tried to push the log to one side (creating eccentricity), the equation no longer holds. But for the perfectly centered, zero-load case, this is the *most accurate* analytical model available. > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway:** Petroff's equation is **not used for design of real bearings** because real bearings carry loads. It is, however, **mandatory** for KTU board exams because it establishes the **viscous friction baseline** and introduces the concept of the **Sommerfeld Number**, which is built on top of it. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Unit | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Dynamic viscosity of lubricant | $\mu$ | **Pa·s** (or **N·s/m²** or **reyns**) | Temperature dependent | | Journal speed | $N$ | **rev/s** (or **rpm**) | Use consistent units | | Bearing length | $L$ | **m** | Axial length of contact | | Journal radius | $r$ | **m** | Half the journal diameter | | Radial clearance | $c$ | **m** | Difference $(R - r)$ | | Coefficient of friction | $\mu_f$ or $f$ | dimensionless | Ratio of friction force to load | | Bearing pressure | $P$ | **N/m²** or **Pa** | Load per unit projected area | # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Assumptions Underlying Petroff's Equation Petroff's model rests on **eight classical assumptions** that the student must memorize — these are frequently asked as 3-mark conceptual questions in KTU exams. 1. **Concentricity**: The journal is perfectly centered in the bearing ($\varepsilon = 0$). Therefore, eccentricity ratio $\varepsilon = e/c = 0$. 2. **No Load Carried**: Because of concentricity, the external load $W = 0$. 3. **Full-Film Lubrication**: The journal is completely separated from the bearing by a continuous lubricant film — there is **no asperity contact**. 4. **Newtonian Fluid**: The lubricant obeys Newton's law of viscosity: $\tau = \mu \, (du/dy)$. 5. **Laminar Flow**: The lubricant film exhibits laminar (not turbulent) viscous shear. 6. **Incompressible Lubricant**: Density of oil remains constant across the film. 7. **Constant Viscosity**: $\mu$ does not vary across the film thickness or with temperature. 8. **Side Leakage Neglected**: The flow is treated as purely circumferential; axial leakage at the ends is ignored. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why these assumptions matter:** Real bearings violate every single one of these assumptions. This is *why* Petroff's equation gives **only the friction component**, not load capacity. The full Sommerfeld / Raimondi-Boyd analysis relaxes assumption #1 to design real load-carrying bearings. ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Component of the Equation - **$\mu$ (viscosity) appears linearly**: Doubling the oil viscosity doubles the frictional drag. This is why low-viscosity oils are used in high-speed bearings. - **$N$ (speed) appears linearly in torque, but quadratically in power loss**: Doubling RPM doubles frictional torque but **quadruples** power lost as heat. This explains why high-speed bearings overheat rapidly. - **$L$ (length) is linear**: Doubling bearing length doubles torque, but also doubles load capacity — a real design trade-off. - **$r^{3}$ (radius cubed)**: The strongest geometric dependency. Larger journal radius increases friction dramatically. - **$c$ (clearance) in denominator**: Tighter clearance means a thinner oil film → more shear rate → more friction. Counter-intuitively, **smaller clearance = more friction** in Petroff's model. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL:** The table below uses `\vert` instead of `|` to avoid breaking markdown syntax. Memorize this entire sheet for ESE. | S.No. | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Frictional Torque | $T = \dfrac{2 \pi^{2} \, \mu \, N \, L \, r^{3}}{c}$ | N·m | Petroff's *primary* equation | | 2 | Frictional Force | $F = \dfrac{T}{r} = \dfrac{2 \pi^{2} \, \mu \, N \, L \, r^{2}}{c}$ | N | Tangential force at journal surface | | 3 | Coefficient of Friction | $\mu_f = \dfrac{2 \pi^{2} \, \mu \, N \, r}{P \, c}$ | dimensionless | Where $P = W \, / \, (2 r L)$ | | 4 | Frictional Power Loss | $P_{loss} = T \, \omega = \dfrac{4 \pi^{3} \, \mu \, N^{2} \, L \, r^{3}}{c}$ | W | Note $N$ must be in rev/s, OR multiply by $2\pi$ | | 5 | Summerfeld Number (Petroff form) | $S = \dfrac{r \, N \, \mu}{P \, c} \cdot \left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^{2}$ | — | Reduced form: $S = \dfrac{\mu_f}{2\pi^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{r^{2}}{c^{2}}$ | | 6 | Shear Stress in Film | $\tau = \mu \, \dfrac{2 \pi N r}{c}$ | Pa | Linear velocity gradient across gap | | 7 | Linear Velocity of Journal | $V = 2 \pi N r$ | m/s | At journal surface | | 8 | Bearing Pressure | $P = \dfrac{W}{2 r L}$ | Pa | Projected area basis | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Petroff's equation is not merely academic — it underpins several practical engineering decisions: - **Heat-balance design** of journal bearings in turbines, generators, and pumps. - **Selection of lubricant viscosity grade** for a given speed (viscosity index charts). - **Setting bearing temperature rise limits** $\Delta T = P_{loss} \, / \, (C_p \, \dot{m}_{oil})$. - **Bench-mark against empirical charts** (Stanton, McKee, etc.) before applying Raimondi-Boyd correction factors. - **Estimating windage losses** in lightly loaded high-speed machinery (turbocompressors, machine tool spindles). # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Petroff's Equation (Exhaustive, Board-Exam Style) ### Step 1 — Establish the Velocity Profile Consider a journal of radius $r$ rotating at $N$ rev/s inside a stationary bearing of radius $R$, with radial clearance $c = R - r$. The journal surface moves with a tangential linear velocity: $$V = 2 \pi r N$$ The bearing surface is stationary ($V_{bearing} = 0$). By **Newton's law of viscosity** for a Newtonian fluid, the shear stress at any radial location $y$ (measured from the journal surface) is: $$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$ ### Step 2 — Apply the Linear Velocity Assumption Because the clearance $c \ll r$ (typically $c/r \approx 0.001$), the curvature of the oil film is negligible. We can treat the film as a **straight sliding plate problem (Couette flow)**. The velocity varies **linearly** from $V$ (at the journal surface, $y = 0$) to $0$ (at the bearing surface, $y = c$): $$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{V - 0}{c} = \frac{2 \pi r N}{c}$$ This gives the **constant shear stress** everywhere in the film: $$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{2 \pi r N}{c}$$ ### Step 3 — Compute the Frictional Force on the Journal The journal surface area in contact with oil is the cylindrical area: $$A = 2 \pi r L$$ The total frictional force resisting rotation is: $$F = \tau \cdot A = \mu \cdot \frac{2 \pi r N}{c} \cdot 2 \pi r L$$ Simplifying: $$F = \frac{4 \pi^{2} \, \mu \, N \, L \, r^{2}}{c}$$ ### Step 4 — Compute the Frictional Torque The torque is force × radius: $$T = F \cdot r = \frac{4 \pi^{2} \, \mu \, N \, L \, r^{2}}{c} \cdot r$$ $$\boxed{T = \frac{2 \pi^{2} \, \mu \, N \, L \, r^{3}}{c}}$$ ### Step 5 — Derive the Coefficient of Friction The coefficient of friction is the ratio of frictional force to the normal load $W$. Under Petroff's assumption $W = 0$, we introduce an **equivalent bearing pressure**: $$P = \frac{W}{2 r L}$$ Substituting: $$\mu_f = \frac{F}{W} = \frac{4 \pi^{2} \mu N L r^{2} \, / \, c}{2 r L P}$$ $$\boxed{\mu_f = \frac{2 \pi^{2} \, \mu \, N \, r}{P \, c}}$$ ### Step 6 — Derive the Frictional Power Loss Power loss equals torque times angular velocity $\omega = 2 \pi N$: $$P_{loss} = T \cdot \omega = \frac{2 \pi^{2} \mu N L r^{3}}{c} \cdot 2 \pi N$$ $$\boxed{P_{loss} = \frac{4 \pi^{3} \, \mu \, N^{2} \, L \, r^{3}}{c}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Observation for KTU Board Exams:** If $N$ is given in **rpm**, the power loss formula must include the factor $(2\pi/60)$: > $$P_{loss} = \frac{2 \pi^{3} \, \mu \, N_{rpm} \, L \, r^{3}}{15 \, c} \quad \text{(W)}$$ > Failing to convert rpm → rev/s is the **#1 mark-losing mistake** in KTU answers. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (For Self-Study) **Problem:** A journal bearing has journal diameter $D = 100$ mm, length $L = 200$ mm, radial clearance $c = 0.1$ mm, speed $N = 1500$ rpm, and lubricant viscosity $\mu = 0.05$ Pa·s. Compute: (a) Frictional torque, (b) Coefficient of friction if load $W = 5000$ N, (c) Power loss. ### Solution: **Given:** $r = 0.05$ m, $L = 0.2$ m, $c = 0.0001$ m, $N = 1500/60 = 25$ rev/s, $\mu = 0.05$ Pa·s, $W = 5000$ N. **(a) Frictional Torque:** $$T = \frac{2 \pi^{2} \cdot 0.05 \cdot 25 \cdot 0.2 \cdot (0.05)^{3}}{0.0001}$$ Computing numerator stepwise: - $\pi^{2} \approx 9.8696$ - $2 \cdot 9.8696 = 19.7392$ - $19.7392 \cdot 0.05 = 0.98696$ - $0.98696 \cdot 25 = 24.674$ - $24.674 \cdot 0.2 = 4.9348$ - $4.9348 \cdot (0.05)^{3} = 4.9348 \cdot 0.000125 = 0.000617$ - Divided by $0.0001$: $T = 6.17$ N·m **(b) Coefficient of Friction:** $$P = \frac{W}{2 r L} = \frac{5000}{2 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 0.2} = 250{,}000 \text{ Pa}$$ $$\mu_f = \frac{2 \pi^{2} \cdot 0.05 \cdot 25 \cdot 0.05}{250000 \cdot 0.0001}$$ Numerator: $2 \cdot 9.8696 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 25 \cdot 0.05 = 1.2335$ Denominator: $25$ $\mu_f = 0.0493$ (dimensionless) **(c) Power Loss:** $$P_{loss} = \frac{4 \pi^{3} \cdot 0.05 \cdot 25^{2} \cdot 0.2 \cdot (0.05)^{3}}{0.0001}$$ $$\pi^{3} \approx 31.006$$ - $4 \cdot 31.006 = 124.025$ - $124.025 \cdot 0.05 = 6.201$ - $6.201 \cdot 625 = 3875.7$ - $3875.7 \cdot 0.2 = 775.15$ - $775.15 \cdot 0.000125 = 0.0969$ - Divided by $0.0001$: $P_{loss} = 969.2$ W > [!NOTE] > **Cross-verification:** $P_{loss} = T \cdot \omega = 6.17 \cdot 2\pi \cdot 25 = 6.17 \cdot 157.08 = 969.2$ W ✓ (matches perfectly). # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Petroff's Bearing — Geometric Block-Level Functional Architecture The Mermaid diagram below illustrates the **functional architecture** of a Petroff concentric journal bearing, mapping the kinematic and force-flow interactions rather than attempting a physical drawing. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[External Load W = 0] --> B[Concentric Journal e = 0] B --> C[Journal Rotates at N rev/s] C --> D[Linear Velocity V = 2pi r N at surface] D --> E[Couette Flow in Clearance c] E --> F[Constant Shear Stress tau = mu V / c] F --> G[Friction Force F on Cylindrical Area A = 2pi r L] G --> H[Frictional Torque T = F * r] H --> I[Power Loss P_loss = T * omega] I --> J[Heat Generation in Lubricant] K[Lubricant Viscosity mu] --> F L[Radial Clearance c] --> F M[Bearing Length L] --> G N[Journal Radius r] --> D N --> G N --> H style A fill:#f9e,stroke:#333 style B fill:#bbf,stroke:#333 style F fill:#ffd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style H fill:#dfd,stroke:#333 style J fill:#fdd,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.2 Velocity Distribution Diagram (Conceptual) The diagram below represents the **linear velocity profile** across the oil film thickness — the core geometric assumption of Petroff's model. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Journal Surface
Velocity V = 2 pi r N] -->|y = 0| B[Linear Velocity Gradient] B -->|y = c| C[Bearing Surface
Velocity = 0] A -.->|V| D[Shear Stress tau = mu * V / c] C -.->|0| D style A fill:#9cf,stroke:#333 style C fill:#fc9,stroke:#333 style B fill:#efe,stroke:#333 style D fill:#fdd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture of Derived Quantities ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Petroff Torque T] --> B[Friction Force F = T / r] A --> C[Power Loss P_loss = T * 2 pi N] B --> D[Coefficient of Friction mu_f = F / W] D --> E[Sommerfeld Number S = r N mu / P c] E --> F[Raimondi-Boyd Correction Factors] F --> G[Real Eccentric Bearing Design] style A fill:#ffd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#bbf,stroke:#333 style G fill:#9f9,stroke:#333 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** Notice how Petroff's equation sits at the **root** of the entire journal bearing design hierarchy. From $T$, every other design quantity (friction, power, coefficient, Sommerfeld number) is mathematically reachable. The Raimondi-Boyd extension (Module 5 in most KTU textbooks) is built directly on this foundation. # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **State Petroff's equation for a journal bearing and list its assumptions.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Petroff's Equation:** $$T = \frac{2 \pi^{2} \, \mu \, N \, L \, r^{3}}{c}$$ where $T$ = frictional torque (N·m), $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s), $N$ = journal speed (rev/s), $L$ = bearing length (m), $r$ = journal radius (m), $c$ = radial clearance (m). **Assumptions (any 4 required for full marks):** 1. Journal is concentric in the bearing ($\varepsilon = 0$). 2. Load carried $W = 0$ (pure viscous friction). 3. Full-film hydrodynamic lubrication, no asperity contact. 4. Lubricant is a Newtonian fluid (linear shear-stress vs. rate-of-strain). 5. Flow is laminar and the film is treated as a flat Couette layer. 6. Lubricant viscosity $\mu$ is constant (temperature and pressure independent). --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Why is Petroff's equation not directly used for the design of real journal bearings?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Petroff's equation assumes a **concentric, zero-load journal** — a condition that **does not exist in practice**, because every real bearing must carry an external load $W$, which necessarily creates an **eccentricity** $e$ between the journal and bearing centers. Furthermore, Petroff's equation **predicts zero load capacity** but only **viscous friction**, whereas the **primary purpose** of a bearing is to **support a load** while minimizing friction. Therefore, real design uses the **Sommerfeld number** combined with **Raimondi-Boyd charts**, which account for eccentricity, load angle, attitude, and flow turbulence. Petroff's equation, however, remains valuable for **estimating frictional power loss and heat generation** in the early stages of design. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]` — **14 Marks** **(a)** Derive Petroff's equation for the frictional torque in a journal bearing. Clearly state the assumptions. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A journal bearing has the following data: journal diameter $D = 80$ mm, length $L = 120$ mm, radial clearance $c = 0.08$ mm, speed $N = 1440$ rpm, lubricant viscosity $\mu = 0.04$ Pa·s, and load $W = 4000$ N. Using Petroff's equation, calculate the **(i)** frictional torque, **(ii)** coefficient of friction, and **(iii)** power loss. **[7 Marks]** ### Model Solution to Question A: #### Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks] *See **Section 3.1** of these notes for the exhaustive derivation. The expected KTU answer should contain:* 1. **Diagram / setup description** (1 mark): Journal of radius $r$, bearing of radius $R$, clearance $c = R - r$, journal rotating at $N$ rev/s. 2. **Velocity gradient derivation** (2 marks): $V = 2\pi r N$, $du/dy = V/c$. 3. **Shear stress** (1 mark): $\tau = \mu \cdot 2\pi r N / c$. 4. **Force and torque** (2 marks): $F = \tau \cdot 2\pi r L$, $T = F \cdot r$, leading to $T = 2\pi^{2}\mu N L r^{3}/c$. 5. **Assumptions list** (1 mark): At least four of the eight listed in Section 2.1. #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks] **Given:** $D = 0.08$ m → $r = 0.04$ m; $L = 0.12$ m; $c = 0.00008$ m; $N = 1440/60 = 24$ rev/s; $\mu = 0.04$ Pa·s; $W = 4000$ N. **(i) Frictional Torque** (2 marks): $$T = \frac{2 \pi^{2} \cdot 0.04 \cdot 24 \cdot 0.12 \cdot (0.04)^{3}}{0.00008}$$ Numerator: $2 \cdot 9.8696 \cdot 0.04 \cdot 24 \cdot 0.12 \cdot 6.4 \times 10^{-5}$ $= 19.7392 \cdot 0.04 \cdot 24 \cdot 0.12 \cdot 6.4 \times 10^{-5}$ $= 19.7392 \cdot 0.04 = 0.7896$ $\cdot 24 = 18.949$ $\cdot 0.12 = 2.2739$ $\cdot 6.4 \times 10^{-5} = 1.455 \times 10^{-4}$ $$T = \frac{1.455 \times 10^{-4}}{0.00008} = 1.819 \text{ N·m}$$ **(ii) Coefficient of Friction** (2 marks): $$P = \frac{4000}{2 \cdot 0.04 \cdot 0.12} = \frac{4000}{0.0096} = 416{,}666.67 \text{ Pa}$$ $$\mu_f = \frac{2 \pi^{2} \cdot 0.04 \cdot 24 \cdot 0.04}{416666.67 \cdot 0.00008}$$ Numerator: $2 \cdot 9.8696 \cdot 0.04 \cdot 24 \cdot 0.04 = 0.7580$ Denominator: $33.333$ $$\mu_f = 0.02274$$ **(iii) Power Loss** (3 marks): $$P_{loss} = \frac{4 \pi^{3} \cdot 0.04 \cdot (24)^{2} \cdot 0.12 \cdot (0.04)^{3}}{0.00008}$$ $\pi^{3} = 31.006$ $4 \cdot 31.006 = 124.025$ $\cdot 0.04 = 4.961$ $\cdot 576 = 2857.5$ $\cdot 0.12 = 342.9$ $\cdot 6.4 \times 10^{-5} = 0.02195$ $/ 0.00008 = 274.3$ W **OR cross-check:** $P_{loss} = T \cdot \omega = 1.819 \cdot 2\pi \cdot 24 = 1.819 \cdot 150.80 = 274.3$ W ✓ **Final Answers:** $T \approx 1.819$ N·m; $\mu_f \approx 0.0227$; $P_{loss} \approx 274.3$ W. --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]` — **14 Marks** *(Alternative Choice)* **(a)** Explain in detail the **eight classical assumptions** underlying Petroff's analysis of journal bearings. Discuss why each assumption is an *idealization* of real bearing behavior. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A hydrodynamic journal bearing carries a load of $W = 8000$ N at a journal speed of $1800$ rpm. The journal diameter is $100$ mm and the bearing length is $150$ mm. The radial clearance is $0.1$ mm and the lubricant viscosity is $0.03$ Pa·s. Using Petroff's equation, determine: - (i) Frictional torque - (ii) Coefficient of friction - (iii) Frictional power loss **[7 Marks]** ### Model Solution to Question B: #### Part (a) — Assumptions Discussion [7 Marks] *(Expected answer: For each assumption, write 0.5 mark for statement + 0.5 mark for idealization justification. KTU requires ALL 8 to be listed for full 7 marks.)* | Assumption | Idealization vs. Reality | |---|---| | **Concentric journal** | Real journals are displaced from center under load — eccentricity $\varepsilon > 0$ | | **Zero load** | Real bearings MUST carry load $W > 0$ to be useful | | **Full-film lubrication** | During start-up/stop, mixed or boundary lubrication occurs | | **Newtonian lubricant** | Most oils show slight non-Newtonian behavior at high shear rates | | **Laminar flow** | High-speed bearings may exhibit turbulent flow (Taylor vortices) | | **Incompressible fluid** | Negligible pressure-density coupling at bearing pressures | | **Constant viscosity** | Viscosity drops significantly with temperature rise during operation | | **No side leakage** | Real bearings leak oil at the ends — flow is genuinely 3D | #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks] **Given:** $W = 8000$ N, $N = 1800/60 = 30$ rev/s, $D = 0.1$ m → $r = 0.05$ m, $L = 0.15$ m, $c = 0.0001$ m, $\mu = 0.03$ Pa·s. **(i) Frictional Torque** (2 marks): $$T = \frac{2 \pi^{2} \cdot 0.03 \cdot 30 \cdot 0.15 \cdot (0.05)^{3}}{0.0001}$$ Numerator: $19.7392 \cdot 0.03 \cdot 30 \cdot 0.15 \cdot 1.25 \times 10^{-4}$ $= 19.7392 \cdot 0.03 = 0.5922$ $\cdot 30 = 17.766$ $\cdot 0.15 = 2.665$ $\cdot 1.25 \times 10^{-4} = 3.331 \times 10^{-4}$ $$T = \frac{3.331 \times 10^{-4}}{0.0001} = 3.331 \text{ N·m}$$ **(ii) Coefficient of Friction** (2 marks): $$P = \frac{8000}{2 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 0.15} = \frac{8000}{0.015} = 533{,}333.33 \text{ Pa}$$ $$\mu_f = \frac{2 \pi^{2} \cdot 0.03 \cdot 30 \cdot 0.05}{533333.33 \cdot 0.0001}$$ Numerator: $19.7392 \cdot 0.03 \cdot 30 \cdot 0.05 = 0.8883$ Denominator: $53.333$ $$\mu_f = 0.01666$$ **(iii) Power Loss** (3 marks): $$P_{loss} = \frac{4 \pi^{3} \cdot 0.03 \cdot (30)^{2} \cdot 0.15 \cdot (0.05)^{3}}{0.0001}$$ $4 \cdot 31.006 = 124.025$ $\cdot 0.03 = 3.721$ $\cdot 900 = 3348.7$ $\cdot 0.15 = 502.3$ $\cdot 1.25 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0628$ $/ 0.0001 = 627.8$ W **Final Answers:** $T \approx 3.331$ N·m; $\mu_f \approx 0.0167$; $P_{loss} \approx 627.8$ W. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Unit inconsistency** is the most common mark-losing error. Always verify whether $N$ is in **rev/s** or **rpm**. Mixing the two in different formulas within the same problem yields **0 marks** for the numerical answer. > 2. **Forgetting to cube the radius** $r^{3}$ loses 1 mark per sub-question. > 3. **Not converting mm → m** in clearance $c$ leads to answers **1000× too large** — another classic trap. > 4. **Forgetting the factor of 2** between $F$ and $T$ (since $T = F \cdot r$) is a frequent mistake. > 5. **Stating Petroff's formula without assumptions** will be penalized: KTU mandates **at least four** assumptions for full marks on the derivation question. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):** - Petroff's equation gives the **viscous frictional torque** for a **concentric, zero-load journal bearing** under **full-film laminar Newtonian flow**. - The **primary equation** is $T = \dfrac{2\pi^{2}\mu N L r^{3}}{c}$. Memorize this verbatim. - **Coefficient of friction** under Petroff's model is $\mu_f = \dfrac{2\pi^{2}\mu N r}{P c}$, where $P = W/(2rL)$. - **Power loss** is $P_{loss} = \dfrac{4\pi^{3}\mu N^{2} L r^{3}}{c}$ when $N$ is in **rev/s**. - If $N$ is given in **rpm**, use $P_{loss} = \dfrac{2\pi^{3}\mu N_{rpm} L r^{3}}{15 c}$ — this is a **frequently tested** conversion. - The **eight classical assumptions** are: concentricity, zero load, full-film, Newtonian, laminar, incompressible, constant viscosity, and no side leakage. - Petroff's equation **cannot** predict load capacity — for that, use **Sommerfeld number + Raimondi-Boyd charts** (Module 5). - The **strongest geometric dependency** is on $r^{3}$ (radius cubed), and the **strongest operating dependency** is on $N^{2}$ (for power loss). - Petroff's equation gives a **theoretical minimum friction** for a given geometry, viscosity, and speed — real bearings always have higher friction due to eccentricity and turbulence. - Typical exam problem: Always verify units (SI vs. CGS), then apply the formula directly — Petroff's problems are **direct substitution** problems once units are consistent.

topics.title: Bearing Characteristic Number
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT521 - DESIGN OF TRANSMISSION ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bearing Characteristic Number

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bearing Characteristic Number (B.C.N.)** — also universally known as the **Sommerfeld Number (S or S₀)** — is a *dimensionless design parameter* that mathematically couples the **operating variables** (load, speed, viscosity) and the **geometric variables** (radial clearance, journal radius) of a hydrodynamic journal bearing. It is the principal design index used to evaluate the **eccentricity ratio (ε)** of a journal inside its bearing shell.

Mathematically, it is defined as:

$$S = \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^{2} \times \frac{\mu \, N_{s}}{P}$$

where every symbol on the right-hand side is a measurable or assumed physical quantity of the bearing assembly.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Convention (SI Units):** When the journal speed $N$ is given in **rpm**, the B.C.N. takes the form shown below to keep the parameter *truly dimensionless*:
> $$S = \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^{2} \times \frac{\mu \, N}{60 \, P}$$
> This is the form students **must** use in the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE scripts unless otherwise stated.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Plain English" Analogy

Imagine you are pushing a **heavy wooden log (the journal)** rolling inside a **hollow tree trunk (the bearing)**, with the gap between them filled with **honey (lubricant)**.

Three things determine how the log "settles" inside the trunk:
1. **How heavy the log is** → the **load (W)**, which controls the bearing pressure $P$.
2. **How fast the log is rotating** → the **journal speed (N)**.
3. **How thick the honey is** → the **dynamic viscosity (μ)** of the lubricant.
4. **How big the gap is** → the **radial clearance (c)** relative to the journal radius **r**.

The *Bearing Characteristic Number* is a single "score" that bundles **all four** of these effects into one number. The KTU board examiner will tell you, in a single glance, **how eccentric** the journal will sit inside the bearing — i.e., how close the rotating journal will come to the bearing surface.

| Physical Effect | Symbol | Role in B.C.N. |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Load on bearing | $W$ | Increases $P$, **decreases** $S$ |
| Journal speed | $N$ | Increases $S$ (more hydrodynamic lift) |
| Oil viscosity | $\mu$ | Increases $S$ (thicker film) |
| Radial clearance | $c$ | Decreases $S$ (tighter clearance helps) |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory hook for KTU exam:** "**S**ommerfeld **N**umber is a **S**ingle **N**umber for the bearing." All four operating parameters are *coiled* into it.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The key physical quantity in the formula is the **dynamic viscosity (μ)** of the lubricating oil. The following are *standard* benchmark values referenced in KTU 2024 problem statements:

| Lubricant Type | Approx. Viscosity μ (N·s/m² or Pa·s) |
| :--- | :---: |
| Light machine oil | $0.025$ – $0.060$ |
| Medium machine oil | $0.060$ – $0.100$ |
| Heavy machine oil | $0.100$ – $0.300$ |
| SAE 30 oil at 50 °C (default in KTU data) | **$0.110$** |

The **unit of viscosity** in the SI system is **N·s/m² (Pa·s)**, while in the CGS (FPS) system it is **poise (P)** or **centipoise (cP)**.

> $1 \text{ N·s/m}^2 = 10 \text{ P} = 10^3 \text{ cP}$

Always keep the unit system consistent — KTU strictly deducts marks for unit-mismatch in the B.C.N. calculation step.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Breaking Down the Operational Concept

The Bearing Characteristic Number is *not* a single derivation from a single equation — it is a **synthesis of three physical sub-systems** of a hydrodynamic journal bearing:

1. **Petroff's hypothesis (frictionless assumption):** Assumes the journal is concentric (ε = 0) and ignores the load-carrying wedge action. This was the **first** attempt to mathematically describe a journal bearing and yields the classical Petroff friction torque:
   $$T_{f} = \frac{4\pi^{2} \, \mu \, N_{s} \, r^{3} \, L}{c}$$
   Petroff's equation is the *theoretical root* from which the B.C.N. concept germinates.

2. **Reynolds Equation (hydrodynamic film pressure):** Governs the pressure generation in the converging wedge of the oil film. The dimensionless B.C.N. is precisely the **load-capacity integral** of the Reynolds pressure profile, normalized by the bearing geometry.

3. **Sommerfeld's boundary conditions:** The classical assumption is that $p = 0$ at both the trailing and leading edges of the film (at $\theta = 0$ and $\theta = \pi$). Using these boundary conditions, integrating Reynolds' equation, and balancing the **integrated pressure × projected area** with the applied load **W**, gives the B.C.N. as a **pure function of the eccentricity ratio (ε)**.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Term

- **Why $(\frac{r}{c})^{2}$?** — A small clearance $c$ creates a *high-pressure wedge* for a given load. The squaring indicates that the film pressure is **quadratically sensitive** to clearance ratio — this is *why* bearings are manufactured with such tight tolerances (typically $\frac{c}{r} \approx 0.001$).
- **Why $\mu$?** — Viscosity is the *only* material property in the B.C.N. Heavier oils give higher $S$, but they also generate more viscous friction — a classic KTU "design trade-off" question.
- **Why $N$?** — Speed drives the **couette flow** component of the Reynolds equation, which is the actual pumping mechanism of the converging wedge.
- **Why $P$ in the denominator?** — $P$ represents *load intensity*. A high load pushes the journal closer to the bearing wall (ε increases), which is mathematically captured as a *decrease* in $S$.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — every KTU problem in Module 4 uses at least one row from it.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Bearing Characteristic Number (SI, $N$ in rpm) | $S = \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^{2} \cdot \frac{\mu N}{60 P}$ | **Dimensionless** | Most common KTU form |
| 2 | Bearing Characteristic Number (SI, $N$ in rev/s) | $S = \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^{2} \cdot \frac{\mu N_{s}}{P}$ | **Dimensionless** | Used when $N_{s}$ is given directly |
| 3 | Bearing Pressure | $P = \dfrac{W}{L \cdot D}$ | $\text{N/m}^2$ | Always positive |
| 4 | Radial Clearance (recommended range) | $c = 0.001 \, r \; \text{to} \; 0.002 \, r$ | m | $c/r$ in 0.001 – 0.002 |
| 5 | Eccentricity Ratio | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{e}{c}$ | Dimensionless | $0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$ |
| 6 | Minimum Film Thickness | $h_{0} = c \,(1 - \varepsilon)$ | m | KTU rule: $h_0 \ge 25$ to $40 \, \mu\text{m}$ |
| 7 | Petroff Friction Torque | $T_{f} = \dfrac{4\pi^{2} \mu N_{s} r^{3} L}{c}$ | N·m | Concentric, no-load wedge |
| 8 | Petroff Power Loss | $P_{f} = \dfrac{2\pi^{3} \mu N_{s}^{2} r^{3} L}{c}$ | W | $P_f = 2\pi N_s T_f$ |
| 9 | Petroff Friction Coefficient | $\mu_{f} = 4\pi^{2} \cdot \dfrac{\mu N_{s}}{P} \cdot \dfrac{r}{c}$ | Dimensionless | High — Petroff overestimates |
| 10 | Sommerfeld Number from Chart (short bearing) | $S = \dfrac{\pi}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\varepsilon \, \sqrt{1 + \varepsilon^{2}}}{1 - \varepsilon^{2}}$ (approx.) | Dimensionless | Read from **BCN vs ε** chart |
| 11 | Sommerfeld Number (long bearing, Gumbel) | $S = \dfrac{2\varepsilon}{(1 - \varepsilon^{2})^{1/2}\, (1 + \varepsilon^{2})^{1/2}}$ (approx.) | Dimensionless | From Gumbel's infinite-bearing solution |
| 12 | Coefficient of Friction (actual bearing) | $\mu_{f} = \dfrac{33 \,(N_{s})^{0.6} \, \mu^{0.7} \, r}{P^{0.3} \, (c/r)^{0.6}}$ | Dimensionless | **McKee's equation** — KTU favourite |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** In Row 1, the factor **60** is in the denominator *only* because $N$ is in **rpm**. If the question gives $N$ in **rev/s** (or labels it $N_s$), **drop the 60**. Marks are routinely lost on this single conversion.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Bearing Characteristic Number is the *single most-used index* in any hydrodynamic bearing design, from the small spindle bearings in CNC machines to the **giant white-metal bearings** used in marine propeller shafts, hydro-turbines, and rolling mills. It is used in industry to:

- **Predict eccentricity**: A given $S$ value corresponds to a *unique* $\varepsilon$ from a standard chart (Shigley/V.B. Bhandari Chart in KTU textbooks).
- **Verify hydrodynamic film formation**: $h_0$ must be greater than the combined surface roughness of the journal and bearing.
- **Estimate friction and power loss**: Using McKee's empirical relation (Row 12).
- **Troubleshoot bearing failure**: A change in $\mu$ due to oil degradation or temperature directly alters $S$ and hence $\varepsilon$.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Foundational Petroff's Equation (Root of the B.C.N.)

**Starting assumption (Petroff, 1883):** The journal is *concentric* (ε = 0) with the bearing. The oil film is therefore of *uniform* thickness $c$, and the load-carrying capacity is theoretically zero. However, **viscous shear** still occurs.

### Step 1: Shear stress in a Couette flow
The velocity gradient in a uniform-thickness film is linear:

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U}{c} = \frac{2\pi r N_{s}}{c}$$

Hence, the viscous shear stress on the journal surface is:

$$\tau = \mu \, \frac{du}{dy} = \frac{2\pi \mu r N_{s}}{c}$$

### Step 2: Total viscous force and torque
Friction force on journal surface: $F_{f} = \tau \cdot (2\pi r L) = \dfrac{4\pi^{2} \mu r^{2} L N_{s}}{c}$

Friction torque about the journal axis (force × radius):

$$T_{f} = F_{f} \times r = \frac{4\pi^{2} \mu N_{s} r^{3} L}{c}$$

### Step 3: Express in terms of B.C.N.
Divide numerator and denominator by $P \cdot c \cdot L$:

$$T_{f} = \left(\frac{r}{c}\right) \cdot \frac{4\pi^{2} \mu N_{s} r^{2} L}{P \cdot c} = \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^{2} \cdot \frac{4\pi^{2} \mu N_{s}}{P} \cdot \frac{L \, r^{2}}{c}$$

When we identify $S$ (B.C.N.) and impose the Petroff geometry constraints, this expression recovers the **Bearing Characteristic Number as a multiplier of Petroff's no-load friction torque**. This is the **first time the dimensionless group $S = (\mu N_s/P)(r/c)^2$** appears naturally in bearing theory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Petroff's equation is the *historical genesis* of the B.C.N.** — although it predicts *zero* load capacity, the same dimensionless group reappears in the modern Sommerfeld analysis.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the B.C.N. from Reynolds' Equation

**Reynolds' equation in 2-D** (circumferential $x = r\theta$, axial $z$) for an incompressible Newtonian fluid in a thin journal-bearing film:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(h^{3} \frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left(h^{3} \frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\right) = 6\mu U \, \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$$

where the surface velocity is $U = r\omega = 2\pi r N_{s}$, and the film thickness profile for a circular journal bearing is:

$$h(\theta) = c \left(1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta\right)$$

### Step 1: Long-Bearing Approximation (Gumbel)
Assume the bearing is *infinitely long* → $\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial z} = 0$. The equation reduces to:

$$\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(h^{3} \frac{dp}{dx}\right) = 6\mu U \, \frac{dh}{dx}$$

### Step 2: First Integration
Integrate both sides with respect to $x$:

$$h^{3} \frac{dp}{dx} = 6\mu U \, h + C_{1}$$

### Step 3: Apply Sommerfeld Boundary Conditions
Classical Sommerfeld's condition: $p = 0$ at $\theta = 0$ (cavitation begins) and $p = 0$ at $\theta = \pi$ (the maximum film thickness). For the long bearing, the second condition is automatically satisfied by symmetry; the constant $C_1$ evaluates from a finite pressure condition:

$$C_{1} = -6\mu U \, \bar{h}$$

where $\bar{h}$ is a mean film thickness. Substituting and integrating a second time:

$$p(\theta) = \frac{6\mu U}{c^{2}} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon \sin\theta \, (2 + \varepsilon \cos\theta)}{(2 + \varepsilon^{2})(1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta)^{2}}$$

### Step 4: Integrate Pressure to Get Load
The vertical load component is obtained by projecting the pressure on the $\cos\theta$ direction and integrating over the bearing surface:

$$W = L \int_{0}^{2\pi} p(\theta) \, \cos\theta \, r \, d\theta$$

After non-dimensionalizing, the result can be cast as:

$$S \equiv \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^{2} \frac{\mu N_{s}}{P} = \frac{2\varepsilon}{\sqrt{(1 - \varepsilon^{2})(1 + \varepsilon^{2})}}$$

This is the **Gumbel long-bearing B.C.N. — eccentricity relation**. The right-hand side is a *pure function of ε* — which is the deep reason why $S$ and $\varepsilon$ are uniquely linked for a long bearing.

### Step 5: Short-Bearing Approximation (Sommerfeld)
For a *short* bearing, neglect the $\partial p/\partial x$ term (circumferential pressure gradient is small compared to axial):

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left(h^{3} \frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\right) = 6\mu U \, \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$$

This is a *Poisson-type* equation in $z$, with $h(\theta)$ as a known coefficient at each $\theta$. Solving with the boundary condition $p = 0$ at $z = \pm L/2$ gives:

$$p(\theta, z) = \frac{3\mu U}{c^{2}} \left(\frac{z^{2}}{(L/2)^{2}} - 1\right) \frac{\varepsilon \sin\theta}{(1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta)^{3}}$$

### Step 6: Integrate Over Length and Angle for Load
The vertical load is:

$$W = \int_{-L/2}^{L/2} \int_{0}^{2\pi} p(\theta, z) \, \cos\theta \, r \, d\theta \, dz$$

After performing both integrations, the **Sommerfeld short-bearing B.C.N. relation** is:

$$S = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon \, \sqrt{1 + \varepsilon^{2}}}{1 - \varepsilon^{2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **canonical equation** that ties the B.C.N. to the eccentricity ratio for a *short* hydrodynamic journal bearing. KTU 2024 board exam problems are predominantly based on this **short-bearing relation** (or chart equivalents).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Demonstration (KTU Style)

> **Q.** A journal bearing has the following data: $D = 100$ mm, $L = 50$ mm, $c = 0.05$ mm, $W = 10\,000$ N, $N = 1440$ rpm, $\mu = 0.020$ N·s/m². Calculate the **bearing characteristic number** and the **eccentricity ratio** (use the short-bearing chart).

**Given (converted to SI):**
$D = 0.100$ m, $L = 0.050$ m, $c = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ m, $r = 0.050$ m
$N = 1440$ rpm, $\mu = 0.020$ N·s/m², $W = 10\,000$ N

### Step 1: Bearing pressure
$$P = \frac{W}{L \cdot D} = \frac{10\,000}{0.050 \times 0.100} = 2.0 \times 10^{6} \text{ N/m}^2$$

### Step 2: Clearance ratio
$$\frac{c}{r} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-5}}{0.050} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \implies \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^{2} = (1000)^{2} = 1.0 \times 10^{6}$$

### Step 3: Bearing characteristic number
$$S = \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^{2} \cdot \frac{\mu N}{60 \, P}$$

$$S = (1.0 \times 10^{6}) \cdot \frac{0.020 \times 1440}{60 \times 2.0 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$S = (1.0 \times 10^{6}) \cdot \frac{28.8}{1.2 \times 10^{8}}$$

$$S = 1.0 \times 10^{6} \times 2.4 \times 10^{-7} = 0.24$$

### Step 4: Eccentricity ratio from short-bearing chart
For $S = 0.24$, the short-bearing BCN-vs-ε chart gives:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon \approx 0.50}$$

(Student would read this directly from the standard B.C.N. chart in the data book.)

### Step 5: Minimum film thickness
$$h_{0} = c(1 - \varepsilon) = 5 \times 10^{-5} \times (1 - 0.50) = 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 25 \, \mu\text{m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $h_0 = 25 \, \mu\text{m} \ge 25 \, \mu\text{m}$ (lower limit), the bearing design is **marginally safe** under the given operating conditions — a typical KTU 2024 design-verdict.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix (Design → Check Map)

| Design Stage | Quantity Computed | Symbol | Check Criterion |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Stage 1 | Bearing pressure | $P$ | $P \le P_{\text{allow}}$ |
| Stage 2 | Clearance ratio | $c/r$ | $0.001 \le c/r \le 0.002$ |
| Stage 3 | B.C.N. | $S$ | $0.05 \le S \le 1.0$ (typical range) |
| Stage 4 | Eccentricity ratio | $\varepsilon$ | $0.5 \le \varepsilon \le 0.8$ |
| Stage 5 | Min. film thickness | $h_0$ | $h_0 \ge 25$ to $40 \, \mu\text{m}$ |
| Stage 6 | Friction coefficient | $\mu_f$ | $\mu_f \le 0.02$ (general-purpose) |
| Stage 7 | Heat balance | $\Delta T$ | $\Delta T \le 10$ – $15$ °C (no oil cooler) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Geometry of a Journal Bearing with Eccentricity

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **kinematic geometry** of an eccentric journal inside a hydrodynamic bearing — i.e., the *physical origin* of every symbol used in the B.C.N. formula.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Geom["Geometry of an Eccentric Journal Bearing (Front View)"]
        direction TB
        A["Bearing Centre O_b (Stationary)"]:::center
        B["Journal Centre O_j (Rotating with N rpm)"]:::center
        C["Radial Load W (downward)"]:::load
        D["Eccentricity Vector e = O_b O_j"]:::vec
        E["Radial Clearance c = R - r"]:::vec
        F["Film Thickness h(θ) = c + e cos θ"]:::film
        G["Pressure Maximum at θ ≈ 75° (Converging Wedge)"]:::film
        H["Cavitation Zone (0° < θ < 180°)"]:::film
    end

    A --- D --- B
    C --> A
    E --- A
    E --- B
    F --- G
    F --- H
    G --- F

    classDef center fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A07000,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#FF7F7F,stroke:#A00000,color:#FFF
    classDef vec fill:#9ECFFF,stroke:#0050A0,color:#000
    classDef film fill:#C8F0C8,stroke:#1A6B1A,color:#000
```

> **Reading guide:** The journal centre $O_j$ is offset from the bearing centre $O_b$ by the eccentricity $e$. The lubricant film is *thinnest* at the point of maximum pressure (right side of the figure), giving rise to the **load-supporting wedge** that makes hydrodynamic lubrication possible.

---

## 4.2 Functional Design Flow Using the B.C.N.

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the **standard KTU 2024 design procedure** for a hydrodynamic journal bearing — every step is anchored in the B.C.N. equation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Step 1: Collect Operating Data\n(W, N, μ, L, D)"]:::step
    S2["Step 2: Compute Bearing Pressure\nP = W / (L × D)"]:::step
    S3["Step 3: Assume Clearance Ratio\nc/r in 0.001 to 0.002"]:::step
    S4["Step 4: Calculate BCN\nS = (r/c)^2 × μN / (60P)"]:::imp
    S5["Step 5: Read Eccentricity ε\nfrom BCN vs ε chart"]:::imp
    S6["Step 6: Compute Min Film Thickness\nh_0 = c × (1 - ε)"]:::step
    S7["Step 7: Verify h_0 >= 25 to 40 μm"]:::check
    S8["Step 8: Compute Friction Coeff.\n(McKee's Equation)"]:::step
    S9["Step 9: Compute Power Loss\nP_f = μ_f × W × U"]:::step
    S10["Step 10: Heat Balance Check\nΔT = P_f / (m_dot × C_p)"]:::check

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7
    S7 -->|Pass| S8 --> S9 --> S10
    S7 -->|Fail| S11["Revise: increase μ, N, or decrease c"]:::fix
    S10 -->|Pass| S12["Design ACCEPTED"]:::ok
    S10 -->|Fail| S13["Add Oil Cooler / Higher Viscosity"]:::fix
    S11 --> S4
    S13 --> S8

    classDef step fill:#E8F0FF,stroke:#0033A0,color:#000
    classDef imp fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#A07000,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef check fill:#FFCFCF,stroke:#A00000,color:#000
    classDef fix fill:#FFD0F0,stroke:#A03080,color:#000
    classDef ok fill:#C8F0C8,stroke:#1A6B1A,color:#000
```

> **Critical sequence:** Step 4 → Step 5 is the **heart of the KTU problem**. Once $S$ is computed, the chart immediately gives $\varepsilon$, and *every subsequent design check cascades from this single number.*
