# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define the term *Bearing Characteristic Number* (B.C.N.) and write down its formula in SI units. State the significance of each term in the formula.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Bearing Characteristic Number (B.C.N.)** — also called the **Sommerfeld Number (S)** — is a dimensionless design parameter used to characterize the operating regime of a hydrodynamic journal bearing. It is the ratio of the viscous (hydrodynamic) forces to the load-carrying pressure of the bearing. **[1 mark]**
>
> Formula in SI units (with journal speed in rpm):
> $$S = \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^{2} \times \frac{\mu N}{60 P}$$
> where $r$ = journal radius, $c$ = radial clearance, $\mu$ = absolute viscosity of lubricant, $N$ = journal speed in rpm, $P$ = bearing pressure. **[1 mark]**
>
> Significance: The B.C.N. uniquely determines the **eccentricity ratio (ε)** of the journal inside the bearing from a standard chart, which in turn controls the **minimum oil-film thickness ($h_0$)** and the **friction and power loss** of the bearing. It is therefore the master design index for all hydrodynamic journal bearings. **[1 mark]**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**State the relation between the B.C.N. and the eccentricity ratio for a short hydrodynamic journal bearing. Under what operating conditions does ε approach zero and unity?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> For a short journal bearing, the Sommerfeld short-bearing analysis yields the relation:
> $$S = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon \sqrt{1 + \varepsilon^{2}}}{1 - \varepsilon^{2}}$$
> **[1 mark]**
>
> Equivalently, the B.C.N. is a **monotonically increasing function of ε** — a higher B.C.N. corresponds to a higher eccentricity ratio. **[1 mark]**
>
> - **ε → 0 (concentric journal):** occurs at *very high speeds*, *very low loads*, or *very high viscosity* — i.e., when $S \to 0$. The journal is centred with a uniform oil film. **[0.5 mark]**
> - **ε → 1 (metal-to-metal contact imminent):** occurs at *very high loads*, *very low speeds*, or *very thin oils* — i.e., when $S \to \infty$ (theoretically). The journal virtually touches the bearing surface. **[0.5 mark]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Convention:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **7 + 7** in sub-parts (a) and (b). Two full alternative questions (Q-A and Q-B) are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** A short journal bearing has the following specifications:
- Journal diameter $D = 80$ mm
- Bearing length $L = 40$ mm
- Radial clearance $c = 0.08$ mm
- Journal speed $N = 1500$ rpm
- Radial load $W = 5\,000$ N
- Lubricant viscosity $\mu = 0.025$ N·s/m²

**Calculate the bearing characteristic number and the eccentricity ratio. Comment on the suitability of the design.** **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A 360° hydrodynamic bearing operates with $\varepsilon = 0.70$ and $\mu = 0.030$ N·s/m². The journal diameter is 120 mm, length is 60 mm, and the radial clearance is 0.06 mm. If the speed is 1200 rpm, **find the safe load capacity of the bearing** using the short-bearing B.C.N. relation. **[7 marks]**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### **Solution to Part (a) (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Convert to SI units.**
- $D = 0.080$ m, $r = 0.040$ m
- $L = 0.040$ m
- $c = 0.08 \times 10^{-3} = 8 \times 10^{-5}$ m
- $N = 1500$ rpm
- $W = 5\,000$ N
- $\mu = 0.025$ N·s/m²

**Step 2: Bearing pressure.**
$$P = \frac{W}{L \cdot D} = \frac{5\,000}{0.040 \times 0.080} = \frac{5\,000}{3.2 \times 10^{-3}}$$
$$P = 1.5625 \times 10^{6} \text{ N/m}^2$$
**∴ $P = 1.5625 \text{ MPa}$** **[1 mark]**

**Step 3: Clearance ratio squared.**
$$\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{0.040}{8 \times 10^{-5}}\right)^{2} = (500)^{2} = 2.5 \times 10^{5}$$
**∴ $(r/c)^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{5}$** **[1 mark]**

**Step 4: Bearing characteristic number.**
$$S = \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^{2} \cdot \frac{\mu N}{60 P}$$
$$S = (2.5 \times 10^{5}) \cdot \frac{0.025 \times 1500}{60 \times 1.5625 \times 10^{6}}$$

Compute numerator of fraction: $0.025 \times 1500 = 37.5$
Denominator: $60 \times 1.5625 \times 10^{6} = 93.75 \times 10^{6}$

$$S = (2.5 \times 10^{5}) \cdot \frac{37.5}{93.75 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$\frac{37.5}{93.75 \times 10^{6}} = 4.0 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$S = 2.5 \times 10^{5} \times 4.0 \times 10^{-7} = 0.10$$
**∴ $S = 0.10$** **[2 marks]**

**Step 5: Eccentricity ratio (from short-bearing chart).**
For $S = 0.10$, the short-bearing BCN-vs-ε chart gives:
$$\boxed{\varepsilon \approx 0.42}$$ **[1 mark]**

**Step 6: Minimum film thickness.**
$$h_{0} = c(1 - \varepsilon) = 8 \times 10^{-5} \times (1 - 0.42) = 4.64 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 46.4 \, \mu\text{m}$$

Since $h_0 = 46.4 \, \mu\text{m} \ge 25 \, \mu\text{m}$ (lower safe limit), the design is **safe**, but a tighter clearance or higher viscosity may be preferred. **[1 mark]**

**Design comment (1 mark):** $\varepsilon = 0.42$ is on the *low side* — the journal is operating in a lightly loaded regime. The bearing has more capacity than needed; a more compact design is feasible.

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Given:** $\varepsilon = 0.70$, $\mu = 0.030$ N·s/m², $D = 0.120$ m, $L = 0.060$ m, $c = 0.06 \times 10^{-3} = 6 \times 10^{-5}$ m, $N = 1200$ rpm.

**Step 1: B.C.N. from the short-bearing relation.**
$$S = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon \sqrt{1 + \varepsilon^{2}}}{1 - \varepsilon^{2}}$$

Compute components:
- $\varepsilon^2 = 0.49$
- $1 + \varepsilon^2 = 1.49$
- $\sqrt{1.49} = 1.2207$
- $1 - \varepsilon^2 = 0.51$
- Numerator: $\varepsilon \sqrt{1 + \varepsilon^2} = 0.70 \times 1.2207 = 0.8545$

$$S = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \frac{0.8545}{0.51} = 1.5708 \times 1.6755 = 2.632$$
**∴ $S \approx 2.63$** **[2 marks]**

**Step 2: Solve for the bearing pressure $P$ from the B.C.N. definition.**
$$P = \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^{2} \cdot \frac{\mu N}{60 \, S}$$

- $r = 0.060$ m
- $\frac{r}{c} = \frac{0.060}{6 \times 10^{-5}} = 1000$, so $\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^{2} = 10^{6}$
- $\mu N = 0.030 \times 1200 = 36$
- $60 \times S = 60 \times 2.632 = 157.92$

$$P = 10^{6} \times \frac{36}{157.92} = 10^{6} \times 0.2280 = 2.280 \times 10^{5} \text{ N/m}^2$$
**∴ $P \approx 0.228 \text{ MPa}$** **[2 marks]**

**Step 3: Safe load capacity $W$.**
$$W = P \times L \times D = 2.280 \times 10^{5} \times 0.060 \times 0.120$$
$$W = 2.280 \times 10^{5} \times 7.2 \times 10^{-3} = 1641.6 \text{ N}$$
**∴ Safe load $W \approx 1.64 \text{ kN}$** **[2 marks]**

**Verification (1 mark):** $h_0 = c(1 - \varepsilon) = 6 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.30 = 18 \, \mu\text{m}$. Since $h_0 < 25 \, \mu\text{m}$, this design is at the *edge of unsafe*. In a KTU exam, mention that the calculated load is the **maximum theoretical** for ε = 0.70.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** A journal bearing has the following data: $D = 100$ mm, $L = 50$ mm, $c = 0.1$ mm, $N = 900$ rpm, $W = 8\,000$ N, $\mu = 0.040$ N·s/m². **Compute the B.C.N., eccentricity ratio, and the coefficient of friction using McKee's empirical equation.** **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain, with sketches, the **significance of the B.C.N. in the design of a hydrodynamic journal bearing.** Show the **complete Sommerfeld short-bearing BCN-vs-ε relation** and indicate the safe operating zone. **[7 marks]**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### **Solution to Part (a) (7 marks):**

**Given:** $D = 0.100$ m, $L = 0.050$ m, $c = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m, $N = 900$ rpm, $W = 8\,000$ N, $\mu = 0.040$ N·s/m².

**Step 1: Bearing pressure $P$.**
$$P = \frac{W}{L \cdot D} = \frac{8\,000}{0.050 \times 0.100} = 1.6 \times 10^{6} \text{ N/m}^2 = 1.6 \text{ MPa}$$
**[1 mark]**

**Step 2: Clearance ratio.**
$$\frac{r}{c} = \frac{0.050}{1.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 500 \implies \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^{2} = 2.5 \times 10^{5}$$
**[1 mark]**

**Step 3: B.C.N.**
$$S = (2.5 \times 10^{5}) \times \frac{0.040 \times 900}{60 \times 1.6 \times 10^{6}}$$

Numerator: $0.040 \times 900 = 36$
Denominator: $60 \times 1.6 \times 10^{6} = 96 \times 10^{6}$

$$S = 2.5 \times 10^{5} \times \frac{36}{96 \times 10^{6}} = 2.5 \times 10^{5} \times 3.75 \times 10^{-7} = 0.09375$$
**∴ $S \approx 0.094$** **[1.5 marks]**

**Step 4: Eccentricity ratio (from short-bearing chart for $S = 0.094$).**
$$\boxed{\varepsilon \approx 0.40}$$ **[1 mark]**

**Step 5: Coefficient of friction by McKee's equation.**
$$\mu_{f} = \frac{33 \, (N_{s})^{0.6} \, \mu^{0.7} \, r}{P^{0.3} \, (c/r)^{0.6}}$$

- $N_{s} = N/60 = 900/60 = 15$ rev/s
- $(N_s)^{0.6} = 15^{0.6}$. Use: $\log(15) = 1.1761$, so $0.6 \times 1.1761 = 0.7057$, antilog $\Rightarrow 5.078$
- $\mu^{0.7} = (0.040)^{0.7}$. $\log(0.040) = -1.3979$, $\times 0.7 = -0.9785$, antilog $\Rightarrow 0.1052$
- $P^{0.3} = (1.6 \times 10^{6})^{0.3}$. $\log(1.6 \times 10^6) = 6.2041$, $\times 0.3 = 1.8612$, antilog $\Rightarrow 72.7$
- $(c/r)^{0.6} = (0.002)^{0.6}$. $\log(0.002) = -2.6990$, $\times 0.6 = -1.6194$, antilog $\Rightarrow 0.02403$
- $r = 0.050$ m

$$\mu_{f} = \frac{33 \times 5.078 \times 0.1052 \times 0.050}{72.7 \times 0.02403}$$
$$= \frac{33 \times 0.02672}{1.747} = \frac{0.8818}{1.747} = 0.5048$$
**∴ $\mu_{f} \approx 0.505$** **[2.5 marks]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. *Unit trap:* McKee's equation requires $N_s$ in **rev/s**, $P$ in **N/m²**, $\mu$ in **N·s/m² (Pa·s)**, $r$ in **metres**, and $c/r$ as a *dimensionless ratio*. Mismatched units will *triple* the value of $\mu_f$.
> 2. *Significance test:* The friction coefficient of $\sim 0.50$ is *very high* — this typically indicates the bearing is running near the boundary-lubrication regime. The design should be reviewed.

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Significance of the B.C.N. in journal bearing design — a structured response:**

1. **Single design index:** The B.C.N. bundles the four critical design variables (load, speed, viscosity, clearance) into one number, allowing the designer to quickly evaluate the *operating regime* of the bearing. **[1 mark]**

2. **Predicts eccentricity:** For any computed $S$, the eccentricity ratio $\varepsilon$ is uniquely read from the B.C.N.-vs-ε chart. This determines how far the journal is offset from the bearing centre. **[1 mark]**

3. **Estimates minimum film thickness:**
$$h_{0} = c(1 - \varepsilon)$$
   This is the **most critical safety check** — KTU 2024 design rule is $h_0 \ge 25$ to $40 \, \mu\text{m}$. **[1 mark]**

4. **Predicts friction and power loss:** Using McKee's empirical relation, the B.C.N. is the *implicit* input for estimating the coefficient of friction $\mu_f$ and the power loss $P_f$. **[1 mark]**

5. **Guides material and oil selection:** If the calculated B.C.N. is *too high* (ε → 1, journal near contact), the designer can either use a *higher viscosity oil* or a *tighter clearance* to bring $\varepsilon$ back into the safe zone. **[1 mark]**

**Sommerfeld short-bearing BCN-vs-ε relation (chart sketch described):**
$$S = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon \sqrt{1 + \varepsilon^{2}}}{1 - \varepsilon^{2}}$$

| $\varepsilon$ | $S$ (short bearing) |
| :---: | :---: |
| 0.0 | 0.000 |
| 0.2 | 0.034 |
| 0.4 | 0.090 |
| 0.6 | 0.213 |
| 0.8 | 0.679 |
| 1.0 | $\infty$ |

**Safe operating zone (textual sketch):** On the BCN-vs-ε curve, the safe zone lies between $\varepsilon = 0.5$ and $\varepsilon = 0.8$ (corresponding to $S$ values of approximately $0.12 \le S \le 0.68$). Beyond $\varepsilon = 0.9$, the design enters the *unsafe* (metal-to-metal contact) regime. **[1 mark]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for BCN Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the '60' factor** when $N$ is in rpm. This single error invalidates the entire answer.
> 2. **Mixing FPS and SI units** — KTU strictly uses SI. Marks deducted for $W$ in kgf, $\mu$ in poise, $r$ in cm, etc.
> 3. **Reading ε from the wrong curve** — There are *two* BCN-vs-ε charts: *short-bearing* and *long-bearing (Gumbel)*. They give different ε for the same $S$. KTU 2024 defaults to the **short-bearing curve** unless the problem explicitly says "long bearing".
> 4. **Not verifying $h_0$** — Even if the B.C.N. is correct, the design is incomplete without the minimum-film-thickness check.
> 5. **Sign error in clearance** — Always use $c = R - r$ (positive). Sometimes the problem gives $c/r$ directly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **B.C.N. = Sommerfeld Number** = dimensionless index of hydrodynamic journal bearings.
- [x] **Master formula (SI, $N$ in rpm):** $S = (r/c)^{2} \cdot \mu N / (60 P)$.
- [x] **Bearing pressure:** $P = W / (L \cdot D)$ — always compute this *first*.
- [x] **Typical clearance ratio:** $c/r = 0.001$ to $0.002$ — the squaring in $(r/c)^2$ makes this hugely influential.
- [x] **Short-bearing BCN-vs-ε relation:** $S = (\pi/2) \cdot \varepsilon \sqrt{1 + \varepsilon^{2}} / (1 - \varepsilon^{2})$.
- [x] **Long-bearing (Gumbel) relation:** $S = 2\varepsilon / \sqrt{(1 - \varepsilon^2)(1 + \varepsilon^2)}$.
- [x] **S is a *monotonically increasing* function of ε** — high $S$ → high ε → journal close to bearing wall.
- [x] **S → 0 (concentric)**: high speed, high viscosity, light load.
- [x] **S → ∞ (boundary contact)**: heavy load, low speed, thin oil.
- [x] **Min film thickness:** $h_0 = c(1 - \varepsilon) \ge 25$ to $40 \, \mu\text{m}$ (KTU safety rule).
- [x] **Petroff's equation** is the *historical origin* of the B.C.N. (concentric, no-load wedge).
- [x] **McKee's empirical equation** for friction coefficient: $\mu_f = 33 (N_s)^{0.6} \mu^{0.7} r / [P^{0.3} (c/r)^{0.6}]$.
- [x] **Always use SI units in KTU 2024** — N, m, N·s/m², N/m², rpm.
- [x] **Sommerfeld's boundary conditions:** $p = 0$ at $\theta = 0$ and $\theta = \pi$ (cavitation assumed sub-atmospheric).
- [x] **Reynolds equation** is the governing PDE from which the B.C.N. arises — never write B.C.N. in the exam without mentioning Reynolds.
- [x] **Two-case judgment:** short vs long bearing → different BCN-vs-ε chart → different ε → different $h_0$ → different verdict.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sommerfeld Number
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT521 - DESIGN OF TRANSMISSION ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sommerfeld Number

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Sommerfeld Number (So)** is a fundamental **dimensionless parameter** in hydrodynamic journal bearing analysis that characterizes the operating regime of a fluid-film bearing by relating the **viscous forces** of the lubricant to the **load-carrying capacity** of the bearing.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PERAT521 – Design of Transmission Elements, Module 4: Sliding Contact Bearings), the Sommerfeld Number is defined as the **master dimensionless group** whose value uniquely determines the position of the journal within the bearing clearance circle, and therefore governs the entire set of bearing performance variables plotted on the **Raimondi–Boyd charts**.

The general mathematical form is:

$$S_o = \frac{\mu \, \omega}{P} \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2$$

where every symbol carries specific SI units, and the resulting number is **purely dimensionless** (no units).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The Sommerfeld Number is the **primary input parameter** for entering Raimondi–Boyd performance charts. From a single value of $S_o$, the student must be able to read off the **eccentricity ratio (ε)**, **coefficient of friction variable (r/c·f)**, **flow ratio variable (Q/r·c·N·L)**, and **temperature rise variable (T·c/μ·N·r)**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are pushing a **heavy wooden log (the journal)** across a **trough of thick honey (the lubricant)** at constant speed inside a circular metal sleeve (the bearing). Three things govern how hard it is to push the log, and how much honey is squeezed out to support the log's weight:

1. **How thick (viscous) the honey is** — represented by **μ** (dynamic viscosity).
2. **How fast you are pushing** — represented by **ω** (angular speed of the journal).
3. **How heavy the log is** — represented by **P** (load per unit projected area).
4. **How tight the sleeve fits around the log** — represented by the **clearance ratio (r/c)**.

The **Sommerfeld Number is simply the ratio of the "squeezing-and-dragging ability of the honey" to the "weight of the log," scaled by the squared clearance ratio.** A small Sommerfeld Number means the log is heavy and slow in thick honey with a loose sleeve — the log will sink deep into the lower part of the sleeve, building strong hydrodynamic pressure. A large Sommerfeld Number means the log is light and fast in thin honey with a tight sleeve — the log floats almost centered.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation Summary**
> - **Low $S_o$** → Heavy load, low speed, thin oil, loose bearing → Journal sits **near the bottom** (low eccentricity) → **High load capacity, higher friction**.
> - **High $S_o$** → Light load, high speed, thick oil, tight bearing → Journal sits **near the center** (high eccentricity) → **Low load capacity, lower friction**.

### 1.3 Standard Nomenclature and Physical Constants

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard SI Unit |
|:--|:--|:--|
| $S_o$ | Sommerfeld Number | **dimensionless** |
| $\mu$ | Dynamic viscosity of lubricant | Pa·s (N·s/m²) |
| $\omega$ | Angular speed of journal | rad/s |
| $P$ | Load per unit projected bearing area ($W/(L \cdot D)$) | N/m² (Pa) |
| $r$ | Journal radius | m |
| $c$ | Radial clearance $(D-d)/2$ | m |
| $N$ | Journal speed | rev/s (or rpm with $2\pi$ factor) |
| $W$ | Applied load on bearing | N |
| $L$ | Bearing length | m |
| $D$ | Bearing bore diameter | m |
| $d$ | Journal diameter | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction (KTU Board-Exam Favourite):**
> There are **two equivalent forms** of the Sommerfeld Number. Students often lose marks by mixing them.
> - **SI form (ω in rad/s):** $\;S_o = \dfrac{\mu \, \omega}{P}\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^2$
> - **rpm form (N in rev/s):** $\;S_o = \dfrac{2\pi \, \mu \, N}{P}\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^2$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic Behind the Sommerfeld Number

To understand *why* the Sommerfeld Number has this exact form, the student must trace the logic of hydrodynamic lubrication step by step.

- **Step 1 — Petroff's Friction Model:** A lightly loaded, concentric journal experiences only **viscous shearing** of the oil film. Petroff derived the friction torque as:
$$T_f = \frac{4\pi^2 \, \mu \, N \, r^3 \, L}{c}$$

This gives a **friction force** $F_f = T_f/r$, and therefore a **Petroff coefficient of friction**:
$$f = \frac{F_f}{W} = 2\pi^2 \, \frac{\mu \, N}{P} \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)$$

- **Step 2 — The Hidden Squared Ratio:** Petroff's equation uses $(r/c)$ to the power **1**, not 2. However, the load-carrying capacity of a real (eccentric) bearing does **not** grow linearly with $1/c$. From Reynolds' equation, the load capacity is governed by a **second-order dependence on the inverse clearance ratio**, because pressure builds up over a wedge whose height is proportional to $c$, and whose integrated thrust is proportional to the velocity $(r\omega)$ and inverse clearance $(1/c)$ acting over the bearing surface. The net load is thus proportional to $1/c^2$.

- **Step 3 — Forming the Dimensionless Group:** Combining the Petroff-type viscosity-speed scaling with the inverse-cleared-squared load capacity produces the natural dimensionless group:
$$S_o = \frac{\mu \, \omega}{P} \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2$$

- **Step 4 — Why It Governs Everything:** The Reynolds equation, when non-dimensionalised, reduces to a function **only** of $S_o$ (and the length-to-diameter ratio $L/D$). All performance variables — friction, flow, temperature, minimum film thickness — therefore become **single-valued functions of $S_o$**, which is exactly why Raimondi and Boyd were able to tabulate them as universal charts.

- **Step 5 — Engineering Utility:** A bearing designer can:
  1. Compute $S_o$ from a given load, speed, viscosity, and geometry.
  2. Enter the Raimondi–Boyd chart at that $S_o$ value.
  3. Read off the **eccentricity ratio (ε)**, **friction variable (r/c·f)**, **flow variable (Q/(r·c·N·L))**, and **temperature-rise variable (T·c/(μ·N·r))**.
  4. Back-calculate the actual friction power loss, oil flow rate, and temperature rise of the bearing.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units Check |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | $S_o = \dfrac{\mu \, \omega}{P}\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^2$ | Master Sommerfeld Number (SI form, $\omega$ in rad/s) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $S_o = \dfrac{2\pi \, \mu \, N}{P}\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^2$ | Sommerfeld Number (N in rev/s) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $P = \dfrac{W}{L \cdot D}$ | Bearing pressure (load per unit projected area) | Pa |
| 4 | $c = \dfrac{D - d}{2}$ | Radial (diametral) clearance | m |
| 5 | $\dfrac{r}{c} = \dfrac{1}{\psi}$ where $\psi = c/r$ | Clearance ratio (reciprocal relationship) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $S_o = \dfrac{\mu \, \omega}{P \, \psi^2}$ | Sommerfeld Number expressed via $\psi$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | $h_{min} = c(1 - \varepsilon)$ | Minimum film thickness from eccentricity ratio | m |
| 8 | $\varepsilon = 1 - \dfrac{h_{min}}{c}$ | Eccentricity ratio from Raimondi–Boyd chart | dimensionless |
| 9 | $f = \left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{r}{c} \cdot f\right)_{chart}$ | Coefficient of friction (back-calculated from chart) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $Q = (r \cdot c \cdot N \cdot L) \cdot \left(\dfrac{Q}{r \cdot c \cdot N \cdot L}\right)_{chart}$ | Lubricant flow rate | m³/s |
| 11 | $\Delta T = \dfrac{\mu \, N \, r}{c} \cdot \left(\dfrac{T \cdot c}{\mu \, N \cdot r}\right)_{chart}$ | Temperature rise across bearing | °C |
| 12 | Petroff: $f_{Petroff} = 2\pi^2 \dfrac{\mu \, N}{P}\dfrac{r}{c}$ | Friction coefficient for lightly loaded concentric journal | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** When writing the radial-clearance ratio, always write it as $r/c$ or use the LaTeX absolute-value-safe form $\vert r \vert / \vert c \vert$ to avoid markdown pipe-symbol conflicts in tables.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Sommerfeld Number is **the single most important scalar** in the design of every rotating machine that uses a hydrodynamic journal bearing:

- **Turbo-machinery:** Steam turbines, gas turbines, large centrifugal pumps, and turbogenerators all rely on $S_o$ to confirm safe operation across their start-up, running, and shut-down speed ranges.
- **Automotive:** Crankshaft main bearings and connecting-rod bearings are sized so that $S_o$ stays in a "safe operating window" — typically between $0.001$ and $1.0$ — guaranteeing adequate film thickness and avoiding metal-to-metal contact.
- **Heavy Industry:** Rolling mills, paper machines, and marine propeller shaft bearings use $S_o$ charts to balance friction losses against load capacity.
- **Aerospace:** Gas turbine engine mainshaft bearings are designed for a target $S_o$ corresponding to a target minimum film thickness ratio (lambda ratio $\Lambda = h_{min}/\sigma$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Mapping:** This topic directly supports **CO4** of PERAT521 — *“Design hydrodynamic journal and thrust bearings for given loading conditions.”* Every bearing design problem on the KTU ESE paper will begin with the student computing $S_o$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Sommerfeld Number from Petroff's Equation

We begin with **Petroff's hypothesis** — a concentric, lightly loaded, full journal bearing — and modify it systematically to reach the full Sommerfeld form.

**Step 1 — Petroff's Shear-Stress Model**

For a journal rotating at angular speed $\omega$ inside a concentric busing with radial clearance $c$, the lubricant film thickness is uniform and equal to $c$. The viscous shear stress in the film is:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = \mu \frac{r\omega - 0}{c} = \frac{\mu \, r \, \omega}{c}$$

**Step 2 — Petroff's Friction Torque**

The shear acts on a cylindrical surface of area $2\pi r L$. Multiplying shear stress by area and by the moment arm $r$:

$$T_f = (\tau)(2\pi r L)(r) = \frac{\mu \, r \, \omega}{c} \cdot 2\pi r^2 L$$

Simplifying:

$$T_f = \frac{2\pi \, \mu \, \omega \, r^3 \, L}{c}$$

**Step 3 — Petroff's Coefficient of Friction**

The friction force on the journal surface is $F_f = T_f / r$. The coefficient of friction is the ratio of this friction force to the applied load $W$:

$$f_{Petroff} = \frac{F_f}{W} = \frac{T_f}{r \, W} = \frac{2\pi \, \mu \, \omega \, r^2 \, L}{c \, W}$$

**Step 4 — Introduce Bearing Pressure**

The bearing pressure is defined as $P = W / (L \cdot D) = W / (2 r L)$. Substituting $W = 2 P r L$:

$$f_{Petroff} = \frac{2\pi \, \mu \, \omega \, r^2 \, L}{c \cdot (2 P r L)} = \frac{\pi \, \mu \, \omega \, r}{P \, c}$$

**Step 5 — Rewriting with $N$ in rev/s**

If we prefer the journal speed in revolutions per second, then $\omega = 2\pi N$, and:

$$f_{Petroff} = \frac{\pi \, (2\pi N) \, \mu \, r}{P \, c} = \frac{2\pi^2 \, \mu \, N}{P} \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Recognizing the Linear $(r/c)$ Dependence**

Petroff's result contains $(r/c)$ to the **first power**. But this is valid only for a **perfectly concentric, lightly loaded** journal with no hydrodynamic pressure buildup. In a real bearing, the journal is **eccentric**, the film is wedge-shaped, and Reynolds' equation produces a **second-order** dependence of load on $1/c$.

**Step 7 — Promoting the Exponent from 1 to 2**

The wedge action of the converging oil film can be shown (from a Reynolds-equation analysis) to yield a load capacity proportional to:

$$W \propto \frac{\mu \, \omega \, r^2 \, L}{c^2}$$

Dividing this load capacity by the projected area $2 r L$ gives the pressure:

$$P \propto \frac{\mu \, \omega \, r}{2 c^2}$$

Inverting to make the dimensionless group explicit:

$$S_o = \frac{\mu \, \omega}{P} \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2$$

**Step 8 — Final Dimensionless Verification**

Let us check units:

$$\frac{[\text{Pa·s}] \cdot [\text{rad/s}]}{[\text{Pa}]} \cdot \left(\frac{[\text{m}]}{[\text{m}]}\right)^2 = \frac{[\text{Pa}]}{[\text{Pa}]} \cdot [\text{dimensionless}] = [\text{dimensionless}] \;\;\checkmark$$

This is the **Sommerfeld Number**.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping $S_o$ to Bearing Behaviour

| Sommerfeld Number $S_o$ | Eccentricity $\varepsilon$ | Min. Film Thickness $h_{min}/c$ | Friction Variable $(r/c)f$ | Behaviour |
|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--|
| $0.001$ | $0.001$ | $0.999$ | $0.0002$ | Almost full film, very high load |
| $0.01$ | $0.010$ | $0.990$ | $0.002$ | Thick film, high load |
| $0.1$ | $0.10$ | $0.90$ | $0.020$ | Moderate eccentricity |
| $1.0$ | $0.4$ | $0.6$ | $0.15$ | Centred, lightly loaded |
| $10.0$ | $0.95$ | $0.05$ | $5.0$ | Very thin film, danger zone |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Insight:** Numerical problems typically give a load, speed, viscosity, and geometry. Students compute $S_o$ and then read (or interpolate from) the Raimondi–Boyd chart. The most common error is using $N$ in rpm without the factor $2\pi$ when required, or omitting the squared $(r/c)^2$ term.

### 3.3 Worked Example (Model KTU-Style Numerical)

**Problem:** A full journal bearing has the following data:
- Load $W = 50\,000$ N
- Journal diameter $d = 100$ mm
- Bearing length $L = 100$ mm
- Radial clearance $c = 0.1$ mm
- Speed $N = 1440$ rpm
- Lubricant viscosity $\mu = 0.020$ Pa·s

Compute the Sommerfeld Number.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Bearing pressure:**
$$P = \frac{W}{L \cdot D} = \frac{50\,000}{(0.100)(0.100)} = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Clearance ratio:**
$$\frac{r}{c} = \frac{0.050}{0.0001} = 500$$

**Step 3 — Angular speed:**
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1440}{60} = 150.80 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Sommerfeld formula (SI form):**
$$S_o = \frac{\mu \, \omega}{P}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 = \frac{(0.020)(150.80)}{(5 \times 10^6)}(500)^2$$

$$S_o = \frac{3.016}{5 \times 10^6} \times 250\,000 = \frac{3.016 \times 250\,000}{5 \times 10^6} = 0.1508$$

$$\boxed{S_o \approx 0.151}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Cross-Check Using the N-rev/s Form:**
> $N = 1440/60 = 24$ rev/s
> $S_o = \dfrac{2\pi \, (0.020)(24)}{(5 \times 10^6)}(500)^2 = \dfrac{3.0159 \times 250\,000}{5 \times 10^6} \approx 0.151$ ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Role of the Sommerfeld Number in Bearing Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Input Data: W, N, mu, d, D, L, c]
    A1[Compute Bearing Pressure P = W / L*D]
    A2[Compute Clearance Ratio r/c]
    A3[Compute Angular Speed omega = 2*pi*N/60]
    A4[Compute Sommerfeld Number So = mu*omega / P * r/c squared]
    A5{Is So within Raimondi-Boyd range?}
    A6[Enter Raimondi-Boyd Chart at So]
    A7[Read Eccentricity Ratio epsilon]
    A8[Read Friction Variable r/c * f]
    A9[Read Flow Variable Q / r*c*N*L]
    A10[Read Temp-Rise Variable T*c / mu*N*r]
    A11[Back-calculate f, Q, Delta T, h_min]
    A12[Bearing Performance Report]
    A13[Re-design: change c, mu, L, or D]

    A0 --> A1
    A0 --> A2
    A0 --> A3
    A1 --> A4
    A2 --> A4
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> A5
    A5 -- yes --> A6
    A5 -- no --> A13
    A6 --> A7
    A6 --> A8
    A6 --> A9
    A6 --> A10
    A7 --> A11
    A8 --> A11
    A9 --> A11
    A10 --> A11
    A11 --> A12
    A13 --> A1
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Physical Interpretation of the Sommerfeld Number

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Low Sommerfeld Number]
    L2[Heavy Load W, Low Speed omega, Loose Clearance, Thin Oil]
    L3[Journal displaced downward: HIGH epsilon]
    L4[Thick Hydrodynamic Wedge Film]
    L5[HIGH Load Capacity]
    L6[HIGH Coefficient of Friction]

    H1[High Sommerfeld Number]
    H2[Light Load W, High Speed omega, Tight Clearance, Thick Oil]
    H3[Journal nearly centered: LOW epsilon]
    H4[Thin Convergent Film]
    H5[LOW Load Capacity]
    H6[LOW Coefficient of Friction]

    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5
    L5 --> L6
    L1 --> L6
    H1 --> H2 --> H3 --> H4 --> H5
    H5 --> H6
    H1 --> H6
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Relationship Between $S_o$, Eccentricity $\varepsilon$, and $h_{min}/c$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[So value computed]
    R2[Enter Raimondi-Boyd Chart horizontally at So]
    R3[Move vertically up to read epsilon]
    R4[epsilon read from chart]
    R5[Compute h_min = c * 1 minus epsilon]
    R6[Compute h0 = c * 1 plus epsilon]
    R7[Check Lambda Ratio = h_min / sigma composite roughness]
    R8{Lambda greater than 3?}
    R9[FULL FILM LUBRICATION OK]
    R10[MIXED or BOUNDARY lubrication - RE-DESIGN]

    R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4
    R4 --> R5
    R4 --> R6
    R5 --> R7
    R6 --> R7
    R7 --> R8
    R8 -- yes --> R9
    R8 -- no --> R10
```
