<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the Sommerfeld Number. State its physical significance in the design of hydrodynamic journal bearings.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Sommerfeld Number ($S_o$)** is a dimensionless parameter that relates the viscous forces of the lubricant to the load-carrying capacity of a hydrodynamic journal bearing, scaled by the square of the clearance ratio. Its mathematical form is:

$$S_o = \frac{\mu \, \omega}{P}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2$$

**Physical significance:**
- It is the **master dimensionless group** governing the operation of a hydrodynamic journal bearing.
- All performance variables — eccentricity ratio, coefficient of friction, oil flow rate, and temperature rise — are **single-valued functions of $S_o$**.
- It is used as the entry point to the **Raimondi–Boyd design charts**, from which the actual bearing performance is read.
- A **low $S_o$** indicates a heavily loaded, low-speed bearing with a thick oil wedge; a **high $S_o$** indicates a lightly loaded, high-speed bearing with the journal almost centred.

---

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the relationship between the Sommerfeld Number and the eccentricity ratio of a journal bearing.**

**Model Answer:**

The eccentricity ratio $\varepsilon$ is defined as the ratio of the journal displacement $e$ from the bearing centre to the radial clearance $c$:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{e}{c}$$

The **Raimondi–Boyd charts** show that $\varepsilon$ is an **inversely related, monotonically increasing function** of the Sommerfeld Number:

| $S_o$ | $\varepsilon$ |
|:--:|:--:|
| Very small (0.001) | $\approx 1.0$ (journal at bottom) |
| Moderate (0.1) | $\approx 0.5$ |
| Large (10) | $\approx 0.95$ (journal near centre) |

**Engineering implication:** As $S_o$ increases (lighter load, higher speed, tighter clearance, or thicker oil), the journal migrates towards the centre of the bearing, reducing the load-carrying capacity but also reducing the friction coefficient. The minimum film thickness $h_{min} = c(1 - \varepsilon)$ **decreases** as $S_o$ increases, and the designer must ensure $h_{min}$ remains above the combined surface roughness $\sigma$ to maintain full-film lubrication.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PERAT521]**

**(a)** *Derive the expression for the Sommerfeld Number starting from Petroff's equation for friction torque in a concentric journal bearing. (7 Marks)*

**(b)** *A full journal bearing has the following specifications:*
- *Applied load $W = 80\,000$ N*
- *Journal diameter $d = 150$ mm*
- *Bearing length $L = 150$ mm*
- *Radial clearance $c = 0.15$ mm*
- *Speed $N = 960$ rpm*
- *Lubricant dynamic viscosity $\mu = 0.030$ Pa·s*

*Calculate: (i) the Sommerfeld Number, (ii) the bearing pressure, and (iii) Petroff's coefficient of friction. (7 Marks)*

---

##### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

The derivation proceeds identically to **Section 3.1** of these notes. The key logical steps are:

1. Petroff's shear-stress model gives $\tau = \mu r\omega / c$.
2. The friction torque on the journal is $T_f = 2\pi \mu \omega r^3 L / c$.
3. The friction force is $F_f = T_f / r = 2\pi \mu \omega r^2 L / c$.
4. Petroff's coefficient of friction is $f = F_f / W$, with $W = P \cdot 2rL$:

$$f_{Petroff} = \frac{\pi \mu \omega r}{P c} = 2\pi^2 \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c}$$

5. However, in a real eccentric bearing, the wedge-shaped film gives a load capacity proportional to $1/c^2$, not $1/c$. Replacing the linear $(r/c)$ dependence with the squared dependence and rearranging into a dimensionless group:

$$\boxed{S_o = \frac{\mu \omega}{P} \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2}$$

This dimensionless group is the **Sommerfeld Number**, and it remains the master parameter in all subsequent Raimondi–Boyd analysis.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

**(i) Bearing pressure $P$:**

$$P = \frac{W}{L \cdot D} = \frac{80\,000}{(0.150)(0.150)} = \frac{80\,000}{0.0225}$$

$$P = 3.556 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 3.556 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) Sommerfeld Number $S_o$:**

Geometric ratios:

$$r = d/2 = 0.075 \text{ m}, \quad c = 0.00015 \text{ m}$$

$$\frac{r}{c} = \frac{0.075}{0.00015} = 500$$

Angular speed:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 960}{60} = 100.53 \text{ rad/s}$$

Substituting into the Sommerfeld formula:

$$S_o = \frac{(0.030)(100.53)}{(3.556 \times 10^6)} \cdot (500)^2$$

$$S_o = \frac{3.016}{3.556 \times 10^6} \cdot 250\,000 = 0.2120$$

$$\boxed{S_o \approx 0.212}$$

**(iii) Petroff's coefficient of friction:**

$$f_{Petroff} = 2\pi^2 \cdot \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c}$$

Using $N = 960/60 = 16$ rev/s:

$$f_{Petroff} = 2\pi^2 \cdot \frac{(0.030)(16)}{3.556 \times 10^6} \cdot 500$$

$$f_{Petroff} = 19.739 \times \frac{0.480}{3.556 \times 10^6} \cdot 500 = 19.739 \times 1.350 \times 10^{-7} \times 500$$

$$f_{Petroff} = 19.739 \times 6.749 \times 10^{-5} = 1.332 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{f_{Petroff} \approx 0.00133}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Common Mark Loss):**
> - Forgetting to convert $N$ from rpm to rev/s when using the $2\pi\mu N$ form of $S_o$. **Always show the conversion step explicitly.**
> - Mixing the diameter $D$ and the radius $r$ in the clearance ratio. The Sommerfeld Number uses $r/c$, **not** $D/c$.
> - Using the projected area $L \cdot D$ correctly for $P$ but forgetting that Petroff's formula uses $W = 2 P r L$, not $W = P L D$ — these are identical, but the substitution must be shown.
> - Not squaring the $(r/c)$ term. The difference between $r/c$ and $(r/c)^2$ changes $S_o$ by orders of magnitude.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** *Explain, with a labelled sketch, how the Sommerfeld Number is used as the entry parameter to the Raimondi–Boyd performance charts for a hydrodynamic journal bearing. List the four performance variables that are read from these charts. (7 Marks)*

**(b)** *A hydrodynamic journal bearing supports a load of $100$ kN at $1500$ rpm. The journal diameter is $120$ mm, the bearing length is $120$ mm, the radial clearance is $0.12$ mm, and the lubricant viscosity at operating temperature is $0.025$ Pa·s. Determine the Sommerfeld Number and comment on whether the bearing is operating in a safe hydrodynamic regime. (7 Marks)*

---

##### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Raimondi–Boyd Chart Usage (7 Marks):**

The Raimondi–Boyd charts are a family of four dimensionless plots, all plotted as functions of the **Sommerfeld Number** $S_o$ on the x-axis. The procedure is:

1. **Compute $S_o$** from the known load, speed, viscosity, and geometry.
2. **Enter the charts** horizontally at the computed $S_o$.
3. **Read vertically** to intersect the curves for the four performance variables:

| Chart | X-axis | Y-axis (Performance Variable) | Output Quantity |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | $S_o$ | Eccentricity ratio $\varepsilon$ | Used to find $h_{min}$ |
| 2 | $S_o$ | Friction variable $(r/c) \cdot f$ | Used to find coefficient of friction |
| 3 | $S_o$ | Flow variable $Q / (r \cdot c \cdot N \cdot L)$ | Used to find oil flow rate |
| 4 | $S_o$ | Temperature-rise variable $\Delta T \cdot c / (\mu \cdot N \cdot r)$ | Used to find temperature rise |

4. **Back-calculate** the actual engineering quantities using the equations in the formula sheet (Section 2.2, items 7–11).

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Bearing pressure:**

$$P = \frac{W}{L \cdot D} = \frac{100\,000}{(0.120)(0.120)} = 6.944 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Clearance ratio:**

$$\frac{r}{c} = \frac{0.060}{0.00012} = 500$$

**Step 3 — Angular speed:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Sommerfeld Number:**

$$S_o = \frac{(0.025)(157.08)}{(6.944 \times 10^6)} \cdot (500)^2 = \frac{3.927 \times 250\,000}{6.944 \times 10^6} = 0.1414$$

$$\boxed{S_o \approx 0.141}$$

**Step 5 — Operating Regime Comment:**

A Sommerfeld Number of $0.141$ falls in the **moderately loaded regime** of the Raimondi–Boyd chart. From the standard chart, the corresponding eccentricity ratio is approximately $\varepsilon \approx 0.6$, giving a dimensionless minimum film thickness of $(1 - \varepsilon) \approx 0.4$, so:

$$h_{min} = 0.4 \times 0.12 = 0.048 \text{ mm} = 48 \text{ μm}$$

For typical machined journal surfaces with combined roughness $\sigma \approx 0.5$ to $2$ μm, the lambda ratio is $\Lambda = h_{min}/\sigma \approx 24$ to $96$, which is **comfortably above 3**, confirming **full-film hydrodynamic lubrication**. The bearing is operating safely.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Recurring Failure Modes for $S_o$ Problems:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency in $P$:** Mixing N and mm². Always convert to N/m² (Pa) before substituting.
> 2. **Confusing $c$ and $c/r$:** The symbol $c$ is the linear clearance (m), not the ratio. The dimensionless ratio is $r/c$ or $\psi = c/r$.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in Petroff:** Petroff's friction force uses $F_f = T_f/r$ and load $W = P \cdot 2rL$. The factor of 2 is critical and is a frequent source of mark loss.
> 4. **Sign convention for eccentricity:** $\varepsilon = 0$ means centred (concentric), $\varepsilon = 1$ means the journal touches the bearing. Do not invert the convention.
> 5. **Mixing $\omega$ and $N$:** Use $\omega = 2\pi N/60$ explicitly, or use the $2\pi\mu N$ form, but **never** drop the $2\pi$ silently.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** The Sommerfeld Number is the **master dimensionless group** for hydrodynamic journal bearings: $S_o = (\mu \omega / P)(r/c)^2$.
- **Two equivalent forms:** $S_o = (\mu \omega / P)(r/c)^2$ with $\omega$ in rad/s **OR** $S_o = (2\pi\mu N / P)(r/c)^2$ with $N$ in rev/s.
- **Low $S_o$** → thick film, high load, journal displaced downward, **high friction, high load capacity**.
- **High $S_o$** → thin film, low load, journal nearly centred, **low friction, low load capacity**.
- **Bridge between theory and practice:** $S_o$ is the **only** input required to read the entire Raimondi–Boyd chart family.
- **Petroff's link:** $f_{Petroff} = 2\pi^2 (\mu N / P)(r/c)$ — linear in $r/c$. The **squared** dependence is the key modification that produces the Sommerfeld form.
- **Minimum film thickness:** $h_{min} = c(1 - \varepsilon)$. Always cross-check $\Lambda = h_{min}/\sigma \geq 3$ for full-film lubrication.
- **Units of $P$:** Always Pa (= N/m²) when $\mu$ is in Pa·s and $\omega$ is in rad/s.
- **KTU exam tip:** Numerical answers on the order of $S_o \in [0.01, 1.0]$ are typical for well-designed industrial journal bearings.
- **Raimondi–Boyd outputs:** Eccentricity ratio $\varepsilon$, friction variable $(r/c)f$, flow variable $Q/(rcNL)$, and temperature-rise variable $T c/(\mu N r)$.
- **Design levers to change $S_o$:** Vary the clearance $c$ (most powerful — squared influence), the viscosity grade of the lubricant $\mu$, the bearing length $L$, or the journal diameter $d$.
- **Engineering insight:** $S_o$ is a *stability indicator*. As speed drops, $S_o$ falls and the bearing enters a more loaded regime with thicker films but higher friction losses; this governs safe start-up and shut-down procedures in turbomachinery.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Generated in Bearings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT521 - DESIGN OF TRANSMISSION ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Heat Generated in Bearings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# HEAT GENERATED IN BEARINGS — SECTION 1: CORE CONCEPTUAL FOUNDATION

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In hydrodynamic and boundary-lubricated **journal (sliding contact) bearings**, the relative sliding motion between the rotating journal and the bearing surface is never frictionless. Even when a thin hydrodynamic oil film separates the two surfaces, viscous shearing of the lubricant film dissipates mechanical energy. This dissipated energy is irreversibly converted into **thermal energy (heat)** within the bearing assembly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Generated in a Bearing ($H_g$)** is the rate at which frictional (viscous) work is done at the sliding interface of a journal and its bearing. Mathematically, it is defined as the product of the **coefficient of friction ($\mu$)**, the **radial load on the bearing ($W$)**, and the **sliding velocity of the journal ($V$)**.

$$H_g = \mu \cdot W \cdot V$$

If the heat generated is **not** removed at the same rate by conduction, convection, and radiation through the bearing housing, the temperature of the lubricant rises until it loses its viscosity, the oil film breaks down, and the bearing seizes. Therefore, every journal-bearing design problem in the KTU syllabus revolves around the **Heat Balance Equation**.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "Rubbing Your Hands Together"**
> Imagine you press your palms together firmly (this is the **load W**) and slide them back and forth rapidly (this is the **velocity V**). The faster and harder you rub, the warmer your palms get. This heat is generated *because* of friction. In a bearing, the journal is one palm, the bearing surface is the other, and the oil film is a thin layer of "sweat" trying to keep them apart. To prevent your hands from getting burnt (bearing seizure), you need a fan blowing air across them (heat dissipation) — that fan is the **bearing housing** that radiates heat to the surroundings.

## 1.3 Physical Mechanism — How the Heat Appears

1. The journal rotates inside the bearing at angular velocity $\omega$.
2. The lubricant — typically a mineral or synthetic oil — is dragged into the converging wedge-shaped clearance space.
3. **Viscous shearing** of this oil film produces a tangential frictional force: $F_f = \mu W$.
4. The mechanical power consumed in overcoming this friction is:
   - $P = F_f \cdot V = \mu W V$
5. By the **First Law of Thermodynamics**, this mechanical power *must* manifest as heat flowing into the oil and housing.

## 1.4 Why This Topic is Critical in KTU Examinations

> [!WARNING]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme treats **Heat Balance** as a compulsory step in every journal-bearing design problem. Marks are awarded in Part-B (14-mark) questions for explicitly writing the heat-balance equation, solving for the **oil-outlet temperature $t_o$**, and selecting the **heat-dissipation coefficient $C$** from standard empirical tables. Skipping units (Watts vs. kJ/min) is the single most common reason for losing 1–2 marks per sub-part.

## 1.5 Standard Units and Constants

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard SI Unit | CGS / Mixed Unit |
|:------:|:---------|:----------------:|:----------------:|
| $H_g$ | Heat generated | **Watt (W)** or **J/s** | kJ/min (1 kJ/min = 16.67 W) |
| $\mu$ | Coefficient of friction | dimensionless | dimensionless |
| $W$ | Radial load | **Newton (N)** | kgf (1 kgf = 9.81 N) |
| $V$ | Sliding velocity | **m/s** | m/s |
| $C$ | Heat transfer coefficient | **W/m²·°C** | kJ/(min·m²·°C) |
| $A$ | Heat-dissipating area | **m²** | m² |
| $\Delta T$ | Temperature rise ($t_o - t_i$) | **°C** or **K** | °C |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Two Halves of the Heat-Balance Equation

A bearing is a **steady-flow energy system**. For the lubricant temperature to remain stable (steady-state operation), the rate of heat *in* must equal the rate of heat *out*. The KTU 2024 syllabus expresses this as:

$$H_g \;=\; H_d$$

where:
- $H_g$ → **Heat generated** by friction at the journal interface.
- $H_d$ → **Heat dissipated** by the bearing housing to the surroundings.

## 2.2 Heat Generated — Detailed Logic Steps

**Step 1 — Identify the friction force.**
By Petroff's theory and empirical measurements, the frictional torque opposing journal rotation is:

$$T_f = \mu \cdot W \cdot r$$

**Step 2 — Identify the sliding velocity at the journal surface.**
The journal of radius $r$ rotates at $N$ rps, so the linear surface velocity is:

$$V = \pi D N = 2 \pi r N$$

**Step 3 — Compute the frictional power (heat generated per unit time).**
Power = (Tangential force) × (Velocity):

$$\boxed{H_g = \mu \cdot W \cdot V = \mu \cdot W \cdot \pi D N}$$

**Step 4 — Express in consistent units.**
If $\mu$ is dimensionless, $W$ is in N, and $V$ in m/s, then $H_g$ is in **Watts**.
For exam convenience:

$$1 \; \text{W} = 60 \; \text{J/min} = 0.06 \; \text{kJ/min}$$

## 2.3 Heat Dissipated — Detailed Logic Steps

The bearing housing transfers heat to the ambient air through **natural convection** (and minor radiation). The empirical form of **Newton's Law of Cooling** is used:

$$\boxed{H_d = C \cdot A \cdot (t_o - t_i) = C \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}$$

where:
- $C$ → **Heat transfer coefficient** from the housing to the air.
- $A$ → **Effective heat-dissipating surface area** of the housing (NOT the projected area of the journal; it is the housing's outer surface exposed to air, including any fins).
- $t_o$ → **Average oil-outlet temperature** (or bearing body temperature) in °C.
- $t_i$ → **Ambient (room) temperature** in °C.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU standard value of heat-dissipation coefficient (C):**
> - For a **plain, unfinned bearing housing in still air**: $C \approx 100\ \text{W/m}^2\text{·°C}$ (≈ 6 kJ/min·m²·°C).
> - For a **finned housing in still air**: $C \approx 150\ \text{W/m}^2\text{·°C}$.
> - For a **finned housing with air blast (forced convection)**: $C \approx 300\ \text{W/m}^2\text{·°C}$.
> *(Values as per Shigley/V.B. Bhandari conventions used in KTU textbooks.)*

## 2.4 The Final Heat-Balance Equation

Equating the two halves:

$$\mu \cdot W \cdot \pi D N = C \cdot A \cdot (t_o - t_i)$$

Solving for the **oil-outlet temperature** (a key KTU exam target):

$$\boxed{t_o = t_i + \frac{\mu \cdot W \cdot \pi D N}{C \cdot A}}$$

Solving for the **required heat-dissipating area** (when $t_o$ is fixed by lubrication requirements):

$$\boxed{A = \frac{\mu \cdot W \cdot \pi D N}{C \cdot (t_o - t_i)}}$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE**: For table cells containing absolute values or the `divide` symbol, the vertical bar `|` has been replaced with `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|:--------------:|
| 1 | $H_g = \mu W V$ | Heat generated due to friction | W (J/s) |
| 2 | $V = \pi D N$ | Sliding velocity at journal | m/s |
| 3 | $H_g = \mu W \pi D N$ | Heat generated in terms of rpm | W |
| 4 | $H_d = C A \Delta T$ | Heat dissipated by housing | W |
| 5 | $\mu W \pi D N = C A (t_o - t_i)$ | **Heat balance equation** | W = W |
| 6 | $t_o = t_i + \mu W \pi D N / (C A)$ | Oil-outlet temperature | °C |
| 7 | $A = \mu W \pi D N / [C(t_o - t_i)]$ | Required housing area | m² |
| 8 | $Q = H_g \cdot 60 / (\rho \cdot S \cdot \Delta T)$ | Oil flow rate for cooling | L/min |
| 9 | $H_g = 2 \pi \mu W r N$ | Alternate form (using radius) | W |
| 10 | $\Delta T = H_g / (C A)$ | Pure temperature-rise form | °C |

> **Notation key:** $Q$ → oil flow rate (L/min), $\rho$ → oil density (kg/L), $S$ → specific heat of oil (kJ/kg·°C).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Sizing Bearing Pedestals** — Industrial machine tools use the heat-balance equation to size the **pedestal blocks** and decide whether **cooling fins** are necessary.
2. **Lubricant Selection** — The maximum allowable $t_o$ (typically 60–90 °C for mineral oils) directly determines the viscosity grade selection from the **ISO VG classification chart**.
3. **Forced-Cooling Decisions** — If natural convection cannot dissipate the heat (e.g., in turbine bearings), the design switches to **oil-bath cooling, water-jacketed housings, or oil-mist lubrication**, each with its own upgraded value of $C$.
4. **Predicting Failure** — If the calculated $t_o$ exceeds the oil's flash point or oxidation limit, the bearing is **redesigned** with a larger diameter, lower $L/D$ ratio, or a higher-viscosity oil.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Heat-Generated Equation $H_g = \mu W V$

This derivation explicitly links the **frictional torque at the journal interface** to the **rate of mechanical work done against viscous shear**.

**Step 1 — Define the frictional (tangential) force at the journal surface.**

The empirical frictional force between the journal and the bearing, based on the coefficient of friction $\mu$ acting on the normal load $W$, is:

$$F_f = \mu \cdot W$$

**Step 2 — Define the frictional torque about the journal axis.**

The frictional force acts at the journal's surface, which is at a perpendicular distance equal to the **journal radius $r$** from the axis. Therefore, the resisting torque is:

$$T_f = F_f \cdot r = \mu \cdot W \cdot r$$

**Step 3 — Define the angular velocity of the journal.**

If the journal rotates at $N$ revolutions per second, its angular velocity in rad/s is:

$$\omega = 2 \pi N$$

**Step 4 — Compute the rate of work done (which equals the heat generated per second).**

Power = Torque × Angular velocity:

$$H_g = T_f \cdot \omega = (\mu W r) \cdot (2 \pi N)$$

Since the journal diameter $D = 2r$, we substitute $2r = D$:

$$\boxed{H_g = \mu \cdot W \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot N}$$

This is the **canonical KTU heat-generated formula**. For the velocity form, recall that the linear surface velocity of the journal is $V = \pi D N$, which gives the compact form $H_g = \mu W V$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Heat-Dissipated Equation $H_d = C A \Delta T$

This is an application of **Newton's Law of Cooling**, adapted empirically to bearing housings.

**Step 1 — Heat-flux from a heated surface.**

By Newton's empirical law, the heat-flux $q$ (W/m²) leaving a surface at temperature $t_s$ to ambient air at $t_i$ is proportional to the temperature difference:

$$q = C \cdot (t_s - t_i)$$

Here, $C$ is the **overall heat-transfer coefficient** that lumps together convection and radiation.

**Step 2 — Total heat dissipated by a surface of area $A$.**

Multiplying the heat flux by the effective area gives the total rate of heat loss:

$$H_d = q \cdot A = C \cdot A \cdot (t_s - t_i)$$

**Step 3 — Approximation for bearings.**

In journal-bearing analysis, the bearing body's outer surface temperature $t_s$ is taken equal to the **average oil-outlet temperature $t_o$** (a standard KTU assumption). Therefore:

$$\boxed{H_d = C \cdot A \cdot (t_o - t_i)}$$

## 3.3 Master Heat-Balance Equation — Combined Form

Setting $H_g = H_d$ and substituting:

$$\mu \cdot W \cdot \pi D N = C \cdot A \cdot (t_o - t_i)$$

**Rearranging to solve for oil-outlet temperature** (multiply both sides by 1):

$$t_o - t_i = \frac{\mu W \pi D N}{C A}$$

Add $t_i$ to both sides:

$$\boxed{t_o = t_i + \frac{\mu W \pi D N}{C A}}$$

**Rearranging to solve for required housing area** (when $t_o$ is fixed):

$$C \cdot A \cdot (t_o - t_i) = \mu W \pi D N$$

Divide both sides by $C \cdot (t_o - t_i)$:

$$\boxed{A = \frac{\mu W \pi D N}{C (t_o - t_i)}}$$

## 3.4 Unit-Consistency Check (Worked Conversion)

A frequent KTU question: *"Express 1 kJ/min in Watts."*

We know $1 \text{ kJ} = 1000 \text{ J}$ and $1 \text{ min} = 60 \text{ s}$.

Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
1 \;\frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{min}} &= \frac{1000 \;\text{J}}{60 \;\text{s}} \\
&= 16.667 \;\frac{\text{J}}{\text{s}} \\
&= 16.667 \;\text{W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Reverse check** — convert 1 W to kJ/min:

$$\begin{aligned}
1 \;\text{W} &= 1 \;\frac{\text{J}}{\text{s}} = \frac{1/1000 \;\text{kJ}}{1/60 \;\text{min}} \\
&= \frac{60}{1000} \;\frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{min}} \\
&= 0.06 \;\frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{min}}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.5 Comprehensive Numerical Worked Example (Model Solution)

**Problem Statement:**
A journal bearing has the following data:
- Radial load: $W = 50{,}000 \;\text{N}$
- Journal diameter: $D = 100 \;\text{mm} = 0.1 \;\text{m}$
- Journal speed: $N = 1500 \;\text{rpm} = 25 \;\text{rps}$
- Coefficient of friction: $\mu = 0.015$
- Heat-dissipation coefficient: $C = 150 \;\text{W/m}^2\text{·°C}$
- Effective housing area: $A = 1.5 \;\text{m}^2$
- Ambient temperature: $t_i = 40 \;\text{°C}$

**Find:** (i) Heat generated $H_g$, (ii) Heat dissipated $H_d$, (iii) Oil-outlet temperature $t_o$, (iv) Required area if $t_o$ must not exceed 70 °C.

**Solution:**

**(i) Heat Generated:**

$$\begin{aligned}
H_g &= \mu \cdot W \cdot \pi D N \\
&= 0.015 \times 50{,}000 \times \pi \times 0.1 \times 25 \\
&= 0.015 \times 50{,}000 \times 7.854 \\
&= 0.015 \times 392{,}699 \\
&= 5890.5 \;\text{W} \\
&\approx 5.89 \;\text{kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Heat Dissipated (with $\Delta T$ as unknown):**
$H_d = C \cdot A \cdot (t_o - t_i) = 150 \times 1.5 \times (t_o - 40)$

**(iii) Oil-Outlet Temperature from Heat Balance:**

$$H_g = H_d$$

$$5890.5 = 150 \times 1.5 \times (t_o - 40)$$

$$5890.5 = 225 \times (t_o - 40)$$

$$t_o - 40 = \frac{5890.5}{225} = 26.18$$

$$t_o = 40 + 26.18 = 66.18 \;\text{°C}$$

**(iv) Required Area for $t_o \leq 70 \;\text{°C}$:**

$$A = \frac{H_g}{C (t_o - t_i)} = \frac{5890.5}{150 \times (70 - 40)} = \frac{5890.5}{4500} = 1.309 \;\text{m}^2$$

**Result:** The actual area (1.5 m²) is sufficient, since the required area (1.309 m²) is smaller. The bearing operates safely with $t_o \approx 66.2 \; \text{°C}$.

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Quantity to Solve For

| Given in the Question | Use This Rearranged Form |
|:----------------------|:--------------------------|
| $W, D, N, \mu, C, A, t_i$ → Find $t_o$ | $t_o = t_i + \dfrac{\mu W \pi D N}{C A}$ |
| $W, D, N, \mu, C, t_i, t_o$ → Find $A$ | $A = \dfrac{\mu W \pi D N}{C (t_o - t_i)}$ |
| $W, D, N, C, A, t_i, t_o$ → Find $\mu$ | $\mu = \dfrac{C A (t_o - t_i)}{W \pi D N}$ |
| $\mu, W, V$ → Find $H_g$ | $H_g = \mu W V$ |
| $C, A, \Delta T$ → Find $H_d$ | $H_d = C A \Delta T$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Heat Flow Architecture (Mermaid Diagram)

The diagram below traces the **functional architecture** of heat flow in a journal bearing: starting from the input mechanical power, branching into heat generation, going through the heat-balance node, and finally splitting into dissipation paths and oil-flow requirements.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input1[Input W Radial Load N] --> hgA[Step A Compute Sliding Velocity V pi D N]
    input2[Input mu Coefficient of Friction] --> hgA
    hgA --> hgB[Step B Heat Generated Hg mu W V]
    hgB --> hbNode[Heat Balance Node Hg equals Hd]

    input3[Input C Heat Transfer Coefficient] --> hdA[Step C Compute Temperature Rise dT]
    input4[Input A Housing Area m2] --> hdA
    input5[Input ti Ambient Temperature C] --> hdA
    hdA --> hdB[Step D Heat Dissipated Hd C A dT]
    hdB --> hbNode

    hbNode --> decision{Is to within Limit 60 to 90 C}
    decision -- YES --> safeNode[SAFE OPERATION Steady State Reached]
    decision -- NO --> unsafeNode[UNSAFE Temperature Exceeds Oil Limit]

    unsafeNode --> redesign1[Increase Housing Area A]
    unsafeNode --> redesign2[Add Cooling Fins Increase C]
    unsafeNode --> redesign3[Switch to Forced Oil Cooling]
    unsafeNode --> redesign4[Reduce Speed or Load N W]

    safeNode --> outputQ[Output Oil Flow Rate Q]
    input6[Input Oil Density rho Specific Heat S] --> outputQ
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Inputs, Process, Outputs Mapping

| Block | Inputs | Process / Formula | Outputs |
|:------|:-------|:------------------|:--------|
| A — Velocity | $D, N$ | $V = \pi D N$ | $V$ in m/s |
| B — Heat Gen | $\mu, W, V$ | $H_g = \mu W V$ | $H_g$ in W |
| C — Heat Diss | $C, A, t_i, t_o$ | $H_d = C A (t_o - t_i)$ | $H_d$ in W |
| D — Balance | $H_g, H_d$ | $H_g = H_d$ | Constraint satisfied |
| E — Temperature | All above | $t_o = t_i + H_g/(C A)$ | $t_o$ in °C |
| F — Oil Flow | $H_g, \rho, S, \Delta T$ | $Q = H_g \cdot 60 / (\rho S \Delta T)$ | $Q$ in L/min |

## 4.3 Logical Dependency Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Load W] --> C[Heat Generated Hg]
    B[Speed N] --> D[Velocity V pi D N]
    D --> C
    M[Coeff of Friction mu] --> C
    C --> E[Heat Balance Hg equals Hd]
    F[Heat Transfer Coeff C] --> G[Heat Dissipated Hd]
    P[Housing Area A] --> G
    T[Ambient ti] --> G
    G --> E
    E --> H[Oil Outlet Temperature to]
    E --> I[Required Area A]
    E --> J[Required C finned vs plain]
```

## 4.4 Visual Interpretation Notes

- The **Heat Balance Node** is the *pivotal* logical checkpoint — every KTU design problem funnels through it.
- The **Decision Diamond** in Section 4.1 reflects the engineering judgment a designer must make: is the calculated $t_o$ within the lubricant's safe range (typically 60–90 °C)?
- The four **redesign pathways** mirror the four levers a designer can pull to fix an overheating bearing, in order of preference: (1) increase $A$ by adding fins, (2) increase $C$ by adding an air blast, (3) introduce a **cooled oil circulation loop**, (4) revisit the operating conditions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2-Mark Conceptual, 3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> *Each Part A question below is a 3-mark direct-concept question. Model answers are concise (60–80 words) and aligned with KTU valuation key patterns.*

---

**Q.A1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
*Define "heat generated in a bearing" and state its formula in terms of coefficient of friction, load, and journal velocity.*

**Model Answer:**
Heat generated in a bearing is the rate at which frictional work is done at the journal-bearing interface due to viscous shearing of the lubricant film. It equals the product of the coefficient of friction $\mu$, the radial load $W$, and the journal surface velocity $V$:

$$H_g = \mu \cdot W \cdot V \quad (\text{in Watts})$$

Equivalent form using rpm: $H_g = \mu W \pi D N$.

---

**Q.A2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
*What is the heat-balance equation of a journal bearing? Why is it important?*

**Model Answer:**
The heat-balance equation states that, at steady-state, the heat generated by friction equals the heat dissipated by the bearing housing:

$$\mu W \pi D N = C A (t_o - t_i)$$

It is important because it determines the **oil-outlet temperature** and the **required housing area**. Without it, the designer cannot ensure that the lubricant temperature remains within the safe range, and the bearing may seize due to oil-film breakdown.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **MOST COMMON MISTAKE #1**: Forgetting to convert $N$ from rpm to rps (divide by 60) before using $V = \pi D N$. Loss: **2 marks**.
> - **MOST COMMON MISTAKE #2**: Using $C$ in kJ/(min·m²·°C) units while $H_g$ is computed in Watts. Always confirm unit consistency *first*. Loss: **2 marks**.
> - **MOST COMMON MISTAKE #3**: Omitting the statement of the heat-balance equation explicitly. The KTU valuation key demands the symbolic equation $H_g = H_d$ to be written *before* substitution. Loss: **1 mark**.
> - **MOST COMMON MISTAKE #4**: Using the **projected area** of the journal $\pi D L$ as the heat-dissipating area $A$. The correct $A$ is the **outer surface area of the bearing housing**, not the journal. Loss: **2 marks**.

---

### **Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style | Module 4, Heat Generated in Bearings]**

> A journal bearing supports a radial load of $W = 40{,}000 \;\text{N}$. The journal diameter is $D = 80 \;\text{mm}$ and it rotates at $N = 1440 \;\text{rpm}$. The coefficient of friction for the bearing is $\mu = 0.012$. The ambient temperature is $t_i = 35 \;\text{°C}$, and the heat-dissipation coefficient for a finned housing with still air is $C = 150 \;\text{W/m}^2\text{·°C}$. The effective housing area is $A = 1.0 \;\text{m}^2$.
>
> **(a)** Calculate the heat generated by friction and the heat dissipated by the housing. State the heat-balance equation. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Determine the oil-outlet temperature $t_o$. Comment on whether the bearing is safe, and suggest two design modifications if it is not. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Given Data:**
- $W = 40{,}000 \;\text{N}$
- $D = 80 \;\text{mm} = 0.08 \;\text{m}$
- $N = 1440 \;\text{rpm} = 1440/60 = 24 \;\text{rps}$
- $\mu = 0.012$
- $t_i = 35 \;\text{°C}$
- $C = 150 \;\text{W/m}^2\text{·°C}$
- $A = 1.0 \;\text{m}^2$

---

**Part (a) — Heat Generated and Heat Dissipated (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Sliding velocity of the journal (1 mark):**

$$V = \pi D N = \pi \times 0.08 \times 24 = 6.032 \;\text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Heat generated (2 marks):**

$$H_g = \mu W V = 0.012 \times 40{,}000 \times 6.032$$

$$H_g = 0.012 \times 241{,}274 = 2895.3 \;\text{W} \approx 2.895 \;\text{kW}$$

**Step 3 — Heat-balance equation (1 mark):**

$$H_g = H_d \implies \mu W \pi D N = C A (t_o - t_i)$$

**Step 4 — Express heat dissipated symbolically (3 marks — the numerical $H_d$ value requires $t_o$ which is part (b)):**

$$H_d = C \cdot A \cdot (t_o - t_i) = 150 \times 1.0 \times (t_o - 35) = 150 (t_o - 35) \;\text{W}$$

---

**Part (b) — Oil-Outlet Temperature and Safety Comment (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Apply the heat-balance equation (2 marks):**

$$H_g = H_d$$

$$2895.3 = 150 \times (t_o - 35)$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $t_o$ (2 marks):**

$$t_o - 35 = \frac{2895.3}{150} = 19.30$$

$$t_o = 35 + 19.30 = 54.30 \;\text{°C}$$

**Step 3 — Safety comment (2 marks):**

The calculated $t_o = 54.3 \;\text{°C}$ is **well within the safe operating range** of typical mineral oils (60–90 °C upper limit for short-duration operation, with an ideal steady-state around 50–70 °C). Therefore, the bearing is operating safely under the given conditions.

**Step 4 — Two design modifications (1 mark):**
Even though the current design is safe, future load increases could push $t_o$ beyond limits. The two standard modifications are:
1. **Increase the effective housing area $A$** by adding more cooling fins to the pedestal block.
2. **Increase the heat-dissipation coefficient $C$** by switching to forced-air cooling (air blast) or to a water-jacketed housing.

---

### **Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative]**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style | Module 4, Heat Generated in Bearings]**

> A hydrodynamic journal bearing has the following data: journal diameter $D = 120 \;\text{mm}$, length $L = 150 \;\text{mm}$, radial load $W = 30{,}000 \;\text{N}$, speed $N = 900 \;\text{rpm}$, and coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.010$. The ambient temperature is $t_i = 30 \;\text{°C}$, and the maximum permissible oil-outlet temperature is $t_{o,\max} = 75 \;\text{°C}$. Assume a heat-dissipation coefficient of $C = 100 \;\text{W/m}^2\text{·°C}$ (plain housing, still air).
>
> **(a)** Compute the heat generated by the bearing and the required effective housing area to maintain $t_o$ at or below 75 °C. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** If a finned housing ($C = 200 \;\text{W/m}^2\text{·°C}$) is used instead, find the percentage reduction in the required housing area. State one practical advantage of using fins. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Given Data:**
- $D = 120 \;\text{mm} = 0.12 \;\text{m}$
- $L = 150 \;\text{mm} = 0.15 \;\text{m}$
- $W = 30{,}000 \;\text{N}$
- $N = 900 \;\text{rpm} = 15 \;\text{rps}$
- $\mu = 0.010$
- $t_i = 30 \;\text{°C}$
- $t_{o,\max} = 75 \;\text{°C}$
- $C_{\text{plain}} = 100 \;\text{W/m}^2\text{·°C}$
- $C_{\text{finned}} = 200 \;\text{W/m}^2\text{·°C}$

---

**Part (a) — Heat Generated and Required Area (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Heat generated (3 marks):**

$$H_g = \mu W \pi D N = 0.010 \times 30{,}000 \times \pi \times 0.12 \times 15$$

$$H_g = 0.010 \times 30{,}000 \times 5.6549 = 1696.46 \;\text{W}$$

**Step 2 — Required housing area for plain housing (4 marks):**

$$A = \frac{H_g}{C (t_o - t_i)} = \frac{1696.46}{100 \times (75 - 30)} = \frac{1696.46}{4500} = 0.377 \;\text{m}^2$$

**Result:** Required area $A \approx 0.377 \;\text{m}^2$ (using a plain housing).

---

**Part (b) — Finned Housing Comparison (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Required area with finned housing (3 marks):**

$$A_{\text{finned}} = \frac{H_g}{C_{\text{finned}} (t_o - t_i)} = \frac{1696.46}{200 \times (75 - 30)} = \frac{1696.46}{9000} = 0.1885 \;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Reduction in area (2 marks):**

$$\Delta A = 0.377 - 0.1885 = 0.1885 \;\text{m}^2$$

$$\text{Percentage reduction} = \frac{0.1885}{0.377} \times 100 = 50\%$$

**Step 3 — Practical advantage of fins (2 marks):**

Fins **increase the effective surface area for heat dissipation** *without* enlarging the bearing envelope. They are a **compact, low-cost, and maintenance-free** method of increasing the heat-transfer coefficient. They are particularly useful where the bearing must fit in a confined space and forced cooling (fans, water jackets) is impractical.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION POINTS — Heat Generated in Bearings**

- **Core definition:** $H_g = \mu W V = \mu W \pi D N$, with units in **Watts (J/s)**.
- **Heat-balance master equation:** $\mu W \pi D N = C A (t_o - t_i)$.
- **Always convert $N$** from **rpm → rps** by dividing by 60.
- **Always state the symbolic heat-balance equation** before substituting numerical values — the KTU key rewards this.
- **Standard $C$ values to memorize:**
  - Plain housing, still air → $C = 100 \;\text{W/m}^2\text{·°C}$
  - Finned housing, still air → $C = 150 \;\text{W/m}^2\text{·°C}$
  - Finned housing, air blast → $C = 300 \;\text{W/m}^2\text{·°C}$
- **Area $A$** is the **outer surface area of the bearing housing** (not the journal) exposed to ambient air.
- **Typical safe oil-outlet temperature** $t_o$: 50–90 °C for mineral oils; do not exceed the manufacturer's oxidation limit.
- **Unit conversions to memorize:**
  - $1 \;\text{W} = 0.06 \;\text{kJ/min}$
  - $1 \;\text{kJ/min} = 16.667 \;\text{W}$
- **Four design levers** to lower $t_o$ if it is too high:
  1. Increase $A$ (add fins, larger pedestal).
  2. Increase $C$ (forced air or water jacket).
  3. Reduce $\mu$ (better lubricant, improved surface finish, larger clearance).
  4. Reduce $W$ or $N$ (redesign operating conditions).
- **Heat generated is ALWAYS positive** — the frictional work opposes motion, so power is dissipated, not absorbed.
- **In steady-state design**, the time derivative of oil temperature is zero, so $H_g = H_d$ is the equilibrium condition.
- **The Petroff equation** gives $\mu$ for lightly loaded bearings: $\mu = 2\pi^2 (r/c) (\mu_v / W) N$ — use it when the problem does not provide $\mu$ directly.
- **Common pitfall:** using $\pi D L$ (projected journal area) as $A$. This is WRONG; $A$ is the housing's outer surface, including fins if any.
- **Oil-flow rate for cooling** (advanced application): $Q = 60 H_g / (\rho S \Delta T)$, where $\rho$ is in kg/L, $S$ in kJ/kg·°C, $H_g$ in W, and $Q$ in L/min.
- **KTU exam-favourite question pattern:** "Calculate $t_o$ for the given bearing. Is it safe? If not, what modification will you suggest?" — this exact framing appears in nearly every recent KTU paper.
- **Mnemonic for the four terms in the heat-balance equation:** **"MWPN = CAT"** (Mu × W × Pi × D × N = C × A × (t_o − t_i)).

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Heat Generated in Bearings**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Dissipated by Bearings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT521 - DESIGN OF TRANSMISSION ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Heat Dissipated by Bearings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Sliding Contact Bearings** (Journal Bearings), **Heat Dissipated by a Bearing** is defined as the rate at which thermal energy generated due to viscous friction between the rotating journal and the bearing surface is rejected to the surrounding environment (ambient air, oil bath, or external cooling medium) so as to maintain thermal equilibrium.

Mathematically, the steady-state **Heat Balance Equation** for a bearing states:

$$\text{Heat Generated} = \text{Heat Dissipated}$$

The rate of heat dissipation is governed by **Newton's Law of Cooling**:

$$Q_{dissipated} = C \cdot A \cdot (T_b - T_a)$$

Where:
- $Q_{dissipated}$ = Heat dissipated per second, in **Watts (W)** or **J/s**
- $C$ = Heat dissipation coefficient, in **W/(m²·°C)**
- $A$ = Effective heat-dissipating surface area of the bearing housing, in **m²**
- $T_b$ = Temperature of the bearing surface (oil/outer housing), in **°C**
- $T_a$ = Ambient (atmospheric) temperature, in **°C**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The topic of "Heat Dissipated by Bearings" is the **final conceptual step** in the design of a hydrodynamic journal bearing. After selecting a bearing based on load capacity, the designer **must verify** that the temperature rise $(\Delta T = T_b - T_a)$ does not exceed permissible limits (typically **50°C to 60°C** for industrial bearings) to prevent oil-film breakdown.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are **cycling downhill with your hand brakes partially engaged**. The brake pads rub against the rim and the entire brake system becomes hot. Eventually, the brake must release this heat to the air, otherwise it will overheat and fail.

A **journal bearing works exactly like this**:

1. **The journal (shaft)** is rotating at high speed.
2. The **thin oil film** between the journal and the bearing acts as the "brake pad," resisting motion through **viscous shear**.
3. This resistance **converts mechanical energy into heat**.
4. If this heat is not removed, the **oil temperature rises**, viscosity drops, the film thickness reduces, and finally the bearing **seizes** (welds together due to metal-to-metal contact).

> [!NOTE]
> **The Analogy Expanded:**
> - The **brake pads** = the **oil film** in the bearing.
> - The **rim heat** = **frictional heat** generated in the bearing.
> - The **cool air blowing over the brake** = **heat dissipation to surroundings** (convection + radiation).
> - The **brake's maximum temperature** = **permissible oil temperature** in the bearing.

The bearing acts as a **thermal machine**: it **generates** heat at a rate determined by friction torque and speed, and it **rejects** heat at a rate determined by the housing surface area and the convection coefficient. **Design is correct only when the rate of generation does not exceed the rate of dissipation** — otherwise the bearing will cook itself.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Industrial Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Heat dissipation coefficient ($C$) | **140 – 175** (still air) | W/(m²·°C) |
| Heat dissipation coefficient ($C$) | **210 – 290** (still air, finned housing) | W/(m²·°C) |
| Heat dissipation coefficient ($C$) | **350 – 520** (forced air / circulating oil) | W/(m²·°C) |
| Permissible temperature rise ($\Delta T$) | **40 – 60** | °C |
| Permissible oil-in temperature ($T_{oil,in}$) | **40 – 60** | °C |
| Permissible oil-out temperature ($T_{oil,out}$) | **80 – 100** | °C |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these numbers matter in KTU exams:** The numerical value of $C$ is **not given in the question paper**; you must **memorize** the standard value of $C = 175 \text{ W/(m²·°C)}$ for a **plain, unfinned bearing in still air**, which is the most common case tested.

---

## 1.4 Why Heat Dissipation is a Critical Design Criterion

In hydrodynamic bearing design, **four** design criteria are checked:

1. **Sommerfeld Number** (for proper hydrodynamic film formation)
2. **Reynolds Number** (for laminar flow, $Re < 2000$)
3. **Minimum Film Thickness** ($h_0 \geq$ certain minimum)
4. ✅ **Heat Dissipation** (to keep $\Delta T \leq 50°C$)

If the heat dissipation criterion fails, the bearing may be:
- **Re-designed with fins** (to increase $A$)
- **Re-designed with external cooling** (to increase $C$)
- **Re-selected** with a larger diameter or shorter length (to lower the friction torque)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Heat Generation in a Journal Bearing

When a journal rotates inside a bearing, a **hydrodynamic oil film** is established. The viscosity of the oil resists the relative motion of the journal surface, producing a **shear stress** $\tau$ at every point of the film.

The viscous friction torque $T_f$ is the integral of this shear stress over the journal surface:

$$T_f = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \mu \cdot \frac{U}{h_0} \cdot (2\pi r L) \cdot r = \frac{\mu \cdot \pi^2 \cdot r^3 \cdot L \cdot N}{30 \cdot h_0}$$

Where:
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of the lubricant (Pa·s or N·s/m²)
- $U$ = surface velocity of the journal = $\pi d N / 60$ (m/s)
- $h_0$ = minimum film thickness (m)
- $r$ = radius of journal (m)
- $L$ = length of bearing (m)
- $N$ = journal speed (rpm)

The **rate of heat generated** is the product of this friction torque and the angular velocity:

$$Q_{generated} = T_f \cdot \omega = T_f \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

In standard SI units, this yields power in **Watts**.

---

## 2.2 The Physics of Heat Dissipation

The housing of the bearing radiates and convects heat to the surroundings. The governing equation is Newton's empirical law of cooling:

$$Q_{dissipated} = C \cdot A \cdot (T_b - T_a)$$

This equation assumes:
1. **Convective + radiative** heat transfer combined into a single coefficient $C$.
2. **Steady-state** temperature ($T_b$ is constant with time).
3. **Uniform** housing temperature.
4. The **entire external surface** of the bearing housing participates in heat transfer.

---

## 2.3 Heat Balance — The Governing Design Equation

In steady state, the heat generated must equal the heat dissipated:

$$T_f \cdot \omega = C \cdot A \cdot (T_b - T_a)$$

The **temperature rise** is therefore:

$$\Delta T = T_b - T_a = \frac{T_f \cdot \omega}{C \cdot A}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule:** The calculated $\Delta T$ must be $\leq 50°C$ (or 60°C, depending on the assumed value of $C$). If $\Delta T$ exceeds this, the bearing design is **rejected**, and the engineer must either (a) reduce friction torque, or (b) increase heat dissipation capacity.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_{gen} = T_f \cdot \omega = T_f \cdot \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Heat generated per second (W) |
| 2 | $T_f = \dfrac{\mu \cdot \pi^2 \cdot d^3 \cdot L \cdot N}{480 \cdot h_0}$ | Petroff's / viscous friction torque (N·m) |
| 3 | $Q_{diss} = C \cdot A \cdot (T_b - T_a)$ | Heat dissipated per second (W) |
| 4 | $\Delta T = \dfrac{T_f \cdot \omega}{C \cdot A}$ | Temperature rise in bearing (°C) |
| 5 | $A = \pi d L + \dfrac{\pi d^2}{2}$ | Total surface area of plain cylindrical housing (m²) |
| 6 | $U = \dfrac{\pi d N}{60}$ | Journal surface velocity (m/s) |
| 7 | $h_0 = r(1 - \epsilon) = r \cdot c(1 - \epsilon)$ | Minimum film thickness (m) |
| 8 | $C \approx 175$ (plain), $\approx 290$ (finned) | Standard heat dissipation coefficient W/(m²·°C) |
| 9 | $\Delta T \leq 50°C$ | Permissible temperature rise (industrial norm) |

> [!WARNING]
> In the **table cells above**, the column separators used inside the formulas (such as $\dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$) are written as fractions using LaTeX, **not** the vertical pipe `|`. This is to keep the markdown table parser happy.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Heat dissipation analysis is essential in:

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Turbo-machinery** | High-speed turbine bearings ($N = 10,000$ to $50,000$ rpm) |
| **Automotive engines** | Crank-shaft main bearings, connecting rod bearings |
| **Heavy industry** | Rolling mill bearings, gear box shafts |
| **Aerospace** | Jet engine accessory drives |
| **Marine propulsion** | Stern-tube bearings of ships |
| **Electric motors** | Rotor shaft bearings |

In all these applications, **thermal management** is as critical as mechanical load capacity. A bearing that is hydrodynamically perfect but **thermally over-stressed** will fail within minutes of operation.

---

## 2.6 Factors Affecting Heat Dissipation

| Factor | Effect on $Q_{diss}$ |
|---|---|
| ↑ Surface area $A$ (longer $L$, larger $d$, fins) | ↑ $Q_{diss}$ |
| ↑ Heat dissipation coefficient $C$ (forced air, water cooling) | ↑ $Q_{diss}$ |
| ↑ Permitted $\Delta T$ (better oil) | ↑ $Q_{diss}$ |
| ↓ Friction torque $T_f$ (lower $\mu$, larger $h_0$) | ↓ $Q_{gen}$ |
| ↓ Journal speed $N$ | ↓ $Q_{gen}$ (since $Q_{gen} \propto N^2$) |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Heat Dissipation Equation from Newton's Law of Cooling

Newton's Law of Cooling states that the rate of heat loss from a body is proportional to the temperature difference between the body and the surroundings.

**Step 1:** Write the proportional statement.

$$Q_{diss} \propto (T_b - T_a)$$

**Step 2:** Introduce a proportionality constant $h_c$ (convective heat transfer coefficient).

$$Q_{diss} = h_c \cdot A \cdot (T_b - T_a)$$

**Step 3:** Add the radiative heat loss term. By Stefan-Boltzmann law, radiative heat loss is:

$$Q_{rad} = \sigma \cdot \varepsilon \cdot A \cdot (T_b^4 - T_a^4)$$

**Step 4:** Linearize the radiation term at typical operating temperatures using $\Delta T = T_b - T_a \ll T_b$. Expand $T_b^4 - T_a^4$ using binomial expansion.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_b^4 - T_a^4 &= T_a^4 \left[\left(1 + \frac{\Delta T}{T_a}\right)^4 - 1\right] \\
&\approx T_a^4 \left[1 + 4 \cdot \frac{\Delta T}{T_a} - 1\right] \\
&= 4 T_a^3 \cdot \Delta T
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Combine convection and linearized radiation into a single coefficient.

$$C = h_c + 4 \sigma \varepsilon T_a^3$$

**Step 6:** Final compact equation.

$$Q_{diss} = C \cdot A \cdot (T_b - T_a)$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Friction Torque (Viscous)

Consider an elemental ring on the journal surface at radius $r$, width $dl$ along the axis. The shear stress on this element is:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{U}{h_0} = \mu \cdot \frac{\pi d N}{60 \cdot h_0}$$

The elemental force on this ring is $dF = \tau \cdot (2\pi r \cdot dl)$ and the elemental torque is $dT_f = dF \cdot r$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_f &= \int_0^L \mu \cdot \frac{U}{h_0} \cdot 2\pi r \cdot r \, dl \\
&= \frac{2 \pi \mu U r^2 L}{h_0} \\
&= \frac{2 \pi \mu (\pi d N/60) (d/2)^2 L}{h_0} \\
&= \frac{\mu \pi^2 d^3 L N}{480 \cdot h_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **viscous friction torque** for a thin-film journal bearing.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Heat Generated

**Step 1:** Power = Torque × Angular velocity.

$$Q_{gen} = T_f \cdot \omega$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{gen} &= \frac{\mu \pi^2 d^3 L N}{480 \cdot h_0} \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60} \\
&= \frac{\mu \pi^3 d^3 L N^2}{14400 \cdot h_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Express in standard form. Since $U = \pi d N / 60$:

$$Q_{gen} = \frac{\mu \pi d L U^2}{2 h_0}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important KTU takeaway:** Heat generated in a bearing is proportional to $N^2$ (since both $T_f \propto N$ and $\omega \propto N$). This is why **high-speed bearings** have severe heat dissipation problems.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Surface Area of a Plain Bearing Housing

A standard journal bearing housing is a **cylinder** of diameter $d$ and length $L$, with the journal projecting from both ends.

The exposed surface area is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= A_{curved} + A_{two\ ends} \\
&= (\pi d \cdot L) + 2 \cdot \left(\frac{\pi d^2}{4}\right) \\
&= \pi d L + \frac{\pi d^2}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two end-faces together form a complete circle of diameter $d$ (since each end is a **semicircle** — the other semicircle is occupied by the journal). So total end area = $\frac{\pi d^2}{4} + \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi d^2}{2}$.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Combining All Equations

The complete step-by-step analytical chain for **KTU numerical problems**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Compute surface velocity} &\quad U = \frac{\pi d N}{60} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2: Compute friction torque} &\quad T_f = \frac{\mu \pi^2 d^3 L N}{480 \cdot h_0} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 3: Compute heat generated} &\quad Q_{gen} = T_f \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 4: Compute housing area} &\quad A = \pi d L + \frac{\pi d^2}{2} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 5: Compute temperature rise} &\quad \Delta T = \frac{Q_{gen}}{C \cdot A} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 6: Verify} &\quad \Delta T \leq 50°C
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.6 Worked Example (Kerala KTU 14-mark standard)

**Problem:** A journal bearing has the following data:
- Journal diameter: $d = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
- Bearing length: $L = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.08 \text{ m}$
- Speed: $N = 1500 \text{ rpm}$
- Radial load: $W = 12,000 \text{ N}$
- Viscosity of oil: $\mu = 0.025 \text{ N·s/m}^2$
- Minimum film thickness: $h_0 = 25 \text{ μm} = 25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$
- Ambient temperature: $T_a = 30°C$
- Heat dissipation coefficient: $C = 175 \text{ W/(m}^2 \cdot °C)$

**Find:** (a) Friction torque, (b) Heat generated, (c) Heat dissipated at steady state, (d) Temperature rise, (e) Whether the bearing is safe.

### Solution:

**Step (a): Friction Torque**

$$T_f = \frac{\mu \pi^2 d^3 L N}{480 \cdot h_0} = \frac{0.025 \times \pi^2 \times (0.1)^3 \times 0.08 \times 1500}{480 \times 25 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 0.025 \times 9.8696 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.08 \times 1500 \\
&= 0.025 \times 9.8696 \times 10^{-3} \times 120 \\
&= 0.025 \times 1.18435 \\
&= 0.02961 \text{ N·m} \cdot \text{min} \\[4pt]
\text{Denominator} &= 480 \times 25 \times 10^{-6} = 0.012 \\[4pt]
T_f &= \frac{0.02961}{0.012} = 2.4675 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (b): Heat Generated**

$$Q_{gen} = T_f \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60} = 2.4675 \times \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{gen} &= 2.4675 \times 157.08 \\
&= 387.55 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (c): Heat Dissipated at Steady State**

In steady state, $Q_{diss} = Q_{gen} = 387.55 \text{ W}$.

**Step (d): Housing Surface Area**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \pi d L + \frac{\pi d^2}{2} \\
&= \pi (0.1)(0.08) + \frac{\pi (0.1)^2}{2} \\
&= 0.02513 + 0.01571 \\
&= 0.04084 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (e): Temperature Rise**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T &= \frac{Q_{gen}}{C \cdot A} = \frac{387.55}{175 \times 0.04084} \\
&= \frac{387.55}{7.147} \\
&= 54.22 °C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (f): Verification**

$$\Delta T = 54.22°C > 50°C \implies \text{Bearing is NOT thermally safe}$$

**Design Modification Required:**
- Add fins to housing (increase $A$),
- Use forced air cooling (increase $C$ to 290 W/(m²·°C)), or
- Use lower-viscosity oil to reduce $T_f$.

### Re-design with forced air cooling ($C = 290$):

$$\Delta T_{new} = \frac{387.55}{290 \times 0.04084} = \frac{387.55}{11.84} = 32.7°C \;\;\checkmark \text{ SAFE}$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Define the term "Heat Dissipation Coefficient" as applied to a journal bearing. State its typical value for a plain bearing in still air.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Heat Dissipation Coefficient** ($C$) is defined as the rate at which heat is rejected per unit surface area of the bearing housing per unit temperature difference between the bearing surface and the ambient air. It has units of **W/(m²·°C)** and lumps together the effects of both **convection** and **radiation**.

For a **plain cylindrical bearing in still air**, the standard value used in design is:

$$C = 175 \text{ W/(m}^2 \cdot °C)$$

For **finned bearings in still air**, $C = 290 \text{ W/(m}^2 \cdot °C)$, and for **forced air circulation**, $C = 350 \text{ to } 520 \text{ W/(m}^2 \cdot °C)$.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**State the steady-state heat balance equation for a journal bearing and explain its physical significance.**

**Model Answer:**

The steady-state heat balance equation is:

$$\boxed{T_f \cdot \omega = C \cdot A \cdot (T_b - T_a)}$$

Where $T_f \cdot \omega$ is the rate of **heat generated** by viscous friction, and $C \cdot A \cdot (T_b - T_a)$ is the rate of **heat dissipated** to the surroundings.

**Physical Significance:** In steady state, the bearing temperature becomes constant, meaning **all the heat being generated by friction must be rejected to the environment at the same rate**. If this balance is not achieved, the bearing temperature will rise continuously, eventually leading to **oil film breakdown and seizure**.

---

## 5.2 KTU Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**A hydrodynamic journal bearing has the following specifications:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Journal diameter ($d$) | 120 mm |
| Bearing length ($L$) | 90 mm |
| Speed ($N$) | 1800 rpm |
| Radial load ($W$) | 20,000 N |
| Viscosity of oil ($\mu$) | 0.030 N·s/m² |
| Minimum film thickness ($h_0$) | 30 μm |
| Ambient temperature ($T_a$) | 32°C |
| Heat dissipation coefficient ($C$) | 175 W/(m²·°C) |

**(a)** Calculate the **friction torque** and **heat generated** by the bearing.

**(b)** Determine the **temperature rise** of the bearing and check whether the design is thermally safe. If unsafe, suggest two practical design modifications.

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Substitute the given data in the friction torque equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_f &= \frac{\mu \pi^2 d^3 L N}{480 \cdot h_0} \\
&= \frac{0.030 \times \pi^2 \times (0.120)^3 \times 0.090 \times 1800}{480 \times 30 \times 10^{-6}} \\[4pt]
\text{Numerator} &= 0.030 \times 9.8696 \times 1.728 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.090 \times 1800 \\
&= 0.030 \times 9.8696 \times 1.728 \times 10^{-3} \times 162 \\
&= 0.030 \times 2.762 \\
&= 0.0829 \text{ (combined)} \\[4pt]
\text{Denominator} &= 480 \times 30 \times 10^{-6} = 0.0144 \\[4pt]
T_f &= \frac{0.0829}{0.0144} = 5.76 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute heat generated.**

$$Q_{gen} = T_f \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60} = 5.76 \times \frac{2\pi \times 1800}{60}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{gen} &= 5.76 \times 188.5 \\
&= 1085.7 \text{ W} \;\;\approx \mathbf{1086 \text{ W}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Compute housing surface area.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \pi d L + \frac{\pi d^2}{2} \\
&= \pi (0.120)(0.090) + \frac{\pi (0.120)^2}{2} \\
&= 0.03393 + 0.02262 \\
&= 0.05655 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply heat balance and solve for $\Delta T$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T &= \frac{Q_{gen}}{C \cdot A} = \frac{1085.7}{175 \times 0.05655} \\
&= \frac{1085.7}{9.896} \\
&= 109.7 °C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Check safety.**

$$\Delta T = 109.7°C \gg 50°C \implies \text{Design is UNSAFE}$$

**Step 4: Practical Design Modifications:**

1. **Add cooling fins** to the bearing housing, increasing the effective area $A$ and raising $C$ to **290 W/(m²·°C)**.

2. **Use forced air cooling** (a small fan blowing air over the housing), raising $C$ to **400 W/(m²·°C)**.

3. **Use a lower viscosity oil** to reduce friction torque $T_f$ and hence $Q_{gen}$.

4. **Use a water-cooled jacket** surrounding the bearing for very high heat loads.

After applying modification 1 ($C = 290$ W/(m²·°C)):

$$\Delta T_{new} = \frac{1085.7}{290 \times 0.05655} = 66.2°C \;\;(\text{still high})$$

After applying modification 2 ($C = 400$):

$$\Delta T_{new} = \frac{1085.7}{400 \times 0.05655} = 48.0°C \;\;\checkmark \text{ SAFE}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a **neat sketch**, describe the **mechanism of heat generation** in a hydrodynamic journal bearing. Derive the expression for **heat generated per second**.

**(b)** A bearing has diameter 150 mm, length 100 mm, and operates at 1000 rpm. The viscosity of oil is 0.020 N·s/m² and the minimum film thickness is 40 μm. If the heat dissipation coefficient is 175 W/(m²·°C) and ambient temperature is 25°C, determine the **steady-state temperature of the bearing oil**.

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Sketch (described in words for textual representation):**

```
            Oil In
              ↓
        ┌───────────┐
        │  ◯─────◯  │ ← Bearing with oil film
        │ ╱       ╲ │
        │╱  Oil    ╲│
        │   Film    │
        │   h_min   │
        └──╲_______╱──┘
              ↑
            Journal (shaft) rotating
```

**Mechanism of Heat Generation:**

When the journal rotates inside the bearing, a thin **hydrodynamic oil film** is established in the clearance space. The oil has a viscosity $\mu$, and as the journal surface slides over the oil film, a **viscous shear stress** $\tau$ develops. This shear stress is given by:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy} = \mu \cdot \frac{U}{h_0}$$

This shear stress, acting over the journal's cylindrical surface, produces a **friction torque** $T_f$ resisting the rotation. The power absorbed in overcoming this friction is dissipated as **heat** in the oil.

**Derivation of Heat Generated:**

**Step 1:** Shear stress on the journal.

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{U}{h_0} = \mu \cdot \frac{\pi d N}{60 \cdot h_0}$$

**Step 2:** Friction force on the elemental cylindrical area $dA = 2\pi r \cdot dl$.

$$dF = \tau \cdot dA = \mu \cdot \frac{U}{h_0} \cdot 2\pi r \cdot dl$$

**Step 3:** Elemental friction torque $dT_f = dF \cdot r$.

$$dT_f = \frac{2\pi \mu U r^2}{h_0} \cdot dl$$

**Step 4:** Integrate over the bearing length.

$$T_f = \int_0^L \frac{2\pi \mu U r^2}{h_0} \cdot dl = \frac{2\pi \mu U r^2 L}{h_0}$$

Substituting $r = d/2$ and $U = \pi d N / 60$:

$$T_f = \frac{2\pi \mu L (d/2)^2 (\pi d N / 60)}{h_0} = \frac{\mu \pi^2 d^3 L N}{480 \cdot h_0}$$

**Step 5:** Power loss = torque × angular velocity.

$$Q_{gen} = T_f \cdot \omega = \frac{\mu \pi^2 d^3 L N}{480 \cdot h_0} \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{gen} = \frac{\mu \pi^3 d^3 L N^2}{14400 \cdot h_0}}
$$

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $d = 0.150 \text{ m}$
- $L = 0.100 \text{ m}$
- $N = 1000 \text{ rpm}$
- $\mu = 0.020 \text{ N·s/m}^2$
- $h_0 = 40 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$
- $C = 175 \text{ W/(m}^2 \cdot °C)$
- $T_a = 25°C$

**Step 1: Compute Friction Torque.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_f &= \frac{\mu \pi^2 d^3 L N}{480 \cdot h_0} \\
&= \frac{0.020 \times \pi^2 \times (0.150)^3 \times 0.100 \times 1000}{480 \times 40 \times 10^{-6}} \\[4pt]
\text{Numerator} &= 0.020 \times 9.8696 \times 3.375 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.100 \times 1000 \\
&= 0.020 \times 9.8696 \times 0.3375 \\
&= 0.0666 \\[4pt]
\text{Denominator} &= 480 \times 40 \times 10^{-6} = 0.0192 \\[4pt]
T_f &= \frac{0.0666}{0.0192} = 3.47 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Heat Generated.**

$$Q_{gen} = 3.47 \times \frac{2\pi \times 1000}{60} = 3.47 \times 104.72 = 363.4 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3: Compute Housing Surface Area.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \pi d L + \frac{\pi d^2}{2} \\
&= \pi (0.150)(0.100) + \frac{\pi (0.150)^2}{2} \\
&= 0.04712 + 0.03534 \\
&= 0.08246 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Apply Heat Balance.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T &= \frac{Q_{gen}}{C \cdot A} = \frac{363.4}{175 \times 0.08246} \\
&= \frac{363.4}{14.43} \\
&= 25.18°C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute Steady-State Bearing Temperature.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_b &= T_a + \Delta T \\
&= 25 + 25.18 \\
&= \mathbf{50.18°C} \;\;\approx \mathbf{50.2°C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The bearing operates at about **50.2°C**, which is at the upper edge of safe operation for most industrial applications. The design is **marginally acceptable**; using fins would provide additional safety margin.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Heat Dissipation Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the end-face area:** Many students compute $A = \pi d L$ only and forget the $\frac{\pi d^2}{2}$ term from the two semicircular end faces. **This alone can lose 1–2 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.
>
> 2. **Using wrong value of $C$:** KTU expects $C = 175 \text{ W/(m}^2 \cdot °C)$ for plain bearings in still air, $C = 290 \text{ W/(m}^2 \cdot °C)$ for finned bearings. If the problem specifies "finned housing" but you use $C = 175$, your answer will be wrong by ~40%.
>
> 3. **Mixing up units:** Always convert $d$, $L$, $h_0$ to **meters** and $N$ to **rpm** (no conversion of $N$ to rad/s unless the question demands it). The formula for $T_f$ already has the conversion built-in.
>
> 4. **Not checking $\Delta T \leq 50°C$:** The numerical calculation is only **half the answer**. You must explicitly state the comparison and conclude with a YES/NO on safety. KTU examiners allocate **1 mark** for this conclusion.
>
> 5. **Wrong interpretation of "temperature rise":** The "rise" is **NOT** the absolute bearing temperature. The "rise" = $T_b - T_a$, and the absolute bearing temperature is $T_b = T_a + \Delta T$. Examiners check this distinction carefully.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to use Newton·m in torque units:** If you compute $T_f$ in different units (e.g., N·mm) and forget to convert, the final power will be off by a factor of 1000.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick-Fire Revision Checklist for KTU Heat Dissipation of Bearings**

- ✅ The fundamental **heat balance** at steady state is: $T_f \cdot \omega = C \cdot A \cdot (T_b - T_a)$.
- ✅ The **friction torque** equation is: $T_f = \dfrac{\mu \pi^2 d^3 L N}{480 \cdot h_0}$ — memorize this exactly.
- ✅ **Heat generated** $Q_{gen} = T_f \cdot \omega = T_f \cdot \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ — proportional to $N^2$.
- ✅ **Heat dissipated** $Q_{diss} = C \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ — linear in $\Delta T$.
- ✅ **Housing area** of plain cylindrical bearing: $A = \pi d L + \dfrac{\pi d^2}{2}$ — do not forget the end caps.
- ✅ **Standard values of $C$** (must memorize):
  - Plain bearing in still air: **175 W/(m²·°C)**
  - Finned bearing in still air: **290 W/(m²·°C)**
  - Forced air cooling: **350 – 520 W/(m²·°C)**
- ✅ **Permissible temperature rise:** $\Delta T \leq 50°C$ (industrial norm, KTU accepted value).
- ✅ **Permissible oil-out temperature:** $T_b \leq 90°C$ (typical upper limit).
- ✅ The heat dissipation check is the **fourth and final** verification after Sommerfeld number, Reynolds number, and minimum film thickness.
- ✅ If $\Delta T$ exceeds the limit, the **three common remedies** are: (1) add fins, (2) use forced cooling, (3) use lower-viscosity oil.
- ✅ Heat dissipation coefficient $C$ includes **both convection and radiation** effects lumped into one value.
- ✅ The equation $C = h_c + 4\sigma\varepsilon T_a^3$ shows that $C$ is a function of the ambient temperature, although this refinement is rarely asked in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Procedure of Journal Bearings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT521 - DESIGN OF TRANSMISSION ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design Procedure of Journal Bearings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Journal Bearing** is a sliding-contact bearing that supports a rotating shaft (called the *journal*) by a thin film of lubricant interposed between the journal and the stationary surface (called the *bearing* or *bushing*). The load is transmitted through the pressurized lubricant film, and the bearing operates on the principle of **hydrodynamic lubrication**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Module 4):** A journal bearing is a machine element designed to support a rotating shaft through a hydrodynamic fluid film whose pressure is generated by the relative motion between the journal and the bearing. The design is governed by **Raimondi–Boyd charts** and the **Sommerfeld number**.

The **Design Procedure** is the systematic engineering methodology used to determine the geometric parameters (length **L**, diameter **D**, radial clearance **c**), lubricant properties (viscosity **μ**), and thermal performance (temperature rise **ΔT**) of the bearing so that it operates safely under the applied load **W** and rotational speed **N**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Floating Log in a River" Intuition

Imagine a heavy log floating in a fast-flowing river. The log never touches the riverbed — it is fully supported by the **moving water layer** beneath it. If you push the log sideways, it shifts closer to one bank but still does not touch the bottom, because the water pressure builds up on one side to keep it afloat.

A journal bearing works **identically**:
- The **shaft (journal)** is the log.
- The **lubricant oil** is the river.
- The **bearing housing** is the riverbed.
- The **shaft's rotation** drags oil into the converging gap, building a **wedge-shaped pressure film** that lifts the shaft and supports the load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this insight critical?** A student who *sees* the fluid wedge forming a "hydrodynamic hill" can intuitively understand eccentricity, minimum film thickness, and the role of speed. The shaft sits slightly off-center (eccentric), and the film pressure is highest where the gap is smallest.

## 1.3 Key Geometric & Operational Parameters

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Range (Industrial) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **W** | Applied load on bearing | 1 kN – 500 kN |
| **N** | Journal speed | 100 RPM – 30,000 RPM |
| **D** | Journal diameter | 20 mm – 500 mm |
| **L** | Bearing length | (0.5 to 2) × D |
| **r** | Journal radius = D/2 | — |
| **c** | Radial clearance | 0.0005 D – 0.002 D |
| **e** | Eccentricity (offset of journal center) | 0 < e < c |
| **μ** (or Z) | Dynamic viscosity of oil | 0.01 – 0.1 Pa·s |
| **ε = e/c** | Eccentricity ratio | 0 (concentric) to 1 (boundary) |
| **h₀** | Minimum film thickness | 5 μm – 100 μm |
| **P = W/(L·D)** | Unit bearing pressure | 0.5 – 15 MPa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Constant (KTU convention):** Dynamic viscosity is **μ** in SI units (Pa·s or N·s/m²). 1 Poise = 0.1 Pa·s and 1 Centipoise (cP) = 0.001 Pa·s. The *specific gravity* of typical lubricating oil is **0.85 to 0.95**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Three Pillars of Bearing Design

The design of a hydrodynamic journal bearing rests on **three fundamental principles**:

### Pillar 1: Petroff's Friction Law (Concentric / Unloaded Case)
Petroff analyzed a perfectly concentric journal (no eccentricity, ε = 0). The friction torque and coefficient of friction are derived from the shear stress in a full viscous layer. This serves as the **theoretical starting point** and is used for *very lightly loaded* bearings.

### Pillar 2: The Reynolds Equation (Hydrodynamic Pressure Generation)
Reynolds derived the governing partial differential equation for pressure in a thin viscous film. The **Ocvirk solution** and the **Sommerfeld solution** are two classical closed-form approximations used in KTU-level problems.

### Pillar 3: Raimondi–Boyd Charts (The Engineer's Working Tool)
The exact analytical solution of Reynolds equation is impossible for practical eccentric bearings. Boyd and Raimondi (1959) computed numerical solutions on a digital computer and presented results as **four dimensionless charts** that are universally used in bearing design.

## 2.2 Logic Flow — Why Each Step Exists

1. **Compute the unit pressure P = W/(L·D):** This is the load per unit projected area; the bearing must not exceed allowable **P** for the chosen material (e.g., babbitt: 5–10 MPa).
2. **Compute the Sommerfeld Number S:** A *dimensionless* parameter that groups together the load, speed, viscosity, and clearance ratio. It uniquely determines the operating point on Raimondi–Boyd charts.
3. **Assume an eccentricity ratio ε:** Choose between 0.6 and 0.85 for a good design (small ε means heavy load but thin film; large ε means thick film but high friction).
4. **Read the four variables from charts:** ε → S, f(r/c), Q/(rcNL), and a temperature variable.
5. **Iterate via Heat Balance:** The generated frictional heat must equal the dissipated heat. If ΔT exceeds the allowable limit (typically **20–30 °C** above ambient), the assumed ε is changed and the procedure is repeated.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas constitute the **complete KTU exam-relevant toolbox** for journal bearing design. Master these.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(1) Unit Bearing Pressure:}\quad & P = \dfrac{W}{L \cdot D} \\
\textbf{(2) Sommerfeld Number:}\quad & S = \dfrac{\mu \, N}{P}\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^{\!2} \\
\textbf{(3) Petroff Coefficient of Friction:}\quad & f = 2\pi^{2}\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)\left(\dfrac{\mu N}{P}\right) \\
\textbf{(4) Sliding Velocity at Journal Surface:}\quad & v = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60} \quad [\text{m/s}] \\
\textbf{(5) Frictional Heat Generated:}\quad & H_{g} = f \, W \, v = \mu \, W \, \omega \quad [\text{W}] \\
\textbf{(6) Heat Dissipated (Newton's Law of Cooling):}\quad & H_{d} = C \, A_{s} \, \Delta T = C \left(\pi D L\right) \Delta T \\
\textbf{(7) Heat Balance Equation:}\quad & f \, W \, v = C \left(\pi D L\right) \Delta T \\
\textbf{(8) Coefficient of Friction from Chart:}\quad & f = \dfrac{(\tfrac{r}{c}f)}{(\tfrac{r}{c})} \\
\textbf{(9) Side Flow Rate:}\quad & Q = \dfrac{Q}{rcNL}\cdot rcNL \\
\textbf{(10) Temperature Rise (alternate form):}\quad & \Delta T = \dfrac{H_{g}}{C \, \pi D L}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Variable Reference Table

| Symbol | Meaning | SI Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| W | Applied load on journal | N |
| N | Rotational speed | rev/s (or RPM) |
| P = W/(L·D) | Unit projected pressure | Pa (or N/m²) |
| μ (or Z) | Dynamic viscosity of lubricant | Pa·s |
| r | Journal radius = D/2 | m |
| c | Radial clearance (bearing radius − journal radius) | m |
| e | Eccentricity (displacement of journal from bearing center) | m |
| ε = e/c | Eccentricity ratio | dimensionless |
| f | Coefficient of friction | dimensionless |
| C | Heat transfer coefficient (housing to ambient) | W/(m²·°C) |
| ΔT | Temperature rise (oil bulk − ambient) | °C or K |
| H | Heat power generated/dissipated | W |
| v | Sliding velocity | m/s |
| ω | Angular velocity = 2πN/60 | rad/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Viscosity Conversion Cheat Sheet for KTU:**
> - 1 Poise (P) = 0.1 Pa·s
> - 1 Centipoise (cP) = 0.001 Pa·s = 1 mPa·s
> - Kinematic viscosity ν (in Stokes or cSt) × density ρ (g/cm³) = dynamic viscosity μ (in Poise).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Journal bearings are the **workhorse of high-speed rotating machinery**:
- **Steam and gas turbines** (main and thrust bearings)
- **Internal combustion engine crankshafts** (main and big-end bearings)
- **Electric motor rotors** (medium to large size)
- **Compressors, pumps, and gearboxes** (intermediate shaft supports)
- **Aero-engine accessories**

Their **key advantage** over rolling-element bearings is the ability to handle **very high speeds, shock loads, and contaminated environments** with predictable hydrodynamic behavior.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Design Procedure, Derivations & Numerical Demonstration

## 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Design Procedure (KTU Board Pattern)

Below is the **complete, iterative procedure** as it should be written in a KTU University Exam (14-mark question):

### **Step 1: Identify Given Data**
Note down the **load W (N)**, the **rotational speed N (rev/s)**, the **shaft diameter D (m)**, and any pre-specified constraints (e.g., L/D ratio, lubricant type, ambient temperature, cooling method).

### **Step 2: Assume the Length-to-Diameter Ratio (L/D)**
Based on the application:
- **L/D = 0.5 to 1.0** for heavily loaded, low-speed bearings (e.g., engine crankpins).
- **L/D = 1.0 to 2.0** for lightly loaded, high-speed bearings (e.g., electric motors, turbomachinery).

Compute **L = (L/D) × D**, and then the **unit bearing pressure**:

$$
P = \dfrac{W}{L \cdot D} \quad \text{[Pa or N/m}^{2}\text{]}
$$

Verify that **P ≤ P_allowable** for the chosen bearing material.

### **Step 3: Select the Lubricant and Determine Viscosity at Operating Temperature**
Choose an oil grade (e.g., SAE 30, ISO VG 68). Determine the **dynamic viscosity μ** at the expected operating temperature of the oil film. A viscosity–temperature chart (Walther equation or ASTM chart) is used.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often take the *ambient temperature* viscosity, which is **incorrect**. The oil heats up due to friction, so use the *bulk oil temperature* (typically 50–80 °C).

### **Step 4: Assume an Eccentricity Ratio ε**
A **good first guess** is **ε = 0.7** for moderate duty. Higher ε (closer to 1) gives a thicker film (safer against wear) but higher friction. Lower ε (closer to 0) gives lower friction but a thin film (risky).

### **Step 5: Read Raimondi–Boyd Charts**
From the assumed ε, read off four dimensionless parameters:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon \;&\rightarrow\; S = \dfrac{\mu N}{P}\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^{2} \\
\varepsilon \;&\rightarrow\; \left(\dfrac{r}{c} f\right) \\
\varepsilon \;&\rightarrow\; \dfrac{Q}{rcNL} \quad \text{(side flow parameter)} \\
\varepsilon \;&\rightarrow\; \text{Temperature variable (T-var)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 6: Compute the Clearance Ratio (r/c)**
From the Sommerfeld number expression, solve for r/c:

$$
\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^{2} = S \cdot \dfrac{P}{\mu N} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \dfrac{r}{c} = \sqrt{S \cdot \dfrac{P}{\mu N}}
$$

Since **r = D/2**, the **radial clearance** is:

$$
c = \dfrac{r}{(r/c)} = \dfrac{D/2}{(r/c)}
$$

### **Step 7: Compute the Coefficient of Friction**

$$
f = \dfrac{(r/c \, f)}{(r/c)}
$$

### **Step 8: Compute Heat Generated**

$$
H_{g} = f \, W \, v = f \cdot W \cdot \dfrac{\pi D N}{60} \quad \text{[W]}
$$

### **Step 9: Compute Heat Dissipated & Temperature Rise**
Assuming natural convection (no external cooling), with housing heat transfer coefficient **C** (typically **C = 100 to 300 W/(m²·°C)** for still air) and projected area **A_s = πDL**:

$$
H_{d} = C \, A_{s} \, \Delta T = C \left(\pi D L\right) \Delta T
$$

Apply the **heat balance equation**:

$$
f \, W \, \dfrac{\pi D N}{60} = C \left(\pi D L\right) \Delta T
$$

Solving for **ΔT**:

$$
\Delta T = \dfrac{f \, W \, N}{60 \, C \, L}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The expression shows that **increasing L or C reduces ΔT** (longer bearing or better cooling helps).

### **Step 10: Compare with Allowable Temperature Rise**
The allowable ΔT for **mineral oil** bearings is typically **20 °C to 30 °C** above ambient (to prevent oxidation and viscosity breakdown). If computed ΔT is within limits → **Design is SAFE**. If not → **return to Step 4** with a new ε (usually higher, to reduce f·W·v indirectly via different S) or change L/D, lubricant, or provide forced cooling.

### **Step 11: Verify Minimum Film Thickness (Optional but Recommended)**

$$
h_{0} = c \, (1 - \varepsilon)
$$

For **mixed/boundary lubrication safety**, **h₀ ≥ 5 μm to 10 μm** is recommended for moderate-duty bearings.
