## 3.2 Complete Numerical Demonstration (Worked Example)

**Problem (Typical 14-Mark KTU Exam Style):**
A journal bearing supports a load of **W = 30 kN** at a speed of **N = 1500 RPM**. The shaft diameter is **D = 100 mm** and the bearing length is **L = 100 mm**. The lubricant has a viscosity of **μ = 0.05 Pa·s** at the operating temperature. The heat transfer coefficient from the bearing housing is **C = 200 W/(m²·°C)**. Using an assumed eccentricity ratio of **ε = 0.7**, determine the **radial clearance, the coefficient of friction, and the temperature rise**.

### **Solution (Exhaustive Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1: Given Data**

$$
W = 30{,}000\ \text{N}, \quad N = \dfrac{1500}{60} = 25\ \text{rev/s}, \quad D = 0.1\ \text{m}, \quad L = 0.1\ \text{m}
$$

$$
\mu = 0.05\ \text{Pa·s}, \quad C = 200\ \text{W/(m}^{2}\!\cdot\!^{\circ}\text{C)}, \quad \varepsilon = 0.7
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Unit Pressure**

$$
P = \dfrac{W}{L \cdot D} = \dfrac{30{,}000}{0.1 \times 0.1} = 3{,}000{,}000\ \text{Pa} = 3\ \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 3: Sliding Velocity at the Journal Surface**

$$
v = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.1 \times 1500}{60} = 7.854\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 4: Read Raimondi–Boyd Chart Values at ε = 0.7**

For a typical Raimondi–Boyd chart (full journal bearing, 360°):

$$
S \approx 0.121, \quad \left(\dfrac{r}{c}f\right) \approx 2.85, \quad \dfrac{Q}{rcNL} \approx 4.33
$$

**Step 5: Solve for the Clearance Ratio (r/c) Using the Sommerfeld Number**

$$
S = \dfrac{\mu N}{P}\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^{2}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^{2} = S \cdot \dfrac{P}{\mu N}
$$

Substitute:

$$
\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^{2} = 0.121 \times \dfrac{3 \times 10^{6}}{0.05 \times 25} = 0.121 \times 2.4 \times 10^{6} = 290{,}400
$$

Taking the square root:

$$
\dfrac{r}{c} = \sqrt{290{,}400} \approx 538.9
$$

**Step 6: Compute the Radial Clearance c**

$$
r = \dfrac{D}{2} = 0.05\ \text{m}
$$

$$
c = \dfrac{r}{r/c} = \dfrac{0.05}{538.9} = 9.28 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m} = 0.0928\ \text{mm} \approx 92.8\ \mu\text{m}
$$

This corresponds to **c/r ≈ 0.00186**, which is within the typical industrial range of **0.0005 to 0.002**.

**Step 7: Compute the Coefficient of Friction f**

$$
f = \dfrac{(r/c \, f)}{(r/c)} = \dfrac{2.85}{538.9} = 5.29 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0.00529
$$

This is a **typical low-friction** value for a hydrodynamic bearing.

**Step 8: Compute Heat Generated**

$$
H_{g} = f \cdot W \cdot v = 0.00529 \times 30{,}000 \times 7.854 = 1246.5\ \text{W}
$$

**Step 9: Compute Heat Dissipated & Temperature Rise**

Projected housing area:

$$
A_{s} = \pi D L = \pi \times 0.1 \times 0.1 = 0.0314\ \text{m}^{2}
$$

Heat balance:

$$
H_{g} = C \, A_{s} \, \Delta T
$$

$$
1246.5 = 200 \times 0.0314 \times \Delta T = 6.28 \, \Delta T
$$

$$
\Delta T = \dfrac{1246.5}{6.28} = 198.5\ ^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**Step 10: Critical Analysis — Design Failure & Iteration**

> [!WARNING]
> **ΔT = 198.5 °C vastly exceeds the allowable 20–30 °C limit for mineral oil.** The design is **UNSAFE** with the assumed ε = 0.7. The student must **iterate**.

**Iteration 1 — Try ε = 0.5 (thicker film, lower friction):**
From the chart at ε = 0.5: **S ≈ 0.0606** and **f(r/c) ≈ 1.70**.

$$
\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^{2} = 0.0606 \times 2.4 \times 10^{6} = 145{,}440
$$

$$
\dfrac{r}{c} = 381.4
$$

$$
f = \dfrac{1.70}{381.4} = 4.46 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
H_{g} = 0.00446 \times 30{,}000 \times 7.854 = 1050\ \text{W}
$$

$$
\Delta T = \dfrac{1050}{6.28} = 167.2\ ^{\circ}\text{C} \quad (\text{still too high})
$$

**Iteration 2 — Increase L/D ratio from 1.0 to 1.5** (L = 150 mm):
Now **A_s = π × 0.1 × 0.15 = 0.0471 m²**, and the unit pressure drops to **P = 30,000/(0.15 × 0.1) = 2 MPa**, so:

$$
\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^{2} = 0.0606 \times \dfrac{2 \times 10^{6}}{0.05 \times 25} = 0.0606 \times 1.6 \times 10^{6} = 96{,}960
$$

$$
\dfrac{r}{c} = 311.4
$$

$$
f = \dfrac{1.70}{311.4} = 5.46 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
H_{g} = 0.00546 \times 30{,}000 \times 7.854 = 1286\ \text{W}
$$

$$
\Delta T = \dfrac{1286}{200 \times 0.0471} = 136.5\ ^{\circ}\text{C} \quad (\text{still too high})
$$

**Iteration 3 — Provide forced cooling** (C rises to 600 W/m²·°C with air blast):

$$
\Delta T = \dfrac{1286}{600 \times 0.0471} = 45.5\ ^{\circ}\text{C} \quad (\text{still high, but approaching safe region})
$$

**Iteration 4 — Forced oil cooling + ε = 0.4 + L/D = 1.5** ultimately brings ΔT under 30 °C. The **iterative convergence** is the heart of the design procedure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A common 7-mark sub-part asks: *"What modifications would you suggest to reduce the temperature rise?"* The answer is: **(i) increase L/D, (ii) increase clearance (larger c, but checks h₀), (iii) use lower-viscosity oil, (iv) add external cooling fins or forced air, (v) add circulating oil with an external heat exchanger.** Each suggestion carries 1 mark.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Journal Bearing Components and Load Transmission

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applied Load W on Shaft] --> B[Shaft Journal of radius r]
    B --> C[Oil Film under Pressure]
    C --> D[Bearing Bush radius r plus c]
    D --> E[Housing with Heat Transfer Coefficient C]
    E --> F[Surrounding Ambient Air]

    G[Rotational Speed N of Shaft] --> H[Sliding Velocity v at Journal]
    H --> I[Friction Force f times W]
    I --> J[Heat Generated Hg]

    J --> K[Heat Balance Equation]
    L[Heat Dissipated Hd equals C As delta T] --> K
    K --> M{Safe Design delta T within Limit}
    M -->|Yes| N[Final Design Parameters L, D, c, mu, f]
    M -->|No| O[Modify L, D, c, mu or Add Cooling]
    O --> G

    P[Raimondi Boyd Charts] --> Q[Read S, f_r/c, Q/rcNL, T-var]
    Q --> N
```

## 4.2 Functional Flowchart — Complete Design Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input W, N, D, ambient temperature] --> B[Assume L by D ratio between 0.5 and 2]
    B --> C[Compute unit pressure P equals W divided by L D]
    C --> P1{P less than P_allowable for material}
    P1 -->|No| Q1[Increase L or change material]
    Q1 --> B
    P1 -->|Yes| D[Select lubricant and determine mu at operating temperature]
    D --> E[Assume eccentricity ratio epsilon 0.6 to 0.8]
    E --> F[Read Sommerfeld S and friction r/c f from Raimondi Boyd chart]
    F --> G[Compute r divided by c from S equals mu N over P times r by c squared]
    G --> H[Compute coefficient of friction f equals r/c f divided by r/c]
    H --> I[Compute heat generated Hg equals f W v]
    I --> J[Compute heat dissipated Hd equals C times pi D L times delta T]
    J --> K[Apply heat balance: solve for delta T]
    K --> L{delta T less than 20 to 30 degree C}
    L -->|Yes| M[Check minimum film thickness h0 equals c times 1 minus epsilon]
    L -->|No| N[Return to step E: increase epsilon or modify design]
    N --> E
    M --> O{h0 greater than 5 to 10 micrometer}
    O -->|Yes| P[Design Approved: Report L, D, c, f, mu, delta T]
    O -->|No| N
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Free-Body of the Hydrodynamic Film

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Bearing Center O_B] -->|c| B[Eccentricity Vector e]
    B --> C[Journal Center O_J]
    C -->|r| D[Contact Point Zone]

    E[Applied Load W vertical downward] --> F[Pressure Film Force P_film vertical upward]
    F --> G[Resultant Force equals Applied Load in equilibrium]

    H[Rotation Direction omega] --> I[Oil Dragged into Converging Wedge]
    I --> J[Pressure Buildup Peak at Minimum Film Thickness h0]
    J --> K[Oil Expelled from Widening Side]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q1.** Define the **Sommerfeld number** for a hydrodynamic journal bearing and state its significance.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **Sommerfeld number** is a dimensionless parameter that groups the operating and geometric variables of a journal bearing, defined as:

$$
S = \dfrac{\mu N}{P}\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^{2}
$$

It is the single most important parameter in hydrodynamic bearing analysis because:
- **(i)** It uniquely determines the **operating point** on the Raimondi–Boyd charts.
- **(ii)** It governs the **load-carrying capacity** of the fluid film.
- **(iii)** It directly links **load, speed, viscosity, and clearance** into a single dimensionless number, allowing comparison across bearings of different sizes.

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q2.** What is the **eccentricity ratio** of a journal bearing? What is its typical range for a well-designed bearing?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **eccentricity ratio** is the ratio of the eccentricity **e** (offset of the journal center from the bearing center) to the radial clearance **c**:

$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{e}{c}
$$

Its value lies in the range **0 < ε < 1**:
- **ε → 0** means the journal is concentric (unloaded, ideal hydrodynamic case).
- **ε → 1** means the journal is in boundary contact with the bearing (no film — failure condition).

A **well-designed bearing** typically operates in the range **ε = 0.6 to 0.85**, balancing load capacity and minimum film thickness.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question Choice A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Similar Pattern]**

A journal bearing is required to support a load of **W = 25 kN** at a shaft speed of **N = 1200 RPM**. The shaft diameter is **D = 80 mm**, the bearing length is **L = 80 mm**, and the lubricant viscosity at the operating temperature is **μ = 0.04 Pa·s**. The heat transfer coefficient is **C = 175 W/(m²·°C)**. Using an assumed eccentricity ratio of **ε = 0.8**, find the **radial clearance, coefficient of friction, and temperature rise**. Comment on the design and suggest modifications if required.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Determine Radial Clearance and Coefficient of Friction

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Convert units.**

$$
N = \dfrac{1200}{60} = 20\ \text{rev/s}, \quad D = 0.08\ \text{m}, \quad L = 0.08\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 2: Compute unit pressure.**

$$
P = \dfrac{W}{L \cdot D} = \dfrac{25{,}000}{0.08 \times 0.08} = 3.906 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Pa} \approx 3.91\ \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 3: Read Raimondi–Boyd values at ε = 0.8.**

From standard charts: **S ≈ 0.264**, **f(r/c) ≈ 3.22**, **Q/(rcNL) ≈ 4.62**.

**Step 4: Solve for r/c.**

$$
\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^{2} = S \cdot \dfrac{P}{\mu N} = 0.264 \times \dfrac{3.906 \times 10^{6}}{0.04 \times 20} = 0.264 \times 4.8825 \times 10^{6} = 1.289 \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
\dfrac{r}{c} = \sqrt{1.289 \times 10^{6}} \approx 1135.3
$$

**Step 5: Compute the radial clearance.**

$$
r = 0.04\ \text{m}, \quad c = \dfrac{0.04}{1135.3} = 3.52 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m} = 0.0352\ \text{mm} = 35.2\ \mu\text{m}
$$

**Step 6: Compute the coefficient of friction.**

$$
f = \dfrac{(r/c \, f)}{r/c} = \dfrac{3.22}{1135.3} = 2.84 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0.00284
$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Compute Temperature Rise and Suggest Modifications

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Sliding velocity.**

$$
v = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.08 \times 1200}{60} = 5.027\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2: Heat generated.**

$$
H_{g} = f \cdot W \cdot v = 0.00284 \times 25{,}000 \times 5.027 = 357\ \text{W}
$$

**Step 3: Projected housing area.**

$$
A_{s} = \pi D L = \pi \times 0.08 \times 0.08 = 0.0201\ \text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 4: Heat balance.**

$$
H_{g} = C \cdot A_{s} \cdot \Delta T \Rightarrow \Delta T = \dfrac{357}{175 \times 0.0201} = \dfrac{357}{3.52} \approx 101.4\ ^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**Step 5: Conclusion.**

> [!WARNING]
> **ΔT = 101.4 °C is unsafe** (allowable ≤ 25–30 °C). The design fails. **Suggested modifications:**
> 1. **Increase L/D ratio** (e.g., L/D = 1.5) — reduces P and increases A_s.
> 2. **Use lower-viscosity oil** — reduces frictional heat.
> 3. **Provide forced air or water cooling** — raises C from 175 to ~500 W/(m²·°C).
> 4. **Increase clearance (larger c)** — reduces r/c and hence f.
> 5. **Iterate with lower ε** (e.g., ε = 0.5) — reduces f(r/c) and hence f.

---

### **Question Choice B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Similar Pattern]**

A shaft of diameter **D = 120 mm** is supported in a journal bearing of length **L = 150 mm**. The bearing carries a load of **W = 40 kN** at a speed of **N = 900 RPM**. The dynamic viscosity of the lubricant at the operating temperature is **μ = 0.06 Pa·s**. Assuming an eccentricity ratio of **ε = 0.6**, determine: **(a)** the **minimum oil film thickness**, **(b)** the **coefficient of friction** and the **power loss** in the bearing, and **(c)** the **temperature rise** of the lubricant, assuming a heat transfer coefficient of **C = 250 W/(m²·°C)**. Verify whether the design is safe.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Find Sommerfeld Number and Minimum Film Thickness

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Unit pressure and speed.**

$$
P = \dfrac{W}{L \cdot D} = \dfrac{40{,}000}{0.15 \times 0.12} = 2.222 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Pa} = 2.22\ \text{MPa}
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{900}{60} = 15\ \text{rev/s}
$$

**Step 2: Read Raimondi–Boyd at ε = 0.6.**

Standard chart values: **S ≈ 0.0806**, **f(r/c) ≈ 2.07**, **Q/(rcNL) ≈ 3.22**.

**Step 3: Solve for r/c.**

$$
\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^{2} = S \cdot \dfrac{P}{\mu N} = 0.0806 \times \dfrac{2.222 \times 10^{6}}{0.06 \times 15} = 0.0806 \times 2.469 \times 10^{6} = 1.99 \times 10^{5}
$$

$$
\dfrac{r}{c} = \sqrt{1.99 \times 10^{5}} \approx 446.1
$$

**Step 4: Compute radial clearance c.**

$$
r = 0.06\ \text{m}, \quad c = \dfrac{0.06}{446.1} = 1.345 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m} = 0.1345\ \text{mm} = 134.5\ \mu\text{m}
$$

**Step 5: Minimum film thickness h₀.**

$$
h_{0} = c (1 - \varepsilon) = 0.1345 \times (1 - 0.6) = 0.1345 \times 0.4 = 0.0538\ \text{mm} = 53.8\ \mu\text{m}
$$

> This is **safe** (well above 5–10 μm).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Coefficient of Friction, Power Loss, and Temperature Rise

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Coefficient of friction.**

$$
f = \dfrac{(r/c \, f)}{r/c} = \dfrac{2.07}{446.1} = 4.64 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0.00464
$$

**Step 2: Sliding velocity and power loss.**

$$
v = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.12 \times 900}{60} = 5.655\ \text{m/s}
$$

$$
P_{\text{loss}} = f \cdot W \cdot v = 0.00464 \times 40{,}000 \times 5.655 = 1049.6\ \text{W} \approx 1.05\ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 3: Temperature rise via heat balance.**

$$
A_{s} = \pi D L = \pi \times 0.12 \times 0.15 = 0.0565\ \text{m}^{2}
$$

$$
\Delta T = \dfrac{H_{g}}{C \cdot A_{s}} = \dfrac{1049.6}{250 \times 0.0565} = \dfrac{1049.6}{14.13} = 74.3\ ^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **ΔT = 74.3 °C is unsafe** (allowable ≤ 30 °C). Although the **film thickness (53.8 μm) is safe**, the **thermal condition is not**. The student must iterate: try a higher ε (e.g., 0.85) to reduce f·W·v, or improve cooling, or lengthen the bearing.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mistakes Students Make in Journal Bearing Design (Lost Marks):**
>
> 1. **Using ambient viscosity instead of operating-temperature viscosity** — costs 1–2 marks. Always use μ at the bulk oil temperature.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert RPM to rev/s** in the Sommerfeld equation — causes 10× numerical error and a chain-reaction failure of all subsequent steps.
> 3. **Wrong sign in heat balance** — students sometimes write H_d = C·A_s·T_ambient instead of C·A_s·ΔT. Always use temperature **rise**, not absolute temperature.
> 4. **Not iterating** when ΔT is too high — the examiner looks for evidence of **engineering judgment**, not a single computation.
> 5. **Confusing Petroff's friction (used for ε = 0) with the actual friction (read from chart for ε > 0)** — Petroff's equation gives 2–3× higher f than reality and is rarely used in real design.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final High-Density Revision Summary for Journal Bearing Design:**
>
> - **Hydrodynamic bearing** supports load via a **pressure-generated oil wedge**, not by direct contact.
> - **Eccentricity ratio ε = e/c** is the key design choice (typical: **0.6–0.85**).
> - **Unit pressure P = W/(L·D)** must be below the allowable value for the bearing material (e.g., **babbitt ≤ 10 MPa, bronze ≤ 25 MPa**).
> - **Sommerfeld number S = (μN/P)(r/c)²** is dimensionless; it locates the operating point on Raimondi–Boyd charts.
> - **Raimondi–Boyd charts** give four parameters: **S, f(r/c), Q/(rcNL), and the temperature variable** — all as functions of ε.
> - **Petroff's equation** (for concentric, ε = 0 case): **f = 2π²(r/c)(μN/P)** — rarely used in real eccentric design.
> - **Heat generated H_g = f·W·v = f·W·(πDN/60)** must equal **heat dissipated H_d = C·(πDL)·ΔT**.
> - **Allowable ΔT = 20–30 °C** for mineral oil bearings (per KTU convention).
> - **Minimum film thickness h₀ = c(1 - ε) ≥ 5–10 μm** is the wear-safety check.
> - **If ΔT is too high:** iterate with a different ε, increase L/D, use lower-viscosity oil, or add external cooling.
> - **Common industrial L/D range: 0.5 to 2.0**; high-speed light-load → L/D = 1.5–2.0; low-speed heavy-load → L/D = 0.5–1.0.
> - **Sliding velocity v = πDN/60** is the velocity at the journal's outer surface.
> - **Heat transfer coefficient C** for still air ≈ **100–300 W/(m²·°C)**; with forced air ≈ **500–600**; with water jacket ≈ **1000+**.
> - **The design procedure is iterative** — there is no single closed-form answer; the student must demonstrate **judgement**.
> - **Viscosity conversions:** 1 Poise = 0.1 Pa·s; 1 cP = 10⁻³ Pa·s; kinematic ν (cSt) × ρ (g/cm³) = μ (cP).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ball and Roller Bearings: Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT521 - DESIGN OF TRANSMISSION ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ball and Roller Bearings: Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **bearing** is a machine element that constrains relative motion to only the desired motion, and reduces friction between moving parts. In the context of transmission elements, **anti-friction bearings** (also called **rolling contact bearings** or **rolling element bearings**) are machine components designed to carry loads by placing spherical or cylindrical rolling elements (balls or rollers) between two raceways, thereby converting sliding friction into rolling friction.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Anti-friction bearings are bearings containing rolling elements such as balls or rollers between the inner and outer raceways. The rolling elements transmit the load from one raceway to another, providing very low starting and running friction compared to sliding contact bearings."*

**Classification of Rolling Contact Bearings:**

1. **Ball Bearings** — Use spherical rolling elements (balls).
2. **Roller Bearings** — Use cylindrical, tapered, needle, or spherical rolling elements (rollers).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to push a heavy **200 kg** wooden crate across a rough concrete floor. The friction is enormous, and you struggle to move it. Now, suppose you slip **6 steel rods (pipes)** underneath the crate. Suddenly, the crate glides effortlessly — you have replaced **sliding friction** with **rolling friction**, which can be **40 to 60 times lower**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Rolling Rod" Intuition:** A bearing is mechanically identical to those steel rods under the crate. The **inner race** is the shaft, the **outer race** is the housing, and the **balls/rollers** are the steel rods. The rolling elements roll without slipping, transforming high sliding friction into negligible rolling friction. This is why every bicycle, electric motor, car wheel, and machine spindle in the world uses rolling bearings instead of plain (sliding) bearings.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard values are universally used in bearing design:

| Parameter | Standard Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Poisson's ratio for steel ($\nu$) | **0.3** | Used in bearing load-life equations |
| Elastic modulus of bearing steel ($E$) | **$2.07 \times 10^5$ MPa** | Through-hardened chromium steel |
| Hertzian contact stress exponent ($p$) | **3 for ball-on-flat** | Standard line/sphere contact |
| Ball-on-flat exponent | **3** | For point contact |
| Roller-on-flat exponent | **10/3 ≈ 3.33** | For line contact |
| Standard radial play (clearance) | **0.010 – 0.040 mm** | For medium series bearings |

---

## 1.4 Callout: Why Bearings Matter in KTU Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Relevance:**
> Approximately **80%** of all rotating machinery failures originate at the bearings. In a typical 4-stroke IC engine, the crankshaft, camshaft, connecting rod, and gearbox shafts all depend on rolling bearings. Proper bearing selection directly impacts machine life, energy efficiency (reduced frictional losses), and operating temperature.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Design Logic of Rolling Bearings

The design of a rolling bearing revolves around five engineering decisions, each mapped to a specific failure mode:

1. **Load Capacity** — determined by the Hertzian contact stress between the rolling element and the raceway.
2. **Life Expectancy** — governed by subsurface fatigue (spalling) of the raceway material.
3. **Speed Capability** — limited by centrifugal forces on the rolling elements and lubricant film breakdown.
4. **Misalignment Tolerance** — depends on the geometry of the rolling element (spherical vs. cylindrical).
5. **Mounting & Sealing** — dictated by accessibility for lubrication and contamination exclusion.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Classification & Types

### 2.2.1 Ball Bearings (Point Contact — Hertzian Exponent = 3)

#### (A) Deep Groove Ball Bearing (Conrad Type)
- **Construction:** Deep, uninterrupted raceway grooves on inner and outer races. Balls are inserted by displacing them axially through a notch and then snapping them back radially.
- **Load Capability:** Carries **radial load** primarily; can also sustain a small **axial (thrust) load** in either direction (typically up to 50–60% of radial rating).
- **Speed:** Highest speed capability among radial bearings.
- **Misalignment:** Very poor (≈ 2 to 10 minutes of arc).
- **Applications:** Electric motors, pumps, gearboxes, conveyor rollers.

#### (B) Angular Contact Ball Bearing
- **Construction:** One race shoulder is relieved (cut). The line joining the ball contact points makes an angle $\alpha$ (contact angle) with the radial plane.
- **Load Capability:** Carries combined **radial + axial (thrust) load** simultaneously.
- **Speed:** High, but lower than deep groove due to higher contact stress.
- **Misalignment:** Very poor.
- **Applications:** Machine tool spindles, pumps with thrust loads, gearboxes requiring combined loading.

#### (C) Self-Aligning Ball Bearing
- **Construction:** Two rows of balls running on a common spherical outer raceway. The inner race has two grooved raceways inclined at a common angle.
- **Load Capability:** Primarily **radial**, very limited axial.
- **Speed:** Moderate.
- **Misalignment:** Excellent (≈ 1.5° to 3° static, ≈ 0.5° dynamic).
- **Applications:** Long shafts prone to bending, agricultural machinery, textile spindles.

#### (D) Thrust Ball Bearing
- **Construction:** Flat (or grooved) raceways. Balls run between two flat washers (shaft washer + housing washer).
- **Load Capability:** **Pure axial (thrust) load only.** Can carry radial load only if specially designed.
- **Speed:** Low to moderate (high centrifugal forces at high speeds).
- **Variants:** Single-direction thrust, double-direction thrust.
- **Applications:** Crane hooks, vertical pump shafts, turntables, machine tool spindles (axial location).

### 2.2.2 Roller Bearings (Line Contact — Hertzian Exponent = 10/3)

#### (A) Cylindrical Roller Bearing
- **Construction:** Cylindrical rollers running between parallel raceways. Most common variants: **NU, N, NUP, NJ**.
- **Load Capability:** Very high **radial load** capacity (line contact gives ~60% higher capacity than equivalent ball bearing).
- **Speed:** High.
- **Misalignment:** Very poor (sensitive to shaft deflection).
- **Applications:** Heavy-duty gearboxes, rolling mills, electric traction motors.

#### (B) Needle Roller Bearing
- **Construction:** Cylindrical rollers of length-to-diameter ratio ≥ 3:1, sometimes without an inner race (drawn cup needle bearing).
- **Load Capability:** High radial load in a **very small radial space**.
- **Speed:** Lower than standard cylindrical.
- **Applications:** Connecting rod ends, rocker arms, planetary gear pins, universal joints.

#### (C) Tapered Roller Bearing
- **Construction:** Tapered rollers arranged such that the lines of contact converge at a single point on the axis. The contact angle $\alpha$ is typically 10° to 30°.
- **Load Capability:** Carries combined **radial + axial (thrust)** loads. The most capable combined-load bearing.
- **Speed:** Moderate to high.
- **Mounting:** Always in opposing pairs (DB, DF, DT arrangements).
- **Applications:** Automotive wheel hubs, gearboxes, rolling mills, railway axle boxes.

#### (D) Spherical (Barrel) Roller Bearing
- **Construction:** Barrel-shaped rollers running on a common spherical outer raceway.
- **Load Capability:** Very high **radial** + moderate **axial** load. Highest load capacity per unit width.
- **Speed:** Moderate.
- **Misalignment:** Excellent (≈ 0.5° to 2°).
- **Applications:** Heavy rolling mills, paper machines, mining crushers, marine propulsion shafts.

#### (E) Thrust Roller Bearing
- **Construction:** Cylindrical or tapered rollers between flat washers.
- **Load Capability:** Very high **pure thrust** load.
- **Speed:** Low.
- **Applications:** Crane slew rings, large gearboxes, ship propeller shafts (with marine thrust blocks).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = P^p$ | Basic dynamic load rating exponent | $p = 3$ (ball), $p = 10/3$ (roller) |
| 2 | $L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p$ | Rating life in millions of revolutions | Millions of revolutions |
| 3 | $L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6}{60 \cdot n} \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p$ | Rating life in hours | Hours |
| 4 | $P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$ | Equivalent dynamic radial load | Newtons (N) |
| 5 | $P_0 = X_0 \cdot F_r + Y_0 \cdot F_a$ | Equivalent static radial load | Newtons (N) |
| 6 | $X,\, Y$ | Radial and thrust factors (from manufacturer catalogue) | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $e$ | Limiting value of $F_a / F_r$ for $X, Y$ selection | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $C_0$ | Static load rating (bearing catalogue value) | Newtons (N) |
| 9 | $C / P \geq 1$ | Minimum required design ratio | Safety threshold |
| 10 | $f_c$ | Bearing type & series factor (ISO/IS catalogue) | Dimensionless |
| 11 | $D_w$ | Ball diameter | mm |
| 12 | $\alpha$ | Contact / pressure cone angle | degrees or radians |
| 13 | $F_a \leq 0.5 \cdot C_0$ | Axial load design limit | N (for thrust bearings) |
| 14 | $K = \dfrac{F_a}{F_r}$ | Axial-to-radial load ratio | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU 2024 Numericals — Most-tested combination:** A problem will provide shaft diameter, radial load $F_r$, axial load $F_a$, speed $n$ (rpm), and required life $L_{10h}$ (hours). The student must:
> 1. Compute the equivalent dynamic load $P$ using $X$ and $Y$.
> 2. Compute required dynamic capacity $C = P \cdot (L_{10h} \cdot 60n / 10^6)^{1/p}$.
> 3. Select the bearing from the catalogue whose $C \geq$ calculated $C$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Bearing Type Used | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Electric Motors (3-phase) | Deep Groove Ball Bearing (2Z/2RS) | High speed, low friction, sealed |
| Automotive Wheel Hubs | Tapered Roller Bearing | Combined radial + thrust loads |
| Wind Turbine Main Shaft | Spherical Roller Bearing | Misalignment due to shaft flex |
| Lathe Spindle | Angular Contact Ball Bearing | Combined load + high precision |
| Bicycle Bottom Bracket | Needle Roller Bearing | Compact radial space |
| Paper Machine Dryer Rolls | Spherical Roller Bearing | Misalignment + heavy load |
| Crane Slew Ring | Thrust Roller Bearing | Pure axial load from tipping moment |
| Helicopter Rotor (MGB) | Tapered Roller or Cylindrical | High combined load, high reliability |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Rating Life Equation (Lundberg–Palmgren Theory)

The **fundamental bearing life equation** used worldwide by ISO, IS, AGMA, and AFBMA is based on the work of **Lundberg and Palmgren (1947, 1952)**. The derivation proceeds from the Hertzian contact stress distribution.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Hertzian Contact Stress

For a ball pressed against a raceway, the maximum contact stress is:

$$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{3F}{2\pi a b}$$

where $a$ and $b$ are the semi-axes of the elliptical contact area. For a sphere on a spherical raceway, the contact is a **point contact** (theoretically zero area, but elastically deformed to a small ellipse).

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Subsurface Shear Stress (Initiation Point of Failure)

Lundberg and Palmgren showed that **fatigue spalling** initiates at a specific depth $z_0$ below the contact surface, where the **orthogonal shear stress** $\tau_0$ is maximum. They proposed a power-law survival probability:

$$\ln\dfrac{1}{S} \propto \dfrac{\tau_0^c \cdot N^e \cdot V}{z_0^{hc}}$$

where $S$ is survival probability, $N$ is number of stress cycles, $V$ is the stressed volume, and $c, e, h$ are experimentally determined exponents.

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Stressed Volume and Number of Cycles

For one revolution of the inner race, each ball experiences one complete stress cycle as it traverses the loaded zone. If $Z$ is the number of balls and $\gamma$ is the proportion of the raceway under load, the number of cycles per revolution is:

$$N_{rev} = \gamma \cdot Z$$

For $L$ revolutions of the inner race:

$$N = L \cdot \gamma \cdot Z$$

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Equivalent Load and Rating Life

Setting survival probability $S = 0.9$ (i.e., 90% of bearings survive, 10% fail — the L10 life convention), and substituting the Hertzian stress equations:

$$\sigma_{max} \propto \left(\dfrac{P}{D_w^2}\right)^{1/3}$$

Combining all terms, the failure probability per stress cycle is proportional to:

$$\dfrac{1}{N} \propto \left(\dfrac{\sigma_{max}}{z_0}\right)^{c/e}$$

For ball bearings, the exponent evaluates to $c/e = 9$, leading to the **cubic relationship** between load and life:

$$L_{10} \cdot P^3 = \text{constant} = C^3$$

For roller bearings (line contact), the exponent is $c/e = 9.4$, giving approximately:

$$L_{10} \cdot P^{10/3} = \text{constant} = C^{10/3}$$

### 3.1.5 Final Standard Form

$$\boxed{\;L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p\;}$$

where:
- $L_{10}$ = rating life in **millions of revolutions**.
- $C$ = basic dynamic load rating (from catalogue, N).
- $P$ = equivalent dynamic radial load (N).
- $p = 3$ for ball bearings, $p = 10/3 \approx 3.33$ for roller bearings.

### 3.1.6 Conversion to Hours

The number of revolutions per hour of the inner ring is $60 \cdot n$ (where $n$ is in rpm). Therefore:

$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{L_{10} \times 10^6}{60 \cdot n} = \dfrac{10^6}{60n}\left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Equivalent Dynamic Load Equation

When a bearing carries **both** radial load $F_r$ and axial load $F_a$ simultaneously, these are combined into a single equivalent load $P$ using manufacturer-tabulated coefficients $X$ and $Y$.

### 3.2.1 Step 1 — Define Load Ratio

Let $K = F_a / F_r$. This ratio determines the operating zone of the bearing.

### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Limiting Ratio $e$

The manufacturer publishes a value $e$ such that:
- If $K \leq e$ → the axial load is small; $X = 1$, $Y = 0$. The equivalent load equals the radial load: $P = F_r$.
- If $K > e$ → the axial load is significant; $X < 1$ and $Y > 0$.

### 3.2.3 Step 3 — Combine Loads

By superposition of contact stresses (linearized for design):

$$P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$$

This is the **standard ISO 15 / IS 6455** equivalent load equation.

### 3.2.4 Worked Example Derivation

**Given:** A single-row angular contact ball bearing carries $F_r = 4000$ N and $F_a = 1500$ N. From the catalogue: $e = 0.68$, $X = 0.56$, $Y = 1.45$ for $K > e$.

**Step 1: Compute $K$**
$$K = \dfrac{F_a}{F_r} = \dfrac{1500}{4000} = 0.375$$

**Step 2: Compare with $e$**
$$K = 0.375 < e = 0.68$$

**Step 3: Since $K < e$**
$$X = 1, \quad Y = 0$$
$$P = 1 \cdot 4000 + 0 \cdot 1500 = 4000 \text{ N}$$

> This means at this load ratio, the axial load is small enough that the bearing's radial load rating alone governs the design.

**Counter-example:** If $F_a = 3500$ N instead:
$$K = 3500 / 4000 = 0.875 > e = 0.68$$

**Then $X = 0.56$ and $Y = 1.45$:**
$$P = 0.56 \cdot 4000 + 1.45 \cdot 3500 = 2240 + 5075 = 7315 \text{ N}$$

This 82.9% increase in $P$ shows the critical effect of axial load on angular contact bearing life.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Bearing Type Selection

The following matrix is the **single most important KTU 2024 exam reference** for choosing the correct bearing type.

| Selection Criterion | Deep Groove Ball | Angular Contact Ball | Self-Aligning Ball | Thrust Ball | Cylindrical Roller | Needle Roller | Tapered Roller | Spherical Roller | Thrust Roller |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Radial Load Capacity** | Medium | Medium | Low-Medium | None | Very High | High | Very High | Very High | None |
| **Axial Load Capacity** | Low | High | Very Low | Very High | Low | None | High | Moderate | Very High |
| **Combined Load** | Poor | Excellent | Poor | N/A | Poor | None | Excellent | Moderate | N/A |
| **Limiting Speed** | Very High | High | Moderate | Low | Very High | Low-Moderate | High | Moderate | Low |
| **Misalignment Tolerance** | Very Low | Very Low | Excellent | Low | Very Low | Low | Low | Excellent | Low |
| **Friction Torque** | Lowest | Low | Low | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate-High | High | High |
| **Cost (per capacity)** | Low | Medium | Medium | Low | Medium | Low | Medium-High | High | High |
| **Noise Level** | Low | Low | Low | Moderate | Low-Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | High |
| **Typical Series** (ISO) | 6000, 6200 | 7200, 7300 | 1200, 1300 | 51000, 52000 | NU, NJ, NUP | K, NK | 30000, 32000 | 22200, 22300 | 29200, 29300 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Rule of Thumb:** If a problem gives a **purely radial** load and high speed, the answer is **Deep Groove Ball**. If it gives **radial + axial** and asks for a single bearing, the answer is **Angular Contact** (or **Tapered Roller** for higher capacity). If **misalignment** is mentioned, the answer is **Self-Aligning Ball** or **Spherical Roller**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture — Rolling Bearing Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Input Shaft Section] --> nodeB[Inner Race Ring]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Raceway Groove on Inner Race]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Rolling Elements - Balls or Rollers]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Raceway Groove on Outer Race]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Outer Race Ring]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Housing Bore]
    nodeH[Cage Retainer] --> nodeD
    nodeI[Shield or Seal 2Z or 2RS] --> nodeF
    nodeI --> nodeB
    nodeJ[Lubricant Grease or Oil] --> nodeC
    nodeJ --> nodeE

    classDef rot fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef fixed fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef rolling fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef aux fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px

    class A rot
    class B rot
    class F fixed
    class G fixed
    class C rot
    class E fixed
    class D rolling
    class H aux
    class I aux
    class J aux
```

**Color Legend:**
- **Gold = Rotating** parts (input shaft, inner race)
- **Sky Blue = Stationary** parts (outer race, housing)
- **Green = Rolling Elements** (balls or rollers)
- **Pink = Auxiliary** parts (cage, seals, lubricant)

---

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Bearing Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Begin Bearing Selection] --> q1{What is the primary load direction}
    q1 -- Pure Radial --> q2{Is high speed required}
    q1 -- Pure Axial --> thrust[Select Thrust Ball or Thrust Roller Bearing]
    q1 -- Combined Radial plus Axial --> combined{Single row or paired}
    q2 -- Yes --> dgrb[Select Deep Groove Ball Bearing]
    q2 -- No --> q3{Is misalignment a concern}
    q3 -- Yes --> sa[Select Self Aligning Ball or Spherical Roller]
    q3 -- No --> q4{Need maximum radial capacity}
    q4 -- Yes --> cyl[Select Cylindrical Roller Bearing]
    q4 -- No and compact radial space --> ndl[Select Needle Roller Bearing]
    combined -- Paired high precision --> acb[Select Angular Contact Ball Bearing DB or DF]
    combined -- Heavy duty high load --> trb[Select Tapered Roller Bearing]
    combined -- Heavy load plus misalignment --> srb[Select Spherical Roller Bearing]

    classDef startNode fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef resultNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    classDef decisionNode fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:1px

    class start startNode
    class thrust,dgrb,sa,cyl,ndl,acb,trb,srb resultNode
    class q1,q2,q3,q4,combined decisionNode
```

---

## 4.3 Block Architecture — Hertzian Contact Types vs. Bearing Type

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a[Point Contact - Sphere on Sphere] --> a1[Deep Groove Ball Bearing]
    a --> a2[Angular Contact Ball Bearing]
    a --> a3[Self Aligning Ball Bearing]
    a --> a4[Thrust Ball Bearing]

    b[Line Contact - Cylinder on Plane] --> b1[Cylindrical Roller Bearing]
    b --> b2[Needle Roller Bearing]

    c[Line Contact - Tapered Cone on Cone] --> c1[Tapered Roller Bearing]

    d[Line Contact - Barrel on Sphere] --> d1[Spherical Roller Bearing]

    e[Line Contact - Cylinder on Flat] --> e1[Thrust Roller Bearing]

    classDef point fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef line fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px
    classDef bear fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px

    class a,b,c,d,e point
    class a1,a2,a3,a4,b1,b2,c1,d1,e1 bear
```

**Key Insight for KTU 2024:**
- **Point contact** (ball bearings) → exponent $p = 3$ in life equation.
- **Line contact** (roller bearings) → exponent $p = 10/3$ in life equation.
- This is why roller bearings have higher load capacity for the same size.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between ball bearings and roller bearings with respect to the type of contact, load-carrying capacity, and limiting speed.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S.No. | Parameter | Ball Bearing | Roller Bearing |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Type of contact | Point contact | Line contact |
| 2 | Load-carrying capacity | Lower (60% less) | Higher |
| 3 | Limiting speed | Higher | Lower (about 70% of ball) |
| 4 | Hertzian exponent $p$ | 3 | 10/3 ≈ 3.33 |
| 5 | Noise & friction | Less | More |
| 6 | Cost (per unit load) | Higher | Lower |

**Concluding line (1 mark):** Ball bearings are preferred for high-speed light-to-medium load applications, while roller bearings are preferred for heavy-load low-to-moderate speed applications.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Why is a self-aligning ball bearing used in long shafts? Mention the typical misalignment angle it can accommodate.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A self-aligning ball bearing has **two rows of balls** running on a **common spherical outer raceway**. The inner race has two grooved raceways inclined at a common angle, allowing the inner assembly (shaft + inner race + balls) to **pivot** relative to the outer race.

**Why used in long shafts:** Long shafts deflect under their own weight and under transverse loads, causing the inner and outer race centers to become non-collinear (misalignment of 1° to 3°). A self-aligning bearing accommodates this without inducing destructive edge loading on the raceways.

**Typical misalignment:** **1.5° to 3° static**, and **0.5° to 1° dynamic**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 / Internal Choice Type]

**A 50 mm diameter shaft is supported by two tapered roller bearings in an O-arrangement (back-to-back). The shaft carries a radial load of 8000 N and an axial load of 2500 N at the bearing location. The radial load is equally shared between the two bearings. Speed = 1450 rpm. Required life = 20,000 hours. Calculate:**
**(a)** The equivalent dynamic load on each bearing. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The required dynamic load rating of the bearing, and recommend a suitable 30000-series bearing. **(7 marks)**

**Catalogue data for the tapered roller bearing series 30206:**

| Bearing No. | $C$ (kN) | $C_0$ (kN) | $e$ | $X$ | $Y$ | $K = F_a/F_r$ condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 30206 | 47.5 | 53.0 | 0.31 | 0.40 | 1.80 | for $K > e$ |
| 30207 | 62.0 | 71.0 | 0.31 | 0.40 | 1.80 | for $K > e$ |
| 30208 | 75.0 | 86.5 | 0.34 | 0.40 | 1.80 | for $K > e$ |

**Assume duty factor $f_d = 1.5$ for moderate shock.**

---

#### (a) Solution — Equivalent Dynamic Load [7 marks]

**Step 1: Radial load on each bearing (load sharing)**
$$F_r = \dfrac{8000}{2} = 4000 \text{ N (per bearing)}$$

**Step 2: Axial load distribution in O-arrangement**

In an O-arrangement, the axial load is borne primarily by one bearing (the most loaded). Since the radial load is equally shared and the axial load is given as 2500 N at the location:
$$F_a = 2500 \text{ N (per bearing, applied to loaded one)}$$

**Step 3: Compute load ratio $K$**
$$K = \dfrac{F_a}{F_r} = \dfrac{2500}{4000} = 0.625$$

**Step 4: Compare with $e = 0.31$**
$$K = 0.625 > e = 0.31$$

Therefore $X = 0.40$ and $Y = 1.80$.

**Step 5: Apply duty factor**
$$P = f_d \cdot (X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a)$$
$$P = 1.5 \cdot (0.40 \cdot 4000 + 1.80 \cdot 2500)$$
$$P = 1.5 \cdot (1600 + 4500)$$
$$P = 1.5 \cdot 6100$$
$$\boxed{P = 9150 \text{ N} = 9.15 \text{ kN}}$$

---

#### (b) Solution — Required Dynamic Rating & Bearing Selection [7 marks]

**Step 1: Convert life to millions of revolutions**
$$L_{10} = \dfrac{60 \cdot n \cdot L_{10h}}{10^6}$$
$$L_{10} = \dfrac{60 \cdot 1450 \cdot 20{,}000}{10^6} = \dfrac{1.74 \times 10^9}{10^6} = 1740 \text{ million rev}$$

**Step 2: Required dynamic load rating (for tapered roller bearing, $p = 10/3$)**
$$C_{req} = P \cdot (L_{10})^{1/p}$$
$$C_{req} = 9150 \cdot (1740)^{3/10}$$
$$(1740)^{0.3} = \text{Compute: } \ln(1740) = 7.461$$
$$0.3 \times 7.461 = 2.238$$
$$e^{2.238} = 9.38$$
$$C_{req} = 9150 \cdot 9.38$$
$$\boxed{C_{req} \approx 85{,}847 \text{ N} = 85.85 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 3: Select bearing from catalogue**
- Bearing **30206** has $C = 47.5$ kN — **Insufficient** (47.5 < 85.85).
- Bearing **30207** has $C = 62.0$ kN — **Insufficient** (62.0 < 85.85).
- Bearing **30208** has $C = 75.0$ kN — **Insufficient** (75.0 < 85.85).

**None of the listed bearings meet the requirement.** Either:
- Use the next higher series (e.g., 30209 with $C \approx 95$ kN), OR
- Reconsider the load sharing or duty factor.

**Conclusion:** Select **Bearing No. 30209** (or equivalent) for safe operation.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks): [Alternative Choice — KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**A single-row deep groove ball bearing is to carry a radial load of 6 kN at 3000 rpm. The bearing is to be selected for a life of 25,000 hours. Catalogue data are given below:**

| Bearing No. | Bore (mm) | $C$ (kN) | $C_0$ (kN) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 6305 | 25 | 23.4 | 11.6 |
| 6306 | 30 | 29.6 | 16.0 |
| 6307 | 35 | 35.1 | 19.0 |
| 6308 | 40 | 41.0 | 22.4 |

**Determine:**
**(a)** The required dynamic load rating. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The appropriate bearing from the catalogue, and compute the actual life in hours of the selected bearing. **(7 marks)**

---

#### (a) Solution — Required Dynamic Load Rating [7 marks]

**Step 1: Identify the case**

Deep groove ball bearing → **p = 3**. Pure radial load (no axial) → $P = F_r = 6$ kN.

**Step 2: Convert required life to millions of revolutions**
$$L_{10} = \dfrac{60 \cdot n \cdot L_{10h}}{10^6} = \dfrac{60 \cdot 3000 \cdot 25{,}000}{10^6} = \dfrac{4.5 \times 10^9}{10^6} = 4500 \text{ million rev}$$

**Step 3: Compute required $C$**
$$C_{req} = P \cdot (L_{10})^{1/p} = 6000 \cdot (4500)^{1/3}$$
$$\sqrt[3]{4500} = ? \quad 16^3 = 4096, \quad 16.5^3 = 4492, \quad 16.51^3 \approx 4500$$
$$C_{req} = 6000 \cdot 16.51$$
$$\boxed{C_{req} \approx 99{,}060 \text{ N} = 99.06 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 4: Conclusion for (a)**

The required dynamic load rating is **99.06 kN**.

---

#### (b) Solution — Bearing Selection & Actual Life [7 marks]

**Step 1: Select from catalogue**

Reviewing the four candidate bearings:
- 6305: $C = 23.4$ kN — **Insufficient** (only 23.6% of required).
- 6306: $C = 29.6$ kN — **Insufficient** (29.9% of required).
- 6307: $C = 35.1$ kN — **Insufficient** (35.4% of required).
- 6308: $C = 41.0$ kN — **Insufficient** (41.4% of required).

**None of the given bearings meets the requirement** of 99.06 kN. The designer must either:
1. Select a larger bearing series (e.g., 6312, 6313, or 6314).
2. Reduce the load, speed, or required life.

**Step 2: For illustration, let us select the largest available bearing 6308 and compute its actual life.**

$$L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^3 = \left(\dfrac{41{,}000}{6000}\right)^3 = (6.833)^3$$
$$L_{10} = 6.833^3 = 319.2 \text{ million rev}$$

**Convert to hours:**
$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{L_{10} \cdot 10^6}{60 \cdot n} = \dfrac{319.2 \times 10^6}{60 \cdot 3000} = \dfrac{319.2 \times 10^6}{180{,}000}$$
$$\boxed{L_{10h} \approx 1773 \text{ hours}}$$

**Step 3: Verify safety**

Actual life (1773 h) is far less than required (25,000 h) → **Bearing 6308 is unsafe for this duty**.

**Final Recommendation:** Upgrade to a larger bearing (e.g., 6312 with $C = 81.9$ kN) or 6313 with $C = 92.3$ kN, or further to 6314/6315 series. The 6313 would give:

$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6}{60 \cdot 3000} \left(\dfrac{92{,}300}{6000}\right)^3 = 5.556 \cdot (15.383)^3 = 5.556 \cdot 3641 = 20{,}228 \text{ h}$$

The 6313 is still slightly short. A **6314** ($C = 102$ kN) would comfortably exceed 25,000 hours.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the duty factor** $f_d$ (or service factor) — accounts for vibration, shock, and starting torque. Forgetting it underestimates $P$ by 50% and leads to **under-design**.
> 2. **Confusing the Hertzian exponent $p$** — students often use $p = 3$ for roller bearings (or $p = 10/3$ for ball bearings). The correct mapping is **ball = 3, roller = 10/3**.
> 3. **Mixing up $L_{10}$ in revolutions and $L_{10h}$ in hours** — convert carefully using $L_{10} = (60n \cdot L_{10h})/10^6$.
> 4. **Wrong $X$ and $Y$ selection** — the values depend on whether $F_a / F_r$ is greater or less than $e$. Always compute $K$ first.
> 5. **Forgetting the load-sharing effect in paired bearings** — radial load is shared, but axial load is taken primarily by one bearing in O- or X-arrangement.
> 6. **Unit mistakes** — $C$ is in Newtons, $L_{10h}$ in hours, $n$ in rpm, $L_{10}$ in millions of revolutions. Keep all consistent.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### High-Density Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Two major categories:** Ball bearings (point contact, $p=3$) and Roller bearings (line contact, $p=10/3$).
- [ ] **Deep Groove Ball** = most common radial bearing, very high speed, no axial capability beyond light thrust.
- [ ] **Angular Contact Ball** = combined radial + axial load; contact angle $\alpha$ = 15°, 25°, or 40°.
- [ ] **Self-Aligning Ball** = 2 rows of balls on spherical outer race; tolerates 1.5°–3° misalignment.
- [ ] **Thrust Ball** = pure axial load; **never** use for pure radial load.
- [ ] **Cylindrical Roller** = very high radial load; NU, NJ, NUP, N types.
- [ ] **Needle Roller** = L/D ≥ 3:1; compact radial space; no inner race option.
- [ ] **Tapered Roller** = combined radial + axial; always mounted in pairs; most common in wheel hubs.
- [ ] **Spherical Roller** = highest load capacity + misalignment (0.5°–2°); barrel-shaped rollers.
- [ ] **Thrust Roller** = pure axial load at high capacity; low speed only.
- [ ] **Fundamental life equation:** $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$.
- [ ] **Conversion to hours:** $L_{10h} = 10^6 L_{10} / (60n)$.
- [ ] **Equivalent load:** $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ (only if $K > e$; otherwise $P = F_r$).
- [ ] **Static equivalent load:** $P_0 = X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a$.
- [ ] **Standard Hertzian exponent $p$ = 3 for ball, 10/3 for roller** — this is the most important single number to remember.
- [ ] **Duty factor $f_d$ = 1.0 (no shock), 1.0–1.2 (light), 1.2–1.8 (moderate), 1.8–3.0 (heavy shock).**
- [ ] **ISO/IS bearing series digits:** first digit = type, second = dimension series, last two = bore code.
- [ ] **Bore code rule:** bore (mm) = 5 × last two digits (for codes 04 to 96, i.e., 20 mm and above).
- [ ] **Material of construction:** through-hardened chromium steel (AISI 52100), case-hardened for shock loads.
- [ ] **Lubrication methods:** grease (sealed-for-life bearings), oil bath, oil mist, oil jet (for high speeds).
- [ ] **Sealing codes:** Z = single shield, 2Z = double shield (metallic), RS = single contact seal, 2RS = double contact seal.
- [ ] **Typical KTU application mapping:** Electric motor = 6200/6300 Deep Groove Ball; Wheel hub = 30200/32200 Tapered Roller; Long shaft = 1200/1300 Self-Aligning; Lathe spindle = 7200 Angular Contact.

### Five-Second Mnemonic

> **"B-A-S-T-N-T-S"** → **B**all (Deep groove), **A**ngular contact, **S**elf-aligning, **T**hrust; Roller (Cylindrical, **N**eedle, **T**apered, **S**pherical, **T**hrust).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bearing Life
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT521 - DESIGN OF TRANSMISSION ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bearing Life

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bearing Life (Standard Terminology per ISO 15:2017 & ISO 281:2007)**
> The **rating life** of a rolling-element bearing, conventionally denoted as **L₁₀**, is defined as the number of revolutions (or operating hours at a constant speed) that **90 % of a group of apparently identical bearings** will attain or exceed before the first evidence of material fatigue develops (i.e., before **spalling** initiates on a raceway or rolling element). The remaining 10 % may fail due to classical rolling-contact fatigue, but for design purposes the L₁₀ population guarantees a 90 % reliability figure.

The complementary term **basic dynamic load rating (C)** is the constant, purely radial (for radial bearings) or central axial (for thrust bearings) load that a bearing can theoretically endure for a rating life of **one million revolutions** under ideal laboratory conditions (no contamination, perfect lubrication, no misalignment).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Engineering Intuition

Think of a rolling-element bearing as a **fleet of identical delivery scooters** working in a city. Every scooter has a *warranty mileage* (the basic dynamic load rating **C**), and the manufacturer guarantees that **9 out of 10 scooters** will not break down before reaching that mileage. The actual mileage any single scooter achieves depends on how heavily it is loaded, how fast it runs, how clean the road is, and how well it is oiled. In bearing language:

| Scooter Analogy | Bearing Equivalent | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Actual weight carried by the scooter | Dynamic equivalent load on the bearing | **P** |
| Warranty mileage of the scooter | Basic dynamic load rating | **C** |
| Distance covered before first breakdown | Rating life | **L₁₀** |
| 9 out of 10 reliability figure | 90 % reliability standard | **L₁₀** |
| Road cleanliness, oil quality, driver style | Adjustment factors | **a₁, a₂₃** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "L₁₀" and not "L₅₀"?** Because 10 % failure is a statistically safe, *economically* acceptable compromise in industry. Designing for 50 % reliability would be conservative and waste material; designing for 99 % reliability would inflate the bearing size and cost. **L₁₀ is the global engineering sweet-spot**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

- **Weibull slope / shape parameter for bearings (e) = 1.5** (approximately 1.57 for ball bearings, 1.11 for roller bearings in classical theory).
- **Load-life exponent (p) = 3** for ball bearings, **p = 10/3 ≈ 3.333** for roller bearings.
- **One million revolutions (10⁶)** is the universal reference life for basic dynamic load rating.
- **Standard reliability of 90 %** is the baseline for L₁₀.
- **Dynamic load rating unit: Newtons (N)** in SI, **kgf** in MKS legacy.
- **Viscosity ratio (κ or ν) ≥ 1** is recommended for full-fluid film lubrication.
- **Maximum permissible ring temperature for standard bearings = 120 °C** (above which dimensional stabilization is required).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Fatigue Theory (The "Why" Behind L₁₀)

Rolling-contact fatigue is a *surface-initiated* failure mode. Every time a rolling element passes over a raceway, it imposes a Hertzian contact stress pulse. After tens of millions of these pulses, sub-surface shear cracks nucleate at the depth of maximum reversed shear stress, propagate parallel to the surface, and finally turn upward to liberate a flake of material — the classic **spall** (also called "pitting").

The Weibull cumulative distribution function for the probability of survival **Fₛ** after **L** revolutions is:

$$F_s(L) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\dfrac{L}{a}\right)^e\right]$$

where **a** is the *scale parameter* (characteristic life) and **e ≈ 1.5** is the *shape parameter*. Because the load per revolution scales with the Hertzian contact stress, which in turn scales with the cube-root of load for balls and 10/3 root for rollers, the inverse-power law **L ∝ (C/P)ᵖ** emerges naturally.

## 2.2 The "How" — Step-by-Step Logic of the Life Equation

1. **Determine the operating load P** on the bearing from external radial and axial forces using the dynamic equivalent load formula.
2. **Read the basic dynamic load rating C** from the manufacturer's catalogue for the selected bearing number.
3. **Apply the inverse-power law** L₁₀ = (C/P)ᵖ to obtain rating life in millions of revolutions.
4. **Convert to operating hours** by dividing by 60 N (N being the speed in rpm).
5. **Apply modification factors** (a₁, a₂₃) if a more accurate estimate of *real-world* life is required (ISO 281:2007 methodology).
6. **Compare calculated life** with the *desired application life* (e.g., 20 000 h for industrial gearboxes, 5 000 h for automotive gearboxes, 40 000 h for wind-turbine gearboxes).
7. **Re-select the bearing** if the calculated life is too low — a larger bore usually raises C substantially.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **must-memorize** set of equations for any KTU ESE / Sessional exam on bearing life. Note the use of `\vert` and `\mid` instead of the vertical pipe character to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Equation (LaTeX form) | Variables & Units | When to use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{p}$ millions of revolutions | $L_{10}$ = rating life in $10^{6}$ rev, $C$ = dynamic load rating in N, $P$ = equivalent dynamic load in N, $p$ = 3 or 10/3 | First-pass rating life under ideal conditions |
| 2 | $L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^{6}}{60\,N}\left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{p}$ hours | $N$ = speed in rpm, $L_{10h}$ = life in hours | Comparing with desired machine life in hours |
| 3 | $P = X\,F_r + Y\,F_a$ | $F_r$ = radial load, $F_a$ = axial load, $X, Y$ from catalogue (depend on $F_a/C_0$) | When both radial and axial loads act |
| 4 | $P = F_r$ when $F_a = 0$ (pure radial case) | — | Pure radial loading |
| 5 | $P = F_a$ when $F_r = 0$ and $\alpha = 90°$ (pure thrust case) | — | Pure thrust loading |
| 6 | $C_0$ = static load rating (N) | from catalogue | Required to find $F_a/C_0$ ratio for $X, Y$ |
| 7 | $L_{nm} = a_1 \cdot a_{23} \cdot L_{10}$ | $a_1$ = reliability factor, $a_{23}$ = combined lubrication-contamination factor | ISO 281 advanced life estimation |
| 8 | $a_1 = \left(\dfrac{0.21 - \ln(R)}{\ln\!\left[1 - (1-R)^{1/e}\right]}\right)^{1/p}$ | $R$ = desired reliability fraction, $e$ = Weibull slope | Reliability ≠ 90 % cases |
| 9 | $a_{23} = f(\kappa, \eta_c, d_m, n)$ | $\kappa$ = viscosity ratio, $\eta_c$ = contamination factor, $d_m$ = pitch diameter, $n$ = rpm | Real-life factor; $a_{23}$ is read from a chart in the data book |
| 10 | $L_{10\;(\text{variable load})} = \dfrac{1}{\displaystyle\sum \dfrac{q_i}{L_{10i}}}$ | $q_i$ = fraction of operating time at load $P_i$ | Mine machinery, rolling mills — fluctuating load |
| 11 | $f_c = 1$ (default), $f_c \in [0.5, 1.5]$ in some standards | application / contamination factor in old ISO standards | Legacy/older problems |
| 12 | Required life criteria: $L_{10h} \ge L_{10h,\;required}$ | $L_{10h,required}$ from design specs (e.g. 20 000 h) | Final check before declaring design safe |

### 2.3.1 Ball-Bearing vs. Roller-Bearing Exponent — Why the Difference?

For a **ball** under Hertzian point contact, contact stress is **∝ P^(1/3)**, and the volume of highly stressed material is **∝ P^(1)**. Sub-surface fatigue life is **∝ (stress)⁻⁹** experimentally, leading to **L ∝ P⁻³** ⇒ **p = 3**.

For a **roller** under Hertzian line contact, the stressed volume is **∝ P^(4/3)**, and experimentally **L ∝ P⁻^(10/3)** ⇒ **p = 10/3 ≈ 3.333**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Higher p-value means rollers are MORE sensitive to overload than balls.** A 50 % overload reduces ball-bearing life by 12.3 %, but roller-bearing life by 16.4 %.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Bearing life governs the design of **every rotating shaft** in industry: gearboxes, electric motors, pumps, compressors, machine-tool spindles, automotive wheel hubs, aircraft turbine mainshafts, wind-turbine gearboxes, paper-machine rolls, and railway axleboxes. The L₁₀ concept is the **lingua franca of reliability engineering** for rotating equipment and underpins the **AGMA, ISO, and DIN** bearing-selection standards. It also feeds into **predictive-maintenance software** where the *remaining useful life (RUL)* of an asset is computed by tracking lubrication condition, contamination ingress, and measured vibration spectra.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Weibull Failure Statistics to the L₁₀ Equation

**Starting premise.** The probability of survival of a single bearing after L revolutions, under Weibull modelling, is:

$$F_s(L) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\dfrac{L}{a}\right)^{e}\right]$$

By definition, **L₁₀** is the life at which the survival probability is 90 %, i.e. Fₛ = 0.90. Setting this:

$$0.90 = \exp\!\left[-\left(\dfrac{L_{10}}{a}\right)^{e}\right]$$

Taking the natural log of both sides:

$$\ln(0.90) = -\left(\dfrac{L_{10}}{a}\right)^{e}$$

Since ln(0.90) = −0.10536:

$$0.10536 = \left(\dfrac{L_{10}}{a}\right)^{e}$$

Raise both sides to the power 1/e:

$$\dfrac{L_{10}}{a} = (0.10536)^{1/e}$$

Therefore:

$$L_{10} = a \cdot (0.10536)^{1/e}$$

Similarly, for an arbitrary equivalent load P, the characteristic life **a** depends on load as **a = b · P⁻ᵖ** (the inverse-power law), where **b** is a constant for a given bearing series. Substituting:

$$L_{10}(P) = b \cdot P^{-p} \cdot (0.10536)^{1/e}$$

By definition, the **basic dynamic load rating C** is the load at which L₁₀ = 1 (one million revolutions):

$$1 = b \cdot C^{-p} \cdot (0.10536)^{1/e} \;\Rightarrow\; b = C^{p} \cdot (0.10536)^{-1/e}$$

Substituting **b** back into the L₁₀(P) expression:

$$L_{10}(P) = C^{p} \cdot (0.10536)^{-1/e} \cdot P^{-p} \cdot (0.10536)^{1/e}$$

The (0.10536) terms cancel exactly to give:

$$\boxed{\,L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{p} \quad \text{millions of revolutions}\,}$$

This is the **fundamental rating-life equation** required by KTU.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Conversion from Revolutions to Hours

Given that a bearing rotates at **N revolutions per minute**, the number of revolutions in **L₁₀ₕ hours** is:

$$L_{10,\;rev} = L_{10h} \times 60 \times N$$

Equating this with the L₁₀ equation in millions of revolutions (i.e. dividing by 10⁶):

$$\dfrac{L_{10h} \times 60 \times N}{10^{6}} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{p}$$

Solving for L₁₀ₕ:

$$\boxed{\,L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^{6}}{60\,N}\left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{p} \quad \text{hours}\,}$$

This is the form used in **every KTU numerical problem** because desired life is almost always specified in hours.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Modified Life (ISO 281) — Combining a₁, a₂₃

The classical L₁₀ assumes a "perfect world". The ISO 281:2007 modification introduces three adjustment factors:

- **a₁** — reliability factor (a₁ = 1 for R = 90 %).
- **a₂₃** — combined factor accounting for:
  - **a₂** (lubricant) — function of viscosity ratio κ = ν/ν₁.
  - **a₃** (contamination) — function of cleanliness level (typical values 0.2 → 1.0).

The modified rating life is:

$$\boxed{\,L_{nm} = a_{1} \cdot a_{23} \cdot \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{p} \quad \text{millions of revolutions}\,}$$

with the explicit Weibull-based formula for a₁:

$$a_{1} = \left(\dfrac{0.21 - \ln R}{\ln\!\left[1 - (1-R)^{1/e}\right]}\right)^{1/p}$$

where **R** is the desired reliability (e.g., R = 0.95 ⇒ 95 %), and **e ≈ 1.5**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Life Under Variable (Piecewise-Constant) Loading

For a duty cycle consisting of *n* load levels, each occupying a fraction **qᵢ** of the operating time (∑qᵢ = 1), the cumulative damage hypothesis (Miner's rule adapted to bearings) gives:

$$L_{10,\; \text{var}} = \dfrac{1}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{q_i}{L_{10i}}}$$

where **L₁₀ᵢ** is the rating life computed at the iᵗʰ load level **Pᵢ** using the standard L₁₀ equation. The result is in the same units as L₁₀ᵢ (usually hours).

> [!NOTE]
> This is the form used in **mining, steel, and railway applications** where the load profile is documented but fluctuating.

## 3.5 Solved Numerical Example (Worked Model)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]**
> A 6308 deep-groove ball bearing carries a radial load of 4 200 N at a speed of 1 450 rpm. The catalogue gives C = 41 000 N and C₀ = 22 400 N. Compute the rating life in hours.

**Step 1 — Identify the load type.** Pure radial load ⇒ Fₐ = 0 ⇒ P = Fᵣ = 4 200 N.

**Step 2 — Apply the rating-life equation (millions of revolutions).**

$$L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{41\,000}{4\,200}\right)^{3}$$

Compute the ratio:

$$\dfrac{41\,000}{4\,200} = 9.7619$$

Cube it:

$$(9.7619)^{3} = 9.7619 \times 9.7619 \times 9.7619 = 95.30 \times 9.7619 = 930.34 \;\text{million revolutions}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to hours.**

$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^{6}}{60 \times 1\,450} \times 930.34 = \dfrac{930.34}{87} = 10.69 \times 10^{3}\,\text{hours}$$

So **L₁₀ₕ ≈ 10 690 hours** — adequate for a 4 000-hour industrial service, but marginal for a 20 000-hour wind-turbine duty, where a 6311 bearing would be selected.

## 3.6 Analytic Matrix — Bearing-Type × Load-Type Decision Table

| Bearing type | p | Line/Point contact | Sensitivity to overload | Typical application | Preferred when |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Deep-groove ball | 3 | Point | Low | General purpose, light-to-medium loads | Cost-effective, high speed |
| Angular-contact ball | 3 | Point | Low | Pumps, gearboxes, machine tools | Combined radial + axial load |
| Self-aligning ball | 3 | Point | Low | Conveyor shafts, textile machinery | Shaft misalignment present |
| Cylindrical roller | 10/3 | Line | High | Gearboxes, rolling mills | High radial load, no axial |
| Spherical roller | 10/3 | Line | High | Crushers, vibrating screens | Heavy radial + shock loads |
| Tapered roller | 10/3 | Line | High | Automotive wheels, gearboxes | Combined radial + axial |
| Needle roller | 10/3 | Line | High | Limited-space, oscillating applications | Compact radial support |
| Thrust ball | 3 | Point | Low (axial only) | Vertical-shaft pumps, turntables | Pure axial load |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Bearing-Life Calculation Workflow

The following **Mermaid flowchart** captures the *exact* decision sequence a KTU-2024 student must follow when solving a bearing-life problem. Every box is a *node*, and every flow is a *decision* or *computation*.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read Shaft Geometry and Loads] --> B{Loads Are Constant or Variable?}
    B -- Constant --> C[Compute Dynamic Equivalent Load P = X*Fr + Y*Fa]
    B -- Variable --> D[Identify Duty Cycle Pi and qi]
    D --> C
    C --> E[Read C and C0 from Manufacturer Catalogue]
    E --> F{Desired Reliability = 90 percent or Higher?}
    F -- 90 percent --> G[Use Classical L10 = C slash P to the power p]
    F -- Higher --> H[Compute a1 from Weibull formula]
    H --> I[Look up a23 from Viscosity and Contamination Chart]
    I --> J[Use Modified Lnm = a1 times a23 times L10]
    G --> K[Convert L10 in million rev to hours using 10 to power 6 over 60 N]
    J --> K
    K --> L{L10h greater than or equal to Lrequired}
    L -- Yes --> M[Bearing is Safe: Report Final Selection]
    L -- No --> N[Re-select Next Larger Bore or Higher C]
    N --> C
    M --> O[End: Check Lubrication Schedule and Mounting]
```

**Reading guide for the diagram:**

- **Nodes `A` and `O`** mark the start and end of the design cycle.
- **Diamonds (`B`, `F`, `L`)** are *decision points* requiring engineering judgement.
- **Rectangles (`C`, `E`, `G`, `J`, `K`)** are *computation steps* that produce intermediate numerical results.
- **Loop-back from `N` to `C`** captures the iterative nature of bearing selection — designers usually try 2–3 candidate bearing numbers before settling.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Factors Affecting Real Bearing Life

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LO[Loading P] --> CC[Core L10 in Million Rev]
    SPEED[Speed N in rpm] --> CC
    CC --> RL[Rated Life]
    SUB[Subsurface Shear Stress] --> RL
    LUB[Lubrication Condition] --> FACTA23[a23 Factor]
    CONT[Contamination Level] --> FACTA23
    TEMP[Operating Temperature] --> FACTA23
    FACTA23 --> ML[Modified Life Lnm]
    REL[Required Reliability R] --> FACTA1[a1 Factor]
    FACTA1 --> ML
    ML --> CHECK{Meets Required Life}
    CHECK -- Yes --> SAFE[Acceptable Design]
    CHECK -- No --> REJECT[Resize Bearing or Improve Lubrication]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Why L₁₀ and Not L₅₀ (Reliability Concept)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    POP[Population of 100 Identical Bearings] --> OPER[Operated Under Identical Conditions]
    OPER --> SURV[90 Bearings Still Survive]
    OPER --> FAIL[10 Bearings Have Spalled]
    SURV --> L10[Time at Which 10 percent Have Failed Equals L10]
    FAIL --> L10
    L10 --> OUT[Rating Life of the Population]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the terms: (i) Basic dynamic load rating, (ii) Static load rating, and (iii) Rating life of a rolling-element bearing as per ISO 15:2017.**

**Model Answer:**

> (i) **Basic dynamic load rating (C):** The constant, purely radial load (for radial bearings) or central axial load (for thrust bearings) that a bearing can theoretically endure for a rating life of **one million revolutions** under ideal laboratory conditions.
>
> (ii) **Static load rating (C₀):** The load that produces a permanent deformation of **0.0001 times the ball diameter** at the most heavily loaded rolling-element contact. It is used to check *static* (non-rotating) safety, not fatigue life.
>
> (iii) **Rating life (L₁₀):** The number of revolutions (or hours at constant speed) that **90 %** of a group of apparently identical bearings will attain or exceed before the first evidence of rolling-contact fatigue develops.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Why is the load-life exponent *p* = 10/3 for roller bearings and *p* = 3 for ball bearings? Justify in two lines.**

**Model Answer:**

The exponent depends on the **Hertzian contact type**: balls create **point contact** where the stressed volume scales linearly with load, while rollers create **line contact** where the stressed volume scales as P^(4/3). Experimental rolling-contact fatigue tests yield an inverse relationship of life with the **9th power of stress**, which collapses to **L ∝ P⁻³** for balls and **L ∝ P⁻^(10/3)** for rollers.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Pattern

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. The student must answer *either* Question A *or* Question B in full.

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Module 4 Set A]`

#### Part (a) (7 Marks)
A 6206 deep-groove ball bearing operates at 2 880 rpm and supports a radial load of 2 500 N. Catalogue values: **C = 19 500 N**, **C₀ = 11 500 N**. Calculate:
- (i) Rating life L₁₀ in million revolutions. (3 marks)
- (ii) Rating life in hours. (2 marks)
- (iii) State whether the bearing is suitable for an application requiring 8 000 hours of life. (2 marks)

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Pure radial case ⇒ P = Fᵣ = 2 500 N.**

**Step 2 — Rating life in million revolutions:**

$$L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{3} = \left(\dfrac{19\,500}{2\,500}\right)^{3} = (7.8)^{3}$$

Compute (7.8)³:

$$(7.8)^{2} = 60.84, \quad (7.8)^{3} = 60.84 \times 7.8 = 474.55 \;\text{million revolutions}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to hours at N = 2 880 rpm:**

$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^{6}}{60 \times 2\,880} \times 474.55 = \dfrac{474.55}{0.1728} = 2\,746.4 \;\text{hours}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with 8 000 h requirement:**

Since 2 746 h < 8 000 h, the **6206 bearing is NOT suitable**. **Recommendation:** upgrade to a **6207** or **6208** bearing (C = 25 800 N for 6207) and re-check.

Quick re-check with 6207: P = 2 500 N, C = 25 800 N.

$$L_{10} = (25\,800 / 2\,500)^{3} = (10.32)^{3} = 1\,098.7 \;\text{million rev}$$

$$L_{10h} = 1\,098.7 \times 10^{6} / (60 \times 2\,880) = 6\,358 \;\text{hours}$$

Still marginal — go to **6208** (C = 32 500 N):

$$L_{10} = (32\,500 / 2\,500)^{3} = (13)^{3} = 2\,197 \;\text{million rev} \;\Rightarrow\; L_{10h} = 12\,706 \;\text{hours}$$

**6208 is acceptable** ✔.

#### Part (b) (7 Marks)
Define **viscosity ratio (κ)**. How does κ affect the **a₂₃** factor in the modified rating-life equation per ISO 281:2007? A 22210 E spherical roller bearing has C = 91 000 N and operates at 1 600 rpm with a load of 18 000 N. If **a₁ = 1** and **a₂₃ = 0.4**, compute the modified life in hours.

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Definition of Viscosity Ratio:**

$$\kappa = \dfrac{\nu_{\text{operating}}}{\nu_{1}}$$

where **ν₁** is the reference viscosity at the operating temperature (the viscosity required for an adequate lubricant film), and **ν_operating** is the actual kinematic viscosity of the chosen lubricant at the operating temperature. Recommended **κ ≥ 1** for full-fluid-film lubrication; **κ < 1** implies boundary or mixed lubrication and shorter life.

**Effect on a₂₃:**

The factor **a₂₃** is read from a chart (in the data book) as a function of κ, contamination factor ηc, pitch diameter d_m, and rotational speed n. Generally:

- **κ ≥ 4 and clean oil** ⇒ a₂₃ ≈ 1.0 (ideal).
- **κ ≈ 1 and clean oil** ⇒ a₂₃ ≈ 0.5.
- **κ < 0.4 and contaminated oil** ⇒ a₂₃ < 0.2 (severe penalty).

**Numerical Solution (Roller, p = 10/3):**

$$L_{nm} = a_{1} \cdot a_{23} \cdot \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{p} = 1 \times 0.4 \times \left(\dfrac{91\,000}{18\,000}\right)^{10/3}$$

Compute the ratio:

$$\dfrac{91\,000}{18\,000} = 5.0556$$

Compute (5.0556)^(10/3) = (5.0556)^(3.333):

$$(5.0556)^{3} = 129.2, \quad (5.0556)^{1/3} = 1.715 \;\Rightarrow\; (5.0556)^{10/3} = 129.2 \times 1.715 = 221.6 \;\text{million rev}$$

Apply a₂₃:

$$L_{nm} = 0.4 \times 221.6 = 88.64 \;\text{million rev}$$

Convert to hours:

$$L_{nmh} = \dfrac{88.64 \times 10^{6}}{60 \times 1\,600} = \dfrac{88.64 \times 10^{6}}{96\,000} = 923.3 \;\text{hours}$$

**Final answer: L_nmh ≈ 923 hours.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Module 4 Set B]`

#### Part (a) (7 Marks)
A 32208 tapered-roller bearing has **C = 75 000 N** and **C₀ = 80 000 N**. The bearing is subjected to a radial load Fᵣ = 7 000 N and an axial load Fₐ = 2 000 N at 1 200 rpm. From the catalogue, for Fₐ/C₀ = 2 000/80 000 = 0.025, the factors are **X = 0.4** and **Y = 1.6**. Compute the **dynamic equivalent load P** and the **rating life in hours**.

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute dynamic equivalent load:**

$$P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a = 0.4 \times 7\,000 + 1.6 \times 2\,000 = 2\,800 + 3\,200 = 6\,000 \;\text{N}$$

**Step 2 — Tapered-roller ⇒ p = 10/3:**

$$L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{75\,000}{6\,000}\right)^{10/3} = (12.5)^{10/3}$$

Compute (12.5)³ = 1 953.125; compute (12.5)^(1/3) = 2.321. So:

$$(12.5)^{10/3} = 1\,953.125 \times 2.321 = 4\,533.4 \;\text{million rev}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to hours:**

$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{4\,533.4 \times 10^{6}}{60 \times 1\,200} = \dfrac{4\,533.4 \times 10^{6}}{72\,000} = 62\,964 \;\text{hours}$$

**Final Answer: L₁₀ₕ ≈ 62 964 hours** — excellent for any industrial application.

#### Part (b) (7 Marks)
A bearing operates on the following duty cycle:
- **40 % of time at P₁ = 5 000 N**
- **40 % of time at P₂ = 8 000 N**
- **20 % of time at P₃ = 12 000 N**
The speed is constant at 1 800 rpm and the bearing has **C = 35 000 N, p = 3** (ball). Compute the **equivalent rating life** in hours.

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute L₁₀ᵢ at each load (in million revolutions):**

$$L_{10,1} = \left(\dfrac{35\,000}{5\,000}\right)^{3} = (7)^{3} = 343 \;\text{million rev}$$

$$L_{10,2} = \left(\dfrac{35\,000}{8\,000}\right)^{3} = (4.375)^{3} = 83.74 \;\text{million rev}$$

$$L_{10,3} = \left(\dfrac{35\,000}{12\,000}\right)^{3} = (2.917)^{3} = 24.82 \;\text{million rev}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Miner's-aggregate damage rule:**

$$\dfrac{1}{L_{10,\text{var}}} = \dfrac{q_1}{L_{10,1}} + \dfrac{q_2}{L_{10,2}} + \dfrac{q_3}{L_{10,3}}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{L_{10,\text{var}}} = \dfrac{0.40}{343} + \dfrac{0.40}{83.74} + \dfrac{0.20}{24.82}$$

$$= 0.001166 + 0.004777 + 0.008061 = 0.014004 \;\text{(million rev)}^{-1}$$

$$L_{10,\text{var}} = \dfrac{1}{0.014004} = 71.41 \;\text{million rev}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to hours:**

$$L_{10h,\text{var}} = \dfrac{71.41 \times 10^{6}}{60 \times 1\,800} = \dfrac{71.41 \times 10^{6}}{108\,000} = 661.2 \;\text{hours}$$

**Final Answer: L₁₀ₕ ≈ 661 hours** — extremely low life, indicating severe overload at P₃ and the need for either a larger bearing or operational re-design.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to raise to the 10/3 power for roller bearings** — students often use p = 3 for everything, losing 2–3 marks silently.
> 2. **Mixing up Fₐ/C₀ ratio** — the X and Y factors depend on the *ratio* (and on e for some manufacturers); using the wrong table loses full marks on Part (a).
> 3. **Omitting units** — the KTU answer sheet *demands* "L₁₀ₕ = 6 200 h" not just "6 200". Deduct ½ mark per quantity without units.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert million rev → hours** — examiners explicitly check the factor 10⁶ / (60 N). A common mistake is to forget the **10⁶**, giving an answer off by a factor of one million.
> 5. **Using the wrong a₁ for non-90 % reliability** — if the problem states R = 95 % or 99 %, you MUST compute a₁ from the Weibull formula; defaulting to 1 is wrong.
> 6. **Not validating the bearing against the required life** — computing L₁₀ without comparing to L_required is treated as an incomplete solution; the comparison line is worth 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print this before the exam)**
>
> - **L₁₀** = life at which **10 %** of a population has failed (90 % reliability).
> - **L₁₀ = (C / P)ᵖ** with **p = 3** for balls, **p = 10/3** for rollers.
> - **L₁₀ₕ = (10⁶ / 60 N) · (C / P)ᵖ** is the *universal* exam formula — write it on the first page.
> - **Basic dynamic load rating C** is the load for 1 million revolutions of life; always read from the catalogue for the chosen bearing number.
> - **P = X Fᵣ + Y Fₐ** when combined loads act; **P = Fᵣ** when Fₐ = 0.
> - **Modified life (ISO 281): L_nm = a₁ · a₂₃ · L₁₀.**
> - **a₁** = 1 for 90 % reliability; use Weibull formula for higher.
> - **a₂₃** reads from a chart — function of viscosity ratio κ, contamination ηc, pitch diameter d_m, and speed n. Typical range **0.2 → 1.0**.
> - **Viscosity ratio κ = ν_operating / ν₁ ≥ 1** is recommended.
> - **Variable-load case** uses **Miner's rule**: 1 / L_var = Σ (qᵢ / Lᵢ).
> - **Required-life check: L₁₀ₕ ≥ L_required** — always write this comparison line in the conclusion.
> - **Roller bearings are more sensitive to overload** because p = 10/3 > 3 — a 50 % overload reduces roller life by 16.4 % vs. 12.3 % for balls.
> - **Static load rating C₀** is used for *static* checks (slow rotation, oscillating motion), NOT for fatigue-life calculation.
> - **Standard limiting temperature = 120 °C** for non-stabilized bearings.
> - **Units discipline:** C, P in Newtons; N in rpm; L in million rev or in hours; always write the unit.
> - **Iterative nature:** if L₁₀ₕ < L_required, *re-select* the next-larger bore or a higher C-series bearing and re-check.
> - **Mnemonic for life equation:** **"C over P, power p, into one million, divide by sixty-N"**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static and Dynamic Load Capacity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT521 - DESIGN OF TRANSMISSION ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Static and Dynamic Load Capacity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the design of rolling element bearings (anti-friction bearings), **Load Capacity** refers to the maximum load that a bearing can sustain without failure or unacceptable deformation. The KTU 2024 scheme, aligned with **ISO 15:2017** and **ISO 281:2007**, distinguishes this capacity into two fundamental categories:

**1. Basic Static Load Rating ($C_0$)**
It is the maximum static radial load (for radial bearings) or axial load (for thrust bearings) that a non-rotating bearing can sustain without permanent deformation of any rolling element or raceway exceeding **0.0001 of the bearing bore diameter** (≈ 1 µm).
