This rating governs the selection of bearings subjected to very slow speeds, oscillating motion, or static loads.

**2. Basic Dynamic Load Rating ($C$)**
It is the constant radial (or axial, for thrust bearings) load that a bearing can theoretically endure for a basic rating life of **one million revolutions** (i.e., $L_{10} = 1 \times 10^6$ revolutions) without fatigue failure of either raceway, with a 90% reliability rating. This is the cornerstone rating for rotating machinery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Bearings are selected based on two criteria — first by **Dynamic Load Capacity ($C$)** for rotating shafts, and second by **Static Load Capacity ($C_0$)** as a safety check against shock/starting loads.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Brick Stack

Imagine you are stacking bricks on a soft mattress.

- **Dynamic Load Capacity ($C$)** is like asking: *"How many bricks can I keep rotating (sliding in circles) on this mattress before the fibers tear?"* Even a moderate load, repeated millions of times, causes **fatigue failure**.
- **Static Load Capacity ($C_0$)** is like asking: *"What is the maximum single brick weight that will not leave a permanent dent in the mattress even after years of being stationary?"* Even at zero rpm, an excessive point load causes **brinelling** (permanent indentation).

A bearing must be checked against **both** — analogous to how a bridge must withstand both cyclic traffic (fatigue) and the weight of a parked truck (static deflection).

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics

- **Reliability** ($R$): Probability of survival; standard is 90% ($R = 0.90$, giving $L_{10}$ life).
- **Life Exponent** ($p$):
  - **$p = 3$** for **Ball Bearings**
  - **$p = 10/3$** for **Roller Bearings** (Tapered, Cylindrical, Spherical, Needle)
- **Basic Rating Life**: Achieved by 90% of a group of identical bearings under identical conditions.
- **Viscosity Ratio** ($\kappa$): Ratio of actual operating viscosity to required viscosity at rated temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Speed (NEP 2020 context):** The KTU 2024 design data book (Shigley's *Mechanical Engineering Design*, Khurmi/Gupta) catalogues $C$ and $C_0$ directly for standardized series — students need not derive them; they must **read** them and **apply** them.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — The Load-Life Relationship

The fundamental postulate of rolling bearing fatigue is **Lundberg-Palmgren Theory (1947)**, which states that the subsurface shear stress under a Hertzian contact governs fatigue. The integration of this stress distribution over the critically stressed volume yields the **load-life equation**:

$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p$$

Where:
- $L_{10}$ = basic rating life in millions of revolutions
- $C$ = basic dynamic load rating (from manufacturer catalogue, N or kN)
- $P$ = equivalent dynamic load (N or kN)
- $p$ = life exponent (3 for balls, 10/3 for rollers)

## 2.2 Why Two Load Ratings? — The Engineering Logic

| Scenario | Governing Failure Mode | Rating Used |
|---|---|---|
| Shaft rotating at $n > 10$ rpm with steady load | Rolling contact fatigue (spalling) | **Dynamic ($C$)** |
| Shaft stationary or oscillating under heavy load | Permanent brinelling at contact points | **Static ($C_0$)** |
| Starting/stopping cycles with shock loads | Combined static + transient fatigue | **Both checked** |
| Very low speeds ($n < 10$ rpm) with continuous load | Plastic indentation dominates | **Static ($C_0$)** governs |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$ | Equivalent Dynamic Load (radial + axial combined) | N |
| 2 | $P_0 = X_0 \cdot F_r + Y_0 \cdot F_a$ | Equivalent Static Load | N |
| 3 | $L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p$ | Life in millions of revolutions | $10^6$ rev |
| 4 | $L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6}{60 \cdot n} \cdot \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p$ | Life in operating hours | hours |
| 5 | $L_{10h} = \dfrac{16666.\bar{6}}{n} \cdot \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p$ | Simplified (using $10^6/60$) | hours |
| 6 | $f_0 = \dfrac{C_0}{P_0}$ | Static Load Factor (safety check) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $P = F_r$ (when $F_a = 0$) | Pure radial loading simplification | N |
| 8 | $C = P \cdot L_{10}^{1/p}$ | Required dynamic load rating for given life | N |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coefficients $X$, $Y$, $X_0$, $Y_0$** are **NOT** constants — they depend on the ratio $\dfrac{F_a}{C_0}$ (axial-to-static capacity) and the bearing type. These values are read from manufacturer tables. For KTU exams, these are typically **given in the problem statement** or assumed from data book.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive**: Wheel bearings, gearbox shafts, differential pinions — selected on dynamic capacity with shock factors applied to $P$.
- **Aerospace**: Turbofan engine mainshafts — operate at extreme speeds ($> 10{,}000$ rpm) where **dynamic rating is paramount**.
- **Heavy Industry**: Rolling mills, crusher bearings — operate at low speed, high radial load where **static rating dominates**.
- **Wind Turbines**: Main rotor bearings — undergo millions of slow oscillations; fatigue life ($L_{10}$) is computed in revolutions.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Revolution Life to Operating Hour Life

**Starting Point:** Bearing life in revolutions.

$$L_{10, \text{rev}} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p \quad [\text{millions of revolutions}]$$

**Step 1:** Convert to actual number of revolutions.

$$N_{\text{rev}} = 10^6 \cdot \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p \quad [\text{revolutions}]$$

**Step 2:** Relate revolutions to operating time via rotational speed $n$ [rpm].

Since 1 revolution takes $\dfrac{1}{n}$ minutes, total time in minutes is:

$$t_{\text{min}} = \frac{N_{\text{rev}}}{n} = \frac{10^6}{n} \cdot \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p$$

**Step 3:** Convert minutes to hours by dividing by 60.

$$L_{10h} = \frac{t_{\text{min}}}{60} = \frac{10^6}{60 \cdot n} \cdot \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the constant.

$$\frac{10^6}{60} = 16666.\bar{6} \approx 1.667 \times 10^4$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{L_{10h} = \frac{1.667 \times 10^4}{n} \cdot \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p \quad [\text{hours}]}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Dynamic Load Rating for a Specified Life

**Given:** Desired life $L_{10h}$ hours at speed $n$ rpm, with equivalent load $P$.

**Starting Equation:**

$$L_{10h} = \frac{10^6}{60 \cdot n} \cdot \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p$$

**Step 1:** Isolate the ratio $\left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p$.

$$\left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p = \frac{L_{10h} \cdot 60 \cdot n}{10^6}$$

**Step 2:** Take the $p$-th root on both sides.

$$\frac{C}{P} = \left(\frac{L_{10h} \cdot 60 \cdot n}{10^6}\right)^{1/p}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by $P$ to solve for $C$.

$$\boxed{C_{\text{required}} = P \cdot \left(\frac{60 \cdot n \cdot L_{10h}}{10^6}\right)^{1/p}}$$

This is the **most-asked form in KTU exams** — "Find the dynamic load capacity of the bearing required."

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Equivalent Dynamic Load for Combined Loading

**Physical Setup:** A radial bearing carries both radial force $F_r$ (perpendicular to shaft axis) and axial force $F_a$ (along shaft axis).

**Empirical Basis (SKF/Schaeffler/ISO 15):**
- A pure radial load $F_r$ produces a radial load on the bearing equal to itself.
- An axial load $F_a$ produces both axial AND radial reaction through the contact angle geometry.
- The factor $Y$ accounts for this load conversion based on contact geometry.

**Linear Superposition** (first-order approximation):

$$P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$$

**Where:**
- $X$ = radial factor (always $\leq 1$; typically 0.56 or 1.0)
- $Y$ = axial factor (typically 0.44 to 2.0 depending on bearing series)

**Step-by-step logic of coefficient selection:**
1. Calculate $\dfrac{F_a}{C_0}$ ratio (axial-to-static).
2. Look up the limiting value $e$ from tables.
3. If $\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} \leq e$: use $X = 1$, $Y = 0$ (radial dominates).
4. If $\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} > e$: use $X$ and $Y$ from the table for that bearing.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Pattern:** Typically, values of $X$, $Y$, and $e$ are **provided in the data book** or problem statement. Students must apply the table logic correctly and compute $P$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Demonstration

**Problem (KTU Standard Pattern):**
> A deep-groove ball bearing operates at 1440 rpm under radial load $F_r = 3000$ N and axial load $F_a = 1000$ N. The catalogue gives $C = 45{,}000$ N, $C_0 = 34{,}000$ N, $e = 0.28$, $X = 0.56$, $Y = 1.30$. Compute the equivalent dynamic load $P$, the basic rating life $L_{10}$ in million revolutions, and life in hours.

**Solution Walkthrough:**

**Step 1:** Compute the load ratio $\dfrac{F_a}{F_r}$.

$$\frac{F_a}{F_r} = \frac{1000}{3000} = 0.333$$

**Step 2:** Compare with $e = 0.28$.

$$\frac{F_a}{F_r} = 0.333 > e = 0.28 \implies \text{Use } X = 0.56, \, Y = 1.30$$

**Step 3:** Compute equivalent dynamic load.

$$P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$$
$$P = 0.56 \times 3000 + 1.30 \times 1000$$
$$P = 1680 + 1300$$
$$\boxed{P = 2980 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 4:** Compute life in million revolutions ($p = 3$ for ball bearing).

$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p = \left(\frac{45000}{2980}\right)^3$$

$$\frac{C}{P} = \frac{45000}{2980} = 15.1007$$

$$L_{10} = (15.1007)^3 = 3443.5 \text{ million revolutions}$$

**Step 5:** Compute life in operating hours.

$$L_{10h} = \frac{10^6 \cdot L_{10}}{60 \cdot n} = \frac{10^6 \times 3443.5}{60 \times 1440}$$

$$L_{10h} = \frac{3.4435 \times 10^9}{86400} = 39{,}857 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 6:** Static Safety Check.

$$P_0 = F_r = 3000 \text{ N (typical for ball bearings, } X_0 = 0.6, Y_0 = 0.5 \text{)}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{C_0}{P_0} = \frac{34000}{3000} = 11.33$$

Since $f_0 \gg 1$, **static load condition is satisfied with high safety margin**. ✅

---

## 3.5 Comparison Matrix — Ball vs. Roller Bearings

| Property | Ball Bearing | Roller Bearing |
|---|---|---|
| Contact type | Point contact | Line contact |
| Load capacity (per unit size) | Lower | Higher (×1.5 to 2 typical) |
| Life exponent $p$ | $3$ | $10/3 \approx 3.33$ |
| Speed capability | Higher | Lower |
| Misalignment tolerance | Low (except self-aligning) | Moderate |
| Friction torque | Lower | Higher |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**Q:** Define **Basic Dynamic Load Rating ($C$)** and **Basic Static Load Rating ($C_0$)** for rolling element bearings. State the conditions under which each is used for bearing selection.

**Model Answer:**
The **Basic Dynamic Load Rating ($C$)** is the constant radial (or axial, for thrust bearings) load that a bearing can theoretically withstand for one million revolutions of the inner race with a 90% reliability, without fatigue failure. It is governed by **ISO 281** and is used for **rotating bearings** under fluctuating or steady cyclic loads, where rolling contact fatigue (spalling) is the dominant failure mode.

The **Basic Static Load Rating ($C_0$)** is the maximum static radial load that a non-rotating bearing can sustain without causing a permanent brinelling (indentation) exceeding **0.0001 times the bore diameter** at the most heavily loaded contact point, as per **ISO 76**. It applies to **stationary, oscillating, or very slowly rotating bearings** ($n < 10$ rpm), where plastic indentation dominates over fatigue.

In bearing selection:
- For **rotating shafts**: $C$ governs selection.
- For **static/oscillatory loads**: $C_0$ governs selection.
- Both ratings must be checked in most practical applications. **[3 Marks]**

---

### Question A2
**Q:** A deep-groove ball bearing has dynamic load rating $C = 25$ kN and is subjected to equivalent dynamic load $P = 8$ kN. Compute the basic rating life of the bearing in million revolutions.

**Model Answer:**

Given:
- $C = 25$ kN $= 25{,}000$ N
- $P = 8$ kN $= 8{,}000$ N
- For ball bearing: $p = 3$

Applying the load-life equation:

$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p = \left(\frac{25000}{8000}\right)^3$$

$$\frac{C}{P} = 3.125$$

$$L_{10} = (3.125)^3 = 30.52 \text{ million revolutions}$$

**The bearing life is approximately 30.52 million revolutions.** **[3 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Style]**
**(a)** A 6308-series deep-groove ball bearing is to operate on a shaft at 1450 rpm under a radial load of 4500 N and an axial load of 1500 N. From the bearing catalogue: $C = 41{,}500$ N, $C_0 = 24{,}500$ N, $e = 0.27$, $X = 0.56$, $Y = 1.45$, $X_0 = 0.6$, $Y_0 = 0.5$. Compute:
1. The equivalent dynamic load $P$.
2. The basic rating life $L_{10}$ in million revolutions and operating hours. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a sketch the difference between static and dynamic load capacities. Discuss why both ratings are necessary when selecting a rolling contact bearing for a shaft application that experiences intermittent heavy shocks during starting. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Given Data:**
- $F_r = 4500$ N, $F_a = 1500$ N, $n = 1450$ rpm
- $C = 41{,}500$ N, $C_0 = 24{,}500$ N
- $e = 0.27$, $X = 0.56$, $Y = 1.45$

**Step 1:** Compute load ratio.

$$\frac{F_a}{F_r} = \frac{1500}{4500} = 0.333$$

**Step 2:** Compare with $e$.

$$\frac{F_a}{F_r} = 0.333 > e = 0.27 \implies \text{Use } X = 0.56, \, Y = 1.45$$

**Step 3:** Compute equivalent dynamic load.

$$P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$$
$$P = 0.56 \times 4500 + 1.45 \times 1500$$
$$P = 2520 + 2175$$
$$\boxed{P = 4695 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 4:** Compute basic rating life in million revolutions.

$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^3 = \left(\frac{41500}{4695}\right)^3$$

$$\frac{C}{P} = 8.839$$

$$L_{10} = (8.839)^3 = 690.6 \text{ million revolutions}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to operating hours.

$$L_{10h} = \frac{10^6 \cdot L_{10}}{60 \cdot n} = \frac{10^6 \times 690.6}{60 \times 1450}$$

$$L_{10h} = \frac{6.906 \times 10^8}{87000} = 7938 \text{ hours}$$

$$\boxed{L_{10} = 690.6 \text{ million rev} \quad \text{or} \quad L_{10h} \approx 7938 \text{ hours}}$$ **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Static vs. Dynamic Load Capacity — Explanation:**

**Static Load Capacity ($C_0$):**
It is defined as the maximum static load that a bearing can withstand without any permanent deformation (brinelling) of the raceway exceeding 0.0001 times the bore diameter. It applies to bearings that are:
- Stationary (shaft not rotating).
- Oscillating with very small angular movement.
- Rotating at very low speed ($n < 10$ rpm).

**Dynamic Load Capacity ($C$):**
It is the constant radial load that the bearing can theoretically endure for **one million revolutions** with a 90% reliability rating, without fatigue failure (spalling). It applies to bearings with rotating inner/outer race under cyclic loading.

**Sketch (Descriptive Block Representation):**

```
        ╔══════════════════╗
        ║  ROTATING SHAFT  ║
        ╚════════╤═════════╝
                 │ F_r (radial)
                 ▼
        ┌────────────────┐
        │  ▓ BALL BEARING ▓ │ <── Inner Race (rotates with shaft)
        └────┬────────┬───┘
             │  C     │ ← Dynamic Load Capacity (cyclic fatigue)
             │  ↑     │
        ┌────┴────────┴───┐
        │  ▒ HOUSING    ▒ │ <── Outer Race (stationary)
        └────────────────┘
             │  C₀
             │  ↑       ← Static Load Capacity (brinelling limit)
        ─────┴───────────
        GROUND/FIXTURE
```

**Why Both Ratings Are Necessary for Intermittent Shock Loads:**

In applications such as rolling mills, crushers, or heavy machinery, the shaft is subjected to:
1. **Steady cyclic load during operation** — causes fatigue failure governed by **dynamic rating $C$**.
2. **Heavy transient shocks during starting/stopping** — cause instantaneous brinelling governed by **static rating $C_0$**.

If only dynamic rating is checked, the bearing may suffer permanent brinelling at the first shock event, even if it could theoretically survive millions of cycles. Conversely, if only static rating is checked, the bearing may survive the initial shock but fail by fatigue within hours. Therefore, **both ratings must be verified**:

$$\text{Dynamic check: } L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p \geq L_{10,\text{required}}$$

$$\text{Static check: } f_0 = \frac{C_0}{P_0} \geq f_{s,\text{minimum}}$$

Typical minimum static safety factor $f_s$ ranges from **1.0 to 2.0** depending on application severity. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**
**(a)** A tapered roller bearing is required to transmit a radial load of 8000 N and an axial load of 2500 N at a shaft speed of 720 rpm. The bearing catalogue gives: $C = 65{,}000$ N, $C_0 = 48{,}000$ N, $e = 0.35$, $X = 0.4$, $Y = 1.10$, $p = 10/3$. Determine:
1. The equivalent dynamic load.
2. The expected bearing life in hours. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the load-life equation for rolling contact bearings starting from the Lundberg-Palmgren fatigue criterion. Explain how the life exponent $p$ differs for ball bearings and roller bearings and justify the same with contact mechanics reasoning. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Given:**
- $F_r = 8000$ N, $F_a = 2500$ N, $n = 720$ rpm
- $C = 65{,}000$ N, $C_0 = 48{,}000$ N
- $e = 0.35$, $X = 0.4$, $Y = 1.10$, $p = 10/3$

**Step 1:** Compute load ratio.

$$\frac{F_a}{F_r} = \frac{2500}{8000} = 0.3125$$

**Step 2:** Compare with $e$.

$$0.3125 < e = 0.35 \implies \text{Use } X = 1, \, Y = 0 \text{ (simplified case)}$$

> **Note:** Since the ratio is below the limit $e$, the axial component's contribution is negligible compared to radial; $X = 1, Y = 0$.

**Step 3:** Equivalent dynamic load.

$$P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a = 1 \times 8000 + 0 \times 2500$$
$$\boxed{P = 8000 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 4:** Life in million revolutions.

$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{p} = \left(\frac{65000}{8000}\right)^{10/3}$$

$$\frac{C}{P} = 8.125$$

$$\log(8.125) = 0.9101$$

$$\frac{10}{3} \times 0.9101 = 3.034$$

$$L_{10} = 10^{3.034} = 1080 \text{ million revolutions}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to hours.

$$L_{10h} = \frac{10^6 \times 1080}{60 \times 720} = \frac{1.08 \times 10^9}{43200}$$

$$\boxed{L_{10h} \approx 25{,}000 \text{ hours}}$$

This bearing has a very long life — indicating it may be **oversized** for the application. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Derivation of Load-Life Equation from Lundberg-Palmgren Criterion:**

**Starting Hypothesis:** Fatigue initiates at a sub-surface point where the **orthogonal shear stress** $\tau_0$ is maximum, located at depth $z_0$ below the rolling/sliding contact surface.

**Step 1:** The probability of survival $S$ over $N$ stress cycles is given by the Weibull cumulative distribution:

$$S = \exp\left[-\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right)^e \cdot \left(\frac{V}{V_0}\right)\right]$$

Where $e$ is the Weibull slope (≈ 10/9 for ball bearings, ≈ 9/8 for roller bearings).

**Step 2:** Relating contact stress to load — From Hertzian contact theory:

- For **ball bearings (point contact):** $\tau_0 \propto F^{1/3}$
- For **roller bearings (line contact):** $\tau_0 \propto F^{1/2}$

**Step 3:** The critically stressed volume $V$ scales as:
- Ball bearing: $V \propto a^3 \propto F$ (where $a$ = contact radius)
- Roller bearing: $V \propto a^2 \cdot \ell \propto F^{3/4}$ (where $\ell$ = roller length)

**Step 4:** Substituting stress and volume into the Weibull relation and solving for $N$ at $S = 0.9$ (90% reliability), the **load-life exponent** emerges:

$$\text{Ball bearing: } p = 3$$

$$\text{Roller bearing: } p = \frac{10}{3} \approx 3.33$$

**Step 5:** The final form of the load-life equation:

$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p$$

**Justification for Different $p$ Values:**

| Aspect | Ball Bearing | Roller Bearing |
|---|---|---|
| Contact geometry | **Point contact** — Hertzian pressure falls off steeply. | **Line contact** — pressure distributed along line. |
| Critically stressed volume | Smaller, more localized | Larger, more distributed |
| Stress concentration | Higher peak stress at center | Lower peak stress, broader distribution |
| Fatigue sensitivity to load | **$p = 3$** — life $\propto 1/P^3$ | **$p = 10/3$** — life $\propto 1/P^{3.33}$ |
| Engineering consequence | Slightly more sensitive to overloads | Marginally better under overload, due to line contact spreading load |

**Physical Reasoning:** A small increase in load on a roller bearing causes a smaller fractional increase in peak shear stress than the same load increase on a ball bearing (because line contact distributes the load over a larger effective volume). Therefore, roller bearings tolerate overloads slightly better — hence the higher exponent $p = 10/3$. **[7 Marks]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 
> **Mistake 1 — Forgetting the application/shock factor:** Students often use $P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$ directly without considering whether an **application factor $K_a$** (shock/vibration multiplier, typically 1.0–3.0) should be applied. For **shock-loaded machinery** like crushers or presses, $P = K_a \cdot (X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a)$ is mandatory.
> 
> **Mistake 2 — Wrong exponent:** Writing $p = 10/3$ for **ball bearings** (should be $3$) or vice-versa. **Always check bearing type first**.
> 
> **Mistake 3 — Unit inconsistency:** Mixing kN and N in the same calculation. The KTU board strictly penalizes this. **Convert all loads to Newtons before substituting**.
> 
> **Mistake 4 — Skipping static check:** Students compute $L_{10}$ and stop. But KTU's expected answer always includes verification against $C_0$ via $f_0 = C_0/P_0$.
> 
> **Mistake 5 — Wrong comparison with $e$:** When $\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} \leq e$, students wrongly apply $X, Y$ values instead of $X = 1, Y = 0$. This double-counts the axial load and inflates $P$ erroneously.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 KTU Rapid Revision Checklist — Static & Dynamic Load Capacity**

- **$C$ = Basic Dynamic Load Rating** — used for **rotating shafts**, governs **fatigue life**, units in **N or kN**.
- **$C_0$ = Basic Static Load Rating** — used for **stationary/oscillating/very low-speed shafts**, governs **brinelling**.
- **Equivalent Dynamic Load:** $P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$ — always check whether $\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} \leq e$ or $> e$.
- **Equivalent Static Load:** $P_0 = X_0 \cdot F_r + Y_0 \cdot F_a$ — used only for static safety check.
- **Life Equation (Revolutions):** $L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p$ — life in **millions of revolutions**.
- **Life Equation (Hours):** $L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6}{60 \cdot n} \cdot \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p = \dfrac{1.667 \times 10^4}{n} \cdot \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p$.
- **Required Dynamic Capacity:** $C_{\text{req}} = P \cdot \left(\dfrac{60 \cdot n \cdot L_{10h}}{10^6}\right)^{1/p}$.
- **Life Exponent $p$:**
  - **$p = 3$** → Ball bearings
  - **$p = 10/3$** → Roller bearings (any type)
- **Static Safety Factor:** $f_0 = \dfrac{C_0}{P_0}$; must exceed $f_s$ from standard tables (typically 1.0 to 2.0+).
- **90% reliability** is the KTU/KTU default; no adjustment for other reliability levels unless specified.
- **Application factor $K_a$** is **not** in the load equation by default — only applied for shock/vibration per problem statement.
- **Standard sources** (KTU 2024): Shigley's *Mechanical Engineering Design*, V.B. Bhandari, Khurmi & Gupta — use these for design data table values.

> **🎯 One-Line Mnemonic:** *"Dynamic = Cycle Fatigue, Static = Indentation. Same Bearing, Two Ratings, Two Checks — Both Mandatory."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stribeck's Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT521 - DESIGN OF TRANSMISSION ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stribeck's Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Stribeck's Equation** is an empirically derived empirical relationship that expresses the **coefficient of friction (μ or f)** in a fluid-film lubricated **journal (sliding) bearing** as a function of the **bearing characteristic number**, also known as the **Sommerfeld Number** or the dimensionless group **$\frac{Z \cdot N}{P}$**.

Mathematically, the canonical form of Stribeck's Equation is:

$$\mu = k \left(\frac{Z \cdot N}{P}\right)^{n}$$

where:
- $\mu$ = Coefficient of friction (dimensionless)
- $Z$ = **Absolute viscosity** of the lubricant at operating temperature (in **reyns** or **Pa·s**)
- $N$ = **Journal rotational speed** in **rpm**
- $P$ = **Bearing pressure** = Load / Projected area ($W / (L \cdot D)$) in **kgf/cm²** or **N/mm²**
- $k$ = Empirical constant (depends on bearing geometry)
- $n$ = Empirical exponent (typically $0.5$ for cylindrical bearings)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Stribeck's equation is classified under **Module 4 – Sliding Contact Bearings / Hydrodynamic Lubrication**. It is the fundamental tool used by examiners to evaluate the *power loss* and *frictional heat generated* in a hydrodynamic journal bearing.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine pushing a heavy wooden log across sand.
> - When the log is **stationary** (low speed, high load) → you must drag it; the sand grains rub against the wood directly. **Friction is HIGH.**
> - When you **roll the log** (medium speed) → the log partially floats on a thin sand layer. **Friction DROPS.**
> - When you **roll it very fast** → sand gets pushed aside, and a hydrodynamic "wedge" of sand forms under the log. **Friction is now dominated only by the sand's internal viscosity.**
>
> Stribeck's curve captures exactly this phenomenon: at low speeds, asperities (roughness peaks) touch; at high speeds, the lubricant film fully separates the surfaces. The transition is governed by the ratio $\frac{ZN}{P}$ — the **lubrication quality index**.

---

### 1.3 Physical Significance

- The term **$\frac{Z \cdot N}{P}$** is the **dimensionless bearing characteristic number** that quantifies the **film-forming capability** of the lubricant.
- Higher viscosity, higher speed, and lower load → better film formation → lower friction.
- Stribeck's equation describes only the **fluid-film (hydrodynamic) region** of operation, NOT the boundary or mixed-lubrication regions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** *"ZN over P — Stribeck made me free!"*
> (Z × N in numerator → more film; P in denominator → less load → better lubrication → lower friction coefficient μ.)

---

### 1.4 The Three Regimes of Lubrication (Foundational Context)

The Stribeck curve is essentially a **plot of μ vs. $\frac{ZN}{P}$** (or vs. Hersey number $\frac{ZN}{P}$) showing **three distinct lubrication regimes**:

| Regime | Lubrication Type | Surface Contact | Friction Level |
|--------|------------------|-----------------|----------------|
| **I – Boundary** | Thin adsorbed film only | Full asperity contact | Very High (μ = 0.05 – 0.20) |
| **II – Mixed** | Partial fluid film + asperities | Partial contact | Decreasing (μ = 0.02 – 0.08) |
| **III – Hydrodynamic** | Full fluid film (η > 3) | No contact | Low (μ = 0.002 – 0.01) |

Stribeck's equation applies strictly in **Regime III** — the **Hydrodynamic Region**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Breakdown of the Stribeck Relationship

Stribeck (1902) conducted pioneering experiments on journal bearings and concluded:

1. **Friction in a hydrodynamic bearing is NOT a constant** — it depends on operating conditions.
2. **Friction is proportional to viscosity and speed** (linearly with Z, linearly with N) and **inversely proportional to load (P)**.
3. By Buckingham's dimensional analysis (Π-theorem), the relationship must be of the form:

$$f = f\left(\frac{ZN}{P}\right)$$

4. Empirical curve-fitting (Stribeck's tests on actual bearings) yielded the **power-law form**:

$$f = k \left(\frac{ZN}{P}\right)^{n}$$

5. For **cylindrical (plain) journal bearings**, the exponent $n \approx 0.5$ and $k \approx 0.002$ when Z is in **reyns**, N in **rpm**, and P in **kgf/cm²**.
6. This equation is **dimensionally inconsistent** (purely empirical) — the constants $k$ and $n$ absorb the dimensional mismatch.

### 2.2 Standard Engineering Form (KTU Convention)

For hydrodynamic journal bearings under KTU examination, the most common form encountered is:

$$f = \frac{9.8 \times \left(\frac{r}{c}\right) \times Z \times N}{P \times 10^{6}}$$

where Z is in **kgf·s/m²** (absolute viscosity), and:

$$\frac{r}{c} = \text{Radius-to-clearance ratio}$$

This is the **Petrov's equation** form, which is essentially a special case / companion to Stribeck's equation.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $\mu = k \left( \dfrac{ZN}{P} \right)^{n}$ | **Stribeck's Equation** (General form) | μ dimensionless; k, n empirical |
| 2 | $\mu = 0.002 \left( \dfrac{ZN}{P} \right)^{0.5}$ | Stribeck for plain journal bearings | Z in reyns; N in rpm; P in kgf/cm² |
| 3 | $S_o = \dfrac{ZN}{P} \cdot \dfrac{r}{c}$ | **Sommerfeld Number** | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $f = \dfrac{9.8 \cdot (r/c) \cdot Z \cdot N}{P \cdot 10^{6}}$ | Petrov's equation (Petroff's) | Z in kgf·s/m²; P in kgf/cm² |
| 5 | $T_f = f \cdot W \cdot r$ | **Frictional torque** | N·mm |
| 6 | $H_f = T_f \cdot \omega$ | **Power loss due to friction** | W (watts) |
| 7 | $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ | Angular velocity | rad/s |
| 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{h_{min}}{c}$ | **Coefficient of film parameter** (often $\eta \geq 3$ for HD regime) | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $Q = \dfrac{r \cdot \omega \cdot c \cdot L}{2}$ | **Side leakage flow** (approx) | mm³/s |
| 10 | $\Delta T = \dfrac{H_f}{Q \cdot \rho \cdot C_p}$ | **Temperature rise** of lubricant | °C |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Trap:** Many students lose marks in KTU exams by mixing units. The constants $k$ and $n$ in Stribeck's equation are **only valid for the specific unit system** stated in the textbook. Always declare units first, then plug in.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Stribeck's equation is the **bedrock of all hydrodynamic bearing design** and is used in:

1. **IC Engine crankshaft bearings** — to predict frictional losses (≈ 5–10% of total mechanical loss).
2. **Electric motor shaft bearings** — for thermal management and efficiency prediction.
3. **Turbomachinery** (steam/gas turbines) — to estimate bearing heat rejection for cooler sizing.
4. **Automotive transmission countershafts** — for fuel-efficiency optimization.
5. **Heavy industrial gearboxes** — where bearing friction dictates sump temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter in KTU exams?** Stribeck's equation is the **gateway** to calculating **power loss, heat generation, oil flow rate, and temperature rise** — all four are routinely asked as 7-mark sub-questions in Part B.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why is $\frac{ZN}{P}$ the Characteristic Group?

We start with the variables affecting friction coefficient $f$ in a journal bearing:

$$f = f(Z, N, P, r, c, L, d, \rho, g)$$

Using **Buckingham's Π-Theorem** with primary dimensions $[M, L, T]$:

- $f$ → dimensionless (zero $\Pi$ terms from itself)
- $Z$ → $[M \cdot L^{-1} \cdot T^{-1}]$
- $N$ → $[T^{-1}]$
- $P$ → $[M \cdot L^{-1} \cdot T^{-2}]$
- $r, c, L, d$ → $[L]$
- $\rho$ → $[M \cdot L^{-3}]$
- $g$ → $[L \cdot T^{-2}]$

Choosing repeating variables $(Z, N, P)$, we obtain:

$$\Pi_1 = \frac{ZN}{P} \quad \text{(Hersey / Bearing Characteristic Number)}$$

$$\Pi_2 = \frac{r}{c} \quad \text{(Clearance ratio)}$$

$$\Pi_3 = \frac{L}{d} \quad \text{(L/D ratio)}$$

Therefore the functional relationship reduces to:

$$f = \phi\left(\frac{ZN}{P}, \frac{r}{c}, \frac{L}{d}\right)$$

When $\frac{r}{c}$ and $\frac{L}{d}$ are fixed (typical bearing designs), the relationship simplifies to:

$$f = f\left(\frac{ZN}{P}\right)$$

Experimentally, this functional dependence is well-approximated by a **power law**, giving Stribeck's Equation:

$$f = k \left( \frac{ZN}{P} \right)^{n}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: From Stribeck's Equation to Power Loss

This is the **KTU-standard derivation** often asked in Part B (14-mark problems).

**Step 1 – Frictional Torque:**

The friction force on the bearing surface is:

$$F_f = f \cdot W$$

where $W$ is the total radial load on the bearing.

The frictional torque (force × radius of journal):

$$T_f = F_f \cdot r = f \cdot W \cdot r$$

**Step 2 – Substitute Stribeck's f:**

$$T_f = k \left(\frac{ZN}{P}\right)^{n} \cdot W \cdot r$$

**Step 3 – Express Power Loss:**

Angular velocity of the journal:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} \quad \text{[rad/s]}$$

Power lost as heat due to friction:

$$H_f = T_f \cdot \omega = k \left(\frac{ZN}{P}\right)^{n} \cdot W \cdot r \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

**Step 4 – Substitute $P = \frac{W}{L \cdot D}$:**

$$H_f = k \left( \frac{ZN \cdot L \cdot D}{W} \right)^{n} \cdot W \cdot r \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

**Step 5 – Final Compact Form:**

$$\boxed{H_f = k \cdot \frac{2\pi}{60} \cdot r \cdot W^{1-n} \cdot (Z \cdot N \cdot L \cdot D)^{n} \cdot N}$$

This is the **engineer's working equation** for computing friction heat dissipation.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Numerical Problem Solving Workflow

> [!TIP]
> **Use this exact 6-step matrix for any KTU numerical involving Stribeck's equation.**

| Step | Action | Checkpoint |
|------|--------|------------|
| **1** | Extract $W$, $N$, $r$, $c$, $L$, $D$ from problem statement | Convert all units to a consistent system (SI or CGS) |
| **2** | Compute bearing pressure $P = \dfrac{W}{L \cdot D}$ | $P$ in same units as stated in your $k$ constant |
| **3** | Look up viscosity $Z$ of lubricant at operating temperature | If temperature not given, use ambient $Z \approx 30$ cP |
| **4** | Compute Hersey number $\dfrac{ZN}{P}$ | Verify it lies in HD regime ($\geq 1$ typically) |
| **5** | Apply Stribeck's equation: $f = k \left(\dfrac{ZN}{P}\right)^{n}$ | Use $k = 0.002$, $n = 0.5$ for standard journal bearings |
| **6** | Compute $T_f = f \cdot W \cdot r$ and $H_f = T_f \cdot \omega$ | Final answer in N·mm and Watts respectively |

---

### 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A hydrodynamic journal bearing has the following specifications:
> - Radial load, $W = 25{,}000$ N
> - Journal speed, $N = 1500$ rpm
> - Journal diameter, $D = 80$ mm
> - Bearing length, $L = 80$ mm (square bearing)
> - Radial clearance, $c = 0.08$ mm
> - Lubricant absolute viscosity, $Z = 0.05$ Pa·s
>
> Using Stribeck's equation ($k = 0.002$, $n = 0.5$), calculate:
> **(a)** Coefficient of friction
> **(b)** Frictional torque
> **(c)** Power loss due to friction

### Solution:

**Given Data Conversion:**

$$W = 25{,}000 \text{ N} = 2548.58 \text{ kgf} \quad (\because 1 \text{ kgf} = 9.81 \text{ N})$$

$$P = \frac{W}{L \cdot D} = \frac{2548.58}{8 \times 8} = 39.82 \text{ kgf/cm}^2$$

$$Z = 0.05 \text{ Pa·s} = 0.05 \times \frac{10^6}{98.1} = 509.68 \text{ reyns}$$

**Part (a) — Coefficient of Friction:**

Compute the Hersey number:

$$\frac{ZN}{P} = \frac{509.68 \times 1500}{39.82} = 19198.7$$

Apply Stribeck's equation:

$$f = 0.002 \times (19198.7)^{0.5}$$

$$f = 0.002 \times 138.56$$

$$\boxed{f = 0.2772 \quad \text{(This value is unrealistically high!})}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Interpretation Issue:** If we get $\mu > 0.05$, the operating point is NOT in the hydrodynamic regime. Stribeck's equation with $k = 0.002$, $n = 0.5$ is calibrated for $ZN/P$ in a specific moderate range. For very high Hersey numbers, the curve flattens to μ ≈ constant. **In KTU exams**, students should compute and report the value as-is, but **comment** on whether the regime is hydrodynamic.

**Part (b) — Frictional Torque:**

Journal radius:

$$r = \frac{D}{2} = \frac{80}{2} = 40 \text{ mm} = 0.04 \text{ m}$$

$$T_f = f \cdot W \cdot r = 0.2772 \times 25000 \times 0.04$$

$$\boxed{T_f = 277.2 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Part (c) — Power Loss:**

Angular velocity:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$H_f = T_f \cdot \omega = 277.2 \times 157.08$$

$$\boxed{H_f = 43{,}543 \text{ W} \approx 43.54 \text{ kW}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** This power loss is enormous because we used a non-physical $\mu$. A realistic hydrodynamic μ = 0.003 – 0.008 would give $H_f \approx 0.5 – 1.2$ kW. The point is — **report the computation AND the regime comment**.

---

### 3.5 Alternative Unit System (SI-Conventional Approach)

If using $Z$ in **Pa·s** and $P$ in **Pa (N/m²)**, the working form is:

$$f = \frac{2\pi^2 \cdot \left(\frac{r}{c}\right) \cdot Z \cdot N}{P \cdot 60}$$

For our problem:

$$P = \frac{25000}{0.08 \times 0.08} = 3.906 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\frac{r}{c} = \frac{40}{0.08} = 500$$

$$f = \frac{2\pi^2 \times 500 \times 0.05 \times 1500}{3.906 \times 10^6 \times 60}$$

$$f = \frac{740{,}220}{2.343 \times 10^8} = 3.158 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{f \approx 0.00316 \quad \text{(Realistic hydrodynamic value!})}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Lesson for Students:** When the KTU paper gives SI units, use the SI form of Stribeck's equation. The answer then falls within realistic hydrodynamic limits (0.002 – 0.008).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Stribeck Curve (Conceptual Flow Diagram)

The following Mermaid block represents the **Stribeck friction-vs-bearing-characteristic-number curve** schematically as a Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow, mapping the three lubrication regimes and the Stribeck equation's role in each.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operating Point: Journal Bearing] --> B{ZN / P Computation}
    B --> C{Regime Identification}
    C -->|Low ZN by P| D[Boundary Lubrication]
    C -->|Medium ZN by P| E[Mixed Lubrication]
    C -->|High ZN by P| F[Hydrodynamic Lubrication]
    D --> D1[Asperity Contact Dominant]
    D --> D2[Mu Range: 0.05 to 0.20]
    D --> D3[Stribeck Not Applicable]
    E --> E1[Partial Fluid Film]
    E --> E2[Mu Range: 0.02 to 0.08]
    E --> E3[Stribeck Not Applicable]
    F --> F1[Full Fluid Film Separation]
    F --> F2[Mu Range: 0.002 to 0.01]
    F --> F3[Stribeck Equation Valid]
    F1 --> G1[Apply f = k times ZN over P to the power n]
    F3 --> G2[Compute Friction Torque Tf]
    G1 --> G2
    G2 --> G3[Compute Power Loss Hf]
    G3 --> G4[Compute Temperature Rise delta T]
    G4 --> H[Bearing Performance Report]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture – Stribeck Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input1[W in N] --> Pcalc
    Input2[L in mm] --> Pcalc
    Input3[D in mm] --> Pcalc
    Pcalc[P equals W by L times D] --> Hers
    Input4[Z in Pa s] --> Hers
    Input5[N in rpm] --> Hers
    Hers[ZN over P] --> Str
    Input6[k constant] --> Str
    Input7[n exponent] --> Str
    Str[f equals k times ZN over P power n] --> Torque
    Input8[r in mm] --> Torque
    Torque[Tf equals f times W times r] --> Power
    Power[Hf equals Tf times omega] --> Output[Final Result in W]
```

### 4.3 Lubrication Regime Decision Tree (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Bearing Starts Operation] --> Q1{Is there asperity contact?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, heavy| R1[Boundary Regime]
    Q1 -->|Partial| R2[Mixed Regime]
    Q1 -->|None| R3[Fluid Film Regime]
    R1 --> X1[Use Boundary Lubrication Theory - NOT Stribeck]
    R2 --> X2[Use Elastohydrodynamic Theory - NOT Stribeck]
    R3 --> X3[Stribeck Equation Applies Here]
    X3 --> Y1[Compute Coefficient of Friction mu]
    Y1 --> Y2[Compute Heat Generation]
    Y2 --> Y3[Verify Thermal Equilibrium]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Block Diagram?** The physical Stribeck curve is a 2D plot of μ vs. $\frac{ZN}{P}$, which is best drawn by hand in exam notebooks. The Mermaid flowchart above captures the **decision logic** of which equation applies where — exactly what KTU examiners test.

### 4.4 Conceptual Schematic – Forces in a Hydrodynamic Journal Bearing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Load[W applied Radial Load] -->|Acts at angle| Journal[Journal Rotating at N rpm]
    Journal -->|Friction Force f times W| Surface[Bearing Lining]
    Surface -->|Fluid Shear Stress| Film[Lubricant Film of thickness h]
    Film -->|Pressure Distribution p theta| Reaction[Resultant Fluid Reaction = W]
    Reaction -->|Balances| Load
    Journal -->|Omega rad per s| Torque[Tf equals f W r]
    Torque --> Heat[Hf equals Tf Omega in W]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define Stribeck's equation. State the significance of the bearing characteristic number $\frac{ZN}{P}$.**

**Model Answer:**

> Stribeck's equation is an empirical relationship that expresses the **coefficient of friction (μ)** of a hydrodynamic journal bearing as a function of the **bearing characteristic number** $\frac{ZN}{P}$:
>
> $$\mu = k \left( \frac{ZN}{P} \right)^{n}$$
>
> where $k$ and $n$ are empirical constants (typically $k = 0.002$ and $n = 0.5$ for cylindrical journal bearings when $Z$ is in reyns, $N$ in rpm, and $P$ in kgf/cm²).
>
> **Significance of $\frac{ZN}{P}$:**
> - It is a **dimensionless group** derived via Buckingham's Π-theorem.
> - It quantifies the **film-forming ability** of the lubricant:
>   - **Higher $\frac{ZN}{P}$** → thicker fluid film → better separation → **lower friction**.
>   - **Lower $\frac{ZN}{P}$** → thinner film → asperity contact → **higher friction**.
> - It serves as the **abscissa** of the Stribeck curve, defining the boundary between boundary, mixed, and hydrodynamic lubrication regimes.
> - It is the **primary design index** used by bearing designers to select lubricant viscosity for a given load and speed.

**\[3 Marks\]**

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Differentiate between Boundary, Mixed, and Hydrodynamic lubrication regimes with respect to the Stribeck curve.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Boundary | Mixed | Hydrodynamic |
|-----------|----------|-------|--------------|
| **Lubricant film** | Thin adsorbed film only | Partial fluid film | Complete fluid film |
| **Surface separation** | None (asperity contact) | Partial | Full (η > 3) |
| **Coefficient of friction** | Very high (0.05 – 0.20) | Moderate (0.02 – 0.08) | Low (0.002 – 0.01) |
| **Hersey number $\frac{ZN}{P}$** | Very low | Medium | High |
| **Stribeck eqn applicable?** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **Friction source** | Surface asperity ploughing | Combined asperity + viscous | Pure viscous shear |

**\[3 Marks\]**

---

### 5.2 PART B – Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Pattern:** KTU allows the student to choose **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### ✅ **Question A – 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**A hydrodynamic journal bearing has the following specifications:**

- Radial load, $W = 30{,}000$ N
- Journal diameter, $D = 100$ mm
- Bearing length, $L = 100$ mm
- Journal speed, $N = 1440$ rpm
- Radial clearance, $c = 0.1$ mm
- Lubricant absolute viscosity, $Z = 0.025$ Pa·s
- Density of oil, $\rho = 870$ kg/m³
- Specific heat of oil, $C_p = 1760$ J/kg·K

**Using Stribeck's equation with $k = 0.002$ and $n = 0.5$ (using SI units), calculate:**

**(a)** The coefficient of friction.

**(b)** The frictional torque and power loss.

**(c)** The temperature rise of the lubricant if the oil flow rate through the bearing is $Q = 4$ L/min.

---

### **Model Solution – Question A**

#### **Part (a) – Coefficient of Friction**  `[7 Marks]`

**Step 1: Compute Bearing Pressure $P$**

$$P = \frac{W}{L \cdot D} = \frac{30000}{0.1 \times 0.1} = 3.0 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2: Compute the Hersey Number $\frac{ZN}{P}$**

$$\frac{ZN}{P} = \frac{0.025 \times (1440/60)}{3.0 \times 10^6}$$

$$\frac{ZN}{P} = \frac{0.025 \times 24}{3.0 \times 10^6} = \frac{0.6}{3.0 \times 10^6} = 2.0 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s}$$

**Step 3: Apply Stribeck's Equation (SI form)**

For SI units, we use the modified Stribeck relation:

$$f = k \cdot \left( \frac{ZN}{P} \right)^{n} \cdot \left( \frac{2\pi}{60} \right)$$

$$f = 0.002 \times (2.0 \times 10^{-7})^{0.5} \times \left( \frac{2\pi}{60} \right)$$

$$f = 0.002 \times 4.472 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.1047$$

$$\boxed{f = 9.36 \times 10^{-8} \quad \text{(unrealistically low)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Regime Check:** This Hersey number is extremely low, placing us in the **boundary lubrication regime** — Stribeck's equation is **NOT strictly valid** here. However, we report the computation as required.

**Alternative SI-Consistent Form (Petrov-based):**

$$f = \frac{2\pi^2 \cdot (r/c) \cdot Z \cdot N}{P \cdot 60}$$

With $\frac{r}{c} = \frac{50}{0.1} = 500$:

$$f = \frac{2\pi^2 \times 500 \times 0.025 \times 24}{3.0 \times 10^6 \times 60}$$

$$f = \frac{5921.76}{1.8 \times 10^8} = 3.29 \times 10^{-5}$$

This remains a very low value — confirming **boundary regime operation**.

---

#### **Part (b) – Frictional Torque and Power Loss**  `[4 Marks]`

Journal radius: $r = 0.05$ m

Using the Petrov-based friction coefficient $f = 3.29 \times 10^{-5}$:

$$T_f = f \cdot W \cdot r = 3.29 \times 10^{-5} \times 30000 \times 0.05$$

$$T_f = 0.0493 \text{ N·m} = 49.3 \text{ N·mm}$$

Angular velocity:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1440}{60} = 150.8 \text{ rad/s}$$

Power loss:

$$H_f = T_f \cdot \omega = 0.0493 \times 150.8$$

$$\boxed{H_f = 7.43 \text{ W}}$$

---

#### **Part (c) – Temperature Rise**  `[3 Marks]**

Convert oil flow rate:

$$Q = 4 \text{ L/min} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-3}}{60} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 6.667 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = 870 \times 6.667 \times 10^{-5} = 0.058 \text{ kg/s}$$

Temperature rise:

$$\Delta T = \frac{H_f}{\dot{m} \cdot C_p} = \frac{7.43}{0.058 \times 1760}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T = 0.073 \text{ °C}}$$

---

#### ✅ **Question B – 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Derive Stribeck's equation from Buckingham's Π-theorem and explain its physical significance in the design of hydrodynamic journal bearings. State typical values of the empirical constants $k$ and $n$. `[7 Marks]`

**(b)** A hydrodynamic journal bearing operates under the following conditions:

- Load $W = 20{,}000$ N
- Speed $N = 1200$ rpm
- Diameter $D = 90$ mm
- Length $L = 90$ mm
- Radial clearance $c = 0.075$ mm
- Viscosity $Z = 0.04$ Pa·s

Using Stribeck's equation with $k = 0.002$ and $n = 0.5$, calculate:

(i) Coefficient of friction.

(ii) Frictional power loss.

(iii) Comment on the operating regime. `[7 Marks]`

---

### **Model Solution – Question B**

#### **Part (a) – Derivation & Significance**  `[7 Marks]`

**Step 1: List all variables affecting friction in a journal bearing:**

$$f = f(Z, N, P, r, c, L, d, \rho, g)$$

Number of variables $n = 9$, Number of fundamental dimensions $m = 3$ (M, L, T).

By Buckingham's Π-Theorem: Number of dimensionless groups = $n - m = 9 - 3 = 6$.

**Step 2: Choose repeating variables** $(Z, N, P)$ — these together contain all three dimensions.

**Step 3: Form the Π-groups:**

$$\Pi_1 = Z^{a_1} N^{b_1} P^{c_1} \cdot f \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Pi_1 = f \text{ (already dimensionless)}$$

$$\Pi_2 = \frac{ZN}{P} \quad \text{(Bearing characteristic / Hersey number)}$$

$$\Pi_3 = \frac{r}{c} \quad \text{(Clearance ratio)}$$

$$\Pi_4 = \frac{L}{d} \quad \text{(L/D ratio)}$$

$$\Pi_5 = \frac{\rho N d^2}{Z} \quad \text{(Reynolds number analogue)}$$

$$\Pi_6 = \frac{gZ}{PN} \quad \text{(Gravity-viscosity group)}$$

**Step 4: Functional relationship:**

$$f = \phi\left(\frac{ZN}{P}, \frac{r}{c}, \frac{L}{d}, \frac{\rho N d^2}{Z}, \frac{gZ}{PN}\right)$$

For typical hydrodynamic journal bearings, geometric ratios $\frac{r}{c}$ and $\frac{L}{d}$ are constants; flow is laminar; gravity effect is negligible. Therefore:

$$f = f\left(\frac{ZN}{P}\right)$$

**Step 5: Empirical power-law fit (Stribeck's experiments):**

$$\boxed{f = k \left(\frac{ZN}{P}\right)^{n}}$$

**Typical values:** $k = 0.002$, $n = 0.5$ for cylindrical journal bearings (Z in reyns, N in rpm, P in kgf/cm²).

**Physical Significance:**

1. The Hersey number $\frac{ZN}{P}$ represents the **ratio of viscous forces to load-carrying forces**.
2. Higher viscosity or speed → more lubricant entrained → better film formation → **lower friction**.
3. Higher load → thinner film → **higher friction**.
4. This equation is the **design basis** for selecting lubricants, bearing dimensions, and operating speeds.

---

#### **Part (b) – Numerical**  `[7 Marks]`

**Given:** $W = 20{,}000$ N, $N = 1200$ rpm, $D = L = 90$ mm, $c = 0.075$ mm, $Z = 0.04$ Pa·s

**Step 1: Bearing pressure (SI):**

$$P = \frac{W}{L \cdot D} = \frac{20000}{0.09 \times 0.09} = \frac{20000}{0.0081} = 2.469 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2: Hersey Number (SI with $N$ in rev/s = 20):**

$$\frac{ZN}{P} = \frac{0.04 \times 20}{2.469 \times 10^6} = \frac{0.8}{2.469 \times 10^6} = 3.24 \times 10^{-7}$$

**Step 3: Apply Stribeck (SI-modified form with $\frac{2\pi}{60}$ factor):**

$$f = k \cdot \left(\frac{ZN}{P}\right)^n \cdot \frac{2\pi}{60}$$

$$f = 0.002 \times (3.24 \times 10^{-7})^{0.5} \times 0.1047$$

$$f = 0.002 \times 5.69 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.1047$$

$$\boxed{f = 1.19 \times 10^{-7}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Regime Comment:** The Hersey number is extremely low. The bearing is operating in the **boundary lubrication regime**, NOT hydrodynamic. Stribeck's equation in its standard form does not apply. The bearing design is **inadequate** for the given load-speed combination — lubricant viscosity should be increased (e.g., to $Z \geq 0.1$ Pa·s) or the load should be reduced.

**Step 4: Frictional Power Loss (informational):**

Using $f = 1.19 \times 10^{-7}$:

$$T_f = f \cdot W \cdot r = 1.19 \times 10^{-7} \times 20000 \times 0.045 = 1.07 \times 10^{-4} \text{ N·m}$$

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 1200}{60} = 125.66 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$H_f = T_f \cdot \omega = 1.34 \times 10^{-2} \text{ W} \approx 13.4 \text{ mW}$$

This artificially low value reinforces the **boundary-regime conclusion**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Stribeck-Equation Problems:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–2 marks):** Mixing SI and CGS units in the same calculation. Example: using $Z$ in Pa·s but $P$ in kgf/cm².
> 2. **Forgetting the $\frac{2\pi}{60}$ conversion (–1 mark):** When $N$ is in rpm but ω needs rad/s.
> 3. **No regime comment (–2 marks):** Failing to check whether $\frac{ZN}{P}$ falls in the hydrodynamic range. KTU examiners actively deduct for this.
> 4. **Wrong constant selection (–1 mark):** Using $k = 0.002$, $n = 0.5$ when the problem specifies different empirical values.
> 5. **Skipping the unit-conversion step for $Z$ (–1 mark):** Forgetting to convert viscosity from cP or Pa·s to reyns if using the CGS form.
> 6. **Omitting dimensional commentary (–1 mark):** Stribeck's equation is **dimensionally inconsistent**; students should note this.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Stribeck's Equation**

- ✅ **Stribeck's Equation** is: $\boxed{f = k \left( \dfrac{ZN}{P} \right)^{n}}$ — purely empirical, dimensionally inconsistent.
- ✅ **Typical constants** for cylindrical journal bearings: $k = 0.002$, $n = 0.5$ (Z in reyns, N in rpm, P in kgf/cm²).
- ✅ **Applicable ONLY** in the **hydrodynamic regime** ($\frac{ZN}{P}$ sufficiently high).
- ✅ **Derived via Buckingham's Π-theorem**: $\frac{ZN}{P}$ is the **bearing characteristic number** (Hersey number).
- ✅ **Sommerfeld Number** $S_o = \frac{ZN}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c}$ — closely related and frequently appears in KTU problems.
- ✅ **Friction torque** $T_f = f \cdot W \cdot r$ — use this to compute frictional losses.
- ✅ **Power loss** $H_f = T_f \cdot \omega$ where $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$ rad/s.
- ✅ **Stribeck curve** has three regimes: **Boundary → Mixed → Hydrodynamic**; friction **decreases** as $\frac{ZN}{P}$ increases (until flattening).
- ✅ **Engineering use:** Engine crankshafts, motors, turbines, gearboxes — for efficiency and thermal design.
- ✅ **For SI calculations**, multiply by $\frac{2\pi}{60}$ factor or use Petrov's form: $f = \frac{2\pi^2 \cdot (r/c) \cdot Z \cdot N}{P \cdot 60}$.
- ✅ **Always state units** at every step — KTU examiners reward unit discipline.
- ✅ **Always comment on the lubrication regime** after computing $\frac{ZN}{P}$ — this shows conceptual understanding.
- ✅ **Higher viscosity, higher speed, lower load** → better film → lower μ.
- ✅ **Mnemonic:** *"ZN up → friction down"* — keep them on the same side of the see-saw.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of Bearings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT521 - DESIGN OF TRANSMISSION ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Selection of Bearings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 | Topic: Selection of Bearings

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Selection of Bearings):**
> *Bearing selection* is the systematic engineering procedure of determining the most appropriate type, size, and arrangement of a bearing (rolling-element or plain/journal) such that it can reliably transmit the applied loads, accommodate the required rotational speed, achieve the specified operational life, and survive the prevailing environmental and lubrication conditions at minimum cost and envelope.

A machine bearing is a constrained mechanical element that **permits relative motion** (rotation or translation) between two machine parts, while **transmitting the load** acting across the joint. KTU's design of transmission elements classifies these into two broad families:

1. **Rolling-Contact Bearings** (Ball bearings, Roller bearings, Needle bearings) — load transmitted through point/line contact of hardened elements separated by a lubricant film.
2. **Sliding-Contact (Plain / Journal) Bearings** — load transmitted through a continuous hydrodynamic or boundary lubricant film between a journal and a bearing shell.

### Real-World Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture — "The Cart-Wheel vs. The Sled":**
> Imagine moving a heavy crate across a workshop. If you place it directly on the floor and push (sliding contact), you need enormous force and the floor gets gouged. If you place it on a set of rollers or wheels (rolling contact), the crate glides effortlessly. The wheels must be the *right size* (capacity), the *right hardness* (fatigue life), and the *right number* (load sharing) — otherwise the wheel cracks or the axle bends. **Bearing selection is exactly this problem applied at the micro-scale of machine design:** pick the right rolling elements (or sliding surfaces) sized for the load, speed, and life the machine demands.

### Standard Metrics Used in Selection
- **Basic Dynamic Load Rating (C):** the constant radial load a bearing can endure for **1 million revolutions** with 90 % survival probability.
- **Basic Static Load Rating (C₀):** the load below which permanent deformation (≈ 0.0001 of ball diameter) does not occur at the contact zone.
- **Rating Life (L₁₀):** the number of revolutions (or hours) at which 90 % of an apparently identical group of bearings will still be operating.
- **Bore Diameter (d):** the standardized shaft seat dimension in **mm** (ISO 15, ISO 20, ISO 25, … series).
- **Operating Temperature:** must remain below the bearing's heat-stabilized limit, typically **≤ 120 °C** for standard bearings, **≤ 200 °C** for heat-stabilized variants.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 of PERAT521 explicitly couples bearing selection with the design outputs of shafts, gears, and couplings from previous modules — students must be prepared to integrate gear forces and torque into equivalent bearing loads.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Bearing Selection

A bearing is selected to satisfy **four concurrent constraints**:

| Constraint | Engineering Question Addressed |
|---|---|
| **Load Capacity** | Will C ≥ P × safety factor for the given life? |
| **Speed Capacity** | Is the DN-value (bore × rpm) within the manufacturer's limit? |
| **Life Requirement** | Does computed L₁₀ ≥ L_required (in hours)? |
| **Geometric / Assembly Fit** | Will it seat on the chosen shaft diameter and inside the housing bore? |

> [!NOTE]
> Selection is *not* a free choice. It is an **iterative constrained optimization**: start with a tentative bore, verify life, refine size, re-verify life, then confirm the static-load and speed checks.

## 2.2 The Five-Stage Selection Logic

**Stage 1 — Identify Service Conditions**
- Magnitude and direction (radial / axial / combined) of load
- Rotational speed *n* (rpm)
- Required operating life *L_h* (hours)
- Ambient temperature, contamination, vibration

**Stage 2 — Choose Bearing Type & Series**
- Pure radial load → **deep-groove ball bearing (6XX)** or **cylindrical roller (NU, N)**
- Combined radial + thrust → **angular contact (7XX)** paired, or **tapered roller (3XX)**
- Heavy radial shock → **spherical roller (2XX)** or **needle roller (NK)**
- Pure thrust → **thrust ball (5XX)** or **spherical roller thrust**

**Stage 3 — Compute Equivalent Dynamic Load P**
Combine the actual radial (F_r) and axial (F_a) loads into a single equivalent radial load using the manufacturer's *X* and *Y* factors.

**Stage 4 — Compute Required Dynamic Load Rating C_req**
From the life equation, size the bearing so that its catalog C meets or exceeds C_req.

**Stage 5 — Static & Speed Verification**
Ensure the applied equivalent static load P₀ ≤ C₀ / S₀ (with safety factor S₀ ≥ 1 for steady, ≥ 1.5 for shock conditions), and that speed limit is not exceeded.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the minimum formula set the KTU examiner expects a candidate to reproduce from memory.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Basic rating life (revolutions) | $L_{10} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{p}$ | revolutions | $p = 3$ ball, $p = 10/3$ roller |
| 2 | Basic rating life (hours) | $L_{10h} \;=\; \dfrac{10^{6}}{60\,n}\left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{p}$ | hours | $n$ in rpm |
| 3 | Required basic dynamic rating | $C_{req} \;=\; P \left(\dfrac{60\,n\,L_{10h}}{10^{6}}\right)^{1/p}$ | N or kN | Inverted from Eq. 2 |
| 4 | Equivalent dynamic load (radial bearing, F_a = 0) | $P \;=\; F_{r}$ | N | Trivial case |
| 5 | Equivalent dynamic load (combined load) | $P \;=\; X\,F_{r} + Y\,F_{a}$ | N | $X,Y$ from manufacturer tables |
| 6 | Modified rating life (ISO 281) | $L_{nm} \;=\; a_{1}\,a_{2}\,a_{3}\,L_{10}$ | h or rev | $a_{1}$ reliability, $a_{2}$ materials, $a_{3}$ operating conditions |
| 7 | Modified required rating | $C_{req,\,mod} \;=\; P\left(\dfrac{L_{nm}}{a_{1}\,a_{2}\,a_{3}}\right)^{1/p}$ | N | Used when reliability > 90 % |
| 8 | Equivalent static load | $P_{0} \;=\; X_{0}\,F_{r} + Y_{0}\,F_{a}$ | N | For slow rotation or stationary check |
| 9 | Static safety check | $S_{0} \;=\; C_{0} / P_{0} \;\geq\; S_{0,\,min}$ | — | $S_{0,min} = 1.0$ (steady), $1.5$ (light shock), $2.0$ (heavy shock) |
| 10 | Speed limit (DN) | $DN \;\leq\; DN_{max}$ | mm·rpm | $DN_{max}$ ≈ $1.5\times10^{6}$ ball, $1.0\times10^{6}$ roller (approx) |
| 11 | Modified life per ISO 281 (general) | $L_{10h} \;=\; a_{1}\,a_{2}\,a_{3}\,\dfrac{10^{6}}{60\,n}\left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{p}$ | h | Full integrated form |
| 12 | Relation between revolutions and hours | $L_{rev} \;=\; 60\,n\,L_{h}$ | rev | Conversions |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Trap:** When writing $\vert F_a \vert$ or $\vert C \vert$ in exam scripts, the KTU evaluator's OCR will mis-read a vertical bar `|`. Always write `\vert F_a \vert` in LaTeX, or simply describe as "magnitude of F_a".

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Electric-motor industry:** almost universally deep-groove ball bearings, selected purely for L₁₀ ≥ 20 000–40 000 h of continuous duty.
- **Wind-turbine main shafts:** spherical roller bearings, selected by L₁₀ ≥ 130 000 h under combined radial + axial + tilting moment loading.
- **Automotive wheel hubs:** tapered roller bearings, selected by L₁₀ ≥ 3 000 h with **$a_{1} = 0.62$** (reliability 95 %).
- **CNC machine-tool spindles:** hybrid ceramic ball bearings with **$a_{2} = 1.3$** material factor and **$a_{3} = 0.8$** because of the demanding contamination and lubrication regime.

The single dominant equation governing the entire selection is the **load-life relationship**, which the next section derives in full.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Basic Rating Life Equation

The Lundberg–Palmgren fatigue theory (1947) — still the international standard basis of ISO 281 and ISO 16281 — is summarised below.

### Step 1 — Hertzian Contact Stress
For a ball or roller pressed against a raceway with load *Q*, the maximum Hertzian contact stress is:

$$p_{max} \;=\; \frac{3F}{2\pi\,a\,b}$$

where $a$ and $b$ are the semi-axes of the contact ellipse. For ball-on-raceway contact the contact area is circular, so $a = b$, giving a single contact radius.

### Step 2 — Stress at Depth *z₀*
Lundberg and Palmgren found that the orthogonal shear stress at a critical depth *z₀* below the contact surface (where $z_0/b \approx 0.5$ for ball bearings) controls the fatigue initiation of subsurface cracks:

$$\tau_{0} \;=\; k_1\,p_{max}^{n}$$

where the exponent $n$ is determined empirically. The stress at the rolling direction is slightly higher, and the orthogonal component is what drives the crack. The result is $n = 3$ for ball bearings, giving rise to a load-life exponent of $p = 3$.

### Step 3 — Probability of Survival
A Weibull-distributed survival probability is assumed for a contact volume $V$:

$$S \;=\; \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{\tau_0}{\tau_u}\right)^{c}\,N^{e}\,\frac{V}{V_0}\right]$$

The constants $c$, $e$ are fitted; the most important outcome is that the exponent of the load term equals $e \cdot n = 3$ for ball bearings, and $e \cdot n = 10/3$ for line-contact roller bearings.

### Step 4 — Setting 10 % Failure Criterion
A survival rate of 90 % corresponds to $S = 0.9$, leading to a constant $\tau_u^{c}$ that defines the **basic dynamic load rating** *C*: the load at which 90 % survive $10^{6}$ revolutions.

### Step 5 — The Working Equation
For a single bearing with equivalent dynamic load $P$, the number of revolutions to reach 10 % failure is:

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{10} \;=\; \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{p}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the celebrated ISO 281 fundamental equation. The exponent $p$ is:

$$p \;=\; \begin{cases} 3 & \text{for ball bearings} \\[4pt] 10/3 & \text{for roller bearings} \end{cases}$$

### Step 6 — Conversion to Hours
Because machines are designed in hours, not revolutions, multiply by $1/(60\,n)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{10h} \;=\; \frac{10^{6}}{60\,n}\left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{p}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 7 — Required Rating Inversion
When the required life is specified and the load is known, invert to size the bearing:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{req} \;=\; P\left(\frac{60\,n\,L_{10h}}{10^{6}}\right)^{1/p}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — Step by Step

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU-style):** A deep-groove ball bearing (6208, C = 32.5 kN) carries a radial load of $F_r = 4.5$ kN with negligible axial load, on a shaft rotating at $n = 1440$ rpm. Compute (a) the basic rating life in hours, and (b) the rating life if the reliability is to be 99 %.

### Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1.** Identify bearing type and exponent: ball → $p = 3$.

**Step 2.** Equivalent dynamic load (no axial component):

$$P \;=\; F_{r} \;=\; 4.5 \;\text{kN}$$

**Step 3.** Compute the life ratio:

$$\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{C}{P}\right) \;=\; \frac{32.5}{4.5} \;=\; 7.222
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4.** Cube to obtain the rating life in revolutions:

$$L_{10} \;=\; (7.222)^{3} \;=\; 376.8 \;\text{million revolutions}$$

**Step 5.** Convert to hours:

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{10h} \;=\; \frac{L_{10}\times 10^{6}}{60\,n} \;=\; \frac{376.8\times 10^{6}}{60\times 1440}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6.** Final numeric evaluation:

$$L_{10h} \;=\; \frac{3.768\times 10^{8}}{8.64\times 10^{4}} \;\approx\; 4361 \;\text{hours}$$

### Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1.** Reliability factor for 99 % (from ISO 281 tables, $a_1 = 0.21$ for ball bearings, but in KTU undergraduate treatments it is acceptable to use the table value of $a_1 \approx 0.21$ when 99 % is required).

**Step 2.** Modified rating life:

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{10h,\,99\%} \;=\; a_{1}\,L_{10h} \;=\; 0.21\times 4361
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3.** Numeric evaluation:

$$L_{10h,\,99\%} \;\approx\; 916 \;\text{hours}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Increasing reliability from 90 % to 99 % reduced the rating life by nearly 80 %. This is why the KTU design data sheet always asks you to specify the reliability requirement *first*, then size the bearing.

## 3.3 The Modified Life Equation (ISO 281:2007)

The advanced form, used when the bearing is selected for non-standard reliability, special materials, or severe operating conditions, is:

$$L_{nm} \;=\; a_{1}\,a_{2}\,a_{3}\,\frac{10^{6}}{60\,n}\left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{p}$$

where:

- $a_{1}$ = reliability factor (1.0 for 90 %, 0.62 for 95 %, 0.21 for 99 %, 0.07 for 99.9 %)
- $a_{2}$ = material factor (1.0 for standard through-hardened, up to 1.3 for case-carburized or hybrid ceramic)
- $a_{3}$ = operating-condition factor (covers lubrication, contamination, temperature, viscosity ratio $\kappa = \nu/\nu_1$).

Inverting this for a required modified life gives:

$$C_{req,\,mod} \;=\; P\left[\frac{60\,n\,L_{nm}}{a_{1}\,a_{2}\,a_{3}\,10^{6}}\right]^{1/p}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bearing Selection — Top-Down Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Identify Load F_r, F_a, n rpm, Life L_h] --> B{Load Direction}
    B -->|Pure Radial| C[Deep Groove Ball 6XX or Cylindrical Roller NU]
    B -->|Combined Radial + Thrust| D[Angular Contact 7XX or Tapered Roller 3XX]
    B -->|Pure Thrust| E[Thrust Ball 5XX]
    B -->|Self Aligning Needed| F[Spherical Roller 2XX or Self Aligning Ball 1XX]

    C --> G[Compute P = F_r or X Fr + Y Fa]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G

    G --> H[Compute C_req from Life Equation]
    H --> I{Bearing from Catalog with C >= C_req}
    I -->|No| J[Increase Bore Diameter Series]
    J --> H
    I -->|Yes| K[Static Check: P_0 <= C_0 / S_0]
    K --> L{S_0 >= S_0_min}
    L -->|No| J
    L -->|Yes| M[Speed Check: DN <= DN_max]
    M --> N{DN Satisfied}
    N -->|No| O[Switch to Lower Friction Bearing Type]
    O --> H
    N -->|Yes| P[FINAL: Selected Bearing with Catalog Code]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Bearing Life Calculation

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 4
    A["INPUT: F_r F_a n rpm L_h hours"]:::in
    B["BLOCK 1: Compute Equivalent Load P = X Fr + Y Fa"]:::proc
    C["BLOCK 2: Compute C_req = P times L factor to 1 over p"]:::proc
    D["OUTPUT 1: Required Dynamic Rating C_req in kN"]:::out
    space
    E["INPUT: C_0 from catalog and S_0 min"]:::in
    F["BLOCK 3: Compute P_0 = X_0 Fr + Y_0 Fa"]:::proc
    G["BLOCK 4: S_0 = C_0 / P_0 >= S_0 min"]:::proc
    H["OUTPUT 2: Pass or Fail Static Check"]:::out
    space
    I["INPUT: a_1 a_2 a_3 and L_required"]:::in
    J["BLOCK 5: L_nm = a_1 a_2 a_3 times 10^6 over 60 n times C over P power p"]:::proc
    K["BLOCK 6: Compare L_nm to L_required"]:::proc
    L["OUTPUT 3: Pass or Fail Life Check"]:::out

    classDef in fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    class A,E,I in
    class B,C,F,G,J,K proc
    class D,H,L out
```

## 4.3 Load-Life Relationship Curve (Schematic Map)

```mermaid
graph LR
    LP["Low Load P small"] -->|High Life| HL["L_10 Large, Long Operation"]
    MP["Medium Load P moderate"] -->|Mid Life| ML["L_10 Medium, Normal Operation"]
    HP["High Load P close to C"] -->|Short Life| SL["L_10 Small, Frequent Replacement"]
    HP2["Static Overload P_0 over C_0"] -->|No Rotation| SD["Permanent Indentation, Bearing Failure"]

    HL --> DEC["Decision: Safe, Oversized"]
    ML --> DEC2["Decision: Economical, Preferred"]
    SL --> DEC3["Decision: Risky, Undersized"]
    SD --> DEC4["Decision: Unacceptable"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read the diagrams for the exam:** the block diagram (Section 4.2) is the one a KTU panel examiner expects to see drawn on the answer sheet for 14-mark selection problems. The decision tree (Section 4.1) is supplementary — a high-quality answer may draw either, but the *block diagram* is more score-positive because it shows explicit inputs, transformations, and outputs.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define "Basic Dynamic Load Rating" of a rolling contact bearing. Why is it quoted at $10^6$ revolutions and not at any other number?**

**Model Answer:**
The *basic dynamic load rating (C)* of a rolling contact bearing is the **constant radial load** that a sufficiently large, apparently identical group of bearings can endure for a basic rating life of **$10^6$ revolutions** with a **90 % probability of survival** (i.e., only 10 % will show evidence of material fatigue).

It is quoted at $10^6$ revolutions for two reasons:

1. **Historical / standardisation:** The number originated from Lundberg–Palmgren experimental work (1947) and was adopted by ISO 15 and ISO 281 as a convenient round figure.
2. **Mathematical convenience:** It makes the life equation a simple power: $L_{10} = (C/P)^{p}$, eliminating a constant from the formula and allowing direct comparison across manufacturers worldwide.

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any four factors that influence the selection of a rolling contact bearing, and state how each is used in the selection procedure.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Magnitude and type of load** — determines the bearing family (radial → deep-groove ball / cylindrical roller; combined → angular contact / tapered roller) and is used in computing the equivalent dynamic load $P = X F_r + Y F_a$.
2. **Rotational speed** — sets the DN-limit, and is used in the conversion from revolutions to hours of life.
3. **Required life in hours** — together with load and speed, gives the required basic dynamic load rating $C_{req}$.
4. **Operating temperature and lubrication** — restricts the bearing's series and influences the operating-condition factor $a_3$ in the modified life equation.

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Module Choice Questions

### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PERAT521]`
**(a)** Derive the basic rating life equation for a rolling-element bearing from first principles, clearly stating the assumptions of the Lundberg–Palmgren theory. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 6310 deep-groove ball bearing is to operate under the following conditions: radial load $F_r = 7$ kN, axial load $F_a = 1.5$ kN, speed $n = 1000$ rpm, and the bearing is required to deliver 5000 hours of life with 99 % reliability. The catalog data are: $C = 62$ kN, $C_0 = 38$ kN, $X = 0.56$, $Y = 1.45$, $e = 0.68$, $X_0 = 0.6$, $Y_0 = 0.5$. Verify whether the bearing is suitable. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)
- State Hertzian contact: load $Q$ creates a contact ellipse with maximum pressure $p_{max} \propto Q^{1/2}$ (ball) or $Q^{1/2}$ (line contact).
- The critical alternating shear stress is at depth $z_0 \approx 0.5 b$ below the surface and is proportional to $Q^{1/2}$ for ball or $Q^{1/2}$ for roller.
- Weibull probability: $S = \exp[-(\tau_0/\tau_u)^c \, N^e \, V/V_0]$.
- Eliminate constants by defining $C$ as the load that gives $S = 0.9$ at $N = 10^6$.
- The result is $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$ with $p = 3$ (ball) or $p = 10/3$ (roller).
- State assumptions: stationary, isotropic, Weibull-distributed defects, identical populations, surface-initiated cracks.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)
**Step 1 — Check axial-load ratio.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{F_a}{F_r} \;=\; \frac{1.5}{7} \;\approx\; 0.214
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with e.** Since $F_a/F_r = 0.214 < e = 0.68$, use the radial-only case: $X = 1$, $Y = 0$.

**Step 3 — Compute P.**

$$P \;=\; X F_r + Y F_a \;=\; 1\times 7 + 0 \times 1.5 \;=\; 7\;\text{kN}$$

**Step 4 — Compute L₁₀ hours (90 % reliability).**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{10h} \;=\; \frac{10^{6}}{60\,n}\left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{3} \;=\; \frac{10^{6}}{60\times 1000}\left(\frac{62}{7}\right)^{3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the cube.**

$$\left(\frac{62}{7}\right)^{3} \;=\; (8.857)^{3} \;\approx\; 694.6$$

**Step 6 — Final numeric.**

$$L_{10h} \;\approx\; \frac{694.6\times 10^{6}}{6\times 10^{4}} \;\approx\; 11\,577 \;\text{h}$$

**Step 7 — Apply reliability factor for 99 %.** $a_1 = 0.21$ (ball, 99 %):

$$L_{10h,\,99\%} \;=\; 0.21 \times 11\,577 \;\approx\; 2\,431 \;\text{h}$$

**Step 8 — Compare with requirement.** Required 5 000 h, but available is only 2 431 h. **Bearing is NOT suitable** — must move to the next larger bore series (e.g., 6312).

**Step 9 — Static check (for completeness).**

$$P_0 \;=\; X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a \;=\; 0.6\times 7 + 0.5 \times 1.5 \;=\; 4.95 \;\text{kN}$$

$$S_0 \;=\; \frac{C_0}{P_0} \;=\; \frac{38}{4.95} \;\approx\; 7.68 \;\geq\; 1.0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{Static OK}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** The most common error is using $X = 0.56, Y = 1.45$ *unconditionally*. KTU candidates are required to first compute $F_a/F_r$ and **compare with $e$** before picking $X$ and $Y$. Failing to do so costs **2 marks** instantly.

---

### Question B-B `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PERAT521 Alternative]`
**(a)** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the step-by-step procedure for selecting a rolling-element bearing for a given application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical roller bearing NU 310 E has the following catalog data: $C = 73$ kN, $C_0 = 73$ kN. It operates at 1800 rpm under a radial load of $F_r = 12$ kN. Compute: (i) the basic rating life in hours, (ii) the rating life in hours at 95 % reliability. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)
The block diagram follows the architecture shown in Section 4.2 of these notes. Steps in narrative form:

1. List service conditions: $F_r, F_a, n, L_h$, temperature, contamination, lubrication.
2. Decide bearing type and series based on load direction and magnitude.
3. Compute equivalent dynamic load $P$ using $X$ and $Y$ from catalog.
4. Compute $C_{req} = P \left[60 n L_{10h} / 10^{6}\right]^{1/p}$.
5. Pick a tentative bearing whose $C \geq C_{req}$ from manufacturer tables.
6. Verify static-load safety: $S_0 = C_0 / P_0 \geq S_{0,min}$.
7. Verify speed limit: $DN \leq DN_{max}$.
8. If any check fails, return to step 5 with a larger bore.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)
**Step 1 — Bearing type & exponent.** Cylindrical roller → $p = 10/3$.

**Step 2 — Equivalent load (pure radial, no thrust).**

$$P \;=\; F_{r} \;=\; 12\;\text{kN}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $(C/P)^{p}$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{C}{P} \;=\; \frac{73}{12} \;\approx\; 6.083
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Raise to the power $p = 10/3$.**

$$(6.083)^{10/3} \;=\; (6.083)^{3.3333}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate using log form.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\ln(6.083) &\;\approx\; 1.805 \\
1.805 \times \tfrac{10}{3} &\;\approx\; 6.018 \\
e^{6.018} &\;\approx\; 410.0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Compute L₁₀ hours.**

$$L_{10h} \;=\; \frac{10^{6}}{60 \times 1800} \times 410.0 \;\approx\; \frac{410.0 \times 10^{6}}{1.08\times 10^{5}} \;\approx\; 3\,796\;\text{h}$$

**Step 7 — Reliability factor for 95 %.** $a_1 = 0.62$:

$$L_{10h,\,95\%} \;=\; 0.62 \times 3\,796 \;\approx\; 2\,354\;\text{h}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** A frequent mistake is to write $p = 3$ for *every* bearing. **Cylindrical, tapered, spherical and needle roller bearings must use $p = 10/3$.** Mixing this up costs **2–3 marks** silently, because the numeric answer appears to come out "okay" but is dimensionally and physically wrong by a factor of 2.5.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's General Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on bearing-selection problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the static load check** — life is OK but the bearing permanently deforms at standstill. KTU deducts 2 marks.
> 2. **Using $F_a/F_r$ and choosing the wrong row of the $X, Y$ table** — read the *operating condition row*, not just the first row of the table.
> 3. **Mixing the exponent $p$**: $p = 3$ for balls, $p = 10/3$ for rollers. Carrying the wrong exponent is **2–3 marks** lost.
> 4. **Not converting to hours** — leaving the answer in revolutions and the examiner cannot verify the life target.
> 5. **Quoting $C$ in N and $L_{10}$ in millions of revolutions inconsistently** — keep units consistent: $C$ in N, $P$ in N, $n$ in rpm, $L$ in hours.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Selection of Bearings**

- [ ] **Bearing families** (radial, angular contact, tapered, spherical, thrust) are chosen by the *direction* of load, not just by magnitude.
- [ ] **Equivalent dynamic load** is $P = X F_r + Y F_a$; $X$ and $Y$ come from the manufacturer catalog and depend on the ratio $F_a/F_r$ compared with $e$.
- [ ] **Life equation (fundamental):** $L_{10} = (C/P)^{p}$ in revolutions; $L_{10h} = (10^6 / 60n)(C/P)^{p}$ in hours.
- [ ] **Exponent:** $p = 3$ ball, $p = 10/3$ roller — *do not* mix.
- [ ] **Required dynamic rating:** $C_{req} = P[60nL_{10h}/10^6]^{1/p}$ — used to size the bearing from the catalog.
- [ ] **Static safety:** $S_0 = C_0/P_0 \geq S_{0,min}$ (1.0 steady, 1.5 light shock, 2.0 heavy shock).
- [ ] **Speed limit:** $DN = d \times n \leq DN_{max}$ (≈ $1.5\times 10^6$ ball, $1.0\times 10^6$ roller).
- [ ] **Modified rating life (ISO 281):** $L_{nm} = a_1 a_2 a_3 L_{10}$. $a_1$ = reliability, $a_2$ = material, $a_3$ = operating condition.
- [ ] **Selection is iterative** — start with a tentative bore, verify life, static and speed, refine if any check fails.
- [ ] **Units discipline:** C and P in kN (or N — pick one), $n$ in rpm, $L$ in hours — never mix minutes with hours or N with kN silently.
- [ ] **Catalogs (SKF, FAG, NTN, NBC, Tata)** must be referenced for $C$, $C_0$, $X$, $Y$, $e$, $X_0$, $Y_0$ — examiners credit cross-references to such catalogs in the answer sheet.
- [ ] **Plain/journal bearings** are chosen by the **Petrov equation** (boundary) or **Reynolds equation** (hydrodynamic); the KTU syllabus treats them in a sibling module — be alert to context.
