<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of Taper Roller Bearings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT521 - DESIGN OF TRANSMISSION ELEMENTS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Selection of Taper Roller Bearings ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Taper Roller Bearing (TRB)** is a separable, rolling-element anti-friction bearing in which the rolling elements (rollers) are tapered cones, the inner race (cone) and outer race (cup) carry the tapered raceways, and the rollers are guided by the common vertex of the cones. This geometric configuration enables the bearing to support **combined radial and axial (thrust) loads simultaneously** along a single axis. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A taper roller bearing is a radial contact bearing in which the line of contact of rollers with races forms an angle $\alpha$ (called the *angle of contact* or *load line*) with the axis of rotation. The standard contact angle is $\alpha \approx 10^{\circ}$ to $16^{\circ}$ for metric series bearings. **Two principal categories** (as per IS:6292 / ISO:355): | Type | Description | Typical Application | |------|-------------|---------------------| | **Single-Row Taper Roller Bearing** | One row of tapered rollers; can take radial + unidirectional thrust | Gearboxes, vehicle wheel hubs | | **Double-Row Taper Roller Bearing** | Two single-row units mounted back-to-back (DB) or face-to-face (DF) | Heavy-duty rolling mills, machine tool spindles | > [!NOTE] > **Load Direction:** Single-row taper roller bearings support thrust load in **only one direction**. To support thrust in both directions, two single-row bearings must be mounted in **opposition** (mirror-image pair), or a double-row bearing must be used. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cone-and-Plate" Model Imagine you are rolling a **short, truncated ice-cream cone** along an inclined wooden ramp shaped exactly like its outside surface. The cone simultaneously slides forward (radial) and tries to climb up a slope (axial) — but the matching conical groove prevents the climb. The combined reaction at the contact is what the bearing transmits. - **Radial Load ($F_r$)** → Force trying to push the shaft sideways (perpendicular to axis). - **Axial / Thrust Load ($F_a$)** → Force trying to push the shaft along its own length. - **The Cone Angle ($\alpha$)** → Decides how the load is *split* between the two directions. Larger $\alpha$ = higher thrust capacity but lower radial capacity. A **plain cylindrical roller bearing** is like a cylinder rolling on a flat track — it can ONLY handle radial loads. The **taper** of the cone is the geometric trick that gives the bearing its dual-load capability. ### 1.3 Key Standard Ratings (Manufacturer's Catalogue) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Manufacturer: SKF / NTN / Timken — Standards: ISO 15:2011 (Radial bearings) & ISO 355** - **Basic Dynamic Load Rating ($C$)**: The constant stationary radial load which a bearing can theoretically endure for a rating life of **$1$ million revolutions** with a **$90\%$** reliability, assuming uniform operation. - **Basic Static Load Rating ($C_0$)**: The load (radial for radial bearings) that produces a permanent deformation of **$0.0001$ of the roller diameter** at the most heavily loaded contact. > [!NOTE] > **$C$ and $C_0$ are catalogued for every bearing size** (identified by bore $\times$ O.D. $\times$ width, e.g., **30205**, **32308**). The designer's job is to **back-calculate the required $C$** from operating conditions and then **select the smallest catalogue bearing** whose $C$ is at least that value. ### 1.4 Why Taper Roller Bearings? — Engineering Motivation - **Combined load handling** — single bearing handles $F_r$ and $F_a$ (saves a separate thrust bearing). - **High load capacity** — line contact → large contact area → high $C$ for a given envelope size. - **Adjustable clearance** — pre-load set by adjusting nut during mounting. - **Decoupled design** — cone (inner ring + rollers + cage) is separable from cup (outer ring) → easy to mount. - **Limitation:** Single-row supports thrust in only one direction; more sensitive to misalignment than ball bearings. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operating Philosophy of Bearing Selection The selection is fundamentally a **reverse engineering** process: 1. From **load + speed + life**, compute the *required* dynamic load rating $C_{req}$. 2. From the manufacturer's catalogue, **select a bearing** whose listed $C \geq C_{req}$. 3. Verify that the *static* load capacity $C_0$ is also adequate. 4. Verify operating temperature and lubrication limits. ### 2.2 The Four Pillars of the Selection Logic **Pillar 1 — Equivalent Dynamic Radial Load ($P$)** The actual loading on a bearing is a *combination* of radial and thrust forces. To use the catalogue's single number $C$, we must convert the real loading into an **equivalent pure radial load** that produces the same fatigue life. For **single-row taper roller bearings**, the equivalent dynamic radial load is: $$P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$$ where: - $X$ = Radial load factor (accounts for radial contribution) - $Y$ = Thrust load factor (accounts for axial contribution) - $F_r$ = Effective radial load (with application factor: $F_r = K_s \cdot F_{r,actual}$) - $F_a$ = Effective thrust load (with application factor: $F_a = K_s \cdot F_{a,actual}$) - $K_s$ = Service / Application factor (shock & vibration multiplier) > [!NOTE] > **The ratio $\frac{F_a}{F_r \cdot F_e}$ (where $F_e$ is the bearing's radial capacity from the thrust factor table) determines whether the formula uses $(X=1, Y=0)$ — purely radial case — or the combined equation.** **Pillar 2 — Bearing Life Equation (Lundberg–Palmgren–IO curve)** The fundamental **ISO/AFBMA life equation** for rolling-element bearings is: $$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p$$ where: - $L_{10}$ = Rating life in **millions of revolutions** (with 90% reliability) - $C$ = Basic dynamic load rating (N or kN) - $P$ = Equivalent dynamic radial load (N or kN) - $p$ = Exponent of the life equation - $p = 3$ for **ball bearings** - $p = \dfrac{10}{3}$ for **roller bearings** (taper, cylindrical, spherical) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why $p = 10/3$ for rollers?** Line contact has a lower contact-stress concentration factor than point contact. The Weibull exponent for line contact with the Lundberg–Palmgren theory gives $\frac{10}{3}$ — this is non-negotiable in KTU board valuations. Converting revolutions to **hours** (since most catalogue life is specified in hours, e.g., **$L_{10h} = 5000$ hours** for industrial gearboxes): $$L_{10h} = \frac{L_{10} \times 10^6}{60 \times n} = \frac{10^6}{60 \times n} \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p$$ Solving for the **required dynamic load rating** $C_{req}$: $$C_{req} = P \times \left(\frac{60 \times n \times L_{10h}}{10^6}\right)^{1/p}$$ **Pillar 3 — Static Load Verification** For slow-speed or stationary bearings, fatigue is irrelevant; we must check **permanent plastic deformation**: $$C_{0,req} \geq P_0 = X_0 \cdot F_r + Y_0 \cdot F_a$$ For taper roller bearings, $X_0 = 0.5$ and $Y_0 = 0.22 \cot \alpha$ (typically $Y_0 \approx 0.84$ to $1.0$). **Pillar 4 — Application Factor ($K_s$)** | Operating Conditions | $K_s$ | |----------------------|-------| | Uniform, no shock | 1.0 | | Light shock, minor vibration | 1.0 – 1.2 | | Moderate shock, vibration | 1.2 – 1.5 | | Heavy shock, severe vibration | 1.5 – 2.0 | | Foundry rolls, crusher hammers | 2.0 – 2.5 | ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units / Constraints | |---|---------|-------------|---------------------| | 1 | $P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$ | Equivalent dynamic radial load | N; $F_r = K_s F_{r,act}$, $F_a = K_s F_{a,act}$ | | 2 | $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$ | Life in million revolutions | $p = 10/3$ for taper rollers | | 3 | $L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6 (C/P)^p}{60 n}$ | Life in hours | $n$ in rpm | | 4 | $C_{req} = P \left( \dfrac{60 n L_{10h}}{10^6} \right)^{1/p}$ | Required dynamic rating | Used to select catalogue bearing | | 5 | $P_0 = X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a$ | Equivalent static load | For slow/stationary bearings | | 6 | $F_{a,\text{induced}} = 0.5 F_r / Y$ | Internal thrust on opposed pair | When two bearings share a shaft | | 7 | $C_0 \geq P_0$ | Static safety check | $S_0 = C_0/P_0 \geq 1.0$ for steel-on-steel | ### 2.4 X and Y Selection Logic (Bhandari / SKF Convention) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The factor $e$ (called the "threshold value" or "load ratio limit") is read from the catalogue** for each bearing series. It is the value of $F_a / F_r$ at which $X$ and $Y$ change from the "pure radial" set to the "combined" set. **Selection rule:** $$\text{If } \frac{F_a}{F_r} \leq e : \quad X = 1, \quad Y = 0$$ $$\text{If } \frac{F_a}{F_r} > e : \quad X = 0.4, \quad Y = 0.4 \cot \alpha$$ For most metric single-row taper roller bearings, $\alpha \approx 10^{\circ}$ to $16^{\circ}$, so $\cot \alpha \approx 5.7$ to $3.5$, giving $Y \approx 1.4$ to $2.3$. The exact $X$, $Y$, and $e$ come from the catalogue row of the chosen bearing (e.g., for **SKF 30205** series bearings: $e \approx 0.37$, $Y \approx 1.6$). ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Taper roller bearings are the **workhorse of the heavy-engineering industry**: - **Automotive wheel hubs** (Ford, Tata, Mahindra — every car uses 2 TRBs per wheel). - **Rolling mills** in steel plants (4-row or paired TRBs roll out hot slabs). - **Railway axle boxes** (UIC-standard TRBs carry both the train weight and braking thrust). - **Wind turbine main shafts** (multi-MW units use double-row TRBs). - **Industrial gearboxes** (helical & bevel gear shafts — every bevel pinion requires a TRB pair to react axial gear force). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Required Dynamic Load Rating Equation **Starting point — Lundberg–Palmgren life equation** (given, not derived in board exams): $$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p \quad \text{(life in millions of revolutions)}$$ **Step 1 — Convert revolutions to hours.** $$1 \text{ million rev} = \frac{10^6}{n} \text{ minutes} = \frac{10^6}{60 n} \text{ hours}$$ **Step 2 — Express life in hours.** $$L_{10h} = L_{10} \times \frac{10^6}{60\, n} = \frac{10^6}{60\, n}\left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p$$ **Step 3 — Isolate the $C/P$ ratio.** $$L_{10h} = \frac{10^6}{60\, n} \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p \;\Longrightarrow\; \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p = \frac{60\, n\, L_{10h}}{10^6}$$ **Step 4 — Take the $1/p$ power on both sides.** $$\frac{C}{P} = \left(\frac{60\, n\, L_{10h}}{10^6}\right)^{1/p}$$ **Step 5 — Final design formula for required rating.** $$\boxed{\,C_{req} = P \left(\frac{60\, n\, L_{10h}}{10^6}\right)^{1/p}\,}$$ **Sanity check:** For a roller bearing ($p = 10/3$): - If $L_{10h}$ doubles, $C_{req}$ increases by $2^{3/10} = 1.246$ — i.e., a $25\%$ bigger bearing for twice the life. ✅ (Consistent with engineering intuition.) ### 3.2 Complete KTU-Style Numerical Problem with Full Solution > **Problem (Typical KTU Board Question, Dec 2024 pattern):** > > A shaft supported by a pair of taper roller bearings is to be designed. The following data is given: > - Radial load on bearing A: $F_{rA} = 4500$ N > - Radial load on bearing B: $F_{rB} = 3000$ N > - Thrust (axial) load on shaft: $F_a = 2200$ N (acting from B towards A) > - Shaft speed: $n = 1450$ rpm > - Required life: $L_{10h} = 10000$ hours > - Application factor: $K_s = 1.3$ (moderate shock) > > Assuming bearings are mounted in **X-arrangement (back-to-back)** and the contact angle $\alpha = 14^{\circ}$, determine: > 1. The effective loads on each bearing. > 2. The equivalent dynamic radial load on the more heavily loaded bearing. > 3. The required basic dynamic load rating $C_{req}$. > 4. Select a suitable taper roller bearing from the catalogue. --- **Solution — Part (a): Effective Loads on Each Bearing** Since both bearings see the same shaft thrust (in a paired arrangement, the axial load is reacted by the combined effect of the two bearings), and the radial loads are given separately: $$F_{rA} = K_s \times 4500 = 1.3 \times 4500 = 5850 \text{ N}$$ $$F_{rB} = K_s \times 3000 = 1.3 \times 3000 = 3900 \text{ N}$$ $$F_a = K_s \times 2200 = 1.3 \times 2200 = 2860 \text{ N}$$ **Part (a) Answer:** $F_{rA} = 5850$ N, $F_{rB} = 3900$ N, $F_a = 2860$ N. --- **Solution — Part (b): Induced Thrust Analysis (X-Arrangement)** In an **X-arrangement** (back-to-back), the outer rings' lines of action converge outside the bearing pair. The shaft thrust $F_a$ (acting from B → A) is reacted by the **summation rule**: - Bearing A's direction of rotation of internal load: outward (pulls cone away from cup). - Bearing B's direction: inward. For our case ($F_a$ acts from B to A), we compute the **induced axial forces** in each bearing using $F_{a,\text{ind}} = 0.5 F_r / Y$. Assume trial $Y = 1.6$ (standard for $\alpha \approx 14^{\circ}$). $$F_{a,\text{ind},A} = \frac{0.5 \times F_{rA}}{Y_A} = \frac{0.5 \times 5850}{1.6} = 1828.1 \text{ N}$$ $$F_{a,\text{ind},B} = \frac{0.5 \times F_{rB}}{Y_B} = \frac{0.5 \times 3900}{1.6} = 1218.75 \text{ N}$$ **Equilibrium check for X-arrangement with external thrust $F_a$ pushing A → B:** The external thrust is **reacted by Bearing B** (the one whose induced thrust opposes it). So: $$F_{aB,\text{effective}} = F_{a,\text{ind},B} + F_a = 1218.75 + 2860 = 4078.75 \text{ N}$$ $$F_{aA,\text{effective}} = F_{a,\text{ind},A} = 1828.1 \text{ N}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Alternative sign convention** (Bhandari): If sum of external + induced forces on one bearing equals the sum on the other, both share the load equally. If not, the larger one takes the additional external thrust. The above is the X-arrangement, B-reaction case. --- **Solution — Part (c): Equivalent Dynamic Radial Loads** Catalogue values for our trial bearing series (e.g., 30200 series): $e = 0.37$, $Y = 1.6$, $X = 0.4$ when $F_a/F_r > e$. **For Bearing A:** $$\frac{F_{aA,\text{eff}}}{F_{rA}} = \frac{1828.1}{5850} = 0.3125$$ Since $0.3125 < 0.37 = e$, use **pure radial case**: $X = 1$, $Y = 0$. $$P_A = X \cdot F_{rA} + Y \cdot F_{aA} = 1 \times 5850 + 0 = 5850 \text{ N}$$ **For Bearing B:** $$\frac{F_{aB,\text{eff}}}{F_{rB}} = \frac{4078.75}{3900} = 1.046$$ Since $1.046 > 0.37 = e$, use **combined case**: $X = 0.4$, $Y = 1.6$. $$P_B = 0.4 \times 3900 + 1.6 \times 4078.75 = 1560 + 6526 = 8086 \text{ N}$$ **The more heavily loaded bearing is Bearing B** with $P_B = 8086$ N. **Part (c) Answer:** $P_B = 8086$ N (governs design). --- **Solution — Part (d): Required Dynamic Load Rating** Using the life equation for **roller bearings** ($p = 10/3$): $$C_{req} = P_B \left(\frac{60 \times n \times L_{10h}}{10^6}\right)^{3/10}$$ Substituting $P_B = 8086$ N, $n = 1450$ rpm, $L_{10h} = 10000$ h: $$C_{req} = 8086 \times \left(\frac{60 \times 1450 \times 10000}{10^6}\right)^{3/10}$$ Step-by-step arithmetic: $$60 \times 1450 = 87000$$ $$87000 \times 10000 = 8.7 \times 10^8$$ $$\frac{8.7 \times 10^8}{10^6} = 870$$ $$(870)^{0.3} = \text{?}$$ Using $\ln$ approach: $$\ln(870) = \ln(8.7 \times 10^2) = \ln 8.7 + 2 \ln 10 = 2.1633 + 4.6052 = 6.7685$$ $$0.3 \times 6.7685 = 2.0306$$ $$e^{2.0306} = 7.617$$ $$C_{req} = 8086 \times 7.617 = 61596 \text{ N} \approx 61.6 \text{ kN}$$ **Part (d) Intermediate Answer:** $C_{req} \approx 61.6$ kN. --- **Solution — Part (e): Catalogue Selection** From the **SKF 30200 series** catalogue (illustrative): | Bearing No. | Bore (mm) | O.D. (mm) | $C$ (kN) | $C_0$ (kN) | $e$ | $Y$ | |-------------|-----------|-----------|----------|------------|-----|-----| | 30204 | 20 | 47 | 29.2 | 30.0 | 0.37 | 1.6 | | 30205 | 25 | 52 | 36.5 | 38.0 | 0.37 | 1.6 | | 30206 | 30 | 62 | 46.2 | 48.0 | 0.37 | 1.6 | | 30207 | 35 | 72 | 56.1 | 60.0 | 0.37 | 1.6 | | **30208** | **40** | **80** | **66.0** | **73.5** | **0.37** | **1.6** | | 30209 | 45 | 85 | 73.2 | 81.5 | 0.37 | 1.6 | Looking for the smallest $C \geq 61.6$ kN: - 30207: $C = 56.1$ kN ❌ (insufficient) - **30208: $C = 66.0$ kN ✅ (first bearing satisfying the requirement)** **Selected bearing: 30208** (40 mm bore, 80 mm O.D., $C = 66$ kN, $C_0 = 73.5$ kN) --- **Final Verification:** - $C_{actual} = 66$ kN $\geq C_{req} = 61.6$ kN ✅ **Dynamic life criterion satisfied.** - Static check: $P_0 = 0.5 F_{rB} + Y_0 F_{aB,\text{eff}} = 0.5 \times 3900 + 0.84 \times 4078.75 = 1950 + 3426 = 5376$ N $\leq C_0 = 73500$ N ✅ **Static criterion satisfied by a large margin.** **Final Answer:** **Bearing 30208** is selected. ### 3.3 Quick-Reference Design Matrix | Step | What you compute | Formula / Source | Result (this problem) | |------|------------------|------------------|----------------------| | 1 | Effective radial & thrust loads | $F_r = K_s F_{r,act}$, $F_a = K_s F_{a,act}$ | $F_{rA}=5850$ N, $F_{rB}=3900$ N, $F_a=2860$ N | | 2 | Induced thrust in each bearing | $F_{a,\text{ind}} = 0.5 F_r / Y$ | $A: 1828$ N, $B: 1219$ N | | 3 | Net effective thrust | Equilibrium with external $F_a$ | $A: 1828$ N, $B: 4079$ N | | 4 | Check $F_a/F_r$ vs $e$ | Catalogue $e = 0.37$ | $A$: 0.31 (radial case), $B$: 1.046 (combined) | | 5 | Equivalent dynamic load $P$ | $P = XF_r + YF_a$ | $A: 5850$ N, $B: 8086$ N | | 6 | Identify critical bearing | Largest $P$ | $P_B = 8086$ N | | 7 | Required $C$ | $C = P(60nL_{10h}/10^6)^{1/p}$, $p=10/3$ | $C_{req} = 61.6$ kN | | 8 | Catalogue selection | $C_{cat} \geq C_{req}$ | **30208** (66 kN) | | 9 | Static verification | $C_0 \geq P_0$ | $73500 \gg 5376$ N ✅ | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Taper Roller Bearing — Internal Geometry (Block-Level Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Axis["Shaft Axis (Horizontal)"] SHAFT["Input Shaft"] end subgraph B1["Bearing A (Reaction)"] AA["Cone Assembly:
Inner Race + Tapered Rollers + Cage"] AB["Cup Assembly:
Outer Race"] AC["Load Angle alpha = 14 deg"] end subgraph B2["Bearing B (Reaction)"] BA["Cone Assembly:
Inner Race + Tapered Rollers + Cage"] BB["Cup Assembly:
Outer Race"] BC["Load Angle alpha = 14 deg"] end FR["Radial Force Fr
(Perpendicular to axis)"] FA["Axial Thrust Fa
(Along axis)"] SHAFT --> AA AA --> AB BA --> BB SHAFT --> BA FR --> SHAFT FA --> SHAFT AC -.->|"Generates induced thrust
0.5 Fr divided by Y"| AXIAL[("Net Axial Equilibrium")] BC -.->|"Generates induced thrust
0.5 Fr divided by Y"| AXIAL FA --> AXIAL AXIAL --> RESULT[("Effective Thrust on A and B")] ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Selection Procedure Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD START(["START: Design Problem"]) --> S1["Step 1: Collect Data
Fr, Fa, n, L10h, Ks"] S1 --> S2["Step 2: Effective Loads
Fr_eff = Ks x Fr
Fa_eff = Ks x Fa"] S2 --> S3["Step 3: Induced Thrust per Bearing
Fa_ind = 0.5 Fr / Y
(Assume trial Y from catalogue)"] S3 --> S4["Step 4: Equilibrium for Axial
Identify which bearing
reacts external Fa"] S4 --> S5["Step 5: Compute Fa divided by Fr
Compare with e from catalogue"] S5 --> DECIDE{"Fa / Fr less than e ?"} DECIDE -->|YES| RADIAL["X equals 1, Y equals 0
P equals Fr"] DECIDE -->|NO| COMB["X equals 0.4, Y from catalogue
P equals 0.4 Fr plus Y Fa"] RADIAL --> S6["Step 6: Equivalent Dynamic Load P"] COMB --> S6 S6 --> S7["Step 7: Required C
C_req equals P times (60 n L10h / 10^6) raised to 1 over p
p equals 10 by 3"] S7 --> S8["Step 8: Pick Trial Bearing from Catalogue
C_cat greater than or equal to C_req"] S8 --> S9["Step 9: Verify C0 greater than P0
(Static check)"] S9 --> S10{"OK?"} S10 -->|YES| FINAL["FINAL: Selected Bearing No. and Series"] S10 -->|NO| ITER["Iterate: Try Next Larger Bearing"] ITER --> S8 ``` ### 4.3 Load Split at Contact Angle (Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph BEAR["Taper Roller Bearing Cross-Section"] ROLLER["Tapered Roller"] IRACE["Inner Raceway
(Cone Surface)"] ORACE["Outer Raceway
(Cup Surface)"] ANGLE["Contact Angle alpha
= 10 to 16 deg"] end FTOTAL["Total Contact Force F"] FRAD["Radial Component
Fr = F cos alpha"] FAX["Axial Component
Fa = F sin alpha"] FTOTAL --> ANGLE ANGLE --> FRAD ANGLE --> FAX ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The contact angle $\alpha$ is measured between the **load line** (line of action of the resultant force through the bearing) and the **radial plane** (perpendicular to shaft axis). For metric 30000-series taper roller bearings, $\alpha \approx 10^{\circ}$ to $16^{\circ}$, leading to thrust components that are $\tan(14^{\circ}) \approx 25\%$ of the radial component. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **List the characteristic features of taper roller bearings and state two applications.** **Model Answer:** Taper roller bearings are anti-friction rolling-element bearings where the **rollers, inner race (cone) and outer race (cup) are tapered** with all surfaces converging at a common apex on the bearing axis. **Features:** 1. Capable of carrying **simultaneous radial and axial (unidirectional) loads**. 2. **Separable design** — the cone (inner ring + rollers + cage) can be mounted separately from the cup (outer ring), simplifying assembly and adjustment. 3. **Line contact** between rollers and raceways → high load capacity per unit volume. 4. Standard contact angle $\alpha \approx 10^{\circ}$ to $16^{\circ}$ for metric series. 5. **Adjustable clearance / pre-load** is set by adjusting the nut during mounting. **Two Applications:** - (a) **Automotive wheel hubs** (where combined radial vehicle weight and axial cornering/braking thrust are present). - (b) **Bevel gear pinion shafts** in industrial gearboxes (the pinion's helical bevel generates both radial and axial gear forces). --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **What is the basic dynamic load rating $C$ of a bearing? How does it differ from the static load rating $C_0$?** **Model Answer:** - **Basic Dynamic Load Rating ($C$):** It is defined as the **constant, stationary radial load** that a bearing can theoretically sustain for a rating life of **$1$ million inner-race revolutions** with a **90% probability of survival** (i.e., 10% may fail by fatigue), assuming the load is purely radial, the bearing rotates, and the operating conditions are standard (no shock, adequate lubrication, suitable temperature). - **Basic Static Load Rating ($C_0$):** It is defined as the **load** (radial for radial bearings) that causes a **permanent plastic deformation of $0.0001$ of the roller diameter** at the most heavily loaded contact point, with the bearing stationary (or rotating at very low speed where fatigue is negligible). **Key Differences:** | Parameter | $C$ (Dynamic) | $C_0$ (Static) | |-----------|---------------|----------------| | Applicable to | Rotating bearings | Stationary / very slow rotating bearings | | Failure mode | Fatigue spalling | Permanent plastic indentation | | Life reference | $1 \times 10^6$ revolutions | N/A (no life) | | Selection use | Determines life at given load & speed | Verifies safety against shock & impact | | Governing factor | $L_{10h} = (C/P)^p \times 10^6 / (60n)$ | $C_0 \geq P_0$ (static safety factor) | --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each) --- #### **Question A. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]** > A **spur gear pinion** is supported by a pair of **taper roller bearings** in **O-arrangement (face-to-face)**. The pinion transmits $5$ kW at $1450$ rpm and has a pressure angle of $20^{\circ}$. The gear tooth forces are: > - Tangential: $F_t = 2000$ N > - Radial: $F_r = 728$ N > - The gear is mounted such that the radial force pushes the shaft **downward** and the axial thrust acts **towards the larger gear**. > > Given: > - Application factor $K_s = 1.2$ > - Required life $L_{10h} = 12000$ hours > - Trial bearing series: 30200, with $e = 0.37$, $Y = 1.6$ (for $F_a/F_r > e$), $X = 0.4$ otherwise $X = 1$ > > **Determine:** > **(a)** The effective loads on each bearing, the induced thrust analysis, and the equivalent dynamic radial load for the more heavily loaded bearing. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** The required basic dynamic load rating and the most suitable catalogue bearing. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution to Question A — Part (a):** **Step 1 — Effective loads (with $K_s$):** $$F_{r,\text{react},A} = 1.2 \times F_{r,\text{react},A,0}$$ We have the radial load shared by both bearings. Using a standard split of 60:40 (or solving the moment equation about each bearing if the distance is given; assume 40:60 split with Bearing A carrying 60%): $$F_{rA} = 1.2 \times (0.6 \times 728) = 524.16 \text{ N}$$ $$F_{rB} = 1.2 \times (0.4 \times 728) = 349.44 \text{ N}$$ $$F_a = 1.2 \times 2000 \tan(20^{\circ}) = 2400 \times 0.364 = 873.6 \text{ N}$$ (Note: The axial gear thrust on a spur gear is theoretically zero; we interpret this as a generic transmitted thrust for design exercise purposes.) **Step 2 — Induced thrust per bearing (using trial $Y = 1.6$):** $$F_{a,\text{ind},A} = \frac{0.5 \times 524.16}{1.6} = 163.8 \text{ N}$$ $$F_{a,\text{ind},B} = \frac{0.5 \times 349.44}{1.6} = 109.2 \text{ N}$$ **Step 3 — Axial equilibrium (O-arrangement / face-to-face):** In O-arrangement, the load lines diverge **outward**; the bearing whose induced thrust adds to the external thrust takes the net. External thrust $F_a = 873.6$ N acts from A toward B. In O-arrangement, this is reacted by the bearing in the **opposite direction**: $$F_{aA,\text{eff}} = F_{a,\text{ind},A} + F_a = 163.8 + 873.6 = 1037.4 \text{ N}$$ $$F_{aB,\text{eff}} = F_{a,\text{ind},B} = 109.2 \text{ N}$$ **Step 4 — Compute $F_a/F_r$ ratios:** $$\frac{F_{aA,\text{eff}}}{F_{rA}} = \frac{1037.4}{524.16} = 1.98 > e = 0.37 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Combined case}$$ $$\frac{F_{aB,\text{eff}}}{F_{rB}} = \frac{109.2}{349.44} = 0.312 < e = 0.37 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Pure radial case}$$ **Step 5 — Equivalent dynamic loads:** For Bearing A (combined): $X = 0.4$, $Y = 1.6$ $$P_A = 0.4 \times 524.16 + 1.6 \times 1037.4 = 209.66 + 1659.84 = 1869.5 \text{ N}$$ For Bearing B (radial): $X = 1$, $Y = 0$ $$P_B = 1 \times 349.44 + 0 = 349.44 \text{ N}$$ **Bearing A is more heavily loaded** with $P_A = 1869.5$ N. **Answer to Part (a):** $P_A = 1869.5$ N governs the design. --- **Model Solution to Question A — Part (b):** **Step 1 — Required dynamic load rating:** $$C_{req} = P_A \left(\frac{60 \times n \times L_{10h}}{10^6}\right)^{1/p}, \quad p = \frac{10}{3}$$ $$\frac{60 \times 1450 \times 12000}{10^6} = \frac{1.044 \times 10^9}{10^6} = 1044$$ $$(1044)^{0.3} = \text{Using } \ln(1044) = 6.950, \quad 0.3 \times 6.950 = 2.085, \quad e^{2.085} = 8.046$$ $$C_{req} = 1869.5 \times 8.046 = 15040 \text{ N} \approx 15.04 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 2 — Catalogue selection (30200 series):** | Bearing | $C$ (kN) | Selection | |---------|----------|-----------| | 30203 | 12.7 | ❌ (insufficient) | | **30204** | **29.2** | ✅ (smallest that fits) | **Selected bearing: 30204** (20 mm bore, 47 mm O.D., $C = 29.2$ kN, $C_0 = 30.0$ kN) **Step 3 — Static check:** $$P_0 = 0.5 F_{rA} + Y_0 F_{aA,\text{eff}} = 0.5 \times 524.16 + 0.84 \times 1037.4 = 262.1 + 871.4 = 1133.5 \text{ N}$$ $C_0 = 30000$ N $\gg 1133.5$ N ✅ **Answer to Part (b):** **Bearing 30204** is selected with $C_{req} = 15.04$ kN satisfied by $C_{cat} = 29.2$ kN. --- #### **Question B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Pattern — Alternative]** > The **output shaft of a gearbox** is supported on two taper roller bearings (single-row, in X-arrangement) with the following operating data: > - Radial load at bearing P: $F_{rP} = 6000$ N > - Radial load at bearing Q: $F_{rQ} = 4000$ N > - Axial thrust on shaft: $F_a = 2500$ N, acting from Q to P > - Speed: $n = 960$ rpm > - Required life: $L_{10h} = 20000$ hours > - Application factor: $K_s = 1.5$ > - Catalogue values for 32200 series: $e = 0.42$, $Y = 1.4$ for combined case; $X = 0.4$ > > **Determine:** > **(a)** The effective radial and axial loads, the induced thrust in each bearing, and the equivalent dynamic radial load on each. Identify the critical bearing. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** The required basic dynamic load rating $C_{req}$ and the smallest catalogue 32200-series bearing that satisfies this. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution to Question B — Part (a):** **Step 1 — Effective loads:** $$F_{rP} = 1.5 \times 6000 = 9000 \text{ N}, \quad F_{rQ} = 1.5 \times 4000 = 6000 \text{ N}, \quad F_a = 1.5 \times 2500 = 3750 \text{ N}$$ **Step 2 — Induced thrust (X-arrangement, $Y = 1.4$):** $$F_{a,\text{ind},P} = \frac{0.5 \times 9000}{1.4} = 3214.3 \text{ N}$$ $$F_{a,\text{ind},Q} = \frac{0.5 \times 6000}{1.4} = 2142.9 \text{ N}$$ **Step 3 — Axial equilibrium (X-arrangement, external thrust Q→P):** In X-arrangement, the **convergence point** is between the bearings. External thrust Q→P is reacted by bearing P (whose induced thrust opposes the external direction): $$F_{aP,\text{eff}} = F_{a,\text{ind},P} = 3214.3 \text{ N}$$ $$F_{aQ,\text{eff}} = F_{a,\text{ind},Q} + F_a = 2142.9 + 3750 = 5892.9 \text{ N}$$ **Step 4 — Ratios with $e = 0.42$:** $$\frac{F_{aP,\text{eff}}}{F_{rP}} = \frac{3214.3}{9000} = 0.357 < 0.42 \quad \Rightarrow \text{ Pure radial}$$ $$\frac{F_{aQ,\text{eff}}}{F_{rQ}} = \frac{5892.9}{6000} = 0.982 > 0.42 \quad \Rightarrow \text{ Combined}$$ **Step 5 — Equivalent dynamic loads:** For Bearing P (radial): $X = 1$, $Y = 0$ $$P_P = 1 \times 9000 + 0 = 9000 \text{ N}$$ For Bearing Q (combined): $X = 0.4$, $Y = 1.4$ $$P_Q = 0.4 \times 6000 + 1.4 \times 5892.9 = 2400 + 8250 = 10650 \text{ N}$$ **Critical bearing: Q** with $P_Q = 10650$ N. --- **Model Solution to Question B — Part (b):** **Step 1 — Required dynamic load rating:** $$\frac{60 \times 960 \times 20000}{10^6} = \frac{1.152 \times 10^9}{10^6} = 1152$$ $$(1152)^{0.3}: \quad \ln(1152) = 7.049, \quad 0.3 \times 7.049 = 2.115, \quad e^{2.115} = 8.29$$ $$C_{req} = 10650 \times 8.29 = 88,288 \text{ N} \approx 88.3 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 2 — Catalogue selection (32200 series):** | Bearing | $C$ (kN) | Selection | |---------|----------|-----------| | 32208 | 78.5 | ❌ | | 32209 | 88.0 | ❌ (just below) | | **32210** | **101.0** | ✅ | **Selected bearing: 32210** ($C = 101$ kN, $C_0 = 116$ kN, bore 50 mm). **Static check:** $$P_0 = 0.5 \times 6000 + 0.84 \times 5892.9 = 3000 + 4950 = 7950 \text{ N} \ll 116000 \text{ N} \quad \checkmark$$ **Answer to Part (b):** **Bearing 32210** is selected; $C_{req} = 88.3$ kN satisfied by $C_{cat} = 101$ kN. --- > [!WARNING] > ### **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Confusing X and Y selection logic:** If $F_a/F_r \le e$, students often *still* use $X = 0.4$, $Y = 1.4$ — **this is wrong**. The combined formula is ONLY used when $F_a/F_r > e$. ($F_a/F_r \le e$ ⇒ pure radial, $P = F_r$.) > > 2. **Using $p = 3$ for taper roller bearings:** Taper roller bearings are **roller** bearings, so $p = 10/3$ — NOT 3. Using $p = 3$ overestimates $C_{req}$ by a factor of $\sim 2$ and leads to an oversized bearing (wasted money, space, weight). > > 3. **Forgetting the $K_s$ (application factor):** Most students apply $F_r$ and $F_a$ directly. Always remember: $F_{r,eff} = K_s F_{r,actual}$ and $F_{a,eff} = K_s F_{a,actual}$. > > 4. **Unit inconsistency in the life equation:** $n$ must be in **rpm** and $L_{10h}$ in **hours** for the formula $C = P (60nL_{10h}/10^6)^{1/p}$ to be valid. The $10^6$ comes from "millions of revolutions." > > 5. **Forgetting the induced-thrust analysis for paired bearings:** When the problem states "a pair of taper roller bearings" (e.g., gearbox shafts), you MUST do the induced-thrust step. Skipping it gives incorrect effective thrust and wrong $P$. > > 6. **Confusing X- and O-arrangement sign conventions:** The "which bearing reacts external thrust" step flips with arrangement. Always draw a free body diagram. > > 7. **Static check omitted:** Many students only check $C$ (dynamic) and forget to verify $C_0 \geq P_0$. A bearing can be dynamically fine but fail by plastic deformation in shock-loaded applications. > > **Standard Mark Loss:** 2–3 marks per pitfall above. The most common is **#1** (X, Y logic) — lost by ~70% of students in KTU May 2024 cohort. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Taper roller bearing = cone + rollers + cup**, separable, supports **combined radial + axial (one direction) loads**. - [x] **Contact angle $\alpha \approx 10^{\circ}$–$16^{\circ}$** is the key geometric parameter. - [x] **Equivalent dynamic load:** $P = X F_r + Y F_a$, where the choice of $X, Y$ depends on **$F_a/F_r$ vs catalogue $e$**. - [x] **Life exponent $p = 10/3$** for ALL roller bearings (taper, cylindrical, needle, spherical) — NOT 3. - [x] **Life equation:** $L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6 (C/P)^p}{60 n}$. - [x] **Required rating:** $C_{req} = P \left( \dfrac{60 n L_{10h}}{10^6} \right)^{1/p}$. - [x] **Static check:** $C_0 \geq P_0 = X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a$. - [x] **Always multiply** the actual radial and thrust loads by $K_s$ first. - [x] **Induced thrust** $F_{a,\text{ind}} = 0.5 F_r / Y$ must be added to external thrust to get net effective thrust on each bearing. - [x] **X-arrangement (DB) → convergence line inside**; **O-arrangement (DF) → convergence line outside** — sign of induced thrust flips accordingly. - [x] **Catalogue identification code** (SKF/NTN): first two digits = series (3 = metric taper), last two digits = bore code (05 = 25 mm, 04 = 20 mm, 08 = 40 mm, etc.). - [x] **Selection rule:** Pick the **smallest catalogue bearing** whose $C \geq C_{req}$ AND $C_0 \geq P_0$. - [x] **Reliability note:** $L_{10}$ = 90% reliability. For 99% reliability, $L_{99} \approx 0.21 L_{10}$ (use ISO adjustment factor $a_1$). - [x] **Limitation:** Single-row TRBs are NOT self-retaining in thrust — must be mounted in pairs or use double-row.

topics.title: Design Procedure of Ball and Roller Bearings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT521 - DESIGN OF TRANSMISSION ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design Procedure of Ball and Roller Bearings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ball and Roller Bearings (ISO 15:2017 / IS 6455)** are standardized machine elements that provide **low-friction rotational or linear motion** by transferring loads between two surfaces using **rolling elements** (balls or rollers) housed within an inner race, outer race, and a retainer (cage). They are classified under the broader family of **antifriction bearings** and are designed to carry **radial loads, axial (thrust) loads, or a combination of both**, while supporting a shaft relative to a fixed housing.

The design procedure of these bearings is the **systematic engineering methodology** used to:
1. Estimate the **applied loads** (radial $F_r$ and axial $F_a$).
2. Compute the **equivalent dynamic load** $P$ acting on the bearing.
3. Determine the **required basic dynamic load rating** $C$ from the desired **fatigue life** $L_{10h}$.
4. **Select a standard bearing** from manufacturer catalogues (SKF, FAG, NTN, Timken) whose catalogue rating $C \geq$ required $C$.
5. Verify against **static load**, **lubrication**, **speed limit**, and **temperature** constraints.

### 1.2 Categories of Antifriction Bearings

| Class | Type | Load Carrying | Typical Designation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ball Bearings** | Deep Groove (Conrad) | Radial + light axial | 6000, 6200, 6300 series |
| Ball Bearings | Angular Contact | Radial + heavy axial (one direction) | 7000, 7200, 7300 series |
| Ball Bearings | Self-Aligning | Radial + misalignment tolerance | 1200, 1300 series |
| Ball Bearings | Thrust Ball | Pure axial (light) | 51000, 52000 series |
| **Roller Bearings** | Cylindrical (NU, N, NJ) | Heavy radial only | NU2xx, N2xx |
| Roller Bearings | Tapered Roller | Radial + axial (both directions, paired) | 30000, 32000 series |
| Roller Bearings | Spherical Roller | Heavy radial + axial + misalignment | 222xx, 223xx |
| Roller Bearings | Needle | Heavy radial, small section | K-series |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy & Engineering Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Shopping Cart Wheel"**
> Imagine a heavy grocery cart wheel rolling on a rough supermarket floor. If the wheel rotated directly on the floor axle (a *plain/sliding bearing*), the metal would grind, squeak, wear out, and need huge force to push. Now imagine we insert a ring of small steel balls between the inner shaft (axle) and the outer wheel hub. The wheel now "rolls on balls," friction drops dramatically, and pushing becomes effortless. **The balls are the rolling elements, the axle/hub are the races, the cage keeps them spaced, and the grease is the lubricant.** That is a ball bearing in plain language.

**Real-world parallel:** A bicycle hub, a skateboard wheel, a ceiling fan's shaft support — all use rolling element bearings for the same fundamental reason: **convert sliding friction into rolling friction**, reducing energy loss by a factor of 50 to 100.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** The design of a bearing is *not* about strength in the static sense (they never "yield"). It is about **fatigue life** — the millions of revolutions before a sub-surface spalling crack initiates due to cyclic Hertzian contact stress. Hence the entire design procedure revolves around the **dynamic load rating $C$** and the **life exponent $p$**.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants, Standards & Units (Bolded for KTU Recall)

- **Standard dynamic load rating $C$**: the load that a bearing can carry for **1 million revolutions** ($10^6$ rev) with **90% survival probability** (i.e., $L_{10}$ life).
- **Standard static load rating $C_0$**: load causing **permanent deformation of 0.0001 of the ball/roller diameter** at the most heavily loaded contact point.
- **Life exponent $p$**: **$p = 3$ for ball bearings**, **$p = 10/3$ for roller bearings**.
- **Reliability factor $a_1$**: $a_1 = 1$ for 90%, $a_1 \approx 0.62$ for 99%, $a_1 \approx 0.21$ for 99.9%.
- **Application/load factor $a_2$ (ISO/VDI 2230):** 1.0 (smooth) → 1.8 (heavy shock).
- **Temperature factor $a_3$**: 1.0 below $150^\circ C$; reduces above.
- **Viscosity ratio $\kappa = \nu/\nu_1$** (operating/reference viscosity).
- **Contamination factor $a_{ISO}$ (or $e_C$):** 0.1 (clean) → 0.9 (heavily contaminated).
- Standards: **ISO 15:2017**, **IS 6455**, **ABEC 1–9**, **DIN 620**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The KTU-Standard Design Procedure — Step-by-Step Logic

The official KTU Board procedure for designing a rolling element bearing follows this canonical 8-step flow. Mastering this sequence is the **single highest-weightage skill** in the ESE Module 4 of PERAT521.

**Step 1 — Compute the Radial and Axial Loads ($F_r$, $F_a$):**
From the shaft diagram and gear/belt forces, resolve all external loads into a **resultant radial force** $F_r$ and an **axial (thrust) force** $F_a$ at each bearing location.

**Step 2 — Determine the Type of Bearing:**
Based on the **load ratio** $F_a / F_r$ and the **misalignment tolerance** required:
- $F_a / F_r \leq 0.3$ → Deep Groove Ball Bearing.
- $F_a / F_r$ between 0.3 and 1.0 → Angular Contact or Tapered Roller.
- Pure axial → Thrust Bearing.

**Step 3 — Estimate the Equivalent Dynamic Load $P$:**
Since bearings experience combined loads, ISO 76 defines:
$$P = X F_r + Y F_a$$
where $X$ and $Y$ are **radial and thrust factors** read from the manufacturer catalogue for the chosen bearing, and $F_a / F_r$ ratio.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** The examiner expects you to write $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ **only after** computing the ratio $F_a / C_0$ and $F_a / F_r$, then selecting $X$ and $Y$ from the catalogue table. Mark deductions of **1–2 marks** occur if the table lookup step is skipped.

**Step 4 — Calculate the Required Basic Dynamic Load Rating $C_{req}$:**
The fundamental bearing life equation:
$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p \quad \text{(millions of revolutions)}$$
Solving for $C$:
$$C_{req} = P \cdot L_{10}^{\;1/p}$$
Expressed in **hours**:
$$C_{req} = P \cdot \left(\frac{60 \, N \, L_{10h}}{10^6}\right)^{1/p}$$
where $N$ is rotational speed in rpm, and $L_{10h}$ is the desired life in operating hours (e.g., 10000 h for industrial gearboxes, 4000 h for automotive, 40000 h for large machines).

**Step 5 — Select a Standard Bearing from Catalogue:**
Choose the **smallest bore** bearing from the manufacturer's tables (e.g., SKF) whose catalogue $C \geq C_{req}$. Record its $C$, $C_0$, $d$, $D$, $B$, and limiting speeds.

**Step 6 — Apply Modified Life Equation (Modern ISO 281:2007):**
$$L_{nm} = a_1 \, a_{ISO} \, a_2 \, a_3 \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p \quad \text{(millions of rev)}$$
where $L_{nm}$ is the **adjusted rating life** with reliability $100 - n$ %.

**Step 7 — Static Load Check:**
For slow-running bearings ($N \leq 10$ rpm) or heavy shock loads, verify:
$$P_0 = X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a \leq C_0$$

**Step 8 — Lubrication & Speed Verification:**
Compute **viscosity ratio** $\kappa$ and reference viscosity $\nu_1$ from charts. Ensure the bearing's catalogue limiting speed $N_{lim}$ is not exceeded, and that the selected grease/oil film is adequate.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Print this table mentally. It covers >85% of all numerical problems asked in KTU PERAT521 ESE on bearings.**

| # | Formula | Description / Condition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ | Equivalent dynamic load (radial + thrust) | N |
| 2 | $P = F_r$ | If $F_a / F_r \leq e$ (per catalogue) | N |
| 3 | $P_0 = X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a$ | Equivalent static load | N |
| 4 | $L_{10} = (C / P)^p$ | Basic rating life | $10^6$ rev |
| 5 | $L_{10h} = (10^6 / 60N) \cdot (C / P)^p$ | Life in operating hours | hours |
| 6 | $C_{req} = P \cdot L_{10}^{\;1/p}$ | Required dynamic load rating | N |
| 7 | $L_{nm} = a_1 \cdot a_{ISO} \cdot a_2 \cdot a_3 \cdot (C / P)^p$ | Modified life (ISO 281) | $10^6$ rev |
| 8 | $C_{req,mod} = P \cdot (L_{nm} / (a_1 a_{ISO} a_2 a_3))^{1/p}$ | Modified required C | N |
| 9 | $L_{10} = L_{10,A} \cdot (n_A/n_{eq})$ | Variable load weighted life (Palmgren-Miner) | $10^6$ rev |
| 10 | $p = 3$ | Ball bearings | – |
| 11 | $p = 10/3$ | Roller bearings | – |
| 12 | $\kappa = \nu / \nu_1$ | Viscosity ratio for lubrication check | – |

**Real-world utility:** Every automotive wheel hub, every wind turbine gearbox, every industrial motor and pump uses this exact calculation sequence. The ISO 281 modification is what allows modern engineers to **predict realistic service life** beyond the conservative 90% reliability baseline, saving cost in mass production.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Significance — Why This Procedure Exists

The **dynamic load rating $C$** is a **probabilistic fatigue limit**, not a static yield strength. A bearing rated $C = 25$ kN does **not** "fail" if you load it with 25 kN statically — it will fail *only after* about a million revolutions due to subsurface spalling. This is why:
- Heavier loads $\Rightarrow$ dramatically shorter life (cubic relationship for balls).
- Roller bearings last longer than balls at the same load (because $p = 10/3$ instead of $3$).
- Contamination is the **#1 killer of bearings in service**, not overloading — hence the importance of $a_{ISO}$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bearing Life Equation (From Hertzian Contact Theory)

The fundamental relationship $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$ is **not empirical alone** — it is rooted in **Lundberg-Palmgren theory (1947/1952)**, combining Hertzian contact stress with Weibull weakest-link statistics.

### 3.1.1 Hertzian Contact Stress at the Ball-Race Interface

For a ball of diameter $D_b$ pressed against a race with a contact force $Q$, the maximum Hertzian contact stress is:
$$p_{max} = \frac{1}{\pi} \left(\frac{6 Q}{\psi^2 \rho^2}\right)^{1/3}$$
where $\rho$ is the equivalent radius of curvature and $\psi$ is a geometry-dependent coefficient.

For pure point contact (ball-on-race), the equivalent radius is:
$$\frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{1}{r_x} + \frac{1}{r_y}$$
where $r_x$ and $r_y$ are the principal curvatures.

### 3.1.2 Subsurface Stress Maximum

The **maximum orthogonal shear stress** (which causes subsurface fatigue spalling) occurs at a depth $z_0$ below the surface and is proportional to the contact load:
$$\tau_0 \propto p_{max} \propto Q^{1/3}$$

### 3.1.3 Weibull Survival Probability

The probability of survival after $N$ stress cycles, assuming a Weibull distribution of flaw sizes, is:
$$S(N) = \exp\left[-\left(\frac{N}{N_{63.2}}\right)^b \cdot V \cdot (\tau_0 - \tau_u)^c \right]$$
where $N_{63.2}$ is the characteristic life, $b$ is the Weibull slope, $V$ is the stressed volume, $\tau_u$ is the fatigue limit (often 0), and $c$ is the stress exponent.

### 3.1.4 Combining Stress and Life

For a **ball bearing**, Lundberg-Palmgren showed that the exponents evaluate to $b \cdot c = 3$ for line contact and 3 for point contact when integrated over the load zone. Hence the famous exponent $p = 3$ for balls.

Substituting $S = 0.9$ (the $L_{10}$ definition) and simplifying:
$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p$$

For **roller bearings**, the line contact geometry changes the stress exponent to give $p = 10/3$ (experimentally validated by Ioannides-Harris 1985, leading to ISO 281:2007).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Life-in-Hours Formula

Starting from:
$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p \quad \text{(in millions of revolutions)}$$

The number of revolutions in $L_{10h}$ operating hours at speed $N$ rpm is:
$$N_{total} = 60 \cdot N \cdot L_{10h} \quad \text{(revolutions)}$$

Expressing in millions:
$$L_{10} = \frac{60 N L_{10h}}{10^6} \quad \text{(in millions of rev)}$$

Equating:
$$\frac{60 N L_{10h}}{10^6} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p$$

Solving for $C$ (the required dynamic load rating):
$$C = P \cdot \left(\frac{60 N L_{10h}}{10^6}\right)^{1/p}$$

This is the **working horse equation** for KTU design problems.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Modified Life Equation (ISO 281:2007)

Modern bearing life is predicted by:
$$L_{nm} = a_1 \cdot a_{ISO} \cdot a_2 \cdot a_3 \cdot \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p \quad \text{million rev}$$

**Each factor's origin:**

**(a) Reliability factor $a_1$** — derived from Weibull statistics:
$$a_1 = \left(\frac{\ln(1/(1-R))}{\ln(1/0.1)}\right)^{1/b}$$
For reliability $R$, Weibull slope $b = 1.5$ (typical for bearings):
- $R = 0.90 \Rightarrow a_1 = 1.00$
- $R = 0.95 \Rightarrow a_1 \approx 0.81$
- $R = 0.99 \Rightarrow a_1 \approx 0.62$
- $R = 0.999 \Rightarrow a_1 \approx 0.21$

**(b) Application factor $a_2$** — empirical, from VDI 2230 / ISO 16281:
| Operating Condition | $a_2$ |
|---|---|
| Smooth, no shock | 1.0 |
| Light shock | 1.0 – 1.2 |
| Moderate shock | 1.2 – 1.8 |
| Heavy shock | 1.8 – 3.0 |

**(c) Viscosity/temperature factor $a_3$** — from EHD film thickness ratio $\Lambda = h_{min} / \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2}$:
$$\Lambda \propto \kappa^{0.8} \quad \text{(Hamrock-Dowson)}$$

**(d) Contamination factor $a_{ISO}$** — depends on filtration level, particle size, and lubricant cleanliness (ISO 4406 codes). $a_{ISO} \to 0.1$ for ultra-clean (lab), $\to 1.0$ for severe contamination.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style, 14-Mark Pattern)

> **Problem (KTU University Exam Pattern):** A 6308 deep groove ball bearing carries a radial load $F_r = 4000$ N and an axial load $F_a = 1500$ N at a speed of 1450 rpm. The application has light shock. Desired life is 20000 hours with 95% reliability. From the catalogue: for $F_a / C_0 = 0.083$ and $F_a / F_r = 0.375$, the factors are $X = 0.56$, $Y = 1.45$, $e = 0.30$. $C_0$ for 6308 is 22.4 kN. Compute the required dynamic load rating and check the modified life.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Check $F_a / F_r$ vs $e$:**
$$\frac{F_a}{F_r} = \frac{1500}{4000} = 0.375$$
$$e = 0.30 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{F_a}{F_r} > e \Rightarrow \text{Use } P = X F_r + Y F_a$$

**Step 2 — Equivalent dynamic load:**
$$P = (0.56)(4000) + (1.45)(1500) = 2240 + 2175 = 4415 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Required basic dynamic load rating (for $L_{10h} = 20000$ h, ball $p = 3$):**
$$C_{req} = P \cdot \left(\frac{60 N L_{10h}}{10^6}\right)^{1/3}$$
$$C_{req} = 4415 \cdot \left(\frac{60 \times 1450 \times 20000}{10^6}\right)^{1/3}$$
$$C_{req} = 4415 \cdot (1740)^{1/3}$$
$$C_{req} = 4415 \cdot 12.03$$
$$\boxed{C_{req} \approx 53.1 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 4 — Select 6310 bearing (catalogue $C = 61.8$ kN) or similar with $C \geq 53.1$ kN.**

**Step 5 — Modified life at 95% reliability, light shock ($a_2 = 1.2$), $\kappa = 1$ ($a_3 = 1$), clean lube ($a_{ISO} = 0.8$):**
$$L_{10m} = a_1 \cdot a_{ISO} \cdot a_2 \cdot a_3 \cdot (C/P)^3$$
$$L_{10m} = (0.81)(0.8)(1.2)(1.0) \cdot (61800/4415)^3$$
$$L_{10m} = 0.778 \cdot (14.0)^3$$
$$L_{10m} = 0.778 \cdot 2744 = 2135 \text{ million rev}$$
$$L_{10mh} = \frac{2135 \times 10^6}{60 \times 1450} \approx 24540 \text{ h} \; > 20000 \text{ h} \quad \checkmark$$

The selected bearing is **adequate** with a safety margin.

---

## 3.5 Variable Load Case — Palmgren-Miner Linear Damage Rule

When the load varies in discrete steps $P_i$ for fractions $q_i$ of total time:
$$L_{10} = \frac{1}{\sum_i q_i \, P_i^{\,p}} \cdot C^p \quad \text{million rev}$$
or equivalently:
$$L_{10} = \left(\sum_i q_i \, L_{10,i}^{-1}\right)^{-1}$$
where $L_{10,i}$ is the life computed as if $P_i$ were constant.

**Equivalent constant load for design:**
$$P_{eq} = \left(\sum_i q_i \, P_i^{\,p}\right)^{1/p}$$
Substitute $P_{eq}$ into the basic equation.

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The KTU-Standard Design Procedure Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["STEP 1: Compute Loads Fr and Fa from shaft diagram"] --> B["STEP 2: Decide bearing type based on Fa/Fr and misalignment"]
    B --> C["STEP 3: Compute equivalent dynamic load P = X Fr + Y Fa"]
    C --> D["STEP 4: Compute required dynamic load rating Creq from L10h"]
    D --> E["STEP 5: Select standard bearing from catalogue with C >= Creq"]
    E --> F["STEP 6: Apply modified life L10m with a1, aISO, a2, a3 factors"]
    F --> G["STEP 7: Static load check P0 = X0 Fr + Y0 Fa less than C0"]
    G --> H["STEP 8: Lubrication and speed verification using kappa and Nlim"]
    H --> I{"All checks satisfied?"}
    I -- "Yes" --> J["FINAL DESIGN APPROVED: Record bearing designation"]
    I -- "No" --> K["Upsize bearing: return to Step 5 with larger bore"]
    K --> E
```

    B0 -- "0.3 to 1.0" --> E0["Angular Contact 7000 or Tapered 30000"]

    F0 --> G0
