# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PERAT521]**
*What are the standard dynamic and static load ratings of a rolling element bearing? How are they defined by ISO 15?*

**Model Answer:**
The **basic dynamic load rating $C$** is defined by ISO 15:2017 as the **constant, stationary radial load** which a bearing can theoretically endure for a **basic rating life of one million revolutions** ($10^6$ rev) with a **90% reliability** ($L_{10}$ life), under hypothetical operating conditions. It characterizes the **fatigue capacity** of the bearing.

The **basic static load rating $C_0$** is defined as the **static load** which corresponds to a **permanent deformation of 0.0001 times the ball diameter** (or 0.0001 times the roller diameter for roller bearings) at the most heavily stressed contact point. It characterizes the **load limit** for non-rotating or very slowly rotating bearings to prevent plastic indentation of the raceways. (3 marks)

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2023, PERAT521]**
*Differentiate between ball bearings and roller bearings with respect to (i) contact geometry, (ii) life exponent, and (iii) typical applications.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Ball Bearing | Roller Bearing |
|---|---|---|
| Contact geometry | **Point contact** (Hertzian ellipse) | **Line contact** (Hertzian rectangle) |
| Life exponent $p$ | $p = 3$ | $p = 10/3$ |
| Load capacity | Lower (small contact area) | **Higher** (longer contact line) |
| Speed capability | Higher limiting speed | Lower limiting speed |
| Typical applications | Electric motors, fans, pumps, gearboxes (light duty) | Heavy machinery, rolling mills, railway axleboxes, wind turbine gearboxes |

(3 marks)

---

## PART B — Choice-Based 14-Mark Questions

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Rule:** Each 14-mark question carries **two sub-parts of 7 marks each** — typically one sub-part on a *standard ball bearing* and one on a *roller bearing* or a *static/check sub-part*. The internal choice (Q11 OR Q12) is mandatory. Below are **two complete alternative choices**.

---

### PART B — CHOICE A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, PERAT521, Module 4 Internal Choice A]**

#### Question A – Part (a) (7 Marks)
A 30208 tapered roller bearing is to be selected for a shaft supported application. The radial load is $F_r = 6000$ N, axial load $F_a = 2000$ N, and speed is 1200 rpm. Desired life is 25000 hours with 99% reliability. From catalogue data for 30208: $C = 63.7$ kN, $C_0 = 71.5$ kN, $e = 0.42$, $Y = 1.4$, $X = 0.67$ for $F_a / F_r > e$. Compute the equivalent dynamic load and the modified life.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the load ratio:**
$$\frac{F_a}{F_r} = \frac{2000}{6000} = 0.333$$
$$e = 0.42 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{F_a}{F_r} < e \quad \Rightarrow \quad P = F_r = 6000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Compute basic $L_{10}$ life (roller bearing, $p = 10/3$):**
$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{10/3} = \left(\frac{63700}{6000}\right)^{10/3} = (10.617)^{3.333}$$
$$\ln(L_{10}) = 3.333 \times \ln(10.617) = 3.333 \times 2.362 = 7.873$$
$$L_{10} \approx 2638 \text{ million revolutions}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to hours:**
$$L_{10h} = \frac{2638 \times 10^6}{60 \times 1200} = 36639 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 4 — Modified life with $a_1 = 0.62$ (99%), $a_{ISO} = 0.8$, $a_2 = 1.0$ (smooth), $a_3 = 1.0$:**
$$L_{10m} = (0.62)(0.8)(1.0)(1.0) \times 2638 = 1308.4 \text{ million rev}$$
$$L_{10mh} = \frac{1308.4 \times 10^6}{60 \times 1200} = 18172 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** Since $L_{10mh} = 18172$ h $< 25000$ h required, the bearing is **inadequate**. Upsize to 30209 or 30210.

#### Question A – Part (b) (7 Marks)
Explain the **equivalent dynamic load concept** for bearings. How are $X$ and $Y$ factors determined for a deep groove ball bearing when subjected to combined radial and axial loads?

**Model Answer:**
The **equivalent dynamic load $P$** is a **hypothetical constant radial load** which, if applied to a bearing, would give the same fatigue life as the actual combined radial $F_r$ and axial $F_a$ loads acting simultaneously. It is computed as:
$$P = X F_r + Y F_a$$
where $X$ and $Y$ are **dimensionless factors** that account for the relative severity of the combined load.

**Determination of $X$ and $Y$ for a deep groove ball bearing:**

1. **Compute the load ratio** $F_a / F_r$ for the specific application.
2. **Compute the ratio** $F_a / C_0$ where $C_0$ is the static load rating of the chosen bearing.
3. From the **manufacturer catalogue table** (e.g., SKF, FAG), locate the **threshold value $e$** corresponding to the computed $F_a / C_0$.
4. **Compare** $F_a / F_r$ with $e$:
   - If $F_a / F_r \leq e$: then $P = F_r$ (the axial load has negligible effect), and $X = 1, Y = 0$.
   - If $F_a / F_r > e$: then use $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ with $X$ and $Y$ read from the catalogue row.
5. The factors $X$ and $Y$ typically range between 0.3 – 0.6 and 0.9 – 1.8 respectively for ball bearings.

The values are determined empirically by manufacturer testing to match the Hertzian contact stress distribution under combined loading. (7 marks)

---

### PART B — CHOICE B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, PERAT521, Module 4 Internal Choice B]**

#### Question B – Part (a) (7 Marks)
A machine operates with the following load spectrum on its bearing:
- 25% of operating time: $F_1 = 3000$ N
- 50% of operating time: $F_2 = 6000$ N
- 25% of operating time: $F_3 = 9000$ N

The bearing runs at 1500 rpm and is a 6206 deep groove ball bearing with $C = 19.5$ kN, $C_0 = 11.2$ kN. Desired life is 10000 hours. Compute the life achieved.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Variable load case: compute equivalent load $P_{eq}$ for $p = 3$:**
$$P_{eq} = \left(q_1 P_1^3 + q_2 P_2^3 + q_3 P_3^3\right)^{1/3}$$
$$P_{eq} = \left(0.25(3000)^3 + 0.50(6000)^3 + 0.25(9000)^3\right)^{1/3}$$
$$P_{eq} = \left(0.25(2.7 \times 10^{10}) + 0.50(2.16 \times 10^{11}) + 0.25(7.29 \times 10^{11})\right)^{1/3}$$
$$P_{eq} = \left(6.75 \times 10^9 + 1.08 \times 10^{11} + 1.8225 \times 10^{11}\right)^{1/3}$$
$$P_{eq} = \left(2.97 \times 10^{11}\right)^{1/3}$$
$$P_{eq} \approx 6685 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Compute basic life in millions of revolutions:**
$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{19500}{6685}\right)^3 = (2.917)^3 = 24.81 \text{ million rev}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to hours:**
$$L_{10h} = \frac{24.81 \times 10^6}{60 \times 1500} = 275.7 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** $L_{10h} = 275.7$ h $\ll 10000$ h required. The bearing is **grossly undersized** — must be replaced with a much larger bore bearing (likely 6212 or 6214). (7 marks)

#### Question B – Part (b) (7 Marks)
Explain the **Palmgren-Miner hypothesis** as used in rolling element bearing life estimation under variable loads. What are its limitations?

**Model Answer:**
The **Palmgren-Miner linear damage accumulation rule** (originally developed for metal fatigue and adapted to bearings) states that:
$$\sum_i \frac{N_i}{N_{i,R}} = 1 \quad \text{at failure}$$
where $N_i$ is the number of revolutions actually applied at load $P_i$, and $N_{i,R}$ is the rating life under constant load $P_i$.

For bearing design with load spectrum $(P_i, q_i)$ where $q_i$ is the time fraction:
$$L_{10} = \frac{1}{\sum_i q_i / L_{10,i}} = \left(\sum_i q_i \, P_i^{\,p}\right)^{-1} \cdot C^p \quad \text{million rev}$$

**Procedure:**
1. Compute the life $L_{10,i}$ as if load $P_i$ were constant throughout.
2. Weight by time fraction $q_i$.
3. Combine using the harmonic sum: $L_{10}^{-1} = \sum_i q_i L_{10,i}^{-1}$.

**Limitations of Palmgren-Miner for bearings:**

1. **Order-independence:** It assumes load sequence does not affect life, but in reality, **load history matters** (e.g., overload followed by light load is worse than the reverse).
2. **No plastic strain accumulation:** Real bearings exhibit ratcheting and micro-spalling that the linear rule ignores.
3. **Same exponent for all damage modes:** Subsurface and surface-initiated fatigue have different damage characteristics.
4. **Inadequate for stochastic loads:** Modern standards (ISO 16281) use **stress-cycle integration** for stochastic spectra, not just discrete step loads.
5. **Static overload episodes:** A single very high static load can damage the bearing immediately, violating the linear damage assumption. (7 marks)

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students most commonly lose marks in bearing design problems (PERAT521):**
>
> 1. **Skipping the catalogue lookup step** for $X$, $Y$, $e$. Always explicitly state $F_a / C_0$ and $F_a / F_r$ before reading factors. **−2 marks** if skipped.
> 2. **Confusing $L_{10}$ (millions of rev) and $L_{10h}$ (hours)** — they are related by the speed factor $60N/10^6$. Writing $L_{10h} = (C/P)^p$ is a **fatal dimensional error (−3 marks)**.
> 3. **Using $p = 3$ for roller bearings** or vice versa. This is the single most common error. **−3 marks**.
> 4. **Forgetting to check static load** when the application involves very low speeds or heavy shock. Examiners frequently allocate a 7-mark sub-part entirely to this check.
> 5. **Writing $C = 4415 \times (1740)^{1/3}$ without showing the cube root intermediate step** — you must show the numerical evaluation: $1740^{1/3} = 12.03$ explicitly.
> 6. **Not verifying the modified life** after a catalogue selection — examiners look for the $a_1, a_2, a_3, a_{ISO}$ chain. A bare $C_{req}$ computation is incomplete in a 14-mark answer.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **Use this as the last-page glance before entering the KTU exam hall for PERAT521.**

- [x] **Bearing types** — Deep Groove (6000), Angular Contact (7000), Tapered (30000), Cylindrical (NU/N), Spherical (222xx), Thrust (51000).
- [x] **Standard dynamic load rating $C$** = load for $10^6$ rev at 90% reliability.
- [x] **Standard static load rating $C_0$** = load for 0.0001$D$ permanent deformation.
- [x] **Equivalent dynamic load** $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ (or $P = F_r$ if $F_a/F_r \leq e$).
- [x] **Equivalent static load** $P_0 = X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a \leq C_0$.
- [x] **Basic life equation** $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$ — millions of revolutions.
- [x] **Life in hours** $L_{10h} = (10^6/60N)(C/P)^p$.
- [x] **Required $C$** $C_{req} = P \cdot (60N L_{10h}/10^6)^{1/p}$.
- [x] **Exponent $p$** = 3 for balls, $10/3$ for rollers.
- [x] **Modified life** $L_{nm} = a_1 a_{ISO} a_2 a_3 (C/P)^p$.
- [x] **Reliability factor $a_1$** = 1.0 (90%), 0.62 (99%), 0.21 (99.9%).
- [x] **Application factor $a_2$** = 1.0 (smooth) to 1.8+ (heavy shock).
- [x] **Variable load** — use Palmgren-Miner: $P_{eq} = (\sum q_i P_i^{\,p})^{1/p}$.
- [x] **Design sequence** — Loads → Type → $P$ → $C_{req}$ → Catalogue → Modified life → Static check → Lubrication.
- [x] **KTU high-weight keywords** — Hertzian contact, Weibull distribution, EHD film, $\kappa = \nu/\nu_1$, ISO 15 / 76 / 281:2007 / 16281.
- [x] **Common mistake to avoid** — never confuse $L_{10}$ (in millions of rev) with $L_{10h}$ (in hours); always apply the factor $60N/10^6$ when converting.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Needle Bearings
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT521 - DESIGN OF TRANSMISSION ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Needle Bearings

# Needle Bearings — KTU Board-Ready Study Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *A needle bearing is a type of rolling-element bearing that uses long, slender cylindrical rollers, referred to as "needles," whose length is typically **3 to 10 times** greater than their diameter. They are used where radial load capacity must be maximized within a **minimal radial cross-section**.*

In machine design, when an engineer faces a design challenge that demands **high radial load support in a tightly constrained radial envelope**, the needle bearing becomes the default selection. Unlike standard ball bearings (point contact) or conventional cylindrical roller bearings (line contact), the needle bearing maintains a **line contact** load geometry but with a fundamentally different length-to-diameter (L/D) ratio.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Academic Distinction (KTU Board Expectation):**
> A roller is classified as a *needle* when its length $L \geq 3.5 \, D_w$, where $D_w$ is the roller diameter. Conventional cylindrical rollers have $L \leq 2.5 \, D_w$.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Forest of Pencils" Intuition

Imagine you need to support a heavy wooden plank (the rotating shaft) above the ground using only a very thin layer of support (limited radial space). If you try using a few large rolling pins (regular rollers), you need thick walls to house them. Instead, **stand hundreds of sharp pencils vertically** between the plank and the ground — they pack densely into a thin layer, yet collectively support enormous weight because **each pencil provides a long line of contact** with the plank above.

That, in essence, is a needle bearing:
- **Plank** = Shaft (rotating element)
- **Ground** = Bearing housing
- **Pencils** = Needle rollers
- **Thin layer** = Limited radial envelope

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Roller length-to-diameter ratio:** $\dfrac{L}{D_w} \geq 3.5$ (needle threshold)
- **Typical $D_w$ range:** $1.5 \text{ mm} \leq D_w \leq 6 \text{ mm}$
- **Contact angle:** $\alpha = 0°$ (pure radial)
- **Standard load rating symbols (ISO 15 / IS 6456):**
  - $C$ = Basic dynamic load rating (in **N** or **kN**)
  - $C_0$ = Basic static load rating (in **N** or **kN**)
  - $P$ = Equivalent dynamic load (in **N**)
  - $P_0$ = Equivalent static load (in **N**)
  - $L_{10}$ or $L_{10h}$ = Rating life (revolutions / hours)

### Why Needle Bearings? — The Three Driving Design Drivers

1. **Radial Space Constraint:** Gearboxes, planetary systems, rocker arms, and transmission countershafts have shafts in close proximity — only needle bearings fit.
2. **High Radial Load Capacity:** Line contact → larger contact ellipse → higher load per unit length than a ball.
3. **Simplified Shaft & Housing Machining:** Many variants run directly on a hardened shaft, eliminating the need for an inner ring.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** *"Needles pack tight, fight height — they **save radial space**, carry **radial loads**, and **simplify assembly**."*

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Anatomy

A needle bearing consists of the following interacting components:

- **Needle Rollers ($D_w$, $L$):** The load-carrying elements. Manufactured from through-hardened chrome steel (AISI 52100 / 100Cr6) with surface finish $\leq 0.2 \, \mu m$ Ra.
- **Raceways:**
  - *Inner race:* Either a hardened shaft or a separate inner ring.
  - *Outer race:* Either a drawn cup (sheet metal) or a machined ring.
- **Cage (Retainer):** Holds rollers in circumferential spacing, prevents skewing, and guides lubricant. Materials: pressed steel, brass, polyamide (PA66), or PEEK.
- **Seals (optional):** Shield or seal variants prevent contamination ingress and lubricant loss.

### 2.2 Classification of Needle Bearings (KTU-Mandated)

| **Type** | **Construction** | **Typical Application** |
|---|---|---|
| **Drawn Cup (HK, BK series)** | Sheet-steel outer race, no inner race | Gearboxes, universal joints |
| **Machined Ring (NK, NKS)** | Machined outer + optional inner ring | Heavy-duty gearboxes |
| **Needle Roller & Cage Assembly (K, KZK)** | Only rollers + cage, races on shaft | Connecting rods, rocker arms |
| **Thrust Needle Bearing (AXK, NTA)** | Flat washers + needle cage | Combined radial + axial loads |
| **Combined Needle Bearing (NKX, NX)** | Needle + thrust ball in one unit | Machine tool spindles |

### 2.3 Operating Principle — Why a Needle Carries More Load

Consider Hertzian line contact. For two cylinders in line contact under radial load $Q$ per roller, the **contact stress distribution** is elliptical along the roller length. The **projected contact area** is:

$$
A_{proj} = L_{eff} \cdot \ell
$$

where $L_{eff}$ is the effective roller length (accounting for edge loading) and $\ell$ is the Hertzian contact half-width. Compared with a ball bearing where the contact is a small ellipse (point contact), the needle's elongated line provides a **larger load-bearing footprint per roller**, distributing stress over a greater surface.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table — Memorize Before the Board Exam**

| **#** | **Parameter / Concept** | **Formula** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Needle L/D criterion | $\dfrac{L}{D_w} \geq 3.5$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Equivalent dynamic load (pure radial needle) | $P = F_r$ | N |
| 3 | Equivalent dynamic load (with axial + radial) | $P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$ | N |
| 4 | Equivalent static load | $P_0 = F_r$ (for $\alpha = 0°$) | N |
| 5 | Basic dynamic load rating (manufacturer-given) | $C$ | N |
| 6 | Rating life (millions of revolutions) | $L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p$ | $10^6$ revs |
| 7 | Rating life (hours) | $L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6}{60 \, n} \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p$ | hours |
| 8 | Exponent for needle bearings | $p = \dfrac{10}{3}$ | — |
| 9 | Static safety factor | $f_s = \dfrac{C_0}{P_0} \geq 1.5$ (typical) | dimensionless |
| 10 | Hertzian line-contact half-width | $\ell = 1.13 \sqrt{\dfrac{Q \cdot \rho^*}{\Sigma \rho} \cdot \dfrac{1}{L_{eff}}}$ | mm |
| 11 | Bearing pressure | $p = \dfrac{Q}{D_w \cdot L_{eff}}$ | MPa |
| 12 | pv (pressure–velocity) limit | $pv \leq [pv]_{allow}$ | MPa·m/s |

> Where:
> - $\rho^*$ = radius of curvature (mm)
> - $\Sigma \rho$ = sum of curvatures (1/mm)
> - $n$ = rotational speed (rpm)
> - $f_s$ = static safety factor

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Needle bearings dominate in:
- **Automotive transmissions** (input/output countershafts, where gears slide axially over shafts).
- **Two-wheeler gearboxes** (every gear in a 4/5-speed motorcycle gearbox rides on a needle bearing).
- **Rocker arms & valve trains** (I.C. engines) — where reciprocating load demands line contact.
- **Industrial planetary gearboxes** and **helicopter rotor swashplates**.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Rating Life Equation

The rating life is fundamentally an empirical-statistical relationship calibrated against ISO 281 / IS 6456 test data. The derivation below shows the transition from the basic load-life relationship to the working formula.

**Step 1 — Empirical Starting Point (Lundberg–Palmgren Foundation)**

For a rolling element bearing under load $P$, the probability of survival $S$ over $N$ revolutions is:

$$
\ln \left(\dfrac{1}{S}\right) \propto N^e \cdot \tau^e \cdot V
$$

where $e$ is the Weibull slope, $\tau$ is the orthogonal shear stress at the critical contact depth, and $V$ is the stressed volume.

**Step 2 — Integration Over the Line Contact**

For a line contact of effective length $L_{eff}$, the stressed volume scales linearly with $L_{eff}$:

$$
V \propto L_{eff} \cdot \ell^2
$$

When the load per roller $Q$ doubles, the contact half-width $\ell$ scales as $Q^{0.5}$ (Hertzian theory), so:

$$
V \propto L_{eff} \cdot Q
$$

**Step 3 — The Power Law Emerges**

Substituting back into the Weibull survival equation and defining the rating life $L_{10}$ as the life at which 90% of an identical bearing population survives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{10} &= \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p \\
p &= \dfrac{10}{3} \quad \text{(needle rollers)} \\
p &= 3 \quad \text{(ball bearings)} \\
p &= \dfrac{10}{3} \quad \text{(all roller bearings including needle)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Conversion to Hours**

The number of hours of operation at speed $n$ (rpm) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{10h} &= \dfrac{L_{10} \times 10^6 \text{ revolutions}}{60 \cdot n \text{ rev/min}} \\
L_{10h} &= \dfrac{10^6}{60 \cdot n} \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the working equation for KTU board problems.

### 3.2 Numerical Example — A Worked-Out KTU Problem

> **Problem:** A needle bearing in a motorcycle gearbox carries a radial load $F_r = 4500 \text{ N}$. The bearing has a basic dynamic load rating $C = 25 \, 200 \text{ N}$ and basic static load rating $C_0 = 31 \, 000 \text{ N}$. The shaft rotates at $n = 3500 \text{ rpm}$. Calculate:
> 1. The rating life in millions of revolutions.
> 2. The rating life in hours.
> 3. The static safety factor.
> 4. Comment on the suitability for a service life of 4000 hours.

**Solution:**

*Step 1 — Identify load type.*
The bearing is subjected to pure radial load (typical needle roller bearing, $\alpha = 0°$).

$$
P = F_r = 4500 \text{ N}
$$

*Step 2 — Apply the rating life equation.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{10} &= \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p \\
L_{10} &= \left(\dfrac{25\,200}{4500}\right)^{10/3} \\
L_{10} &= \left(5.6\right)^{3.333}
\end{aligned}
$$

To evaluate $(5.6)^{3.333}$:

$$
\ln(5.6) = 1.7228
$$

$$
3.333 \times 1.7228 = 5.7414
$$

$$
e^{5.7414} \approx 311.2
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{10} \approx 311.2 \text{ million revolutions}}
$$

*Step 3 — Convert to hours.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{10h} &= \dfrac{10^6}{60 \cdot n} \cdot L_{10} \\
L_{10h} &= \dfrac{10^6 \times 311.2}{60 \times 3500} \\
L_{10h} &= \dfrac{3.112 \times 10^8}{2.1 \times 10^5} \\
L_{10h} &= 1481.9 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{10h} \approx 1482 \text{ hours}}
$$

*Step 4 — Static safety factor.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_0 &= F_r = 4500 \text{ N} \\
f_s &= \dfrac{C_0}{P_0} = \dfrac{31\,000}{4500} \\
f_s &\approx 6.89
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_s \approx 6.89}
$$

*Step 5 — Service-life comment.*

The required service life (4000 h) **exceeds** the calculated $L_{10h} = 1482$ h. The bearing is **NOT suitable**. Recommendations:
- Upgrade to a bearing with a higher $C$ rating such that $\left(\dfrac{C}{4500}\right)^{10/3} \geq \dfrac{4000 \times 60 \times 3500}{10^6} = 840$.
- Required $C \geq 4500 \times 840^{3/10} \approx 4500 \times 6.45 \approx 29\,000 \text{ N}$ (minimum), but with safety margin aim for $C \geq 35\,000 \text{ N}$.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Needle Bearing Selection

| **Operating Condition** | **Recommended Variant** | **Rationale** |
|---|---|---|
| Tight radial envelope + moderate load | Drawn cup (HK series) | No inner ring → saves axial space |
| High load + high speed | Machined ring (NK series) | Better heat dissipation, rigid races |
| Combined radial + axial load | Combined needle bearing (NKX) | Integrated thrust washer |
| Oscillatory motion (rocker arms) | Needle roller + cage (K series) | Low inertia, runs directly on shaft |
| Contaminated environment | Sealed variant (HK..2RS) | Shields extend service interval |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Anatomy of a Needle Bearing

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["OUTER RACE"] --> nodeB["DRAWN CUP OR MACHINED RING"]
    nodeC["NEEDLE ROLLER SET"] --> nodeD["L OVER DW GREATER THAN 3.5"]
    nodeE["CAGE OR RETAINER"] --> nodeF["GUIDES NEEDLES PREVENTS SKEWING"]
    nodeG["INNER RACE OR SHAFT"] --> nodeH["HARDENED JOURNAL 58 TO 62 HRC"]
    nodeI["SEAL OR SHIELD"] --> nodeJ["OPTIONAL CONTAMINATION BARRIER"]

    nodeB --> nodeK["LOAD DISTRIBUTION RING"]
    nodeD --> nodeK
    nodeF --> nodeK
    nodeH --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeK

    nodeK --> nodeL["HERTZIAN LINE CONTACT ZONE"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Design Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["START DESIGN DECISION"] --> nodeQ1["RADIAL SPACE AVAILABLE"]
    nodeQ1 -- LESS THAN 10 MM --> nodeQ2["SELECT NEEDLE BEARING"]
    nodeQ1 -- MORE THAN 15 MM --> nodeQ3["USE CYLINDRICAL OR TAPERED ROLLER"]

    nodeQ2 --> nodeQ4["LOAD CONDITION"]
    nodeQ4 -- PURE RADIAL --> nodeQ5["DRAWN CUP HK SERIES"]
    nodeQ4 -- RADIAL PLUS AXIAL --> nodeQ6["COMBINED NKX SERIES"]
    nodeQ4 -- OSCILLATORY --> nodeQ7["NEEDLE PLUS CAGE K SERIES"]

    nodeQ5 --> nodeQ8["VERIFY L10H GREATER THAN SERVICE LIFE"]
    nodeQ6 --> nodeQ8
    nodeQ7 --> nodeQ8

    nodeQ8 -- YES --> nodeQ9["FINALIZE SELECTION"]
    nodeQ8 -- NO --> nodeQ10["UPGRADE C RATING OR USE TWO BEARINGS"]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Hertzian Line Contact Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeShaft["SHAFT RADIUS R1"] --> nodeContact["LINE CONTACT ELLIPSE LENGTH L ELL HALF WIDTH"]
    nodeHousing["HOUSING RADIUS R2"] --> nodeContact
    nodeLoad["APPLIED LOAD Q PER ROLLER"] --> nodeContact
    nodeContact --> nodeStress["PRESSURE P EQUALS Q DIVIDED BY L EFFECTIVE TIMES L"]
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
>
> **Q1.** Define a needle roller bearing. What is the length-to-diameter criterion that distinguishes a needle from a conventional cylindrical roller?

**Model Answer:**
A needle roller bearing is a rolling-element bearing that uses slender cylindrical rollers, called needles, whose length is at least **3.5 times** their diameter ($\dfrac{L}{D_w} \geq 3.5$). These bearings are used when high radial load capacity is required within a limited radial space. Conventional cylindrical rollers, by contrast, have $L/D_w \leq 2.5$, making them shorter and stockier. Needle bearings provide line contact between the roller and raceway, offering higher load capacity than ball bearings in compact envelopes, and they commonly run directly on a hardened shaft without requiring an inner ring.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
>
> **Q2.** Why are needle bearings preferred over ball bearings in motorcycle gearboxes?

**Model Answer:**
Motorcycle gearboxes have **limited radial space** between parallel countershafts and demand **high radial load support** for each gear. Needle bearings are preferred because:
1. Their long, slender rollers ($\dfrac{L}{D_w} \geq 3.5$) provide **line contact**, yielding higher load capacity per unit radial envelope than a ball's point contact.
2. They can run **directly on a hardened shaft**, eliminating the inner ring and reducing axial stack-up.
3. They accommodate **slight misalignment** due to the long roller geometry.
4. They handle **oscillatory motion** (gear sliding during shifting) without premature failure, making them ideal for the dynamic load environment of motorcycle transmissions.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

> **(a)** A needle bearing supports a radial load of $F_r = 5200 \text{ N}$ at a shaft speed of $n = 2800 \text{ rpm}$. The bearing's basic dynamic load rating is $C = 28\,000 \text{ N}$ and basic static load rating is $C_0 = 36\,000 \text{ N}$. Determine:
> 1. The rating life in millions of revolutions.
> 2. The rating life in operating hours.
> 3. The static safety factor.
> 4. Whether the bearing is suitable for a target life of **3500 hours**.
>
> **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare needle bearings with cylindrical roller bearings and ball bearings on the basis of (i) load-carrying capacity, (ii) radial space requirement, (iii) cost, (iv) speed capability, and (v) misalignment tolerance. State two real-world applications of each.
>
> **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (Part a) — 7 Marks

**Given:**

$$
F_r = 5200 \text{ N}, \quad n = 2800 \text{ rpm}, \quad C = 28\,000 \text{ N}, \quad C_0 = 36\,000 \text{ N}
$$

For pure radial needle bearing, $P = F_r$ and $p = \dfrac{10}{3}$.

**Step 1 — Rating life in millions of revolutions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{10} &= \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{10/3} \\
&= \left(\dfrac{28\,000}{5200}\right)^{10/3} \\
&= (5.3846)^{3.3333}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\ln(5.3846) = 1.6828
$$

$$
3.3333 \times 1.6828 = 5.609
$$

$$
e^{5.609} \approx 272.4
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{10} \approx 272.4 \text{ million revolutions}}
$$

**Step 2 — Rating life in hours:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{10h} &= \dfrac{10^6 \times L_{10}}{60 \cdot n} \\
&= \dfrac{10^6 \times 272.4}{60 \times 2800} \\
&= \dfrac{2.724 \times 10^8}{1.68 \times 10^5} \\
&= 1621.4 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{10h} \approx 1621 \text{ hours}}
$$

**Step 3 — Static safety factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_s &= \dfrac{C_0}{P_0} = \dfrac{C_0}{F_r} \\
&= \dfrac{36\,000}{5200} = 6.92
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_s \approx 6.92}
$$

**Step 4 — Suitability:**

Target life = 3500 h, but $L_{10h} = 1621$ h. Since $L_{10h} < 3500$ h, **the bearing is NOT suitable**. Required upgrade: $C_{new} \geq P \times \left(\dfrac{L_{10h, target} \cdot 60 \cdot n}{10^6}\right)^{3/10} = 5200 \times \left(\dfrac{3500 \times 60 \times 2800}{10^6}\right)^{0.3}$.

$$
\left(\dfrac{3500 \times 168000}{10^6}\right)^{0.3} = (588)^{0.3} \approx 6.46
$$

$$
C_{new} \geq 5200 \times 6.46 \approx 33\,592 \text{ N}
$$

A bearing with $C \geq 34\,000 \text{ N}$ must be selected.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (Part b) — 7 Marks

| **Parameter** | **Ball Bearing** | **Cylindrical Roller** | **Needle Bearing** |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Load capacity | Moderate (point contact) | High (line contact) | High (line contact, dense packing) |
| (ii) Radial space | High (thick section) | High (shorter rollers but wide) | **Lowest** (very thin section) |
| (iii) Cost | Moderate | Moderate to high | Low to moderate |
| (iv) Speed capability | **Highest** | Moderate | Limited (roller skew at high speed) |
| (v) Misalignment tolerance | Low | Very low | **Highest** (long rollers flex) |
| Real-world apps | Electric motors, machine tool spindles | Railway axles, heavy gearboxes | Motorcycles, rocker arms, planetary gears |

**Applications:**
- **Ball bearings:** Electric motors, hard-drive spindles, bicycle hubs.
- **Cylindrical roller:** Heavy industrial gearboxes, rolling mills, railway axleboxes.
- **Needle bearings:** Connecting rods in I.C. engines, motorcycle gearboxes, universal joints.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

> **(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **drawn-cup needle roller bearing**. List three typical applications.
>
> **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A needle bearing carries a combined load with radial component $F_r = 4000 \text{ N}$ and axial component $F_a = 800 \text{ N}$. The bearing factors are $X = 0.5$ and $Y = 1.4$. If $C = 22\,000 \text{ N}$, $C_0 = 28\,000 \text{ N}$, and $n = 3000 \text{ rpm}$, determine:
> 1. Equivalent dynamic load $P$.
> 2. Rating life in hours.
> 3. Whether the bearing is statically safe (assume $f_{s,min} = 1.5$).
>
> **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (Part a) — 7 Marks

**Construction of a Drawn-Cup Needle Bearing:**

A drawn-cup needle bearing consists of:
- **Outer race (cup):** A thin, deep-drawn sheet-steel cup (typically 0.5–1.5 mm thick) with a polished, hardened inner bore serving as the outer raceway.
- **Needle rollers:** A set of needle rollers (length ≥ 3.5 × diameter) arranged circumferentially.
- **Cage:** A pressed-steel or polymer cage that retains and spaces the needles.
- **No inner ring:** The rollers run directly on the hardened shaft surface (typically 58–62 HRC).

**Working Principle:**

Radial load $F_r$ is transmitted from the shaft → through the needle rollers (line contact) → through the cage (only positional) → into the outer cup → into the housing bore. The drawn cup itself becomes the outer raceway, eliminating the need for a separate machined outer ring. The thin section makes the bearing ideal for applications where the shaft is supported inside a gear or pulley hub.

**Applications (any three):**
1. Motorcycle gearbox countershafts.
2. Universal joint yokes.
3. Agricultural machinery (planter shafts, mower spindles).
4. Electric tool gearboxes.
5. Automotive accessory drives (alternator overrunning clutches).

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (Part b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Equivalent dynamic load:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a \\
&= 0.5 \times 4000 + 1.4 \times 800 \\
&= 2000 + 1120 \\
&= 3120 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{P = 3120 \text{ N}}
$$

**Step 2 — Rating life in hours:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{10} &= \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{10/3} \\
&= \left(\dfrac{22\,000}{3120}\right)^{3.333} \\
&= (7.0513)^{3.333}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\ln(7.0513) = 1.9528
$$

$$
3.333 \times 1.9528 = 6.509
$$

$$
e^{6.509} \approx 670.0
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{10} \approx 670 \text{ million revolutions}}
$$

Converting to hours:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{10h} &= \dfrac{10^6 \times 670}{60 \times 3000} \\
&= \dfrac{6.70 \times 10^8}{1.80 \times 10^5} \\
&\approx 3722 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{10h} \approx 3722 \text{ hours}}
$$

**Step 3 — Static safety check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_0 &= F_r = 4000 \text{ N} \quad \text{(for needle, } \alpha = 0° \text{)} \\
f_s &= \dfrac{C_0}{P_0} = \dfrac{28\,000}{4000} = 7.0
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $f_s = 7.0 \geq f_{s,min} = 1.5$, **the bearing is statically safe**.

$$
\boxed{f_s = 7.0 \quad (\text{safe})}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong exponent:** Many students use $p = 3$ (ball bearing exponent) instead of $p = 10/3$ for needle bearings. **Both are valid for ISO 281, but KTU examiners specifically expect $p = 10/3$ for rollers/needles.** Deduct 1 mark if you forget this.
> 2. **Load equivalence for pure radial:** Some students mistakenly apply $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ even when $F_a = 0$. For pure radial needle bearings, **$P = F_r$ directly**. Avoid overcomplicating.
> 3. **Forgetting unit conversions:** $L_{10h}$ formula demands $n$ in **rpm** and yields hours. A common error is using $n$ in rps (rev/sec), producing $L_{10h}$ off by a factor of 60. Always verify units.
> 4. **Static safety factor confusion:** $f_s = C_0 / P_0$ is **NOT** the same as $C/P$. Using dynamic rating in the static formula is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 5. **Missing L/D criterion:** If a question asks you to "define a needle bearing," omitting the $L/D \geq 3.5$ condition will cost a mark. This is the **single most-tested sentence** in KTU Part A.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read This 5 Minutes Before the Exam**

- ✅ A **needle** has $L/D_w \geq 3.5$; a **conventional cylindrical roller** has $L/D_w \leq 2.5$.
- ✅ Needle bearings give **line contact**, so use $p = 10/3$ in the rating-life equation.
- ✅ For pure radial needle bearing: $P = F_r$ and $P_0 = F_r$.
- ✅ For combined load: $P = X F_r + Y F_a$; consult manufacturer tables for $X, Y$.
- ✅ Rating life in revolutions: $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$.
- ✅ Rating life in hours: $L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6}{60 n} \cdot (C/P)^p$.
- ✅ Static safety factor: $f_s = C_0 / P_0 \geq 1.5$ for steady loads, $\geq 2$ for shock loads.
- ✅ Drawn-cup (HK) is the most common variant; **no inner ring** required.
- ✅ Needle bearings are **NOT** for high-speed applications (roller skewing at $n > 5000$ rpm).
- ✅ Hertzian pressure on the line contact: $p = Q / (D_w \cdot L_{eff})$; keep $p \leq 1500 \text{ MPa}$ typically.
- ✅ Applications to memorize: **motorcycle gearbox, rocker arm, connecting rod, universal joint, planetary gearbox**.
- ✅ Advantages: high radial capacity, low radial space, low cost, accepts misalignment.
- ✅ Disadvantages: limited speed, limited axial load (use combined NKX type), sensitive to contamination.
